bodyModel: NoiseBody (plate-ratio 32-mode modal bank + internal noise layer)
exciter: NoiseBurst (violet-noise bandpass burst, short contact)

params.rationale: The 808 hat has no struck transient; a violet-noise bandpass burst is the closest match to gating the bright high-passed square stack (no beater/impulse click).

NoiseBody = plate-ratio (inharmonic, metallic) 32-mode bank + internal noise layer; the modal cluster stands in for the six inharmonic square oscillators and supplies the metallic 'hum', while its noise carries the high-passed sizzle.

params.physicalTarget: NoiseBody brightness=0.7+0.3*0.92=0.976 (near-max metallic); modal base decay=lerp(0.2,1.0,0.92)=0.94 scaled; internal-noise cutoff=clamp(1500+5000*0.92)=6100 Hz, reso=0.6+0.4*0.92=0.97
params.rationale: Maxes the metallic/bright character and pushes the body's internal-noise cutoff high to mimic the ~7 kHz high-pass / very bright filtered square stack.

Small body puts the inharmonic modal cluster fundamental near the top of the 808 oscillator band (~205-800 Hz), so the metallic partials sit where the six squares do; smaller=higher=more 'tiny cymbal' synthetic.

params.physicalTarget: modal decayTime mult = exp(lerp(ln0.3,ln3.0,0.07)) = ~0.32x (near the 0.3x floor); also scales internal-noise env to ~0.32*60ms~=19 ms
params.rationale: The closed hat's VCA decay is fixed and very short (~50 ms); a near-floor Decay gives the body the right snap. Tighter than the acoustic-hat preset (0.1).
There is no physical strike location on a synthetic hat; left at default -- a mid sampling keeps a full, balanced metallic mode set.
Hats sit below kick/snare; 0.75 leaves headroom and matches the existing 808 hat voicing for kit balance.
ToneShaper SVF left as LP and effectively bypassed (cutoff at max); the brightness/high-pass character is carried by the noise layer's high cutoff + Violet color, not the post-body SVF.

params.physicalTarget: 20*1000^1 = 20000 Hz -> filter BYPASSED (cutoff>=20k and env amount 0)
params.rationale: Keep the ToneShaper transparent; the body's own brightness and the noise layer define the spectrum. Bypass avoids double-filtering.
No-op while the filter is bypassed; default.

params.physicalTarget: amount=2*0.5-1=0 -> no cutoff modulation (keeps filter bypassed)
params.rationale: No filter sweep on a static-spectrum hat; neutral keeps the ToneShaper transparent.
The 808 hat is clean filtered squares; no saturation flavour wanted.
No west-coast folding; off.
The 808 hat has no per-hit pitch glide; disabling the whole pitch-env feature is correct (sentinel).
No-op while pitch env disabled; default.
No-op while pitch env disabled; default.

params.physicalTarget: NoiseLayer mix 0.85 -> dominant parallel filtered-noise path
params.rationale: The defining sound of the 808 hat is bright high-passed noise/sizzle; the parallel noise layer carries it and must dominate over the modal body.
Very high corner passes the bright top end, emulating the 808's series BP(~3.4k)+HP(~7.1k) which leaves only the high metallic content.
Mild emphasis; default keeps the noise broad/sizzly rather than pitched (the lowpass path has no strong peak).
Short noise envelope realizes the fixed ~50 ms closed-hat VCA (body+noise together land near that perceived length); shorter than open hat.
Violet (differentiated) noise matches the high-passed, treble-tilted spectrum of the filtered square stack -- brighter than the acoustic hat's White.
The 808 hat has no discrete contact/beater click -- it is just the VCA gating the steady square sum. Explicitly off.

params.physicalTarget: b1 = 0.2+0.65*49.8 = ~33 s^-1 flat damping (overrides Decay-derived flat damping)
params.rationale: High flat damping forces a very short modal ring so the metallic body matches the ~50 ms VCA; complements the low Decay knob.

params.physicalTarget: b3 = 0 -> pure flat damping, no f^2 rolloff (metallic, highs ring as long as lows)
params.rationale: A metallic synthetic source has minimal frequency-dependent (woody) damping; keeping b3=0 preserves bright high modes (b1 already shortens overall ring).

params.physicalTarget: 0 = no Bessel air-loading correction (and a no-op on NoiseBody anyway)
params.rationale: airLoading is a Membrane-only depression of low modes; irrelevant to a metallic synthetic hat. Explicitly 0 per the archetype spec.

params.physicalTarget: ~5% sinusoidal frequency dither on the plate ratios
params.rationale: A touch of detune dither de-correlates the modal partials so the cluster beats like the six anharmonic square oscillators (which 'avoid even multiples'), rather than sounding like a tidy plate.
Plate ratios are already inharmonic/metallic; the scatter (not stretch) supplies the square-osc anharmonicity. Left neutral.

params.physicalTarget: 0.0 = no per-mode tilt / no global skew
params.rationale: No need to tilt the metallic spectrum high/low; the body brightness + noise already set the tilt. Neutral.
Closed/open/pedal 808 hats mute each other; group 1 ties this closed hat to the open/pedal hats so a new hit chokes the ring.
808 hat is centered in the classic mix; default center. (Could be nudged per kit, but center is the canonical placement.)
Active pad.

defaultedParams.why: No physical strike point on a synthetic hat; default mid-sampling keeps a full metallic mode set.

defaultedParams.param: Filter Type / Cutoff / Resonance / Env Amount/Atk/Dec/Sus/Rel
defaultedParams.why: ToneShaper SVF deliberately bypassed (cutoff 1.0, env amount 0.5); the noise layer owns the brightness, so all filter-env params are inert.
The 808 hat is clean filtered squares; no saturation or wavefolding flavour.
PitchEnv Time=0 disables the entire pitch-env feature; the 808 hat has no per-hit glide.
Plate ratios are already inharmonic; anharmonicity is supplied by Mode Scatter instead, so stretch stays neutral.
No high/low spectral-decay tilt needed beyond what body brightness + noise color set.
Injecting a 1/k harmonic series would add a tonal/pitched fundamental; the 808 hat is explicitly anharmonic/atonal, so off.
No amplitude-driven brightening wanted; the hat's brightness is static and the stage should stay exact-bypassed.
No per-note material sweep; the timbre is static. Morph off.
Global sympathetic coupling is off at kit level for an electronic hat; per-pad weight is harmless at default.
Macros are neutral deltas; the explicit base params already encode the voicing, so macros stay at zero-delta.
No head<->shell body coupling on a synthetic hat; secondary bank disabled (Coupling Strength 0).
Energy-driven pitch glide is Membrane-only and is a tom 'kerthump' effect; meaningless for a NoiseBody hat.
No-op because Click Mix=0 (the 808 hat has no contact click).

defaultedParams.param: FM Ratio / Feedback Amount / Friction Pressure
defaultedParams.why: Secondary-exciter params for FMImpulse/Feedback/Friction; no-ops with NoiseBurst selected.

Left at default; the perceived attack length is dominated by the very short noise/body decay, not the burst length, on a near-instantaneous-gate hat. Could be shortened (~0.08) for an even tighter tick if desired.
Standard routing; no aux send needed.

physics:
REAL CIRCUIT (TR-808 closed hi-hat is fully synthetic, NOT a sampled/recorded cymbal):
- Source: SIX square-wave oscillators (the SAME six shared with the 808 cymbal). Measured factory tunings (Baratatronix circuit analysis): OSC1 ~800 Hz (G5+35c, tuneable 359-1150 Hz), OSC2 ~540 Hz (C#5-45c), OSC3 ~523 Hz, OSC4 ~370 Hz, OSC5 ~304 Hz, OSC6 ~205 Hz. Span ~200-800 Hz. The intervals are deliberately INHARMONIC and avoid even multiples so the sum is a dense, beating "metallic hum" with no fused pitch -- this is the design rule for Roland-style cymbals (square waves at random/anharmonic intervals).
- Filtering: the six-square sum runs through a BANDPASS (~3.4 kHz center) in SERIES with a HIGH-PASS (~7.1 kHz). The result is a very BRIGHT, thin, high-passed metallic noise -- most low/mid energy of the squares is removed, leaving the upper harmonics/aliasing-rich edges of the square stack. Spectral centroid is high (several kHz), much brighter and more "electronic/buzzy" than an acoustic hat's broadband shimmer.
- Envelope: closed hat uses an envelope-controlled VCA with a FIXED, very short decay. TR-808 service-manual value ~50 ms (closed); fast exponential-ish VCA decay, no sustain. (Open hat = same oscillator bank, longer decay; closed/open share a choke group.)
- Transient: there is NO discrete "contact click"/beater transcient -- the attack is just the VCA opening on the steady square-osc sum (effectively instantaneous gate). Far more synthetic than an acoustic hat's stick contact.
- No pitch glide. Square-osc frequencies are static (the only modulation is decay-time via pitch CV, which selects closed vs open, not a per-hit glide).
- Material: none -- there is no physical cymbal; the timbre is 100% the inharmonic square cluster + series BP/HP filter + fast VCA.

WHAT DRIVES THE PARAM CHOICES:
- Inharmonic 200-800 Hz square cluster -> a small-Size NoiseBody (plate Chladni ratios are inharmonic & metallic; f0 ~750-950 Hz puts the modal cluster squarely in the oscillator band) PLUS the parallel noise layer carrying the bright high-passed sizzle.
- "Extremely short, fixed ~50 ms decay" -> very low Decay (~0.06-0.08 norm) and short Noise Decay (~50-60 ms), short body damping (high b1).
- "Very bright, high-passed/metallic" -> high Noise Cutoff, Violet noise color, high modal brightness (high Material), zero airLoading (membrane-only anyway, irrelevant to a metallic body).
- "No click" -> Click Mix = 0.
- "Choke (open/closed)" -> chokeGroup 1.

bodyModel: Membrane
exciter: Impulse

params.rationale: The 808 trigger is a short ~1 ms pulse that kicks the resonator; an impulse is the closest exciter and seeds a clean decaying sine, not a sustained or noisy excitation.
params.citation: Werner et al. CCRMA TR-808 BD model; Baratatronix: 1 ms trigger pulse excites self-oscillation

params.rationale: Brief mandates Membrane; with all inharmonicity axes zeroed the modal bank degenerates toward a near-single low mode = the clean decaying sub-sine of the bridged-T resonator.
params.citation: task brief; bridged-T = single resonant pole pair (Werner et al.)

params.physicalTarget: membrane brightness=0.15 (dark), decay base=lerp(0.15,0.8,0.15)=0.247x, low b3 high-freq damping
params.rationale: Dark, near-sine spectrum - the 808 has no metallic/woody material; low material kills high-mode brightness so the body reads as a pure sub.
params.citation: steady-state output is a pure sine (Werner/Baratatronix)

params.physicalTarget: f0 = 500*0.1^0.9 = 62.9 Hz (large body, low natural fundamental)
params.rationale: Puts the body's natural mode in the 808 sub range so the post-glide ring sits at the real fundamental; low f0 = weak b3*f^2 damping = long sine ring.
params.citation: 808 fundamental 48-56 Hz, sub range 30-80 Hz (service manual 56 Hz; calc 49.4 Hz)

params.physicalTarget: decayTime = base * exp(lerp(ln0.3,ln3.0,0.35)) ~= base*0.49; ~250-400 ms boom
params.rationale: Lands near the 808's 300 ms center-detent decay - boomy but not a drone.
params.citation: 808 BD decay 50-800 ms, 300 ms at knob center (Baratatronix)

params.rationale: Default; with inharmonicity zeroed strike position barely matters (near-single mode).
The kick is the loudest element; slightly below unity leaves headroom for the -1 dBTP bus limiter (post N-1 measured-strike norm keeps the single voice off the rail).
Start of the 808 boom sweep; an audible widening of the real ~130 Hz attack peak so the iconic downward boom is present.
params.citation: 808 attack pushes tuning up to ~130 Hz then relaxes (Baratatronix); brief specifies 200 Hz

params.rationale: Settle pitch of the glide = the 808 fundamental (slightly below the ~50 Hz measured center for a deep trap-flavoured sub, per brief).

params.physicalTarget: 0.06*500 = 30 ms glide duration (enables the pitch env)
params.rationale: Master-enables the sweep; 30 ms is the audible boom compromise between the real 6 ms snap and the longer producer-dialed sweep.
params.citation: real glide ~6 ms snap (Baratatronix); brief specifies 30 ms

params.rationale: Classic 808 exponential pitch drop - the tuning falls fast then settles, matching the RC relaxation of the attack envelope.
params.citation: 808 pitch envelope is an RC relaxation toward the fundamental (Werner et al.)

params.rationale: Inharmonicity OFF - the bridged-T is a single pole, no inharmonic ladder.
params.citation: single resonant pole, no inharmonic partials (Werner et al.)

params.rationale: No spectral decay tilt needed for a single-mode sine.
No synthetic harmonic series - the 808 sub is a clean sine; added harmonics would defeat the archetype.
No amplitude-driven brightening; the 808 does not brighten with level (saturation is added downstream by the user).
params.citation: users add saturation post-hoc to boost overtones (search corpus)

params.rationale: The 808 BD voice has no noise generator - it is a deterministic resonator; any hiss is wrong.
params.citation: 808 BD = bridged-T resonator, no noise source (Werner et al.)

params.physicalTarget: low click transient level (0.35) into exciter+output
params.rationale: The 808 has a complex transient but is intentionally click-LIGHT (a lowpass reduces its click); a small click adds attack definition without an acoustic-beater knock.
params.citation: transient smoothly becomes the sine; lowpass reduces transient click (Baratatronix)

params.rationale: Very short contact matching the ~1 ms pulse edge / fast onset.
Dark click - the 808 transient is low-mid, not a sharp HF tick; keeps the snap subby.
params.citation: transient is low/smooth, click reducible by lowpass (Baratatronix)

params.rationale: Brief mandates airLoading 0 - this is a circuit, not an air-loaded membrane.
params.citation: task brief; 808 is electronic not acoustic

params.rationale: A circuit resonator is deterministic - no organic detune.
Brief mandates coupling 0 - no shell body in an 808 sub.
Brief mandates secondary off - single clean resonator only.

params.citation: 808 attack briefly pushes tuning up ~130 Hz then relaxes (Baratatronix); energy-linear tension (Avanzini-Marogna-Bank 2012)

params.rationale: Kick belongs dead-center in the stereo image.

physics:
REAL ACOUSTICS / ESTABLISHED SYNTHESIS OF THE TR-808 BASS DRUM (the target is the CIRCUIT, not a real membrane).

Sound source: a bridged-T bandpass network momentarily kicked into self-oscillation by a ~1 ms trigger pulse (3.5 V baseline, up to 13.5 V with accent), producing a DECAYING SINE WAVE at the network's resonant frequency. Roland chose the bridged-T because it self-oscillates with very few parts. (Werner et al. CCRMA "A Physically-Informed, Circuit-Bendable Digital Model of the Roland TR-808 Bass Drum"; Baratatronix 808 BD synthesis breakdown.)

Fundamental / pitch: service manual lists 56 Hz; measured units as low as 48 Hz; transfer-function calc 49.4 Hz (G1 +14 cents). So the canonical settle pitch is ~50 Hz, in the 30-80 Hz sub range. Membrum maps this with PitchEnv End and body Size.

Partial structure / inharmonicity: in steady state the output is an essentially PURE single sinusoid (one resonant pole pair) - there is NO modal/inharmonic partial ladder (this is exactly why the brief zeroes all physics axes - a real membrane's Bessel inharmonicity is WRONG here). Harmonics exist only at the very onset (transient + slight transistor saturation seeding 2nd harmonic) and fade within tens of ms; users add distortion downstream to boost the overtones that fade out.

Pitch glide ("snap"): an attack envelope briefly pushes the tuning UP to ~130 Hz (C3 -11 cents) and relaxes back to the ~50 Hz fundamental in ~6 ms. Too fast to hear as a pitch SHIFT; it is heard as the percussive SNAP/attack. The brief widens this to an audible 200->40 Hz / 30 ms glide so the iconic downward boom sweep is musically present on Membrum's body (a hybrid of the real 6 ms snap and the longer 808 boom sweep producers dial in).

Per-band decay (T60): single-band exponential, knob range 50-800 ms, 300 ms at center. Decay length is set by how much signal is fed back into the bridged-T (more feedback = longer boom, less = short/clicky).

Transient / contact click: a complex transient that quickly but smoothly becomes the decaying sine. Click energy is low-mid (the pulse edge), and a lowpass is the documented way to REDUCE the transient click - the 808 kick is intentionally click-light vs an acoustic kick beater. Hence Membrum's click layer is set low and dark.

Noise content: essentially NONE. The 808 BD voice has no noise generator; it is a deterministic resonator. So Membrum's parallel noise layer is muted.

Material: not a physical material - it is an electronic resonator. Membrum's Material is set very low (0.15) to make the body spectrum dark/sine-like; the perceptual lever is Size pushing f0 low (~63 Hz) so the b3*f^2 high-mode damping is weak and the near-sine rings long.

Net Membrum target: Membrane body driven by an Impulse, with inharmonicity/air-loading/scatter/coupling/secondary all OFF so the bank degenerates toward a near-single low mode (clean decaying sub-sine), a pitch envelope doing the 808 boom-sweep, a touch of tension glide reinforcing the attack snap, and a minimal dark click for definition. Noise off. This reproduces the bridged-T resonant-sine-with-swept-tuning rather than a struck drumhead.

bodyModel: Membrane (primary, carrying the lower 808 tone ~238 Hz) + Secondary shell bank enabled (the metallic upper resonance ~470 Hz, giving the characteristic two-tone "snappy" ring)
exciter: NoiseBurst (violet-noise bandpass burst — models the trigger-pulse "kick" into the self-damping bridged-T pair plus the initial stick snap; the sustained hiss is carried by the parallel Noise Layer)

params.rationale: Violet-noise bandpass burst models the 808 trigger-pulse kick into the bridged-T pair plus the stick snap; bright broadband attack is exactly what a snare wants.
Carries the lower 808 tone (238 Hz) as a struck-membrane body; the octave-up metallic tone is added by the secondary shell bank.
Slightly metallic-leaning so the membrane tone reads as the bright 808 ping rather than a dull tom, without losing the low-tone weight.
Tunes the primary Membrane fundamental to the measured 808 lower oscillator frequency (238 Hz, Service Notes).

params.physicalTarget: decay multiplier exp(lerp(ln0.3,ln3,0.28))~0.41x on ~0.51 s base -> body t60 ~0.21 s
params.rationale: Bridged-T pings are short and self-damping; a tight body ring keeps the tonal pop punchy and out of the way of the noise tail.
Off-centre strike de-weights the pure fundamental and excites higher membrane modes, giving the body the harder, more inharmonic 'rap' of a snare hit.
Default per-pad loudness; snare sits prominent in a kit, master/bus limiter owns the ceiling.
params.citation: AUDIT gain-staging appendix (kBodyHeadroom -6 dBFS + bus -1 dBTP)

params.rationale: ToneShaper kept as a gentle lowpass; brightness is supplied by the noise layer, so we only tame any harsh top from drive.
Leave the body full-bandwidth; the 808 snare's brightness lives in the noise path, not a body filter.

params.physicalTarget: internalDrive=1+0.48*9=5.32 into recipSqrt, unity-makeup (M-2) -> adds odd harmonics, not level
params.rationale: Prompt-specified 0.48. Post-M-2 this is a flavour saturator that thickens/grits the two-tone body the way the analog 808 output stage colours the snare.
params.citation: AUDIT M-2 (Drive = timbre not level after 1/internalDrive makeup)

params.rationale: Prompt-specified 400 Hz start: a fast downward body 'thwack' attack (stylistic 808-snare voicing; the original circuit has fixed-frequency oscillators).
Prompt-specified 110 Hz settle: the body drops to a low thud under the noise, adding weight to the hit.
Very fast drop so the pitch motion reads as attack punch, not an audible sweep; also the master-enable for the pitch env (must be >0).
Classic 808 exponential pitch decay shape — energy dumps immediately then settles.
A touch of stretch detunes the upper membrane partials so the body reads as a slightly metallic snare ping rather than a pure-pitched tom.
params.citation: AUDIT 3-B (stretch index/B_max corrected; stretch now musically audible)

params.physicalTarget: phys=0.62*2-1=+0.24 (tilt energy toward faster high modes)
params.rationale: Snare highs should die faster than the low tone; positive skew shortens the bright modes for a snappy, non-ringy body.
params.citation: AUDIT M-5 (per-mode decaySkew tilt now on all bodies)

params.rationale: The two-tone body already supplies the tonal content; added 1/k harmonics would muddy the octave dyad.
Drive already supplies the harmonic grit; keep the dynamic brightener off to avoid double-saturation.
params.citation: AUDIT M-3/M-4 (env-level waveshaping redesign; exact bypass at 0)

params.rationale: Prompt-specified 0.62 — this IS the 808 'Snappy' white-noise generator, the loudest perceptual element of a snare.
Places the noise energy in the snare-wire buzz band (3-5 kHz); the 808 noise is bright/high-passed, here approximated by a high lowpass corner over a bright noise colour.
A mild emphasis at the cutoff focuses the 'crack' band without a pitched whistle.
params.citation: AUDIT L-6 (keep noise-path Q modest on the forced-lowpass)

params.rationale: Medium Snappy tail — matches the snares-on decay (head at ~23% by 80 ms; wire buzz dies in ~100-200 ms).
The 808 noise is high-passed/bright; violet noise plus the lowpass yields a band-focused bright hiss like the snare wires.

params.physicalTarget: 0.45*vel*env*filteredNoise, half into exciter feed + full to output *0.445
params.rationale: Adds the stick-contact tick / trigger-pulse attack definition on top of the NoiseBurst.
Very short contact = a crisp transient tick, consistent with the instantaneous bridged-T trigger pulse.
Bright click centred in the snare 'smack' band for a sharp attack edge.

params.physicalTarget: 2+0.3*13 = 5.9 ms violet-noise burst into the body
params.rationale: Short, snappy contact time so the exciter kicks the body sharply (the trigger 'pings' the resonators) without smearing the attack.
Sets the membrane's metal<->wood balance explicitly so the bright modes decay a bit faster than pure-metal but slower than a dull tom — the 808 ping is metallic-tinged.
params.citation: AUDIT 3-A (Chaigne b1+b3*f^2 law; b3 = perceived material)

params.physicalTarget: 60% blend toward the Rossing air-loaded membrane curve
params.rationale: Keeps the membrane fundamental from sounding whistly/over-pitched, giving a more solid drum tone (membrane-only correction).
params.citation: AUDIT 3-A (airLoading = Rossing 1982 membrane frequency correction, Membrane-only)

params.physicalTarget: feedforward drive 0.5 from body into the secondary shell bank
params.rationale: Drives the metallic-shimmer secondary at moderate level so the octave-up tone is clearly audible but doesn't overpower the fundamental — this realises the two-tone 808 ring.
params.citation: AUDIT L-7/L-8 (couplingStrength now full 0..1 feedforward range)

params.physicalTarget: >=0.5 -> secondary shell bank spun up at noteOn
params.rationale: Enables the parallel metallic resonator that emulates the second bridged-T oscillator (the upper 808 tone).

params.physicalTarget: sizeRatio = 1 - 0.38*0.75 = 0.715; shell f0 = 238*0.715 = 170 Hz; 2nd free-free partial 2.757x ~ 469 Hz
params.rationale: Prompt-specified 0.38: places the shell's 2nd partial at ~469 Hz, ~the measured 476 Hz upper 808 oscillator — the octave-spaced two-tone dual resonance.

params.physicalTarget: shell b1 = max(25,2.5*bodyB1)+(1-0.85)*15; shell b3 = 0.85*1e-5 (bright, longer metallic highs)
params.rationale: Prompt-specified 0.85: a bright, ringy metallic shell so the secondary reads as a tuned 'shimmer' ping, not a damped thud.
Snare belongs dead-centre in a kit image.
The snare must sound.

No filter sweep wanted on a snare; default 0.5 = no modulation keeps the filter bypassed.
Wavefolding is a west-coast timbre alien to an 808 snare; Drive already supplies the harmonic grit.
Filter envelope is a no-op because Filter Env Amount is at its neutral 0.5.
No intra-hit material sweep needed; the snare's character is static after the strike.
A snare is not part of an open/closed mute group.
Snare goes to the main mix bus by default.
No-op: FMImpulse exciter is not selected.
No-op: Feedback exciter is not selected.
No-op: Friction exciter is not selected.
Global cross-pad sympathetic resonance is off at kit level for this archetype; this is distinct from the per-pad head<->shell coupling that IS used.
All left neutral at 0.5 so the explicitly-voiced underlying params are not nudged; the recipe sets the real params directly.
Left at the spec mid-default (0.5 -> 25 s^-1) rather than overriding Decay; the Decay knob (0.28) already sets the body ring length and b1 only seeds the flat-damping floor.
Pure modal ratios kept; the 808 two-tone is a clean dyad, not a detuned organic body.
Energy-dependent pitch glide ('kerthump') is a tom behaviour; the 808 snare has fixed-frequency oscillators and the prompt's pitch motion is handled by the pitch envelope instead.
Single-segment 400->110 Hz glide is sufficient; no middle breakpoint needed for a fast snare thwack.

physics: Fundamental/pitch: real 14" snare body ~150-250 Hz; TR-808 archetype = two fixed bridged-T oscillator tones at MEASURED 238 Hz and 476 Hz (octave, 2:1; Roland Service Notes 238/476 Hz). Membrum mapping: primary Membrane f0 -> 238 Hz (Size norm 0.322 = 500*0.1^0.322); secondary shell bank f0 ~170 Hz whose 2nd free-free partial (2.757x) ~469 Hz ~ the 476 Hz upper tone (Secondary Size 0.38). Partial/inharmonicity: clean octave dyad, slightly inharmonic; mild Mode Stretch 1.13 metallises the membrane upper modes. Decay per band: body ping short (Decay 0.28 -> ~0.21 s body t60, self-damping bridged-T character); noise/Snappy tail medium (~145 ms, Noise Decay 0.45) matching snares-on damping (head ~23% of peak by 80 ms, wire buzz 100-200 ms; Madsen UIUC). Transient/click: trigger pulse / stick contact = NoiseBurst exciter (5.9 ms) + Click layer (2.6 ms, ~5.2 kHz). Noise content: bright high-passed white noise -> here Violet colour + ~4.3 kHz lowpass + Q 1.48, seated in the snare-wire buzz band 3-5 kHz; Noise Mix 0.62 (the dominant 'Snappy' element). Pitch glide: original has NO glide (fixed oscillators); the prompt-specified 400->110 Hz over 25 ms (exp curve) is applied as a stylistic fast body 'thwack' and flagged as a voicing choice, not circuit-accurate.

bodyModel: Membrane (offset 1, norm 0.0). Membrane f0 = 500·0.1^size. The 808 tom is NOT a struck acoustic membrane — it is a self-oscillating bridged-T SINE with a downward pitch sweep. We pick Membrane (not String/Plate) because: (a) the pitch glide is driven by the ToneShaper pitch envelope, which the audit H-3 fix made work on ALL bodies, so any modal body would glide — but Membrane's low-order Bessel ratios collapse to a near-pure fundamental when material is low and b3 damping kills the overtones (audit §3-A: Membrane Bessel + Chaigne damping CONFIRMED correct), giving the tuned-sine 'boom'; (b) all the membrane-specific PHYSICS axes (airLoading, tension-mod) are deliberately disabled so the body behaves as a tuned resonator, not an acoustic drumhead. This matches the existing 808ElectronicKit tom builder (Mallet+Membrane), tools/membrum_preset_generator.cpp:834-863.
exciter: Mallet (offset 0, norm 0.2 → idx round(0.2·5)=1=Mallet). A soft beater deposits energy gently and band-limited (little HF), so the excited body rings as a near-sine 'tonk' rather than a bright struck-skin crack — the closest match to the 808's self-oscillating sine that rings briefly and fades (Baratatronix: 'self-dampening sine wave that rings briefly when triggered'). Impulse (raised-cosine click) would add too much broadband attack energy; Mallet is the documented choice in the existing 808 tom builder.

params.rationale: Soft band-limited excitation → near-sine ring, matches the 808's self-oscillating sine 'tonk' not a bright skin crack.
Low-order Bessel body collapses to a tuned fundamental when material is low + b3 high; pitch glide from ToneShaper env works on it (audit H-3). Matches existing 808 tom builder.

params.physicalTarget: Membrane brightness=0.22, baseDecay=lerp(0.15,0.8,0.22)·(1+0.1·size)≈0.29 base; low brightness → strong HF mode damping → near-sine
params.rationale: Low material kills the upper Bessel modes so the body reads as one pure low tone — the 808 sine, not a struck skin. Graded 0.18→0.60 small→large per builder; 0.22 = representative low-mid tom.

Toms are prominent kit voices; 0.85 = builder value, leaves headroom under the −6 dBFS body budget + bus limiter (post-audit gain staging).

physics: REAL TR-808 tom circuit: a bridged-T network (2R+2C) driven into self-oscillation by a trigger pulse — it combines VCO+VCA+ENV in minimal components, producing a self-dampening SINE that rings briefly and fades (Baratatronix). The pitch sweep is created by diodes (D80–D85) whose resistance rises as the sound decays, starving the circuit, which shortens decay AND drags the pitch down — but the real sweep is SUBTLE: 'less like a boing, more like a tonk' (Baratatronix). Tom = the conga circuit + added filtered (pink) noise. Authentic fundamentals: HIGH tom 165–220 Hz (center ~185), MID 120–160 (center ~135), LOW 80–100 Hz (center ~90). Amplitude decay (T60-ish, exponential): HIGH ~100 ms, MID ~130 ms, LOW ~200 ms — low toms ring longest. A subtle pink-noise layer ('artificial reverb') runs through its own VCA with a slightly LONGER decay than the main body. The initial attack has a click. INHARMONICITY: essentially none — it is a single tuned sine resonator, no rich modal/partial structure (this is what distinguishes the electronic 808 tom from a struck acoustic tom, which has true Bessel membrane modes). PITCH GLIDE: the 'boom-glide' / boom that descends in pitch is the iconic extended-sweep version (sine + decay-only pitch envelope, e.g. ~220→80 Hz; in the Attack Magazine boom recipe the pitch-env decay is long, ~1 s, 'longer decays = booms, shorter = kicks', pitch-env modulation amount ~half). The brief's archetype (220→80 Hz over a long pitchEnv) is this musically-exaggerated boom-glide; the real circuit's diode sweep is the subtle end of the same mechanism. DRIVING PARAM CHOICES: f0 set by Size so the body sits near the sweep END (~80–120 Hz settle); Decay long (200 ms-class); pitch env Start>End with a long time and a fast-initial (exp) curve to mimic the diode starve; material LOW + b3 high so only the fundamental survives = sine; tiny noise + tiny click for the pink-noise 'reverb' and attack tick; ALL acoustic-membrane physics OFF.

bodyModel: NoiseBody (offset 1 = 5). Plate-ratio 32-mode metallic modal bank PLUS NoiseBody's own internal filtered-noise layer — the exact hybrid the TR-808 open hat needs: an inharmonic metallic "hum" cluster sitting under a bright filtered-noise wash. f0 = 1500·0.1^size.
exciter: NoiseBurst (offset 0 = 2). Violet-noise → bandpass burst. This is the correct synthetic-hat exciter: the 808 hat is NOT a struck membrane, it is filtered noise/square-wave energy gated through bandpass+highpass. NoiseBurst injects a short broadband transient that lights up the metallic plate modes without an impulsive "click", giving the chht onset rather than a tonal strike.

params.rationale: Violet-noise bandpass burst is the correct synthetic-hat excitation (filtered noise, not a struck membrane / mechanical click).

physics:
REAL ACOUSTICS + ESTABLISHED 808 SYNTHESIS (the 808 hat is a circuit, not a miked cymbal, so the circuit IS the ground truth):

FUNDAMENTAL / PITCH: No single pitch — the 808 hat/cymbal source is six analog square-wave oscillators summed into an INHARMONIC hum. Measured factory frequencies: 205.3, 304.4, 369.6, 522.7, 540, 800 Hz (osc 1 & 2 are trimpot-tunable to 800 & 540 Hz; osc 3-6 fixed; ±20% unit-to-unit from resistor tolerance). The perceptual "body" cluster sits ~520-800 Hz; this is what we target with the NoiseBody modal f0 (size→~600 Hz) so the metallic bank reproduces the inharmonic hum rather than a tuned pitch. Square waves are odd-harmonic-rich, so the summed spectrum extends far above the oscillator fundamentals.

PARTIAL / MODE STRUCTURE & INHARMONICITY: Strongly INHARMONIC by design — the whole point of the six detuned square oscillators is a non-integer partial relationship (a "metallic clang", not a pitch). Real cymbal/hat physics (Rossing & Peterson, free-plate Chladni f∝(m+2n)^~1.7) gives a dense inharmonic mode field; Membrum's NoiseBody uses exactly this plate-ratio table, so high material/brightness + a touch of mode stretch reproduces the clangy inharmonic top.

SPECTRAL SHAPE / FILTERING (the signature): the six oscillators are summed, fed through TWO bandpass filters (centers ~3440 Hz and ~7100 Hz), then an envelope-controlled VCA, then a HIGH-PASS filter. Net result: energy concentrated 3-10 kHz, very little below ~1 kHz reaching the output — a bright, thin, "tssss" timbre. The 7100 Hz band defines the shimmer; the 3440 Hz band carries the body. We reproduce this with the NoiseBody internal noise (material→~6 kHz cutoff) plus the parallel Noise Layer (cutoff→~6 kHz, Violet color) and a high-pass tilt.

PER-BAND DECAY (T60): the defining open-vs-closed difference. Closed hat ≈ 50 ms fixed; OPEN hat decay is a front-panel pot spanning ~90-600 ms (typical musical open-hat tail ~300-550 ms). The high band decays slightly faster than the low band in the real circuit (separate VCAs/time-constants), giving a tail that darkens as it rings. We voice the open-hat wash at ~0.5-0.55 of the noise-decay range and a moderate modal Decay so the metallic ring sustains ~300-500 ms. NoiseBody also scales its 60 ms internal-noise base by the Decay knob's 0.3-3.0× factor.

ATTACK / CONTACT CLICK: there is NO mechanical beater click — the onset is the bandpass-gated noise/square burst itself (a fast VCA open). So the dedicated Click layer is OFF; the NoiseBurst exciter (short violet-noise bandpass burst, ~3-4 ms) provides the correct soft "chht" onset.

NOISE CONTENT: the source is effectively broadband (square edges + 6 detuned oscillators ≈ dense noisy spectrum). Spectral tilt is bright (violet/blue weighting after the high-pass). This is why NoiseBody (modal+noise hybrid) + a hot parallel Noise Layer with Violet color is the right model.

PITCH GLIDE: none. The 808 hat has no pitch envelope / "kerthump" (that is the kick/tom behavior). PitchEnv Time stays 0.

MATERIAL: synthetic "metal" — there is no real alloy; the perceived material is bright metallic, so material→~0.9 (NoiseBody brightness 0.7+0.3·material ≈ 0.97, internal-noise cutoff ≈ 6 kHz).

Sources: Baratatronix 808 Cymbal/Hi-Hat synthesis breakdown (six osc freqs, dual bandpass 3440/7100 Hz, HP, open 90-600 ms vs closed 50 ms); joesul.li "Synthesizing Hi-Hats"; ModeAudio hi-hat design; Werner/Abel/Smith "The TR-808 Cymbal: a Physically-Informed, Circuit-Bendable, Digital Model" (DAFx) for the oscillator+filter+VCA topology; Rossing & Peterson free-plate Chladni law underlying Membrum's plate-ratio NoiseBody.

physics:
TR-909 BASS DRUM — what makes the sound (researched).

SYNTHESIS TOPOLOGY (the 909 is NOT a sample; it is an analog voice). The 909 BD = (a) a self-resonating bridged-T sine oscillator (a triangle saturated into a hex/quasi-square then low-pass filtered back to a near-sine) whose pitch is pulled UP at note-on and relaxes back; plus (b) a separate ATTACK section = a short impulse + a low-pass-filtered noise burst summed into their own fast VCA. The "Tune" pot actually sets the DECAY TIME of the PITCH envelope (how long the up-swept pitch takes to relax), "Decay" sets the AMP decay, and "Attack" sets the level of the impulse+noise click. (gearspace 909-synthesis thread; analog-synth.de 909 BD PCB; firstpr.com.au TR-909 sound-mods PDF; Sound-on-Sound Synth Secrets kick articles.)

FUNDAMENTAL / PITCH. Settled (sustain) fundamental measured ~47-50 Hz (around F#1), Tune can raise it to ~74 Hz (~D2). The transient is a sine GLIDING DOWN from a markedly higher start to that fundamental. This maps to Membrum's ToneShaper pitch envelope rather than the body's natural f0: I use start 180 Hz -> end 55 Hz. The 909's settle is a touch higher and the START is higher-and-faster than the 808 (which sits on a near-static ~50 Hz sub) — the audible "rolling" fundamental during the 909 attack is exactly this faster, deeper glide (mixedbymarcmozart kick analysis: 808 has a "solid" fundamental, the 909 fundamental "rolls around" in the attack).

PITCH-GLIDE TIME ("kerthump"/punch). The 909 glide is FAST — the punch lives in the difference between the swept-up start and the low settle, collapsing in roughly 15-25 ms (vs the 808's slower, longer boom decay). I use 20 ms (norm 0.04). Faster attack = tighter/brighter, slower = bigger/duller (gearspace; SOS Synth Secrets).

PARTIAL STRUCTURE / INHARMONICITY. The body is an essentially SINUSOIDAL analog oscillator — near-pure fundamental with only mild even/odd harmonics from the triangle-saturation stage and from drive. There is NO membrane modal inharmonicity to model and NO metallic content; all physics/inharmonicity axes are therefore zeroed (modeStretch neutral, scatter 0, airLoading 0, coupling 0). Membrum's Membrane body is used as a cheap low-mode sine surrogate driven hard by the pitch env; material is pulled DARK (0.18) so the body reads as a woody sine, not a tuned drumhead with audible upper Bessel modes.

PER-BAND DECAY (T60). Short and punchy: the 909 BD body decays faster than the 808. With body decay multiplier ~0.5x base (decay norm 0.22) and an explicit flat damping b1 ~21 s^-1, the body RT60 lands ~0.1-0.15 s — tight, not a long sub-boom. Moderate b3 (f^2 HF damping ~3e-4) keeps the few harmonics from ringing, preserving the clean analog-sine settle.

ATTACK TRANSIENT / CONTACT CLICK. The defining 909-vs-808 difference: the 909 has a prominent, BRIGHT attack click (impulse + LP-filtered noise) that the 808 essentially lacks. This is the "click-forward, punchier" character. Modeled with Membrum's always-on click layer at a strong mix (0.55) with a mid-bright bandpass center ~1.9 kHz (brightness 0.55) and a ~2.4 ms contact — sharp tick, not a dull thud. Because the click is also routed half-amplitude into the exciter feed, it drives the body's low modes (the "strong b1/b3 click partials" called for) giving the body a harder beater knock. A small amount of broadband drive (0.20, internal gain ~2.8x, timbre-only after the M-2 makeup) adds odd-harmonic grit to the click/body for the "harder" 909 attack.

NOISE CONTENT. Minimal sustained noise — the 909 BD noise is part of the brief click, not a sustained hiss (unlike a snare). The parallel noise layer is kept very low (0.05) just to add a hair of broadband air to the transient; spectral tilt left mid/neutral, decay short.

EXCITER CHOICE. Impulse (raised-cosine click) is the correct beater: it injects a coherent transient that excites the body's low modes and pairs with the explicit click layer for the 909's hard, defined attack — matching the original's impulse+noise attack section. Mallet would soften the knock; NoiseBurst would smear it; FM/Feedback/Friction are wrong archetypes.

archetype: 909 Pure-Noise Hat (hat) — closed variant as baseline; pedal + open as decay/choke variants
bodyModel: NoiseBody (BodyModelType::NoiseBody, body-model selector norm 1.0 / enum idx 5). Chosen because the 909 hat is NOT a modal cymbal: it is enveloped/filtered broadband noise. NoiseBody pairs the always-on internal white-noise→SVF layer (the dominant 909 'hiss/sizzle' generator) with a plate-ratio modal bank (32 modes) that we deliberately suppress toward pure noise (airLoading is a no-op here; modeScatter 0 = pure ratios; we keep modal energy present but let the noise + parallel NoiseLayer dominate). This is the closest match to '6-bit cymbal sample through analog VCF/VCA' — a clean, bright, hissy hat with little perceptible pitch.
exciter: NoiseBurst (ExciterType::NoiseBurst, exciter selector norm 0.4 / enum idx 2). The 909 hat has no struck-membrane transient — it is a noise envelope, not an impulse. NoiseBurst (violet-noise bandpass burst) feeds the body a broadband noise excitation rather than a tonal click, reinforcing the noise character. Burst duration kept very short so the attack is a sharp filtered-noise 'tss' edge, matching the fast-attack VCA of the 909 hat circuit.

params.rationale: 909 hat is a noise envelope, not a strike; NoiseBurst feeds broadband noise into the body for a pure-noise 'tss', not a tonal click.
NoiseBody = plate-ratio modal bank + internal white-noise SVF; the noise path is the dominant 909 hat generator, modal layer kept secondary.

params.physicalTarget: NoiseBody brightness=0.7+0.3*0.95=0.985 (very metallic); internal noise cutoff=clamp(1500+5000*0.95)=6250 Hz, noise reso=0.6+0.4*0.95=0.98; baseDecay=lerp(0.2,1.0,0.95)=0.96*(1+0.1*size)
params.rationale: Thin bright cymbal bronze: maximally metallic modal tilt + hot internal-noise cutoff for the high sizzle. Matches 'very bright' archetype spec (color 0.95).
Hats have no usable pitch; mid size keeps the residual modal bank in a high, hat-appropriate region without a low boom. Pitch is irrelevant for a noise hat so this is left near-neutral.

params.physicalTarget: decay multiplier=exp(lerp(ln0.3,ln3.0,0.07))=~0.33x; noiseDecayMs=60*0.33≈20 ms internal, modal decayTime≈0.96*0.33*(1+0.1*0.5)≈0.33 s pre-damping → very short closed hat ~50-70 ms perceived
params.rationale: Closed-hat baseline. Roland family spec: closed hat ~50 ms. norm 0.07 gives the shortest practical decay = tight 'tick'.
Default off-center strike; for a noise-dominated hat the modal mode-shape weighting is barely audible, so left at the archetype-neutral 0.3.
Hats sit slightly below kick/snare in a 909 mix; trim a touch under the 0.8 default so the bright broadband layer doesn't dominate the bus limiter. Closed hat is a supporting element.
Archetype spec cutoff 0.95: an almost-open lowpass keeps the full hissy/sizzle band (3-16 kHz) while taming only the very top — the 'bright, hissy' 909 hat.
Lowpass at a high cutoff is the natural shaping for an already-bright noise source (removes only ultra-top fizz). HP would thin it further than the 909 character wants.
Neutral; the modal layer is intentionally backgrounded, so no inharmonic stretch is added — keeps the noise reading 'clean'.
Neutral spectral tilt; the hat's brightness is set by Material/noise color, not by a per-mode decay skew.

params.physicalTarget: 0 = pure ratios (and a NO-OP on NoiseBody — membrane-only frequency correction)
params.rationale: Archetype spec airLoading 0. Deliberately zero: no membrane air-loading; this is a metal noise hat, not a tuned drumhead.
Archetype spec modeScatter 0. A deliberately 'pure' hat: no organic detune dither, keeping the modal residue clean so the noise layers define the sound.

params.physicalTarget: parallel NoiseLayer mix 0.9 → strong always-on filtered-noise path (×0.243 standalone gain into mix)
params.rationale: This parallel noise layer IS the 909 hat. Pushed high (archetype 'strong b1'/pure-noise) so hiss/sizzle dominates over the modal body.
Archetype 'very bright cutoff 0.95': passes the full sizzle band so the noise reads bright and airy, not muffled. Hat energy is strongest 8-16 kHz.
Low resonance keeps the noise flat/broadband (no pitched peak); the 909 hat is wideband filtered noise, not a resonant whistle.
Closed-hat noise envelope ≈47 ms, matching Roland's ~50 ms closed-hat decay spec — the fast 'tick' that defines a closed hat.
Archetype 'color 0.95'. Violet noise gives the steeply rising high-frequency tilt that reads as cymbal sizzle/air.

params.physicalTarget: click transient mix 0.35 (×0.445 standalone gain; half-amp into exciter feed)
params.rationale: A modest bright click sharpens the fast-attack edge of the hat (the VCA snap), without a thuddy body. Lower than snare since a hat's attack is mostly the noise burst itself.
Very short contact = a crisp tick edge, matching the near-instant attack of the sampled hat.
High bandpass center so the click is a bright 'tss' tick consistent with the hat's spectral centroid, not a dull thud.

params.physicalTarget: ms=2+0.1*13=3.3 ms violet-noise burst (velocity shortens further)
params.rationale: Short, sharp noise burst feeds the body a crisp filtered-noise attack — the only excitation a noise hat needs.
Archetype 'choke 1'. Closed/pedal/open share group 1 so a new hat cuts the ringing open hat — mirrors the 909 ROM that can't play open+closed simultaneously.
909 hats are mixed center by default; stereo placement is a kit-arrangement choice, left centered (audit M-9 default).
Pad must sound.

defaultedParams.why: Lowpass at ~14 kHz needs no emphasis; Butterworth Q keeps the top transparent on an already-bright source.
norm 0.5 = no filter sweep; the hat's brightness is static, no envelope-driven cutoff motion.
0 = bypass; the 909 'crunch' is 6-bit quantization, not analog saturation — adding Drive would muddy the clean hiss.
0 = bypass; wavefolding would add west-coast harmonics alien to a filtered-noise hat.
PitchEnv Time=0 disables the whole pitch envelope; a hat has no pitch glide / 'kerthump'.
No-ops while Filter Env Amount=0.5; no filter envelope is used.
0 = exact bypass; injecting a tonal harmonic series would contradict the deliberately pitch-less pure-noise hat.
0 = exact bypass; no amplitude-driven brightening wanted on an already-bright, short noise hat.
Morph off; the hat timbre is static over its ~50 ms life, no material sweep.
0 = main bus; standard routing, no aux send for the baseline kit.
No-op: FMImpulse exciter not selected.
No-op: Feedback exciter not selected; would also force the costly per-sample path.
No-op: Friction exciter not selected.
Left at 0.5 default; only audible if the kit-level Global Coupling/Snare-Buzz network is engaged, irrelevant to an isolated hat.
All 0.5 = neutral (zero delta); the explicit per-param values above already define the sound, no macro nudge needed.
Sentinel -1 (norm shown 0.5 but stored -1 until written): let the Decay knob drive flat damping; an explicit b1 would override the decay-based ring we tuned.
Sentinel -1: derive high-freq damping from Material (0.95→very metallic, long highs) — exactly the bright-metal behavior we want.
Secondary shell disabled (0); a hat has no head↔shell body to add — would only fatten/lower it incorrectly.
0 = off, and Membrane-only anyway; hats have no energy-dependent pitch glide.

physics: PITCH / FUNDAMENTAL: A real hi-hat / the 909 hat has no clear musical pitch — it is broadband, inharmonic-to-noise. Cymbal/hat vibration spans ~100 Hz up to 5 kHz and well beyond, with energy strongly weighted above 1 kHz (the metal's natural high-Q 'ring' region) and sparkle/sizzle reaching 10–16 kHz+ (Musical-U / emastered / audiorecording.me). The 909 specifically is a 6-bit PCM cymbal recording (~18 kHz nominal, ~60 kHz effective at stock tune per spectral analysis) replayed through an analog VCA envelope + VCF, so it is fundamentally enveloped filtered noise with low-bit 'crunch', not a synthesized modal bank (Electric Druid; Knobula; modwiggler/samplefocus). PARTIAL/MODE STRUCTURE & INHARMONICITY: minimal usable modal structure — the perceptual content is dense, noise-like, with no identifiable harmonic series; the 909's distinctive crunch comes from 6-bit quantization noise on top of the cymbal recording, not from tuned partials. So the Membrum recipe deliberately runs airLoading=0 and modeScatter=0 (no membrane air-loading curve applies anyway on NoiseBody, and 0 scatter keeps the modal layer 'clean'), and leans on the NoiseBody internal noise + parallel NoiseLayer + click as the actual sound. DECAY (T60) per band: hi-hat decay is the defining open/closed contrast. Roland's own family spec lists a closed hat ~50 ms decay (TR-808 closed = 50 ms; 909 closed is similarly very short, ~50–80 ms) with fast attack/short sustain; pedal (foot) hat is shorter still; open hat is a long, slowly-decaying sizzle commonly 300–600 ms+ before choke (audiorecording.me / baratatronix / Roland TR-808 spec). The 909 envelope is an exponential decay generated by DACs on the ROM address lines, identical length regardless of tune (Electric Druid). High band (sizzle, >8 kHz) decays with the body; there is no separate low-band ring because there is essentially no low end. ATTACK / CONTACT CLICK: a sharp, very fast filtered-noise edge (a few ms), no pitched click — modeled with a short, bright Click layer + short NoiseBurst. NOISE CONTENT & SPECTRAL SHAPE: this IS the sound — bright, high-passed/band-limited broadband noise. Spectral centroid high; little below ~1 kHz, strong 3–16 kHz. We pick Violet noise color (rising +6 dB/oct, brightest available band) and an almost-open lowpass to get the hissy/sizzly tilt. PITCH GLIDE: none — hats do not 'kerthump'; tensionMod stays 0 (and is Membrane-only anyway). MATERIAL: thin bronze/brass alloy cymbal metal — bright/metallic, which we set via high material so the residual modal layer's brightness and the noise cutoff both run hot. The recipe's driving values: very bright tone (filter cutoff ~near-bypass, noise cutoff hot + violet), modal layer present but secondary, decay norm 0.07 → ~55–60 ms closed (matches Roland's ~50 ms closed spec), choke group 1 so a new hat cuts the ringing open hat (the 909 ROM cannot sound open+closed at once — Electric Druid).

bodyModel: Membrane (primary "boop"/shell tonal body) + a metallic secondary shell bank (the second 909 tone oscillator / drumhead mode). Rationale: a real snare and the TR-909's synthesis are explicitly a TWO-tonal-mode design — two decaying oscillators for the dominant 0,1 drumhead modes, the lower one with a longer decay — driven over a broadband noise (snare-wire) layer. Membrane gives the Bessel drumhead character + tension-glide capability; the Phase-8D secondary shell adds the higher metallic "ring" that the 909's second oscillator provides.
exciter: NoiseBurst (violet-noise bandpass burst). Rationale: the 909 snare's onset is a broadband filtered-noise transient, not a clean impulse; NoiseBurst hands the body a short noisy contact that fattens the attack and seeds the modes the way a stick-on-batter strike does. Established Membrum 808/909-snare idiom (electronicKit pads 2/4) uses NoiseBurst+Membrane.

params.rationale: Snare onset is a broadband filtered-noise contact, not a clean impulse; seeds the membrane modes like stick-on-batter.

params.physicalTarget: idx 0 = Membrane; base f0 500*0.1^size Hz, 48 Bessel modes
params.rationale: Drumhead tonal modes + air-loading + tension-glide; the canonical 909 'two drumhead-mode oscillators' map to Membrane primary + secondary shell.
909 body is brighter/tighter than a wood acoustic snare; mid-high material keeps the head crisp without going metallic-thin.

params.physicalTarget: f0 = 500*0.1^0.398 ≈ 200 Hz (head fundamental in the ~180-200 Hz region)
params.rationale: Places the Membrane fundamental in the snare body's dominant low-mode region (~180-200 Hz), so the pitch-env settle (140 Hz) lands just under it.

params.physicalTarget: decayTime = baseDecay*exp(lerp(ln0.3,ln3,0.30)) ≈ 0.45x base; short tonal body (~120-200 ms)
params.rationale: The 909 TONE oscillators decay faster than the noise tail; the body is a quick thwack, not a ring.

params.physicalTarget: r/a = 0.35*0.9 = 0.315; off-center batter strike, Bessel mode-shape weighting
params.rationale: Real snares are struck between center and edge for body+overtones; balances low-mode weight with brightness.

params.physicalTarget: linear pre-rail gain 1.0 (full per-pad make-up; bus TruePeakLimiter at -1 dBTP owns the ceiling)
params.rationale: Snare is a lead percussion element; full level, with loudness governed downstream per the corrected gain-staging.
params.citation: AUDIT gain-staging appendix (kBodyHeadroom/measuredStrikeOutputGain + -1 dBTP bus limiter)

params.rationale: Tame the very bright violet noise + drive harmonics with a gentle LP, matching the 909's noise lowpass stage.

params.physicalTarget: hz = 20*1000^0.92 ≈ 11.7 kHz (engaged, just rolling off top air)
params.rationale: Keeps the 909's bright hiss but rolls off only the harshest top; <20 kHz engages the LP so the noise isn't ear-piercing.
Slight presence bump at the cutoff for snare 'crack' without ringing.

params.physicalTarget: internalDrive = 1+0.48*9 ≈ 5.3; ReciprocalSqrt waveshape, unity small-signal makeup (M-2)
params.rationale: Task target: drive fattens body harmonics / adds the 909's nonlinear bite as TIMBRE (post-M-2 makeup = no level pump).
Task target 380 Hz: bright body onset before the fast drop ('tssk').
Task target 140 Hz: settle pitch just under the ~200 Hz body fundamental for weight.

params.physicalTarget: ms = 0.05*500 = 25 ms glide (enables the pitch env)
params.rationale: Fast 380->140 Hz drop; >0 is the master enable for the glide.

params.physicalTarget: curveAmount = 2*0.12-1 = -0.76; fast initial drop (exponential decay shape)
params.rationale: Classic percussion pitch-env: snaps down immediately then settles, like the 909's envelope-driven body.

params.physicalTarget: NoiseLayer.mix=0.72*env*filteredNoise *0.243 standalone gain; dominant parallel hiss
params.rationale: The 909 snare is noise-FORWARD (broadband LFSR noise is the defining element); pushed high so the 'fffft' weight dominates.
Task target 0.78: a bright, hissy noise tail (909 is brighter than 808's ~darker bandpass).
LFSR noise is spectrally flat; keep the layer broadband, not pitched.

params.physicalTarget: ms = exp(ln20 + 0.48*(ln2000-ln20)) ≈ 200 ms; tail longer than the body
params.rationale: Task target 0.48: the famous LONG, hissy sampled-noise 909 tail that outlasts the tonal body.
909 LFSR noise is flat-to-bright; Violet best emulates the digital hiss that makes the 909 brighter than the 808.

params.physicalTarget: ClickLayer.mix=0.55*vel*env*filteredNoise; drives body half-amp + full to output *0.445
params.rationale: Sharp stick-crack tick for attack definition (the snare's transient 'snap').
Near-instant attack (A=0); a tight tick, not a thud.

params.physicalTarget: hz = exp(ln200 + 0.72*(ln12000-ln200)) ≈ 4.7 kHz bandpass center; sharp tick
params.rationale: Bright stick crack to cut through the mix, consistent with the 909's crisp character.

params.physicalTarget: effectiveCoupling=0.32 feedforward drive body->secondary shell (engages, secondary on)
params.rationale: Task target 0.32: drives the metallic secondary ('second oscillator') ping at a balanced level under the body.

params.physicalTarget: >=0.5 = ON; spins up the 24-mode secondary shell bank
params.rationale: Provides the 909's SECOND tonal oscillator — the metallic high 'ring' above the main body.

params.physicalTarget: sizeRatio = 1 - 0.32*0.75 = 0.76; shell f0 ≈ 0.76*head_f0 (a higher metallic partner just above the body)
params.rationale: Task target metallic ping at 0.32: a smaller secondary body tuned to sit as the bright second oscillator over the ~200 Hz head.
