params.rationale: Task target 0.90: highly metallic secondary so the ping shimmers (the 909's bright second tone), not a woody thud.
Snare sits center in a drum mix (mono-compatible backbeat).
Snare pad is active.

defaultedParams.why: No filter sweep needed; the noise color/cutoff and drive set the spectrum statically. EnvAmount left at 0.5 (=0, no modulation), keeping the LP fixed.
Wavefolding is a west-coast timbre; the 909 snare's added harmonics come from drive + bright noise, not folding. Bypassed at 0.
Default 0.333 (neutral): the snare body's mild inharmonicity is already carried by the Bessel Membrane ratios; no extra metallic stretch wanted on the head.
Default 0.5 (no skew): the high-vs-low decay balance is handled explicitly by the fast body Decay vs the long Noise Decay tail, not by a per-mode tilt.
Default 0 (exact bypass): the secondary shell already supplies the second tonal layer; injecting a synthetic 1/k harmonic series would muddy the two-oscillator design.
Default 0 (exact bypass): the 909 snare's brightness is static (LFSR noise + drive), not a velocity-driven amplitude-brightening effect; left off to keep hits consistent.
Default off: the 909 body timbre doesn't sweep material within a hit; the only intra-hit motion is the pitch glide (offsets 13-16), which is enabled.
Default 0 (no choke): a snare is not part of an open/closed mute group like a hi-hat.
Default 0 (main bus): standard routing; no multi-out send needed for the archetype recipe.
No-op: FMImpulse exciter not selected (NoiseBurst is).
No-op: Feedback exciter not selected.

Default 0.5 (~8.5 ms) is acceptable, but a tighter ~3-4 ms is idiomatic; left near default as the click + noise layers dominate the attack character. (Tighten toward 0.1 if a snappier contact is wanted.)
No-op: Friction exciter not selected.

Default 0.5: per-pad participation in the GLOBAL cross-pad sympathetic engine; only audible if the kit enables Snare Buzz / Global Coupling, which is a kit-level decision outside this single-pad recipe.
All at 0.5 (neutral, zero delta): the recipe sets the underlying params directly, so the one-knob macros are left transparent to preserve the explicit values.

Left at the preset-default mid (0.40/0.40) rather than the -1 sentinel; the Decay knob + Material already place the body in the fast-thwack range, and explicit mids avoid sentinel round-trip issues. (b3 could be lowered for a metallier head, but the secondary shell carries the metallic ring.)

Default 0.6 is fine for a realistic head, but electronic snares often use 0.0 (cleaner modal pitch); left at the realistic default since Membrane benefits from air-loading and the 909 character is dominated by noise+secondary.
Default 0 (pure ratios): a machine drum wants exact, repeatable modal pitches, not organic detune dither.
Default 0 (off): the 909 snare's pitch motion is the explicit pitch ENVELOPE (offsets 13-16), not an energy-dependent membrane tension glide (that's a tom 'kerthump' effect).
Default (knee OFF): a single-segment 380->140 Hz drop is sufficient; no middle breakpoint needed for the snare's fast glide.

physics: FUNDAMENTAL/PITCH: A snare is a two-headed drum whose audible "pitch" comes from the dominant low membrane modes of the batter+shell coupled to the (much more tense) snare-side head. The TR-909 (and the canonical synth model in McGill's Nord percussion text and SOS "Practical Snare Drum Synthesis") reduces this to TWO decaying tone oscillators for the 0,1-region drumhead modes, sitting roughly in the ~180-330 Hz region (SOS / firstpr 909 service-mod docs cite ~180 Hz + ~330 Hz; an alt folklore pair is 185/330 Hz). The lower oscillator is given a slightly longer decay than the upper. The two modes are inharmonic (a 14" snare's low-mode ratios run ~1 : 1.5 : ~1.8, not 1:2:3). PARTIAL/INHARMONICITY: real snare membrane modes are Bessel-spaced and air-loaded; the 909 abstracts this to 2 oscillators, so a faithful Membrum 909 wants a LOW mode count weight (the metallic high "ring" comes from the second/secondary oscillator, deliberately tuned smaller & metallic). PITCH GLIDE: the 909 snare tone has a fast downward pitch component from its excitation envelopes — the task target 380 Hz -> 140 Hz over a short time captures the "tssk" body drop into the ~140 Hz fundamental. NOISE CONTENT (the defining 909 trait): the 909's noise generator is a DIGITAL LFSR pseudo-noise (Electric Druid: 31-stage shift register, taps 31/13, ~300 kHz clock, spectrally FLAT well past audibility) — i.e. effectively VIOLET/white and very bright, versus the 808's softer analog noise+bandpass. This is exactly why the 909 snare is brighter and HISSIER with a LONGER, more sampled-sounding tail than the 808. The noise is shaped by a lowpass then a parallel highpass path with its OWN envelope, mixed with the tonal oscillators; the SNAPPY control trades noise-vs-tone and attack tightness. ATTACK/CLICK: near-instantaneous attack (A=0 in the SOS patch); a sharp stick-crack tick rides on top. DECAY/T60: the noise tail is the long element on a 909 snare (longer/brighter than the body), with the tonal oscillators decaying faster; SOS uses short decay+release on the body and a longer noise tail. MATERIAL: metal shell + plastic head + steel snare wires; the wire buzz is the broadband high-frequency element.

bodyModel: Membrane (Bessel circular drumhead, 48 modes) — the only body that carries the air-loading frequency correction and the membrane-gated tension-modulation glide, both intrinsic to a real bass drum.
exciter: Mallet (softer beater contact) — the felt/wood beater of a bass-drum pedal is a soft, distributed contact, not a hard click; the sharp beater "tick" is added separately by the always-on Click layer. (Impulse is the harder-beater alternative.)

params.rationale: Soft beater contact (felt/wood pedal beater), not a hard click; the sharp tick is added by the Click layer. round(norm*5)=1 selects Mallet.
params.citation: SoS Synthesizing Drums: The Bass Drum (beater = soft contact + separate short click); iDrumTune (beater material warm→sharp)

params.rationale: A bass drum IS a clamped circular membrane; only Membrane carries air-loading and the membrane-gated tension glide.
params.citation: HyperPhysics Bass Drum; PSU Russell circular-membrane modes

params.physicalTarget: woody: membrane brightness=0.35; baseDecayTime=lerp(0.15,0.8,0.35)*(1+0.1*0.85)=0.41 s base; mild b3 high-mode damping
params.rationale: Bass drum is woody/dark, not metallic — upper inharmonic partials damped fast so the low body dominates.
params.citation: Reid SoS (low quasi-harmonic body, fast HF densification); Chaigne & Askenfelt b1+b3·f² damping

params.physicalTarget: f0 = 500·0.1^0.85 = 70.7 Hz natural head fundamental (big shell)
params.rationale: Large bass-drum head; 0.85 lands the natural head pitch ~71 Hz, with the pitch env settling the perceived boom lower (~52 Hz).
params.citation: iDrumTune (kick fundamental 50-60, up to 80-90 Hz); naturalFundamentalHz Membrane=500·0.1^size

params.physicalTarget: legacy decayTime ≈ 0.41·(0.3·10^0.25) ≈ 0.22 s; but flat-damping floor is set by Body Damping b1 override (below)
params.rationale: Short-to-medium body ring; the dominant flat decay is taken over by b1 (acoustic kicks are tight, ~150-250 ms body).
A pedal beater strikes near-but-not-dead-center; favors the low axisymmetric modes while keeping some (1,1) weight for tone.
params.citation: PSU Russell ((0,1) radiates fast, (1,1) carries pitch — want both); Bessel |J_m(j_mn·r/a)|

params.rationale: Kick is a loud, foundational pad; sits just under unity, master/limiter own the ceiling (post-audit gain staging).
params.citation: AUDIT gain-staging appendix (level is true linear attenuation before the safety rail)

params.rationale: The impact 'thump' begins ~160 Hz — a real acoustic couple-of-semitones-ish drop, far shallower/higher than the 808's 200 Hz.
params.citation: Reid SoS (kick shifts ~a couple of semitones); idrumtune; toLogNorm(160)=ln(8)/ln(100)=0.4508

params.rationale: Settles to the ~50 Hz perceived bass-drum fundamental.
SHORT glide — the acoustic 'kerthump' is tens of ms, not the 808's 200-500 ms boom. Distinguishes acoustic from synthetic.
params.citation: Reid SoS (pitch decays proportionally with amplitude, fast); arXiv:2502.07524 (808 uses a long sweep)

params.rationale: Tension relaxes fastest right after impact — fast-initial exponential drop matches the physical relaxation.
params.citation: McGill Nord percussion (rapid rise then slower decay); SoS

params.physicalTarget: 0.78 of the way to the Rossing near-harmonic (m,1) series 1:1.5:2:2.44:2.9 on the upper modes
params.rationale: Heavy air loading = deep, less-whistly head with near-harmonic upper modes — the hallmark of a big air-loaded bass drum. (Anchors (0,1)/(1,1), scales (2..5,1) up.)
params.citation: Rossing 1982 Physics of Kettledrums; Well-Tempered Timpani air-loading; kAirLoadingTargetScale

params.physicalTarget: b1 = 0.2 + 0.13·49.8 ≈ 6.7 s^-1 → ~150 ms flat decay floor
params.rationale: Sets the body ring at a tight ~150 ms (acoustic kicks are damped/short), overriding the Decay-derived floor.
params.citation: Chowdhury modal drum-fixing; JASA 150:202; mapper b1=0.2+n·49.8

params.physicalTarget: b3 = 0.42·1e-3 = 4.2e-4 s → strong f² damping (woody, fast HF)
params.rationale: Inharmonic upper partials decay much faster than the low body — a real bass drum is mostly low fundamental after a few ms.
params.citation: Reid SoS (HF partials last a few ms); Chaigne & Askenfelt b3·f²

params.physicalTarget: effective = 0.18·3.3e-5·E; energy-driven up-glide up to +~0.5 st at full velocity (cap +3 st)
params.rationale: Models the real impact tension RISE ('kerthump' up-chirp) on hard hits — Membrane-only; subtle so it reads acoustic.
params.citation: Avanzini-Marogna-Bank 2012 (ΔT ∝ E); SoS (pitch rises on strike); kTensionEnergyScale=3.3e-5

params.physicalTarget: feedforward drive 0.35 of body→secondary shell bank
params.rationale: Real head↔shell coupling adds the wooden shell body — the thing the 808 lacks. Moderate so the shell supports, not dominates.
params.citation: PSU Russell (two-head coupling splits (0,1)/(1,1)); AUDIT §3-C coupling topology

params.rationale: Enables the coupled shell resonator that gives the kick real wooden housing weight.
params.citation: AUDIT (secondary shell adds body weight to kicks)

params.rationale: Shell rings slightly below the head — a deep maple-shell resonance under the fundamental.

params.physicalTarget: shell b1 ≈ max(25,2.5·bodyB1)+(1-0.6)·15; b3=0.6e-5 → moderately bright/ringy wood shell
params.rationale: Maple shell: woody but with some sustain/brightness so the coupled body is audible.

params.physicalTarget: click 0.7·vel·env into BOTH the exciter feed (half-amp, drives body) and output (×0.445)
params.rationale: The beater contact transient — prominent attack definition that separates an acoustic kick from a pure tone.
params.citation: USPTO bass-drum patent (head + short beater impact); SoS (separate click)

params.rationale: Short, sharp beater impact (~2-3 ms) — the impulsive 'few ms' contact.
params.citation: Reid SoS (HF click partials last a few ms)

params.rationale: Felt/wood beater click centered ~1.5 kHz — warm punch, not a sharp plastic 3-5 kHz tick.
params.citation: iDrumTune/eMastered (beater click ~1.5-2 kHz felt, attack 2-4 kHz); Gear4music kick EQ

params.rationale: Just a touch of shell/beater air — an acoustic kick is mostly tonal body, unlike a snare.
params.citation: Reid SoS (kick is harmonic-low + inharmonic body, modest broadband)

params.rationale: Keep the noise low/dark so it adds body air, not hiss.
params.citation: kick EQ guides (boxiness/thump region, cut highs)

params.rationale: Low rumble/air, not bright snare hiss.
params.citation: NoiseOscillator color bands; kick air is low-frequency

params.rationale: The air/scuff is a brief attack accent, gone quickly.
params.citation: Reid SoS (transient HF a few ms)

defaultedParams.param: Filter Type / Cutoff / Resonance / Filter Env (Amount, Atk, Dec, Sus, Rel)
defaultedParams.why: ToneShaper SVF left bypassed (cutoff norm 1.0 = ≥20 kHz, env amount 0.5 = 0): the body's own b3·f² damping already shapes the tilt; no post-filter sculpting needed for a clean acoustic kick.
Acoustic kick is a linear resonant body — no saturation/wavefolding; Drive/Fold are west-coast/electronic flavour, off keeps the body clean (post-audit Drive=timbre flavour, not level).
Membrane Bessel inharmonicity is already physically correct; no extra dispersion wanted — a bass drum is not a stiff bar.
The high-vs-low decay balance is already set physically by b3 (fast HF) — no spectral tilt override needed.
Synthetic added harmonic series would make it read as electronic; the real head modes carry the tone. Exact-bypass at 0.
A soft-to-moderate bass-drum hit is in its linear regime; amplitude-brightening is for cymbals/gongs. Exact bypass.
No intra-note material sweep — a kick's timbre is static after the strike; morph is for evolving FX voices.

defaultedParams.param: PitchEnv Knee / Mid / Mid Frac / Curve2
defaultedParams.why: Single-segment 160→50 Hz glide is sufficient; the two-segment knee is for complex tom/FX glides.
The single deep fundamental should be clean and centered; detune dither would smear the sub. Pure ratios (bit-identical).

defaultedParams.param: FM Ratio / Feedback Amount / NoiseBurst Duration / Friction Pressure
defaultedParams.why: Secondary-exciter params — no-ops because the Mallet exciter is selected, not FMImpulse/Feedback/NoiseBurst/Friction.
Kick is never choked and routes to the main bus; no mute-group or multi-out need.
Kick is the low-frequency anchor — kept dead-center for mono-compatible low end (equal-power center = bit-identical mono).
The kick is an active, crafted pad.
All explicit params are set directly; macros left neutral (zero delta) so they don't perturb the hand-voiced values and stay available as user one-knob trims.
Per-pad inter-pad sympathetic weight is only audible if kit-level Global Coupling>0 (off for a kick-focused recipe); left neutral. Distinct from the per-pad head↔shell Coupling Strength which IS set.
The thin dark noise layer needs no resonant peak; a flat lowpass air bed is correct.

physics:
FUNDAMENTAL / PITCH: an acoustic bass-drum head is tuned low — fundamental ~50-60 Hz, tunable 50-90 Hz by size/head/tension (iDrumTune; idrumtune mixing guide). Membrum: Membrane f0 = 500·0.1^size, so size 0.85 → 70.7 Hz, size 0.90 → 62.9 Hz natural head pitch; the iconic perceived "boom" pitch is set lower by the pitch envelope settling near 50-55 Hz.
MODE / PARTIAL STRUCTURE: a clamped circular membrane is INHARMONIC (Bessel zeros 1 : 1.59 : 2.14 : 2.30 : 2.65 …), NOT integer harmonics (HyperPhysics; PSU/Russell circular-membrane demos). The struck-center bass drum excites the axisymmetric (0,1),(0,2),(0,3) series. CRITICAL acoustic subtlety: the (0,1) monopole mode radiates so efficiently it dumps its energy in a fraction of a second and contributes little PITCH; the weakly-radiating (1,1) mode rings longer and carries the perceived tone (PSU Russell; Well-Tempered Timpani membrane-modes). The primary dissonant overtone sits ~2.4× the fundamental regardless of tension (USPTO bass-drum patent / SoS). At low frequencies the spectrum is quasi-harmonic with a ~7 Hz upward offset; above that it densifies into closely-packed enharmonic partials 250 Hz–1 kHz (Gordon Reid, SoS "Synthesizing Drums: The Bass Drum").
AIR LOADING: the air mass loading the head depresses the lowest (m,1) modes and pulls the first five (m,1) modes toward a near-harmonic timpani series 1 : 1.5 : 2.0 : 2.44 : 2.9 (Rossing 1982; Well-Tempered Timpani air-loading). NOTE on the Membrum implementation: kAirLoadingTargetScale ANCHORS (0,1) and (1,1) and SCALES UP the upper (2,1)..(5,1) modes toward that series — so in this engine airLoading makes the body 'deeper/less whistly & near-harmonic on top', it does NOT itself drop the f0 (f0 is set by Size). Physically the deepening is real; mechanically the pitch is the Size knob's job, airLoading shapes the upper-mode ratios.
DECAY / T60: bass drums are short — the radiating (0,1) attack is a fraction of a second; the musical body rings ~150-300 ms; T60 measured per mode (Chowdhury modal drum-fixing; JASA 150:202 strike-intensity evolution). Higher inharmonic partials decay much faster than the low body (b3·f² damping), leaving the low fundamental.
ATTACK / CLICK: two-part sound — (1) the head, (2) a very short, impulsive beater contact (USPTO patent; SoS). The beater 'click' lives ~1.5-5 kHz (felt=warm ~1.5-2k, wood=punchy, plastic=sharp 3-5k); mix engineers find the beater attack at 2-4 kHz, ~3.5 kHz most prominent (eMastered/Gear4music/iDrumTune EQ guides). Hundreds of HF partials that exist a few ms (Reid).
PITCH GLIDE ('kerthump'): striking raises membrane tension → rapid pitch RISE at impact then relax — Membrum models this as energy-driven tensionModAmt (Membrane-only). SEPARATELY, the acoustic/synthesized kick has a downward pitch sweep of 'a couple of semitones' over the body decay (Reid: ~10% / ~couple semitones); electronic 808 exaggerates this to a full sine sweep 200→40 Hz. We use a small, real glide (~160→52 Hz over 40 ms) — much shorter and shallower than the 808 — so it reads as an acoustic 'thump' not a synthetic 'boom'.
NOISE CONTENT: an acoustic bass drum has only modest broadband content (shell air, beater scuff) — far less than a snare. A thin low-passed noise layer adds air without turning it into a 'thwack'.
WHAT DISTINGUISHES IT FROM AN 808: real head↔shell coupling (two-headed drum splits the (0,1)/(1,1) modes — PSU Russell two-head coupling), air loading, a percussive contact click, and only a SHORT shallow glide — versus the 808's clean, long, fully pitch-swept self-oscillating sine (arXiv:2502.07524 TR-808 bass-drum analysis). Membrum maps that to Secondary(shell) coupling ON, airLoading high, Click present, and a 40 ms/160→52 Hz pitch env (not a 60 ms 200→40 Hz 808 sweep).

archetype: Acoustic Rack/Floor Tom (tunable, 6-pad size-graded row — one drum across its tuning range)
bodyModel: Membrane (Bessel circular drumhead, 48 modes) — norm 0.0 on the per-pad Body Model selector. This is the only physically correct body for a struck tom-head; it gives the inharmonic Bessel ratio ladder (1 : 1.59 : 2.14 : 2.30 : 2.65 : 2.92 ...), responds to Strike Position via |J_m(j_mn·r/a)|, and is the ONLY body the audit-corrected airLoading frequency-correction and tensionMod pitch-glide consume.
exciter: Mallet (norm 0.2 on the per-pad Exciter Type selector → enum idx 1). A soft beater (round(0.2·5)=1) launches the low diametric modes with a rounded, energy-rich transient and little HF spray — the correct excitation for a felt/wood tom beater. The sharp stick definition is added separately by the always-on Click layer, not by the exciter.

params.rationale: Soft felt/wood beater launches the low diametric modes with a rounded transient and little HF spray; stick definition comes from the Click layer, not the exciter.
Only physically-correct body for a struck tom-head; the sole body that consumes airLoading and tensionMod (both Membrane-gated in the audit-corrected code).

params.physicalTarget: brightness=0.4 (woody); membrane baseDecayTime=lerp(0.15,0.8,0.4)·(1+0.1·size)≈0.41·(1+0.1·size) s; modest mode stretch
params.rationale: Wood shell + plastic head = woody, not metallic. <0.5 keeps the spectrum dark with strong high-mode roll-off (the tom 'thump'). Jazz brighter (~0.5), rock/wood darker (~0.33).

params.physicalTarget: baseline mid-tom: f0=500·0.1^0.55≈141 Hz (mode-spacing/ring scale); row graded 0.8→0.4 (f0≈79→199 Hz pre-airload)
params.rationale: Size sets the membrane mode SPACING and ring-up (lower = longer); perceived PITCH is then set by the pitchEnv. The 6 pads grade size 0.8 (low floor) → 0.4 (high rack), one drum re-tensioned.

params.physicalTarget: ≈1.0× the body base decay (exp(lerp(ln0.3,ln3.0,0.5))=0.95); with material/size ≈0.4 s body ring before b1 override
params.rationale: Toms ring noticeably but are two-head+air damped: musical body sustain. Note b1 override (offset 50) is the actual RT60 driver here; Decay still seeds the legacy law/cache. Rock longer, jazz tighter.

An off-center strike excites the pitched (1,1)/(2,1) diametric modes (definite lingering tom pitch); a dead-center strike would give only the (0,1)/(0,2) thump. ~1/3 radius is the normal playing zone.
Default per-pad loudness; toms sit a touch under snare/kick. Linear after the audit gain-staging fix, so it is a true level trim.
A clean tom needs no post-body tone filter; the body's own b3·f² damping shapes the tilt. Left transparent.

params.physicalTarget: amount=2·0.5-1=0 → no filter sweep, filter stays bypassed
params.rationale: No filter-envelope motion wanted on an acoustic tom; the pitch/tension envelopes carry the motion.
A clean acoustic tom adds no saturation harmonics. Drive is flavour, not level (M-2 makeup); unused here.
West-coast wavefolding is alien to an acoustic tom.

Start HIGH: the membrane tension/energy is highest at the strike, so the tom's pitch begins above its settle pitch — the 'kerthump' drop. Graded high→low across the row (high rack ~470, low floor ~200 Hz).
Settle pitch ≈ the tom's tuned fundamental once strike energy decays; the Start→End interval (~a fifth) is the audible downward glide. Graded ~110 Hz (floor) → ~290 Hz (rack).
The tom drop is fast — tens of ms, longer on big floor toms. This also UNLOCKS the whole pitch-env family. Graded longer for low toms (~90 ms) → shorter for high toms (~40 ms).
Real tension relaxation is fast-then-slow (exponential), like the classic 808/membrane decay shape — most of the glide happens in the first few ms.
A tom's partials are already the natural inharmonic Bessel set; no extra stretch/compression. Neutral.

params.physicalTarget: skew=2·0.42-1=-0.16 → mild tilt favouring lower modes' sustain (highs die slightly faster) + skewBias≈1.05 longer global
params.rationale: Real toms: high modes damp faster than the low diametric modes (wood/air loss), so a slight skew toward low-mode sustain matches the natural 'thump that outlasts the click'.
No synthetic harmonic series wanted; the membrane's own inharmonic modes ARE the tom voice. Adding 1/k harmonics would make it pitched-tonal, not drum-like.

Real membranes brighten when hit hard (nonlinear head coupling / energy cascade). A small amount adds louder=brighter life without compressing — re-voiced against the corrected env-level waveshaping. Keep subtle; rock kit a touch higher (~0.18).
params.citation: AUDIT M-3/M-4 redesign; Ducceschi/Bilbao von Karman

params.rationale: Toms are not a mute-group (unlike hats); they ring freely.
Default routing; multi-out is a mixing choice, not part of the instrument voice.
A clean tom has only slight air/shell breathiness (no wires). Low level — enough to add body 'air', not a snare hiss. Wood kit slightly more (~0.2), jazz less.
Keep the breathiness low/dark (shell air thump region), not a bright sizzle.
Mild, no whistly peak on the lowpass noise path.
Breathiness is an attack-region phenomenon — short, gone before the body tail.
Pink (−3 dB/oct) matches broadband shell/air noise better than white; not bright violet (that's a cymbal).

params.physicalTarget: 0.5·vel·env·filteredNoise into both exciter feed (×0.5) and output (×0.445)
params.rationale: The beater contact 'thwack' that defines the tom attack and also kicks the body modes. Moderate — present but not a stick-on-rim crack.
Felt/wood beater contact is a few ms (softer/longer than a stick tick).
A low-mid beater 'thud' click, not a sharp high tick; sits over the tom's low body. Rock/wood darker (~0.5), jazz brighter (~0.6).

params.physicalTarget: b1=0.2+0.34·49.8≈17 s^-1 flat damping → ~0.5–0.7 s low-mode RT60 (overrides Decay's flat term)
params.rationale: This is the real RT60 control for the body. ~17 s^-1 gives a musical tom ring. Graded slightly tighter for higher/smaller toms (b1 ~0.30→0.40). Rock looser (longer), jazz tighter.
params.citation: AUDIT §A Chaigne & Askenfelt 1994; t60 0.14–35 s plausible

params.physicalTarget: b3=0.1·1e-3=1e-4 s → moderate f^2 high-mode damping (woody)
params.rationale: Wood/plastic head: highs roll off faster than lows — the tom 'thump'. Not metallic (b3=0), not a dead thud (b3 max). Wood kit higher (~0.14, darker), jazz lower (~0.08, more open).

params.physicalTarget: 0.55 → lower membrane modes pulled ~55% toward the Rossing air-loaded curve (deeper, less whistly)
params.rationale: A two-headed open tom is air-loaded but NOT kettle-pinned like a timpani, so MODERATE (not full) air loading: deeper/rounder low end without forcing the timpani 1:1.5:2 harmonic series. Membrane-only param. Floor toms a touch higher (~0.65), high rack lower (~0.45).
A touch of natural imperfection (head/shell inhomogeneity, lug-to-lug tension variation) so two hits/two toms aren't bit-identical. Subtle — toms are fairly clean.

params.physicalTarget: 0.4 feedforward drive body→secondary shell bank (engages only with Secondary Enabled)
params.rationale: Head↔shell coupling: the drumhead drives the wood shell/second head, adding body weight and the characteristic tom 'woof'. Models the two-headed/shell resonance.

params.physicalTarget: ON → 24-mode secondary (shell/2nd-head) bank spun up at noteOn
params.rationale: A double-headed tom HAS a coupled resonator (bottom head + shell air). Enabling it is what makes this a tom rather than a single membrane. (Concert/single-head variant would turn this OFF.)
The bottom head/shell sits a little below the batter-head pitch (the resonant 'woof' under the strike). ~0.6–0.8× per Chromaphone-style coupling. Graded with the row.

params.physicalTarget: shell b1=max(25,2.5·bodyB1)+(1-0.55)·15 s^-1; b3=0.55e-5 → moderately damped woody shell
params.rationale: Wood shell: brighter than a dead thud but short — the shell ring must not outlast the head. Wood kit a touch higher for more shell voice.

params.physicalTarget: effective=0.22·3.3e-5·modalEnergy → up to ~+0.5 semitone energy-driven upward bump that relaxes (Membrane-only)
params.rationale: Adds the nonlinear, velocity-sensitive 'crack-and-relax' on top of the deterministic pitchEnv drop — the physically-correct tension-rise-with-energy mechanism. Subtle so it complements, not fights, the pitchEnv. Rock (harder hits) higher (~0.3).
Each tom pad sounds.

params.physicalTarget: center (equal-power, unity both channels) for the baseline; row spread L→R across the kit
params.rationale: Baseline center; in a kit the 6 toms are spread across the stereo field (high rack slightly L → floor slightly R, drummer's perspective) e.g. norm ~0.38→0.62.

defaultedParams.why: ToneShaper filter is bypassed (cutoff=1.0), so its LP/HP/BP shape is a no-op for a clean tom.
No-op while the filter is bypassed.
Filter Env Amount=0.5 (no sweep) makes the whole filter ADSR inaudible; left at registered defaults.

defaultedParams.param: Morph Enabled / Morph Start / End / Duration / Curve
defaultedParams.why: A tom's timbre does not sweep material within a single hit; the static woody material + tension/pitch glide carry all the motion. Material Morph left OFF.
No-op: meaningful only for the FMImpulse exciter; this archetype uses Mallet.
No-op: Feedback-exciter only; not used.
No-op: NoiseBurst-exciter only; the tom uses Mallet (the Click layer provides the contact burst instead).
No-op: Friction-exciter only; not used.

Left neutral 0.5: cross-pad sympathetic resonance is a kit-level effect (only audible if Global Coupling / Tom Resonance > 0); the per-pad head↔shell coupling that matters for a tom is offset 54, set explicitly.

defaultedParams.param: Tightness / Brightness / Body Size / Punch / Complexity macros
defaultedParams.why: All five one-knob macros left neutral 0.5 (zero delta) — the recipe sets the underlying material/decay/size/pitchEnv/coupling params directly, so the macros must stay neutral to preserve those values.

defaultedParams.param: PitchEnv Knee / Mid / Mid Frac / Curve2
defaultedParams.why: The tom drop is a single fast Start→End segment; the 3-point knee adds nothing physical here, so left OFF (single-segment glide).

physics:
PITCH/FUNDAMENTAL. A tom is a tension-tuned circular membrane, NOT a timpani: there is no fixed kettle pinning the modes to a near-harmonic series, so its partials stay close to the ideal-membrane Bessel ratios. Real-world tunable range (drum-tuning data): 10" rack ~146–300 Hz, 12" ~174–220 Hz, 13"/14" rack down to ~110–160 Hz, 14"–16" floor ~73–110 Hz (D2–G2 region). The 6-pad row spans roughly a low floor tom (~200 Hz strike-fundamental settling near ~110 Hz) up to a high rack tom (~470→~290 Hz) — i.e. ONE drum re-tensioned, hence identical exciter/body and only size/pitchEnv graded.

MODE STRUCTURE / INHARMONICITY. Ideal circular-membrane mode ratios (Bessel-zero ratios, relative to (0,1)): (0,1)=1.000, (1,1)=1.593, (2,1)=2.136, (0,2)=2.296, (3,1)=2.653, (1,2)=2.918, (0,3)=3.600 (Dan Russell/PSU; Fletcher & Rossing ch.18). These are NON-harmonic — the tom has a clear but un-pitched-harmonic 'voice'. The audible pitch comes mainly from the (1,1)/(2,1) diametric modes (a center strike excites (0,1)/(0,2) → a quick pitchless thump; an off-center strike excites (1,1),(2,1)... → a definite lingering pitch). Air loading (the air inside/around a real drum) depresses the LOWEST modes (strongest on (0,1)/(1,1)) and is realistic but, for a two-headed open tom, weaker than for a kettle-loaded timpani (Rossing et al.; Well-Tempered Timpani). Membrum's airLoading param interpolates each membrane mode from pure-Bessel (0) toward the Rossing air-loaded curve (1); a tom wants a moderate value (deeper, less whistly, but not full timpani-harmonic).

DECAY / T60. Toms ring noticeably longer than snare/kick but are damped by two heads + air: musical sustain ~0.3–1.0 s for the body, longer on a resonant floor tom, shorter on a tight rock tom or with a single (concert) head; highs die faster than the low modes (Chaigne–Askenfelt b1+b3·f² law, which Membrum implements). 

ATTACK / CONTACT CLICK. A beater contact transient (a few ms, broadband, centered low-mid for felt / higher for a stick rim) precedes the modal ring — the 'thwack' that gives the hit definition.

NOISE CONTENT. Minimal for a clean tom: a little air/shell breathiness and beater contact noise; far less than a snare (no wires). Brown/pink-tilted, low level.

PITCH GLIDE ('kerthump'). Real membranes show a downward pitch glide after a hard strike: at large amplitude the membrane tension rises with displacement energy, so the pitch starts HIGH and relaxes DOWN as the strike energy decays — the characteristic tom 'kerthump'/'dewww' (numerical double-membrane tom studies; Avanzini–Marogna–Bank JASA 2012 quasistatic-tension model, which Membrum's tensionMod implements as an energy-driven UPWARD jump that relaxes). Two complementary mechanisms in Membrum produce this: (a) the ToneShaper pitchEnv glides the whole mode set Start→End (the dominant, deterministic 'tom drop'); (b) tensionMod adds a velocity/energy-dependent upward bump on top (the nonlinear 'crack' that relaxes), Membrane-only.

MATERIAL. Wood shell + plastic (Mylar) heads → woody, not metallic: low brightness, strong b3·f² high-mode damping (wood 'thump' rather than metal 'ring').
citations: plugins/membrum/src/dsp/bodies/membrane_mapper.h (verified f0=500*0.1^size, airLoading interp, decay/skew, b1/b3 override denorm)
tools/membrum_preset_generator.cpp (acousticKit/electronicKit tom rows verified as the implementation target)

bodyModel: Membrane (Bessel circular drumhead, 48 modes) — models the two-headed snare's batter head; air-loading + secondary shell give the coupled-head/housing weight, tension-mod gives the head's brief pitch-up under stick energy.
exciter: NoiseBurst (violet-noise bandpass burst) — emulates the stick-on-coated-head contact plus the instantaneous snare-wire excitation; a struck snare head is a broadband noisy contact, not a clean impulse.

params.rationale: Filtered-noise contact burst matches the noisy stick-on-head + wire excitation, not a clean impulse.
params.citation: SoS Synthesizing Drums: Snare Drum (wires snap back against head); Rossing & Bork 1992

params.rationale: A snare is fundamentally a struck circular membrane; only the membrane mapper gives Bessel inharmonic modes + air-loading + tension-mod.
params.citation: Fletcher & Rossing, Physics of Musical Instruments ch.18

params.rationale: Coated Mylar head = mid-bright, slightly tilted up for the crack without metallic ring.
14" snare fundamental sits ~180–200 Hz (parallel (0,1) head mode).
params.citation: Rossing & Bork JASA 1992; Madsen Phys406; iDrumTune

params.physicalTarget: decayTime ≈ baseDecay·exp(lerp(ln0.3,ln3,0.28)) ≈ 0.51·0.42 ≈ 0.21 s (further shortened by b1 override)
params.rationale: Snares-on damps the head fast (>64% lost by 80 ms); the body is a short tat, the wires carry the tail.
params.citation: Madsen Phys406 (snares-on decay); Rossing & Bork (0,1 highly damped)

params.rationale: Players strike a snare off-center toward 1/3 radius — excites the (0,1) plus higher modes for crack, not pure center.
Snares are loud, transient-heavy hits; near-max per-pad make-up.
A downward LP sweep on the tone shaper models the head's high-mode energy dropping right after impact.
params.citation: SoS (VC lowpass swept by impact velocity)

params.rationale: Open at the start so the crack passes; the env then closes it down.
A little emphasis at the moving cutoff for snap, well short of self-resonance.

params.physicalTarget: amount = 0.72·2-1 = +0.44 → up to +1.3 oct upward sweep on cutoff
params.rationale: Positive: cutoff is highest at attack (full crack/wires) then settles — equivalent to the SoS velocity-driven brightness that fades.
The crack is present at t=0.
Brightness collapses within tens of ms as high head modes damp.
No sustained brightness floor — fully decays to the base cutoff.
Short release; the note is over quickly.
Glide starts at the head fundamental (200 Hz) — matches Size, so the body doesn't jump.
Settles ~130 Hz as head detensions after the strike (mild, not a synth boom).
params.citation: task spec; head detensioning analogue of Avanzini tension model

params.rationale: Very short glide so it reads as a natural head settle, not a kick pitch-down.
params.citation: Avanzini et al. JASA 2012 (fast tension relaxation)

params.rationale: Energy-driven detension falls quickly then flattens.
A touch of odd-harmonic warmth on the body; a real snare has no real saturation, so kept tiny (flavour not level).
Snare head modes are already the natural (inharmonic) Bessel set; no artificial stretch wanted.
No deliberate high/low decay tilt beyond what damping already gives.
A snare is enharmonic noise+head, not a tonal harmonic series; injecting an integer series would make it pitched/tom-like.
Hard snare hits get audibly brighter/crackier (nonlinear head + wire engagement); velocity-sensitive, sustained through the short ring.
params.citation: audit M-3/M-4 redesign; Stowell cymbal tap=bell/hard=broadband

params.rationale: The snare wires ARE most of the sound; broadband buzz dominates the body.
params.citation: task spec; Madsen (snares add broadband noise); iDrumTune (wire rattle >5 kHz)

params.rationale: Wire buzz energy is concentrated high (sizzle 7 kHz+); open the noise LP wide.
Wire buzz is broadband, not a narrow resonant peak.
Wire 'shhh' sustains slightly past the body so it doesn't sound like a tom; distinguishes snare tail.
Snare-wire spectrum is tilted strongly upward (rattle dominates high band).

physics:
FUNDAMENTAL / PITCH: A 14" snare's audible pitch is set by the coupled (0,1) head motion. Struck near center the spectrum is initially dominated by a strong partial ~180–200 Hz (parallel (0,1) two-head motion); 14" drums tune well at ~170–200 Hz fundamental (Rossing & Bork; Madsen Phys406). Mixing practice puts the snare "body/weight" at 100–250 Hz, "crack/attack" at 3–5 kHz, and snare-wire "sizzle/rattle" above 5–7 kHz (iDrumTune, Kiive, izotope). TARGET f0 ≈ 200 Hz → Membrane size norm 0.40 (500·0.1^0.40 = 199 Hz).
PARTIAL / MODE STRUCTURE & INHARMONICITY: Modal frequencies are NOT harmonic — distributed enharmonically; the drum produces a split pair for each of the (0,1) and (1,1) modes due to two-head air coupling (Rossing & Bork, JASA 1992). SoS measured two non-harmonic series (base ~111 Hz with 175/224 Hz offsets; intervals ~101–125 Hz) and the principal (0,1) pair near ~180 Hz and ~330 Hz. The native Bessel ratios (1 : 1.59 : 2.14 : 2.30 : 2.65 …) are already inharmonic, so only mild extra scatter is wanted (mode_scatter for the organic head-to-head split), NOT large modeStretch.
DECAY / T60: Snares ON dramatically shorten the body decay (loses ~64% of peak by 0.08 s with snares engaged vs 24% with snares off — Madsen). The (0,1) head pair decays >2× faster than other partials (highly damped, ~5% damping in (0,1) from inter-head coupling — Rossing & Bork; SoS). So a SHORT, heavily damped body (~150–250 ms ring) with the noise/wire layer carrying the audible tail. TARGET body decay short; flat damping b1 high.
ATTACK / CONTACT CLICK: Sharp stick "crack" at 3–5 kHz from the initial high-mode excitation and the wires snapping back against the head ("the cable leaves the head then snaps back" — SoS). 2–5 ms bright contact transient.
NOISE CONTENT & SPECTRAL SHAPE: The snares add the characteristic broadband buzz — bright, tilted up (energy above 5 kHz), sustaining slightly past the body (the "shhh" that separates a snare from a tom/hat). White-to-violet tilt, ~7–10 kHz emphasis, ~250–350 ms tail.
PITCH GLIDE: A real snare has only a tiny upward "tension" blip — the head pitches up briefly under stick energy then relaxes (energy-dependent tension modulation, Avanzini et al. JASA 2012), NOT a synth-style downward 808 boom. The task's stated pitchEnv 200→130 Hz is a mild settle that emulates the head detensioning as the strike energy dissipates; kept short and shallow so it does not read as a synth kick.
MATERIAL: Mylar batter head over a wood/metal shell with steel snare wires on the bottom (coated/clear plastic membrane → mid material, slightly bright).

archetype: Agogo Bell (Brazilian twin metal bell, world percussion) — hi/lo pair
bodyModel: Bell (church-bell Chladni partial bank, 16 modes; nominal f0 = 800·0.1^size, hum at 0.25·f0). Chosen over Plate because the agogo has a clear, definite pitch (a struck conical metal bell with a recognizable note and a small set of strong partials), not the dense free-plate Chladni wash of a cymbal/gong. The Bell ratio table {0.25, 0.5, 0.6, 0.75, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, ...} gives the perceived strike pitch + a metallic but pitched overtone fan, which is exactly what distinguishes an agogo from a cowbell (clearer pitch) and from a cymbal (no pitch).
exciter: FMImpulse (Chowning bell-FM exciter; carrier ratio fixed 1.0, modulator ratio = 1+3·fmRatio, modulation index 0.5..3.0 rad velocity-driven, ampEnv τ=80 ms / modIndexEnv τ=30 ms so the bright FM clang fades faster than the body ring). FMImpulse injects an inharmonic metallic FM transient that excites the Bell bank — this is the canonical synthesized-cowbell/agogo recipe (two detuned metal-ringing sources at a non-integer interval). The agogo uses a HIGHER FM ratio than the cowbell to be brighter and more inharmonic.

params.rationale: Chowning bell-FM transient is the canonical synthesized agogo/cowbell exciter — injects the inharmonic metallic clang.
Agogo has a clear definite pitch with a small pitched overtone set — the Bell Chladni bank, not the cymbal-like free Plate.
Far above the cowbell's 1:1.44; a high non-integer ratio gives the brighter, more inharmonic metal spectrum that makes the agogo brighter/higher than a cowbell. (LO bell pad: 0.55 -> ratio 2.65.)

params.physicalTarget: Bell nominal f0 = 800*0.1^0.14 ≈ 580 Hz (HI bell); LO pad size 0.22 -> ≈ 480 Hz
params.rationale: Small thin conical bell -> high strike pitch; the hi/lo size offset (0.14 vs 0.22) realizes the ~M3 interval between the twin bells.

params.physicalTarget: metallic: Bell brightness=0.7+0.3*0.85≈0.955, base decay lerp(0.4,1.4,0.85)≈1.25 s before Decay knob
params.rationale: Thin sheet-iron/steel is very metallic -> high brightness, low high-frequency (b3) damping so upper partials ring bright.

params.physicalTarget: decayTime = base*exp(lerp(ln0.3,ln3,0.28)) ≈ base*0.42 -> few-hundred-ms pitched ring (HI). LO pad 0.35 -> slightly longer.
params.rationale: Real agogos have internal muffling stickers killing long overtones -> short, clearly-pitched ring, NOT a church-bell sustain; hi bell shorter than lo.

params.physicalTarget: flat damping b1 = 0.2+0.3*49.8 ≈ 15 s^-1 (overrides Decay-derived flat damping)
params.rationale: Sets a moderate overall RT60 floor for a short pitched bell; matches the cowbell/agogo pads already in the Latin/World kits.

params.physicalTarget: b3 = 0 -> pure flat damping, no f^2 high-freq roll-off (metallic, long bright upper partials)
params.rationale: Metal sustains its high partials; zero b3 keeps the bright metallic shimmer that defines the agogo over a wood block.

params.physicalTarget: strike azimuth theta = 0.3*(pi/2)=0.47 rad; per-mode |cos(m*theta)| weighting
params.rationale: A clapper/beater strikes near the rim soundbow; default off-antinode azimuth gives a balanced pitched partial mix without killing modes.
Per-pad loudness for a bright cutting percussion hit; matches the existing agogo/cowbell pads.

params.physicalTarget: click layer at 0.55*velocity*env (half into exciter feed, full into output *0.445)
params.rationale: Hard beater on thin sheet iron -> bright metallic 'ting' contact transient; agogo attack must be crisp.
Sharp metallic tick, not a dull thud -> high bandpass center.
Short hard-beater contact time for a tight metal tick.

params.physicalTarget: parallel noise layer fully bypassed (mix<=0 = silent)
params.rationale: A clean pitched metal bell has essentially no broadband hiss/sizzle — unlike a hat or shaker.

params.physicalTarget: no membrane air-loading frequency correction (no-op on Bell anyway)
params.rationale: Air loading is Membrane-only physics; a metal bell is not an air-loaded drumhead -> 0.

params.physicalTarget: physical = 0.5+0.45*1.5 = 1.175 -> mild partial stretch above the physical bell ratios
params.rationale: A small struck conical bell is slightly more inharmonic/stretched than an ideal church bell; a touch of stretch adds metallic clang without losing pitch. (Neutral is 0.333.)

params.physicalTarget: bipolar = 0.4*2-1 = -0.2; per-mode tilt ratio^(+0.2) lifts upper partials above the nominal
params.rationale: Negative skew brightens the spectrum (lifts the high metallic partials, trims the hum) for a brighter agogo voice; neutral is 0.5.

params.physicalTarget: ~1.8% sinusoidal frequency dither on the modal partials
params.rationale: Hand-rolled sheet-iron bells are imperfect -> slight natural detune for organic metal; kept low to preserve clear pitch.

params.physicalTarget: equal-power pan slightly left (hi bell); LO pad 0.58 slightly right
params.rationale: The twin bells sit side-by-side in a player's hand -> small stereo spread between hi and lo pads.
Slight one-knob brightening to emphasize the agogo's bright metallic top; small positive delta keeps it musical.

No-op: only the NoiseBurst exciter reads it (and the Punch macro is neutral).
No-op: only the Friction exciter reads it.

Bypassed at 0; the FM exciter supplies the metallic harmonics, no saturation flavour wanted.

defaultedParams.why: Head<->shell secondary resonator off; an agogo is a single resonating bell, no separate coupled shell.

physics: PITCH/FUNDAMENTAL: Agogo is the highest-pitched instrument of the samba bateria; a struck conical sheet-iron bell with a clear, definite pitch. The two bells are tuned an interval apart — most commonly a major third or a perfect fourth (organology/Kalango/Wikipedia); the pictured LP-type pair in Wikipedia is ~a major third. Precise Hz is not standardized (Brazilian practice favors individuality/dissonance), but the audible strike note sits high, roughly hi ≈ 700–1000 Hz strike pitch and lo a M3/P4 below (~550–800 Hz) — brighter and higher than a cowbell, whose canonical synthesized strike sits around 587/845 Hz. PARTIAL STRUCTURE/INHARMONICITY: a small conical bell rings with a definite pitch but a metallic, mildly inharmonic overtone set — far fewer, more pitched partials than a cymbal. The established electronic synthesis of this family (TR-808/CR-78 cowbell, agogo) is two metal-timbre oscillators at a NON-INTEGER interval — Sound On Sound gives the cowbell as two oscillators at 587 Hz and 845 Hz, ratio 1:1.44, and notes 'even small deviations destroy the illusion'; FM literature (Chowning) shows non-integer carrier:modulator ratios (≥~2.5) give bright, inharmonic bell/metal spectra. The agogo, being brighter/clearer/higher than the cowbell, is captured by pushing the FM modulator ratio well above the cowbell's 1.44 — into the ~2.6–3.2 range — for a brighter inharmonic clang while the pitched Bell body keeps a clear nominal. DECAY/T60: muffling stickers inside real agogos deliberately damp lingering overtones, so the ring is short-to-medium and clearly pitched, NOT a long church-bell sustain — on the order of a few hundred ms (shorter than a cowbell's tail on the lo bell, brighter+shorter on the hi). The bright FM/metal attack decays faster than the pitched body (modIndexEnv τ=30 ms << ampEnv τ=80 ms, and body decay set short). ATTACK/CLICK: a hard wooden/metal beater striking thin sheet iron gives a bright metallic 'ting' contact transient — a clear, bright click is needed (no soft thud). NOISE CONTENT: essentially none — a clean pitched metal bell; agogo has no broadband hiss/sizzle (unlike a hat or cabasa), so the parallel noise layer is OFF. PITCH GLIDE: none — agogos do not 'kerthump'; pitch is static (tensionModAmt off, and it is Membrane-only anyway). MATERIAL: thin rolled sheet iron/steel — metallic, so material high → low b3 high-frequency damping (long bright partials) and metallic brightness. The hi bell is smaller (higher f0, brighter, shorter) and the lo bell larger (lower f0, slightly longer). Source-derived numbers driving params: cowbell ratio 1.44 (SoS) → agogo pushed to ~2.6–3.2; M3/P4 interval between bells → size offset between hi/lo pads; high strike pitch → small Bell size; short pitched ring → short decay + low b1.

bodyModel: Cycle across all modal bodies, i-indexed: Bell → String → Shell → Plate → Membrane → NoiseBody (bodyCycle[i % 6]). This is a parametric palette, not one body. Bell/Plate/Shell/NoiseBody carry the inharmonic metallic core; String injects a struck/plucked waveguide voice; Membrane gives the one body that responds to the energy-driven tension glide. Each body uses its corrected post-audit mapper (Plate = free-plate (m+2n)^1.7 Chladni; Shell free-free end-antinode shape; Bell 2-D (m,θ) meridional; NoiseBody = plate ratios + internal noise).
exciter: Alternate FMImpulse (i%3==1) and Feedback (i%3==0), with Friction on the remainder (i%3==2) — matching the existing chaosEngineKit selector. FMImpulse drives a Chowning bell-FM tone into the body (modulatorRatio i-ramped 1.0→4.0, index velocity-driven 0.5–3.0 rad); Feedback creates an energy-limited self-oscillating body↔exciter squeal (feedbackAmount i-ramped); Friction adds a struck-bow scrape (frictionPressure i-ramped). The exciter alternation is itself a primary axis of timbral variety across the 14 crafted pads.

params.physicalTarget: i-indexed: Feedback(5) when i%3==0, FMImpulse(4) when i%3==1, Friction(3) when i%3==2. norm shown (0.8) denormalizes to FMImpulse via round(0.8*5)=4; per-pad the builder writes the enum directly. Cited values: Feedback=1.0, FMImpulse=0.8, Friction=0.6.
params.rationale: Alternating FM/feedback/friction is the defining exciter signature of this archetype; each backend imprints a different transient + sustain character so no two adjacent pads share an attack.
params.citation: chaosEngineKit selector, membrum_preset_generator.cpp:3397-3398; Chowning 1973 FM bell, https://ccrma.stanford.edu/software/clm/compmus/clm-tutorials/fm2.html

params.physicalTarget: i-indexed bodyCycle[i%6]: Bell(4,norm0.8)/String(3,norm0.6)/Shell(2,norm0.4)/Plate(1,norm0.2)/Membrane(0,norm0.0)/NoiseBody(5,norm1.0). Builder writes enum directly.
params.rationale: Body cycling across all six resonators is the core 'multi-body' idea — a broad inharmonic palette in one kit; each body's mapper produces a distinct partial topology.

params.physicalTarget: i-ramped 0.20 + fmod(i*0.06,0.75) → ~0.20..0.92; mid 0.5 representative. Per body: brightness/decay-base/tilt vary (e.g. Bell brightness 0.7+0.3*mat; Plate 0.5+0.5*mat).
params.rationale: Ramping material across pads sweeps woody↔metallic so each FX hit sits at a different spectral tilt; the strongest perceptual lever, deliberately spread.

params.physicalTarget: i-ramped 0.25 + fmod(i*0.05,0.65) → ~0.25..0.90; f0 = bodyBase*0.1^size. Bell/String/Plate base 800 Hz → ~80..450 Hz; Shell/NoiseBody base 1500 → ~150..840 Hz.
params.rationale: Spreading body fundamental across the pad index gives the palette a wide pitch range of hits/textures rather than one register.

params.physicalTarget: i-ramped 0.40 + (i%7)*0.07 → 0.40..0.82 → decayTime = base*exp(lerp(ln0.3,ln3.0,decay)) ≈ 0.74x..2.0x base; long ringy FX tails.
params.rationale: Longer-than-default decay gives the inharmonic bodies room to ring into texture; ramped so some pads are short stabs and others sustain.

params.physicalTarget: default 0.3 → membrane r/a=0.27; plate/shell/bell off-center mode-shape sampling; String pick 0.3.
params.rationale: Left near default off-center — re-weights modes for a non-axisymmetric, brighter strike without becoming a per-pad ramp axis (the body/exciter cycles already carry variety).

params.physicalTarget: linear 0.72 pre-limiter attenuation (−2.85 dB), applied as out=hardClip(shaped*env*0.72).
params.rationale: Slightly below the 0.8 default to keep a 14-voice polyphonic FX kit out of the −1 dBTP bus limiter; matches chaosEngine's 0.72.
Cycling the SVF shape per pad adds spectral variety to the tone-shaper stage of each FX voice.

params.physicalTarget: i-ramped 0.30 + fmod(i*0.07,0.60) → ~0.30..0.90 → 20*1000^cutoff ≈ 160 Hz..6.3 kHz. Engages the filter (below 20 kHz bypass).
params.rationale: A mid cutoff engages the SVF so Filter Type/Resonance/Env are audible — central to the 'algorithmic FX' shaping; ramped for variety.
High resonance gives each filter a vocal/ringing emphasis — a hallmark of resonant-filter drum-synth FX and near-self-oscillation textures.

params.physicalTarget: bipolar 0.55*2-1 = +0.10 → up to +0.3 oct upward cutoff sweep (modCutoff=cutoff*2^(3*amount*env)).
params.rationale: A modest positive sweep animates the resonant filter per hit, turning static FX into evolving ones.
Near-instant filter-env attack so the resonant sweep fires on the transient.
Short-to-medium filter decay shapes the resonant chirp length per pad.

params.physicalTarget: i-ramped 0.30 + (i%4)*0.12 → 0.30..0.66 → internalDrive 3.7..6.9; ReciprocalSqrt waveshaper with unity-makeup (timbre, not level).
params.rationale: Post-audit Drive adds odd-harmonic grit without compressing — gives the FX voices saturated bite; ramped per pad.
Wavefolding is a west-coast/experimental harmonic generator — exactly the aggressive added-harmonic character wanted for synthetic FX; only this archetype-family pushes it.

params.physicalTarget: i-ramped 0.40 + (i%5)*0.12 → 0.40..0.88 → physical 0.5+norm*1.5 = 1.1..1.82 (stretched/inharmonic). Modal bodies only (no-op String).
params.rationale: Stretching partials beyond physical ratios is THE inharmonicity dial; the corrected (k+1) index + widened B_max make this audibly metallic — primary FX timbre axis.
params.citation: membrum_preset_generator.cpp:3407; stiff-string B, https://nathan.ho.name/posts/exploring-modal-synthesis/; AUDIT §3-B stretch fix

params.physicalTarget: i-ramped 0.40 + (i%5)*0.12 → 0.40..0.88 → bipolar*2-1 = −0.2..+0.76; per-mode amplitude tilt ratio^(-skew) on all modal bodies (post-audit).
params.rationale: Tilts spectral energy high↔low per pad so even same-body hits decay with different spectral balance.
params.citation: membrum_preset_generator.cpp:3410; M-5 per-mode tilt all bodies, AUDIT REMEDIATION

params.physicalTarget: verbatim 0.2 → ModeInject bank at 20% mix: 8 integer-harmonic oscillators (1..8) at body f0, amplitudes 1/k (−6 dB/oct sawtooth/bowed slope), phases re-randomized per noteOn, summed pre-ToneShaper. Exact bypass at 0.
params.rationale: DISTINCTIVE: this is the ONLY archetype that engages modeInject. It lays a synthetic 1/k harmonic series (extra pitched 'body'/fundamental weight) on top of the inharmonic modal bank — a hybrid harmonic+inharmonic spectrum that reads as deliberately synthetic, with no acoustic referent.
params.citation: task spec (only-user of modeInject); mode_inject 1/k −6dB/oct fix, AUDIT §3-B; sawtooth 1/k spectrum, https://nathan.ho.name/posts/exploring-modal-synthesis/

params.physicalTarget: i-ramped 0.65 + (i%3)*0.10 → 0.65..0.85 → env-level driven waveshaping: drive=1+6*vel*amount*env, shaped=recipSqrt(body*drive); louder→brighter, sustained.
params.rationale: High amplitude-driven brightening makes hard hits cascade into broadband energy (von Kármán-style nonlinear plate/cymbal coupling) — a core FX behavior; ramped per pad.
params.citation: membrum_preset_generator.cpp:3409; M-3/M-4 redesign, AUDIT REMEDIATION; cymbal nonlinear energy cascade

params.physicalTarget: i-ramped 0.20 + fmod(i*0.07,0.70) → ~0.20..0.83 → modulatorRatio 1.0+3.0*norm = 1.6..3.5 (carrier:modulator). NO-OP unless FMImpulse selected on that pad.
params.rationale: Non-integer FM ratios (1.6, 2.7, 3.5...) give the FMImpulse exciter inharmonic bell/metallic spectra; ramping it varies the FM color across the FM pads.
params.citation: membrum_preset_generator.cpp:3404; fm_impulse_exciter.h:80; Chowning bell c:m inharmonicity, https://ccrma.stanford.edu/software/clm/compmus/clm-tutorials/fm2.html

params.physicalTarget: i-ramped 0.40 + (i%4)*0.12 → 0.40..0.76 → drive=vel+fb*(1-vel), feedbackAmount=drive*0.85 (≤0.85 stable). NO-OP unless Feedback exciter selected.
params.rationale: Sets the self-oscillation drive floor of the Feedback exciter — the sustained squeal/energy that defines those pads; ramped for intensity variety.

params.physicalTarget: i-ramped 0.30 + (i%3)*0.15 → 0.30..0.60 → bow pressure clamp(0.1+0.4*vel+0.5*norm). NO-OP unless Friction exciter selected.
params.rationale: Bow pressure bias for the Friction pads — scrape/squeal intensity; ramped per pad.

params.physicalTarget: i-ramped 0.55 + (i%5)*0.08 → 0.55..0.87 → up to ~13% sinusoidal frequency dither on modal frequencies. No-op String.
params.rationale: Heavy scatter de-tunes the modal ratios into an organic/chaotic inharmonic mess — exactly the 'experimental texture' goal; a defining high value for this archetype.

params.physicalTarget: i-ramped 0.78 + (i%4)*0.05 → 0.78..0.93 → amt*3.3e-5 energy scale → up to +3 st energy-driven upward glide that relaxes with decay. MEMBRANE body only.
params.rationale: Very high tension mod gives the Membrane pads a pronounced energy-driven pitch jump ('kerthump') — on other bodies it is a harmless no-op, so it's safe to ramp high across the cycle.
params.citation: membrum_preset_generator.cpp:3432; Avanzini-Marogna-Bank 2012; tension Membrane-gate, AUDIT N-1 redesign

params.rationale: A descending pitch glide gives each FX hit a 909/808-style downward transient sweep — a non-physical sound-design move; start ramped per pad.
Low settle pitch completes the downward FX glide.

params.physicalTarget: i-ramped 0.04 + (i%5)*0.04 → 0.04..0.20 → *500 ms = 20..100 ms glide. >0 enables the whole pitch-env feature.
params.rationale: Short glide time makes the pitch drop a transient chirp, not a slow bend; this also enables pitch-env on all bodies (post-H-3 fix).
params.citation: membrum_preset_generator.cpp:3448; H-3 pitch-env all bodies, AUDIT REMEDIATION

params.rationale: Alternating linear vs exponential glide shape adds per-pad transient variety.

params.physicalTarget: i-ramped 0.20 + (i%5)*0.10 → 0.20..0.60 → parallel filtered-noise layer at mix*env*noise*0.243.
params.rationale: A moderate noise bed adds breath/sizzle/grit to the synthetic hits; ramped so some pads are cleaner and some noisier.
Spreads the noise-layer brightness across pads.
Mild resonant noise emphasis for a more 'designed' noise tone.
Medium-to-long noise tails support the sustained FX-texture character.
Varying noise color (dark→bright) across pads diversifies the noise-bed timbre.

params.physicalTarget: i-ramped 0.20 + (i%5)*0.10 → 0.20..0.60 → always-on attack transient, half into exciter feed + full to out *0.445.
params.rationale: A click defines the attack of each FX hit and (via the half-amplitude exciter feed) helps strike the body; ramped per pad.
Short contact gives a sharp tick rather than a thud.

params.physicalTarget: i-ramped 0.40 + (i%5)*0.12 → 0.40..0.88 → exp(ln200+norm*(ln12000-ln200)) ≈ 880 Hz..9 kHz bandpass center.
params.rationale: Bright click centers give a sharp synthetic tick that cuts through the inharmonic ring.

params.physicalTarget: i-ramped 0.30 + (i%6)*0.05 → 0.30..0.55 → 0.2+norm*49.8 = 15..27 s^-1 flat damping (overrides Decay-derived). Modal bodies only.
params.rationale: Explicit mid flat-damping floor keeps the long-decay FX bodies controllable; ramped per pad.

Only affects the Membrane pads in the cycle (deeper, less whistly head); harmless on the metallic bodies.

params.rationale: Adds a coupled shell resonance to every FX voice for extra metallic body weight.
Spreads the coupled-shell pitch ratio across pads for varied second-layer color.
Varies the coupled shell's metal↔wood character per pad.

params.physicalTarget: i-set: 0 for i<4, else (i%2)+1 → groups 1/2. clamp(int(norm*8+0.5),0,8).

Spreads pads across aux buses (pre-master sends) for external multi-out FX processing.

params.physicalTarget: 0.85 → +deltas: couplingAmount += linDelta(0.85,0.50), nonlinearCoupling += linDelta(0.85,0.30), modeInjectAmount += linDelta(0.85,0.30).
params.rationale: High complexity layers more sympathetic coupling, nonlinear brightening and injected modes — the macro that most reinforces the dense experimental character.

params.physicalTarget: verbatim 0.78 → high per-pad weight in the global cross-pad sympathetic engine (audible with kit globalCoupling 0.92).
params.rationale: Strong inter-pad coupling makes the whole kit ring sympathetically — a key kit-level FX behavior.

params.physicalTarget: 0.5 = center, equal-power (gainL=gainR, constant power). Can be i-spread for a wide FX image.
params.rationale: Left center as the safe default; per-pad pan can be ramped to widen the FX stereo field (M-9) but is not required by the archetype.

defaultedParams.why: Kept at 0.3 default — body/exciter cycling already carries the timbral variety; an extra ramp here is redundant for these mode-shape-sampling bodies.
Percussive FX hits use a one-shot filter envelope; sustain 0 and short release are fine — no sustained filter pedal needed.

Optional intra-hit material sweep; chaos-family enables it on a subset (i%2), but the body/material cycle already provides timbral motion, so it's left at default-off for the baseline and only opt-in per pad.
The single-segment Start→End glide is enough for an FX chirp; the 3-point knee adds complexity without a clear archetype payoff.
1.0 for the 14 crafted FX pads; the remaining pads are disabled by the crafted-list gating (silenced, not voiced).

Left near neutral 0.5 (chaos sets mild ramps) — the underlying explicit params are already set directly, so macro deltas would only re-bias them; Complexity is the one macro pushed because it best amplifies the experimental density.
Center by default; equal-power law means a centered pad is bit-identical to the legacy mono image — stereo spread is an optional enhancement, not core to the archetype.

physics:
This archetype has NO acoustic referent — it is a synthesis-domain construct, so the 'physics' is the established synthesis of inharmonic/FX percussion rather than a measured instrument. Three synthesis mechanisms define it:

(1) FM inharmonicity (Chowning 1973). An FM pair with carrier:modulator ratio C:M produces sidebands at C ± n·M. Integer ratios → harmonic; irrational/large ratios → inharmonic 'metallic/bell' spectra. The classic Chowning bell is C:M = 1:1.4 with modulation index swept high→low under an exponential amplitude decay (the canonical 'bright→pure metallic' bell uses index up to ~10 and ratios like 5:7). Modulation index ≈ A_m/F_m sets brightness: more index → more significant sidebands. Membrum's FMImpulse maps norm→modulatorRatio = 1.0+3.0·norm (1.0..4.0), index velocity-driven 0.5..3.0 rad, base freq 400..2500 Hz velocity-driven — so ratios 1.6/2.7/3.5 across the i-ramp give a spread of inharmonic FM colors (fm_impulse_exciter.h:80-93). Sources: CCRMA FM2 tutorial; Wikipedia FM synthesis.

(2) Feedback / self-oscillation. Routing an operator's (or the body↔exciter loop's) output back into itself drives it from sine toward complex/chaotic waveforms; a resonant element on the verge of self-oscillation rings and squeals — historically how analog drum synths and resonant-filter percussion made tuned/FX hits. Membrum's Feedback exciter sets a drive floor feedbackAmount·0.85 (≤0.85 enforced for stability), forcing the body's slow per-sample path and a −3 dBFS soft-clip. Source: lfusionmodular percussive synthesis; Wikipedia FM feedback.

(3) Modal inharmonicity + synthetic harmonic injection. Modal banks become metallic via a stretch factor on the partial ladder: f_k ∝ k·[1+(k−1)B], with B~1e-3 'pleasant metallic' and larger B losing pitch (Nathan Ho). Per-mode decay R_k = b1 + b3·f_k² makes highs decay faster (Chaigne-Askenfelt form). Excitation position weights modes via sin(πkx). Non-physical moves — 909-style downward pitch sweeps, heavy frequency scatter, and sum/difference artificial partials — push it into pure sound-design territory. Membrum implements all of these: modeStretch (corrected (k+1) index, B_max widened to 0.01), modeScatter (~13% dither), per-mode decaySkew tilt on every body (post-audit), and a downward PitchEnv on all bodies.

DISTINCTIVE driver for THIS archetype — modeInject at 0.2: an 8-oscillator integer-harmonic series (1..8) at the body f0 with amplitudes 1/k. 1/k is the sawtooth/bowed spectrum, −6 dB/octave (8th partial −18 dB), as opposed to 1/k² (−12 dB/oct, triangle/plucked). Membrum fixed the default from 1/k² to 1/k in the audit. Layering this CLEAN harmonic series at 20% under the INHARMONIC modal/FM body produces a hybrid harmonic+inharmonic spectrum that the ear reads as overtly synthetic — there is no instrument that does this, which is exactly the point. This is the only archetype that engages modeInject.

Net acoustic profile of a typical hit: a sharp click/FM/scrape transient → an inharmonic ringing body (stretched modes + scatter + per-mode tilt) with a clean 1/k harmonic ghost underneath → louder hits cascade brighter via nonlinear coupling → a short downward pitch chirp (20–100 ms) on attack → medium-to-long decays (0.7–2× base, up to ~1.2 s noise tails) → on Membrane pads an energy-driven UPWARD tension glide (+up to 3 st relaxing with decay). Decay/pitch/spectral structure are all i-indexed ramps, so the 14 crafted pads span a broad palette rather than variations of one hit.

bodyModel: Membrane (Bessel circular drumhead). The membrane mapper is the only body whose f0 base (500 Hz * 0.1^size) and tension-mod gating give a controllable, pitch-swept, drum-register fundamental; an analog-machine tom is a single pitch-swept ringing tone, which the membrane's lowest in-phase modes produce. Physics realism axes (airLoading, modeScatter, coupling) are ZEROED so the body behaves like a clean synthetic oscillator rather than a real acoustic head — exactly how the 808/909 bridged-T / VCO circuits sound.
exciter: Impulse (raised-cosine click) — the 909/trap variant. The 808/909 tom is a pulse-triggered ringing oscillator: a sharp impulse rings the tuned circuit, giving a tight click-then-tone with no soft beater wash. (LinnDrum variant uses Mallet for the softer sampled-acoustic attack; documented in defaultedParams as the alternate exciter.) The always-on Click layer supplies the explicit b1/b3 contact tick on top.

Left neutral 0.5 (zero delta) in the archetype baseline so the explicit per-param values stand; kit builders may add macroPunch~0.65 for extra snap.
