physics:
FUNDAMENTAL / PITCH RANGE. Analog-machine toms are a tuned ringing oscillator, not a struck membrane. TR-808 toms (bridged-T oscillator = VCO+VCA+ENV in one cell): High 165-220 Hz, Mid 120-160 Hz, Low 80-100 Hz (baratatronix). TR-909 toms use multiple VCOs at ratio 1:1.5:2.77 (low tom), TUNE-controlled, with a Tom Noise Generator; snappier/shorter than the 808 and the model for the punchy "impulse" tom (modwiggler). The 6-tom size grade here sweeps START 240->590 Hz and END 85->210 Hz across small->large register, matching machine-tom tuning. SETTLE (perceived) pitch = the END value; the START is the iconic "kerthump" peak.

PITCH GLIDE ("kerthump"). The 808 produces a SUBTLE downward sweep purely from diode current-starvation as the envelope decays (baratatronix); machines with a real pitch sweep (and the synthesis canon, SOS "synthesizing percussion": start higher, decay to lower over 50-100 ms) make it explicit. Modeled as ToneShaper pitch env: fast (60-100 ms) exponential (curveAmount<0, fast initial drop) downward glide of ~1.5 octaves. The trap variant ADDS energy-dependent membrane tension glide (tensionModAmt) on top for the modern up-then-relax pitch bump (Avanzini-Marogna-Bank 2012: dT proportional to membrane energy; gated Membrane-only).

PARTIAL / MODE STRUCTURE & INHARMONICITY. A REAL acoustic tom is an inharmonic Bessel membrane (ratios 1.0, 1.59, 2.14, 2.30, 2.65, 2.92...; Illinois PHYS406; euphonics.org notes "no obvious harmonic relations") with no definite pitch; air-loading lowers the low modes. But an ANALOG-MACHINE tom is deliberately the OPPOSITE: a near-pure pitched oscillator. So physics realism axes are zeroed (airLoading 0, modeScatter 0, coupling off) and modeStretch left neutral — the model should NOT spray inharmonic partials or it stops sounding like a 909. The b1/b3 damping law (Chaigne & Askenfelt 1994, R = b1 + b3*f^2; AUDIT 3-A CONFIRMED) is used instead to set ring length and a mild woody high-mode rolloff.

DECAY / T60. 808 tom decay-only envelopes: High 100 ms, Mid 130 ms, Low 200 ms (baratatronix); 909 toms shorter/snappier. So an analog tom is SHORT (snappier than the 808 boom-tom): small toms ~100-150 ms, large ~200-300 ms. Set via Decay knob + bodyDampingB1 (flat damping floor) graded by size. decaySkew slightly positive on the kick-adjacent toms; neutral here.

TRANSIENT / CONTACT CLICK. The Impulse exciter is itself a sharp click; the always-on Click layer adds an explicit b1/b3 stick-tick (2-5 ms bandpassed noise) routed half into the body and full to output. 909 tom attack is bright and present (clickBrightness ~ 2-5 kHz); contact short.

NOISE CONTENT. The 808 adds a filtered-PINK-noise "fake reverb" with an envelope SLIGHTLY LONGER than the body (baratatronix) for dimensionality; the 909 has a Tom Noise Generator. This is a small, dark, low-level layer — NOT a snare hiss. Modeled as Noise layer mix ~0.05, brown/pink, low cutoff. Kept tiny so the pitched body dominates.

MATERIAL. Synthetic / electronic — there is no real shell or membrane material; "material" here just biases brightness and base decay. Graded LOW on small/dark toms (0.20) up to mid (0.55) on the highest, smallest tom for a slightly brighter snap.

bodyModel: Bell
exciter: NoiseBurst

params.rationale: A mallet glissando excites many bowl modes near-simultaneously; a filtered-noise burst spreads strike energy across the whole modal bank for the dense overlapping ring, unlike a single hard impulse.
params.citation: Wikipedia Bell tree (glissando, many bowls); param dictionary exciter enum 0..5

params.physicalTarget: idx = round(0.8*5) = 4 = Bell (church-bell Chladni partials, hum at 0.25*f0, nominal=1.0, 16 modes)
params.rationale: A bell tree is literally a stack of small tuned bells/bowls; the Bell body's hum/prime/tierce/quint/nominal series is the correct resonator.
params.citation: bell_modes.h kBellRatios; Hibberts Five Main Partials; Perrin/Charnley/DePont 1983 JSV

params.physicalTarget: Bell brightness = 0.7+0.3*0.95 = 0.985 (very metallic); baseDecay = lerp(0.4,1.4,0.95) = 1.35 s
params.rationale: Bronze/brass bowls are hard metal: maximal brightness and longest base ring, the bright shimmer.
params.citation: bell_mapper.h:53,62; Wikipedia Crotales (bronze/brass, very bright, long resonance)

params.physicalTarget: f0_nominal = 800*0.1^0.30 = 800*0.501 = 401 Hz nominal; hum ~100 Hz, upper partials up to ~4 kHz
params.rationale: Voices one bowl in the upper-mid register where small bells/crotales sit; high and bright, representing a bowl high in the cascade.
params.citation: bell_mapper.h:38; Wikipedia Crotales (small tuned discs, high register)

params.physicalTarget: decay multiplier = exp(lerp(ln0.3,ln3.0,0.85)) = 2.30x on the 1.35 s base, *skewBias(0.766) ~ 2.38 s ring
params.rationale: Crotales/small bells have 'much longer resonance' - a long shimmering tail is the whole point of a bell tree.

params.physicalTarget: strike azimuth theta = 0.3*(pi/2) = 0.471 rad; meridional |cos(m*theta)| weighting, floored 0.05
params.rationale: Default-ish off-antinode strike keeps all principal partials present with a natural balance; the bell strike-shape maps azimuth, not radius.
Slightly below default to leave headroom for the many overlapping rings in a glissando run summing in the pool.
params.citation: drum_voice.h:511 level applied linearly; param dictionary

params.physicalTarget: 20*1000^1 = 20000 Hz -> ToneShaper filter BYPASSED (cutoff>=20k and env amt 0)
params.rationale: No post-body tone shaping wanted; the body itself is the bright source. Transparent default.

params.physicalTarget: phys = 0.5 + 0.55*1.5 = 1.325; stretchNorm = (1.325-0.5)/1.5 = 0.55 fed to bank -> partials pushed off canonical ratios (more inharmonic)
params.rationale: Real bell-tree bowls are microtonally detuned and the run reads as inharmonic shimmer, not a tuned scale; stretching the partials breaks them off the textbook ratios.
params.citation: voice_pool.cpp:876; Wikipedia Bell tree ('microtonal, no formal scale'); modal_resonator_bank.h stretch warp

params.physicalTarget: phys = 0.78*2-1 = +0.56; global skewBias = 1-0.3*0.56 = 0.832; per-mode tilt ratio^(-0.56) lifts upper partials above the nominal
params.rationale: Tilts spectral energy toward the bright upper partials (the shimmering top of the cascade) while keeping the ring metallic; positive skew is the audit's per-mode tilt now active on Bell.
params.citation: voice_pool.cpp:878; bell_mapper.h:76-86 (M-5 per-mode tilt); audit Phase-2 small physics batch

params.physicalTarget: morph ON: Material is driven by the Morph envelope each noteOn
params.rationale: Enables a per-note timbral sweep so the bowl dims as the beater passes - the cascade's evolving brightness.

params.physicalTarget: Material starts at 0.85 (very bright) at the instant of strike
params.rationale: Bright initial contact (metal struck hard), then settling - matches a glissando bowl's bright onset.

params.physicalTarget: Material settles to 0.55 (less bright) over the morph duration
params.rationale: Brightness falls as the strike energy in the high modes decays - the dense overlap thins to a darker ring.
Slow ~1.1 s brightness sweep matches the long ring; the timbre evolves over most of the tail.
Even, gradual brightness decline; no need for the exponential snap.

params.physicalTarget: parallel noise layer at mix 0.42 (*0.243 standalone gain into the mix)
params.rationale: Airy metallic sizzle from many simultaneous bowl contacts; a moderate noise overlay adds the shimmer's hiss bed.
Bright noise - the shimmer is high-frequency sizzle, not low rumble.
Metal-contact noise is high-tilted; violet gives the brightest sizzle for the shimmer.
Long-ish noise tail so the sizzle decays WITH the metal ring rather than snapping off.

params.physicalTarget: click layer at 0.55 (half into exciter feed, full into output *0.445)
params.rationale: Each mallet contact is a sharp metallic tick; a prominent click defines the bright attack of every bowl in the run.
Sharp, glassy tick - hard mallet on thin metal is a very bright contact, not a dull thud.

params.physicalTarget: b1 = 0.2 + 0.30*49.8 = 15.14 s^-1 flat damping floor (overrides Decay-derived flat damping)
params.rationale: A modest flat-damping floor sets an overall long-but-bounded RT60 for the metal ring, consistent across modes.

params.physicalTarget: b3 = 0.0*1e-3 = 0 s -> NO frequency-squared damping: upper partials ring as long as low ones (pure metallic)
params.rationale: The defining trait of a bright bell/crotale is that high partials do NOT roll off - this keeps the shimmer alive; b3=0 = the most metallic setting.
params.citation: voice_pool.cpp:804; membrane_mapper.h:75-77; Wikipedia Crotales (bright, long high resonance)

params.rationale: Air-loading is a Membrane-only frequency table; on a Bell body it is a no-op, set 0 for clarity.
Adds organic bowl-to-bowl detune so the overlapping rings of a glissando never sound like one cleanly-tuned bell - the microtonal pile-up.
params.citation: voice_pool.cpp:807; ModalResonatorBank scatter; Wikipedia Bell tree (microtonal, no formal scale)

defaultedParams.why: NoiseBurst is the selected exciter, so fmRatio (FMImpulse-only) is a pure no-op; the spec's 0.55 has no audible effect. Left effectively inert.
Feedback exciter not selected - no-op.
Left at default 0.5 -> ~8.5 ms burst; a medium metallic contact length suits the dense run, no reason to push it short or long.
Friction exciter not selected - no-op.
ToneShaper filter is bypassed (cutoff at 20 kHz, env amount 0), so all SVF params are inert; the body is already the bright source.
Bypassed at 0; the bell spectrum is already rich and metallic, added saturation/folding would muddy the clean shimmer.
Rigid metal bowls have NO pitch glide; PitchEnv Time stays 0 so the whole feature is disabled - keeps the bell at its natural fundamental.
0 (exact bypass): the Bell body already supplies a full inharmonic partial set; injecting a synthetic 1/k harmonic series would add an un-bell-like tonal fundamental and fight the inharmonicity.
0 (exact bypass): a softly-struck small bowl is near-linear; amplitude-driven brightening is for plates/cymbals driven hard, not a gentle glissando tap.
0 = no choke; a bell tree's whole character is rings overlapping and decaying freely - choking would kill the cascade.
0 = main bus; no multi-out routing intended for this archetype.
Left at neutral default 0.5; only audible if a kit-level sympathetic-coupling engine is engaged, which is a kit-routing decision, not part of this voice's identity.
All neutral 0.5 (zero delta): the underlying params are set directly to their target values, so the one-knob macros are deliberately left transparent.
Default 0.2 (Q~1.2): a broad sizzle bed is wanted, not a resonant whistle that would pull a pitch out of the noise.
Default 0.3 -> ~2.9 ms; a short sharp tick is exactly right for a hard metallic contact, no need to lengthen.

defaultedParams.param: Coupling Strength / Secondary Enabled / Size / Material (off 54-57)
defaultedParams.why: Secondary shell off: a small bell has no separate head<->shell body to couple; the single Bell bank carries the whole sound.
0: tension-mod pitch glide is Membrane-only (gated), a no-op on the Bell body and physically wrong for rigid metal.
1.0 = enabled (default); the pad plays.
0.5 = center default; stereo placement of a bell-tree pad is a kit-layout choice (a real run sweeps across the field), left center here for a neutral baseline.

physics:
PHYSICAL OBJECT. A bell tree (a.k.a. orchestral "bell tree", not the bar/mark chime) is a vertical stack of 14-28 nested inverted small metal bowls/cup-bells graduated roughly by size, struck with a mallet in a downward glissando to give a bright shimmering metallic cascade (Wikipedia "Bell tree"). The constituent objects are small tuned bells/crotale-like discs: bronze/brass, "rather like a small tuned bell, only with a much brighter sound and a much longer resonance" (Wikipedia "Crotales"). Pitch is deliberately MICROTONAL / not a formal scale, so the run reads as an inharmonic shimmer rather than a melody.

FUNDAMENTAL / PITCH. Each bowl is small and high: small bells/crotales sit roughly 1-4 kHz for the principal partials, mid-to-high register. In Membrum the Bell body sets f0_nominal = 800 * 0.1^size Hz (bell_mapper.h:38). I voice size to the SMALL end so a single hit sits high and bright, representing one bowl near the top of the cascade.

PARTIAL / MODE STRUCTURE & INHARMONICITY. A church-bell-class object is tuned around 5 principal partials hum/prime/tierce/quint/nominal at ratios 0.250 : 0.500 : 0.600 : 0.750 : 1.000 (nominal=1), i.e. the minor-third tierce is the signature inharmonic interval (Hibberts, "The Five Main Partials of a Church Bell"; Perrin/Charnley/DePont 1983 JSV 90(1); ratios also given as 1:2:2.4:3:4 by modern founders, keltektrust.org.uk/sob04). Membrum's kBellRatios encodes exactly this (bell_modes.h:55) and extends upward (1.5, 2.0, 2.6, 3.2, 4.0...) for 16 modes. Real bell trees / crotale runs are explicitly INHARMONIC and microtonally detuned bowl-to-bowl, so I add modeStretch (away from the canonical ratios) and modeScatter (per-mode dither) to break the spectrum off the textbook ratios and emulate the "no formal scale" pile-up of overlapping detuned bells.

DECAY / T60 PER BAND. Crotales/small bells have a "much longer resonance" than ordinary small bells (Wikipedia "Crotales"); the metal rings for several seconds with the HIGH partials sustaining as long as or longer than the low ones (low b3 high-frequency damping = metallic). The Bell body already rings the longest of all Membrum bodies (bell_mapper.h:60). I push Decay high and set the per-mode b3 override to 0 (pure flat damping, no f^2 roll-off) so the upper partials keep ringing - the defining "shimmer". The decaySkew at 0.78 tilts energy toward the upper partials (the bright top of the cascade) while modestly shortening global decay (skewBias = 1-0.3*skew).

ATTACK / CONTACT CLICK. A mallet glissando across metal produces a dense train of short bright metallic contact transients (ticks). I model the strike with a bright click layer (high bandpass center) and, crucially, use the NoiseBurst exciter (violet-noise bandpass burst) rather than a single hard impulse, because the burst's filtered-noise contact spreads energy across many modes at once - the closest single-voice analogue to the "many bowls hit in quick succession" overlapping excitation. A Material Morph (0.85->0.55) sweeps the body brightness DOWN over the note, mimicking the cascade dimming as the beater leaves the bowls.

NOISE CONTENT. The shimmer has audible airy/sizzle content from many simultaneous metal contacts. I add a parallel noise overlay, violet-coloured (bright, +6 dB/oct) and high-cutoff, with a fairly long decay so the hiss tails with the ring.

PITCH GLIDE. None intrinsic - bowls are rigid metal, no membrane tension "kerthump". tensionModAmt is Membrane-only and stays off; pitch envelope stays disabled. The only time-variation is the Material Morph (timbral, not pitch).

MATERIAL. Hard metal (bronze/brass): material voiced very high (0.95) -> Bell brightness 0.7+0.3*0.95 = 0.985 and longest base decay (lerp(0.4,1.4,0.95) = 1.35 s before the Decay multiplier).

archetype: Bodhran (world) — Irish goatskin frame drum struck with a wooden tipper/beater
bodyModel: Membrane (Bessel circular drumhead, 48 modes) — the only physically-correct choice: a thin goatskin head stretched over a 35–45 cm frame radiating over an open back. airLoading is membrane-only and is the whole point of the deep bodhran tone.
exciter: Impulse (raised-cosine click) — the task brief specifies Impulse, and it is correct here: the bodhran is struck with a hard wooden double-ended tipper (ash/holly/hickory), giving a crisp, near-instantaneous contact transient. The softer Mallet exciter is reserved for the "soft frame-drum tap" variant; the tipper-struck bodhran wants the sharper Impulse plus a hot Click layer (the audible beater tick).

params.rationale: Hard wooden tipper = sharp raised-cosine contact, the click-forward voicing the brief asks for (vs Mallet for the soft tap).
A stretched goatskin head is a circular membrane; only Membrane runs the Bessel mapper and consumes airLoading + tensionMod.

params.physicalTarget: membrane brightness 0.45; baseDecay=lerp(0.15,0.8,0.45)·(1+0.1·0.72)=~0.31 s; mild woody high-mode damping
params.rationale: Goatskin on wood is woody-warm, slightly below neutral — dark enough for a deep tone but not a dead thud.

params.physicalTarget: f0=500·0.1^0.72=95.3 Hz (0,1 anchor); perceptual (1,1) fundamental 95.3·1.5933≈152 Hz; large-frame ring-up
params.rationale: Brief value; matches a 35–45 cm bodhran's deep, low membrane tone (decade map: 0.72 → ~95 Hz base).

params.physicalTarget: decayTime=base·exp(lerp(ln0.3,ln3,0.4))≈base·0.86 → ~0.27 s flat-damping term (overridden by b1 below; feeds legacy/cache)
params.rationale: Medium decay — taped/hand-damped bodhran rings briefly then is controlled, not a long open tom.

params.physicalTarget: r/a=0.4·0.9=0.36 — Bessel mode-shape sampling ~1/3 from center toward edge
params.rationale: A tipper hits between center and rim; 0.36 excites the (1,1)/(2,1) modes for a full body, not a pure central boom or thin edge slap.
Default per-pad loudness; a lead world-percussion voice sits up front, master/limiter own the ceiling (audit gain-staging).
No post-body tone-shaping needed; the membrane + air-loading already shape the spectrum. Keep the ToneShaper transparent.

params.rationale: A touch of brightness/sustain in the upper modes so the wooden-tipper tick and skin shimmer read through the deep body, without going thin.
No synthetic harmonic series wanted — the membrane + secondary shell supply all the tonal body.

params.rationale: Brief value, and the defining trait: a hard wooden tipper makes a pronounced beater contact — MORE click than the soft frame tap.
A wooden stick tick is very short and sharp — short contact = crisp click, not a soft thud.

params.rationale: Sets the bodhran's controlled medium RT60 directly (overrides Decay's flat term); tape/hand damping gives a warm, not infinite, decay.

params.physicalTarget: 78% interpolation of (m,1) modes from pure Bessel toward Rossing timpani series 1:1.5:2:2.44:2.9
params.rationale: Brief value; the open-frame + hand load deepens the membrane and pulls partials toward a clearer near-harmonic pitch — the hallmark deep bodhran 'bass note'.

params.physicalTarget: ~3% sinusoidal frequency dither on modal freqs

params.rationale: Brief asks for head/shell coupling — the goatskin head drives the wooden frame; 0.32 adds a felt wooden undertone without dominating.
Brief: 'head/shell coupling with secondary'. The wooden frame is the physical secondary resonator.
The frame body undertone sits below the head fundamental — a deep wooden 'thump' reinforcing the low end (Chromaphone ~0.6 shell rule).
Wood frame = woody/short shell decay (lower material = darker, faster flat decay) so the undertone supports the attack and gets out of the way.

params.physicalTarget: tensionAmtEff=0.3·3.3e-5; energy-driven upward pitch factor ≈ a few tenths of a semitone, relaxing as the body decays

params.citation: Avanzini–Marogna–Bank (energy-dependent tension); thesession.org/Ceolas bodhran pitch bend

physics: FUNDAMENTAL/PITCH: A bodhran is a 35–45 cm (typ.) goatskin frame drum, frame 9–20 cm deep, open-backed, hand pressed against the inside of the head to control pitch/timbre (Wikipedia; Roosebeck; Ceolas Bodhran tuning). Measured frame-drum heads driven at center show the first three axisymmetric (0,n) peaks at ~177 / 426 / 663 Hz on a small drum (Tronel/Errede NAH, UIUC PHYS406 2010); a larger, hand-loaded, air-coupled bodhran sits much lower — the perceived "deep, punchy bass note" lands roughly in the ~70–160 Hz region (general low-percussion F0 80–450 Hz; bodhran described as deep bass thud, Roosebeck/InstrumentHeritage). PARTIALS/INHARMONICITY: an ideal membrane has the strongly INHARMONIC Bessel ratios (1 : 1.59 : 2.14 : 2.30 : 2.65 …, j_mn/j_01; A&S Table 9.5). Real timpani-style air loading depresses the (m,1) modes toward the near-harmonic timpani series 1 : 1.5 : 2 : 2.44 : 2.9 (Rossing, "The Physics of Kettledrums", Sci. Am. 1982; wtt.pauken.org air-loading) — a bodhran over its open frame + the hand load behaves part-way toward this, giving a clearer sense of pitch than a bare membrane but not a fully tuned timpano. DECAY/T60: edge "overtones" are deliberately damped (tape on the rim, hand on the head) to a warm, fairly short, controllable ring — medium decay, low modes ~0.3–0.6 s, highs much shorter (Chaigne–Askenfelt b1+b3·f² loss). TRANSIENT/CLICK: pronounced — a hard lathe-turned wooden tipper striking taut goatskin produces a bright wooden contact tick (~2–3 kHz band) layered on the membrane tone; the brief specifically wants MORE click than the soft frame tap. NOISE: modest skin/air rustle, not snare-like; dark, low broadband. PITCH GLIDE: yes — the hand-on-head pressure technique and the high-tension goatskin give an audible downward "kerthump" pitch bend on accented strokes, analogous to tom tension modulation (Avanzini–Marogna–Bank, energy-dependent membrane tension; bodhran pitch-bend technique, thesession.org/Ceolas). MATERIAL: goatskin head (woody-membranous, NOT metallic) on a wooden (ash/birch ply) frame — Membrane body with mid-low material, a coupled wood-frame undertone via the secondary shell.

archetype: Bongo (world) — small high-pitched Afro-Cuban hand drum (macho/hembra), reusable high-perc filler
bodyModel: Membrane (Bessel circular drumhead, 48 modes) — the only physically-correct choice: a bongo is a small, thin calfskin/synthetic head stretched tight over a short wooden shell. Membrane is also the ONLY body that consumes airLoading and tensionModAmt, both of which matter for a real hand-drum tone.
exciter: Impulse (raised-cosine click) — a bongo is struck with bare fingertips/palm edge; the contact is a hard, very short, broadband click, not a soft mallet, a noise burst, or a bowed/feedback excitation. Impulse + a bright Click layer reproduces the sharp fingertip/slap attack. (Slap variants push Click brightness/mix up and strikePosition toward the edge.)

params.rationale: Bare-finger/edge strike = hard raised-cosine click, not mallet/noise/bow/feedback.
params.citation: Wikipedia: bongos 'played by striking the edge of the drumheads with the fingers and palms'.

params.rationale: Thin tensioned skin head = circular membrane; also the only body honoring airLoading + tension glide.
Woody/skin head biased slightly bright for the tight high macho tone (not metallic, not dull).

params.citation: Anchor handDrumsKit Bongo hi size=0.32 / lo size=0.40; macho ~8in vs hembra ~10in (Wikipedia).

params.physicalTarget: each (m,1) mode freq scaled 1+0.42*(RossingTarget-1); partial depression of lowest modes

params.physicalTarget: ~1.5% sinusoidal frequency dither on modal f_k

params.physicalTarget: b1 = 0.2 + 0.30*49.8 = 15.1 s^-1 flat damping (overrides Decay-derived flat term)

params.citation: Anchor Bongo hi bodyDampingB1=0.30; Chaigne & Askenfelt 1994 damping law.

params.physicalTarget: b3 = 0.10*1e-3 = 1e-4 s; moderate f^2 high-mode damping (woody, not metallic)

params.citation: Anchor Bongo hi bodyDampingB3=0.10; Aramaki/Kronland-Martinet wood vs metal damping.

params.rationale: Hard, very short fingertip contact -> tight click. Slap variant even shorter (~2.3 ms).

physics: PITCH / FUNDAMENTAL. Bongos are a tuned pair: macho (smaller, ~20 cm / 8 in head) tuned high — "B through D, about two octaves above middle C" (~B4–D5, ≈ 490–590 Hz nominal slap, with the sustained open-tone principal mode lower, ~390–500 Hz, G4–B4 per SoundCy), and hembra (larger, ~25 cm / 10 in) tuned roughly an octave below the macho (get-tuned.com; SoundCy; Wikipedia). The deliverable's targets (macho ~420→350 Hz, hembra ~340→280 Hz settle) sit squarely in this measured open-tone band and keep the pair the conventional ~ a 4th–5th apart (a "tight high membrane tone", not a deep conga). MODE STRUCTURE / INHARMONICITY. A struck circular membrane is strongly INHARMONIC: ideal mode ratios (Bessel zeros) are 1.000 : 1.594 : 2.136 : 2.296 : 2.653 : 2.918 : 3.156 … (Wikipedia, Vibrations of a circular membrane). These are NOT integer multiples, so a bare drumhead has indefinite pitch; a bongo gets its sense of pitch from being small + very tightly tensioned (raising f0 and crest of the principal (0,1)/(1,1) modes) and from air loading depressing the lowest modes toward a more focused tone. Membrum's MembraneMapper uses exactly these kMembraneRatios with f0 = 500·0.1^size, plus the Rossing air-loading correction — so a Membrane body already reproduces the correct inharmonic skeleton. DECAY / T60. Short — a small, tightly-tensioned, heavily hand-damped head rings only briefly; the open tone is a quick "tok", the slap is even shorter (the playing hand mutes it). Practical body T60 a few hundred ms (open) down to <100 ms (slap). No long sustain. TRANSIENT / CLICK. Sharp, bright fingertip/edge contact — a broadband click of ~1–3 ms; the slap stroke "makes a popping sound" (SoundCy). This is the most salient part of a bongo hit, so the Click layer must be present and fairly bright. NOISE. Very little — a tuned skin drum has almost no sustained hiss (unlike a snare); only a touch of contact noise on the attack. Keep the parallel noise layer low and dark. PITCH GLIDE. Real bongos show a small downward "kerthump": the membrane is momentarily over-tensioned by the strike energy and relaxes as it decays (Avanzini–Marogna–Bank 2012 tension modulation; tom/hand-drum tightening). Modest amount only (a small fraction of a semitone to ~1 semitone), reproduced by tensionModAmt (Membrane-only) plus a short pitch-env settle. MATERIAL. Thin calfskin or synthetic head on a wood shell (Wikipedia) — a woody/skin (not metallic) modal character → material around the middle, biased slightly bright for the high tight head; b3 damping kept woody. A specialty rub technique (bramido / "howl", finger dragged across the head) exists but is not the core voice.

Avanzini, Marogna, Bank, JASA 2012 — tension modulation / energy-dependent pitch glide
Chaigne & Askenfelt 1994 — R = b1 + b3*f^2 modal damping law

bodyModel: Membrane
exciter: NoiseBurst

params.rationale: Soft filtered-noise burst, not a hard click — matches the smeared, transient-free brush onset (patent RC-softened gate). Brief-specified.
A real snare is a circular membrane; we want the faint coated-head body color under the noise, with air-loading available (Membrane-only). Brief-specified.
Neutral-to-slightly-woody coated head; not metallic. Keeps the body subdued so the noise bed dominates.
Deep 14" snare body fundamental ~170-200 Hz; voiced just under so it warms rather than rings.
Coated heads are well damped; the body is a short warm thud under the long swept noise, not a sustained tom.
A brush sweeps across a broad area, not a point center strike; mid-radius spreads energy across modes for a diffuse head color.
Default per-pad loudness; brush snares sit quietly in a mix, master/Level trim downstream. Left at archetype default.
OVERRIDDEN to Highpass below — see note. Set to 0.34 for HP (idx=int(0.34*3)=1).
Brief specifies HP-filtered noise; the brush bed is mid/high energy with the low body rolled out of the ToneShaper path. (Authoritative value; the 1.0 row above is a stray — use 0.34.)
With Filter Type=HP, rolls the body/low-mid out of the ToneShaper sum so only the bright swept-noise presence passes — patent bands sit at 6.7k/17k; mixing 'head/swish' 6-10 kHz.
Gentle emphasis at the HP corner; broadband noise needs no sharp peak.
No filter-envelope motion wanted; the cyclic sweep is a slow amplitude effect, not a cutoff glide. Neutral default.
Brush noise is clean; no saturation flavour. Bypassed.
No west-coast harmonics on a naturalistic brush. Bypassed.
Unpitched swept noise has no pitch glide; the body does not boom-drop. Disabled (sentinel).
Keep the membrane physically tuned; no extra inharmonicity needed under a noise bed. Neutral.
Body is incidental; no per-mode decay rebalancing. Neutral.
A synthetic tonal harmonic series would fight the unpitched character. Off.
No amplitude-driven brightening; the brush dynamic comes from the noise envelope, not body nonlinearity. Off.
No per-note timbre morph; the sweep variation is amplitude/noise, not material. Off.
THE defining layer: the swept brush bed dominates over the faint body (patent: brush sound IS the noise). High mix so the sweep is the sound.
Noise-layer filter is LOWPASS; set high so the bright 6-10 kHz brush 'swish/head' band and air above pass. Combined with the ToneShaper HP this brackets a bright mid/high bed.
Near-flat; broadband hiss, no resonant whistle.
Long, SUSTAINED sweep — a brush whisk lasts hundreds of ms, not an impulse; the long noise envelope IS the swept friction sound.
Patent bands the noise at 6.7k/17k (mid/high emphasis); violet tilt + high LP gives the bright airy brush character vs a dull brown hiss.
DEFINING: the body crack is deliberately absent — a brush drag has a soft transient-free onset (patent RC-softens the gate). Brief: click 0.
Deepens the membrane and removes whistly highs, giving a realistic 'less whistly' coated-head body under the noise (Membrane-only). Archetype default kept.
A touch of natural detune imperfection on the body so it reads as a real (not synthetic) head; tiny so it stays subtle.

params.physicalTarget: per-pad weight 0.6 in the global sympathetic-resonance engine
params.rationale: Brief-specified strong coupling: feeds snare-buzz/sympathetic resonance so the swept head excites the kit network (only audible when the kit-level Global Coupling/Snare Buzz > 0).
Brief-specified long burst: the longest practical NoiseBurst gives a soft, smeared, non-cracky onset that blends into the swept bed instead of a sharp contact.

params.physicalTarget: would bias bow pressure +0.30 — but NO-OP under NoiseBurst exciter
params.rationale: Brief-specified 0.60, but Friction Pressure only affects the FRICTION exciter; with NoiseBurst selected it is inert. The swept-friction character is instead carried by the long-decay HP noise layer. Documented as a deliberate no-op per the brief.
Snare sits center in a kit image. Default.

Filter Env Amount=0, so the filter ADSR is inaudible — no cutoff sweep on broadband noise.
Morph Enabled=off; no per-note material morph for a static-timbre brush bed.
Let the Decay knob derive flat damping; the body is a short incidental warmth, no special RT60 target.
Let Material derive HF damping; coated-head material color is set via Material, not an explicit b3.

defaultedParams.param: Coupling Strength / Secondary Enabled / Secondary Size / Secondary Material

Membrane tension glide off; brushes produce no energy-dependent pitch jump.

defaultedParams.param: Choke Group / Output Bus / Pad Enabled
defaultedParams.why: Routing: no mute group, main bus, pad enabled — kit-level concerns, not part of the sound design.

physics:
FUNDAMENTAL / PITCH: A struck 14" snare body sits at f0 ~170-220 Hz (coated head; idrumtune/Madsen Physics-406 measured center-strike fundamental 211-213 Hz snares-off, +5 Hz snares-on). BUT this archetype is the BRUSH SWEEP, which is acoustically UNPITCHED: US Patent 4181059A ("Circuit for simulating sound of wire brush rotated around head of snare drum") states the sound "originates from random contact of the wires of the brush with the head" and is "basically unpitched." So pitch is a weak, secondary cue — a faint coated-head body color (~180-200 Hz) sits UNDER a dominant broadband swept-noise bed. We voice the body deep and subdued (membrane f0 ~150-180 Hz) so it merely warms the noise rather than producing a tom-like pitched ring.

PARTIAL/MODE STRUCTURE & INHARMONICITY: The membrane contributes ideal circular-membrane Bessel modes (1 : 1.59 : 2.14 : 2.30 : 2.65 ...), strongly inharmonic. Air-loading (Rossing 1982) depresses the lowest (m,1) modes toward the timpani-like 1:1.5:2:2.44:2.9 series and removes the "whistly" high partials — appropriate for a deep coated head. The swept-noise bed itself has NO discrete partial structure (continuous spectrum).

NOISE CONTENT & SPECTRAL SHAPE (the dominant component): Patent 4181059A derives the brush sound from a white-noise source split into two bandpass tank circuits centered ~6.7 kHz and ~17 kHz, "combined in different amplitude proportions," i.e. a MID/HIGH-emphasized, high-pass-flavored broadband hiss. Mixing literature corroborates: the snare "head/swish" sound lives 6-10 kHz and brush airiness extends >8 kHz (idrumtune, MusicGuyMixing). So the noise should be bright (violet/white tilt) and HIGH-PASS filtered (energy concentrated above ~1.5-2 kHz, peak presence 4-10 kHz). This maps to: bright noise color + high noise cutoff, with the parallel noise LAYER carrying the sweep (not a discrete exciter burst).

DECAY TIMES (T60): A brush sweep is SUSTAINED, not impulsive — a continuous whisk lasting hundreds of ms to seconds while the brush travels across the head, with a slow cyclic loudness variation (one cycle/360° rotation, a half-cycle/180°; patent's divide-by-4 / divide-by-8 envelope shapers). The faint body ring is short (coated heads are damped, body T60 ~0.1-0.3 s). So: LONG noise-layer decay (~0.5-1 s) over a SHORT, subdued body decay.

TRANSIENT / CONTACT CLICK: CRITICAL — there is essentially NO sharp attack/crack. The patent deliberately RC-smooths the gate to "soften the turn on and turnoff and minimize transients." A brush dragged across the head has a soft, smeared onset, the opposite of a stick crack. Therefore the Click layer must be ZERO and the exciter onset must be soft (a LONG NoiseBurst, not a hard impulse). This is the defining contrast vs a normal snare: body crack deliberately absent.

PITCH GLIDE: None. No tension "kerthump" — the swept noise has no pitch envelope; the soft membrane underneath does not glide.

MATERIAL: Coated mylar batter head over a metal/wood shell, swept by a wire/nylon brush. Perceptually a soft, woody-to-neutral membrane (material mid), deep (air-loaded), with a bright broadband noise bed on top.

bodyModel: Membrane (Bessel circular drumhead, 48 modes) — base f0 = 500·0.1^size Hz, air-loading active, tension-mod available
exciter: NoiseBurst (violet-noise bandpass burst → bandpass; ~5 ms contact, the soft "brush tap" contact instead of a hard stick impulse)

params.rationale: A brush tap is a soft, slightly diffuse filtered-noise contact, not a hard impulse/mallet — NoiseBurst is the violet-noise bandpass burst that drives the membrane like a brush hit.
params.citation: Andertons brush guide (multiple micro-hits + friction); Nord Modular percussion ch.5 (noise-burst snare excitation)

params.rationale: A snare head is a circular tensioned membrane; only Membrane gives Bessel inharmonic modes, air-loading and tension-mod, which are membrane-only.

params.rationale: Coated head on a wood shell = woody, not metallic; slightly darker than mid so the body sits under the wires.

params.rationale: Wood shell: medium damping, short enough that the housing supports the hit without outlasting it.

defaultedParams.why: Wavefolding is a west-coast synthetic timbre; a brush snare wants natural saturation (Drive) at most, not aggressive folded harmonics.
A single short Start→End thump is sufficient; a three-point glide adds no physical 'kerthump' detail for a brush tap.
Injecting a synthetic 1/k integer-harmonic series would HARMONICIZE an intentionally inharmonic membrane — wrong for a drumhead; the body's own Bessel modes carry the tone.
The tapped hit is a single static timbre (unlike the swept brush, which morphs material over the sweep); no per-note material sweep needed.
A snare is not part of an open/closed mute pair; it rings out freely.
Snare goes to the main bus so master gain/limiter apply; no aux send required.

No-op — only the Feedback exciter reads it.

physics:
FUNDAMENTAL / PITCH: A 14" snare batter head tuned for a jazz/brush context sits with its perceived "body/pulse" in the 200–400 Hz region (Recording Magazine / Sonicbids snare-EQ guides: body=200–400 Hz, smack/crack=900 Hz–2 kHz, snare-wire buzz=3–5 kHz, head-contact=6–10 kHz). The recipe's pitch-env target of ~210→150 Hz places the audible head fundamental at the low end of that body band, giving "housing weight" without becoming a tom.

PARTIAL / MODE STRUCTURE & INHARMONICITY: A struck circular membrane is strongly INHARMONIC — modes are NOT integer multiples. Ideal (tension-only) circular-membrane ratios relative to the (0,1) fundamental are (0,1)=1.000, (1,1)=1.593, (2,1)=2.136, (0,2)=2.296, (3,1)=2.653, (1,2)=2.918, (0,3)=3.598 (Penn State / D. Russell membrane demo; UBC PHYS341). The first overtone at 1.59× (not 2×) is the signature non-harmonicity. Real drums depart further: air loading and shell coupling pull the low (m,1) modes toward the Rossing timpani-like 1 : 1.5 : 2 : 2.4 : 2.9 series and add shell/housing modes in the 250–600 Hz region. Membrum's Membrane body already carries the A&S Bessel ratios; airLoading morphs the low modes onto the Rossing curve; modeScatter + a coupled secondary (shell) bank supply the housing inharmonicity.

ATTACK / CONTACT CLICK: A brush TAP is fundamentally softer than a stick. Brushes produce "softer, more nuanced sounds … a warm and understated tone" with a reduced HF transient versus a stick (Andertons; DrumRadar; Yamaha Hub). The acoustic brush sound is "multiple simultaneous micro-hits by the individual strands plus friction sweep" (Andertons) — so even the tapped (non-swept) hit is a short, slightly diffuse, lower-brightness contact rather than a sharp crack. This maps to a moderate-level, mid-bright click (~0.62 mix, ~2.4 ms, center ~2.4 kHz — in the snare "smack/crack" band but rolled off relative to a stick's 6–10 kHz HF tick), not the near-unity bright crack a studio stick snare uses.

NOISE CONTENT & SPECTRAL SHAPE: The snare WIRES against the bottom head couple nonlinearly and generate broadband, inharmonic energy concentrated around 3–5 kHz (Recording Magazine; Sonicbids). Classic synthesis (Nord Modular percussion ch.5 / TR-909 patch) models the wires as a white/violet noise source through a low/high-pass filter chain with its own envelope, mixed with the membrane-mode oscillators; velocity scales the noise-to-tone ratio. For a brush tap the wire noise is GENTLER than a stick snare (softer excitation = less wire excursion), so a moderate noise mix (~0.65 vs a stick snare's ~0.82) with a fairly bright but not harsh cutoff (~3.3 kHz, violet/white tilt) and a SHORT decay (the "snapped sound that dies quickly on contact" — DrumRadar).

DECAY / T60 PER BAND: Snare body/ring is short-to-medium, ~0.3–1.5 s for a crisp pop/jazz snare (Sonicbids). The wire buzz decays faster than the body for a tight articulation; the (1,1) mode rings longer than the more-efficiently-radiating (0,1) (Penn State / UBC). Recipe: body decay ~0.58 (medium tail, woody) with the wire-noise layer decay short (~0.28 → roughly 110–130 ms) so the "fffft" snaps and the membrane carries the tone.

PITCH GLIDE ("kerthump"): A struck membrane stiffens under transient energy, giving a small energy-dependent UPward pitch transient that relaxes as it decays (Avanzini–Marogna–Bank 2012, JASA; tension is ~linear in membrane energy). On top of that, a small programmed downward pitch-env (~210→150 Hz over ~60 ms) supplies the housing "thump" that separates a snare from a hat. Both are modest for a brush (gentle attack), so tensionModAmt ~0.25 and a shallow pitch-env.

MATERIAL: Coated batter head + wood shell (jazz brush context). Woody, not metallic → low material, moderate b3 frequency-squared damping, b1 flat damping for a controlled medium decay.

archetype: Cajon (world) — box-drum tapa plate; two voices: BASS (center strike) + SLAP (top-corner strike)
bodyModel: Plate (free-plate Chladni, post-audit). The radiating element of a cajon is the thin plywood front board (tapa, 2.5–3 mm) clamped on its edges and free to flex/radiate — a struck PLATE, not a circular membrane. The corrected Plate body (free-plate (m+2n)^1.7 Chladni law, fundamental (2,0), kappa=0.11 splitting) gives the slightly inharmonic, woody-box partial structure of a tapa; airLoading (membrane-only) and the Bessel membrane skeleton would be physically wrong here.
exciter: Impulse (raised-cosine click). A bare hand/palm strike on a stiff plywood plate is a short broadband contact impulse — the Impulse exciter is the right transient backend for both the palm BASS hit (center) and the fingertip SLAP (corner). The audible attack character is then dialed with the always-on Click layer (per-strike) rather than a softer Mallet/NoiseBurst exciter, matching the hard, snappy plywood contact.

params.rationale: Palm strike on plywood = short broadband contact impulse driving the plate.
The tapa is a thin clamped plywood plate that radiates; free-plate Chladni body is the correct resonator (not Membrane).

params.physicalTarget: woody plate: Plate brightness=0.5+0.5*0.3=0.65, decay base=lerp(0.4,2.0,0.3)=0.88 s; b3 high-freq damping moderate
params.rationale: Plywood is woody, not metallic — keep highs damped so the bass tone is round and boxy, not ringy.
Anchors the modal bank in the measured cajon bass band (80–140 Hz, (0,0)/Helmholtz cluster ~63–131 Hz) so partials land in the boom/boxy region.
Cajon bass tone decays fast (body damping from the player sitting on it); short-to-medium ring.

physics: FUNDAMENTAL/PITCH. The cajon's two voices have distinct pitch behavior. (a) BASS tone (palm, center of tapa): low-frequency spectrum has 3 peaks from ~60–400 Hz — a Helmholtz cavity resonance (first peak measured to rise from ~63 Hz to ~94 Hz as the rear sound-hole diameter increases) plus the tapa's fundamental plate mode (0,0) measured at 131.5 Hz, and higher plate modes (2,0),(1,2). Recording-engineer practice treats the cajon 'kick' fundamental as 80–140 Hz — notably HIGHER than a real kick (50–80 Hz). I voice the perceived boom at ~85 Hz with a short downward glide from ~165 Hz (plate stiffening under impact → relaxation, the audible 'thump'). (b) SLAP (fingertips, top corner/edge): no defined low fundamental — energy is in high plate modes plus snare-wire/buzz, a bright snap. PARTIAL STRUCTURE & INHARMONICITY. The tapa is a thin (2.5–3 mm) plywood plate clamped on its edges and free to flex/radiate — a struck PLATE. Free-plate partials follow Chladni's (m+2n)^P law (Rossing & Peterson cymbal exponent P≈1.7 in the corrected Plate body), i.e. inharmonic, denser than a harmonic series; a stiff plywood plate sits slightly sharp of the pure law → modest positive Mode Stretch. Center strike weights the low axisymmetric modes (max bass); corner strike weights high (m,n) modes (snap). DECAY/T60. Percussion attack–decay envelope with minimal sustain; bass tones decay FAST and faster than the mid 'low' tone (UBC modal study). A seated player heavily damps the box (decay much longer when not sitting on it) → I keep both voices short (bass ~0.7 s, slap shorter, high modes b3-damped). TRANSIENT/CLICK. Hard contact of hand/finger on plywood = sharp broadband click; engineers boost ~5–10 kHz (typ. +8–10 dB @ ~8 kHz) for slap snap → Click Brightness ~7.5 kHz on the slap, ~1.1 kHz dull thud on the bass. NOISE CONTENT. Snare-cajon corner slap adds bright snare-wire sizzle (5–10 kHz, +tilt) → Violet-noise parallel layer on the slap only; clean center bass has no hiss. PITCH GLIDE. Short impact-stiffening downward glide on the bass (~165→85 Hz, ~35 ms), exp-fast initial drop; none on the slap. MATERIAL. Plywood = woody (not metallic): low Material on bass for round/boxy tone, higher on slap for the bright corner snap. CAVITY. The box/back-board behind the tapa is modeled with the per-pad Secondary shell bank (coupling+secondary, shell f0 ≈0.59× tapa ≈51 Hz) supplying the Helmholtz-region sub weight.

archetype: Cajon Snare-Side (bright edge/slap tone of the tapa with internal snare-wire buzz)
bodyModel: Plate (Kirchhoff/free-plate Chladni square-plate modal bank — the thin plywood tapa front panel; inharmonic, woody-metallic partials rather than a tuned circular membrane)
exciter: NoiseBurst (violet-noise bandpass contact burst — the slap of the hand against the thin edge of the tapa, a short broadband contact rather than a pure beater impulse)

params.rationale: The snare-side hit is a hand-slap contact against a thin plate, not a hard beater — a short violet-noise bandpass burst is the correct contact model (spec-mandated).
params.citation: Param dictionary enum 2=NoiseBurst; SoundOnSound snare synthesis uses contoured noise for the snare mechanism.

params.rationale: The tapa is a thin rectangular plywood plate; its free-plate Chladni modal bank gives the inharmonic woody-metallic edge tone, not a tuned circular membrane (spec-mandated; corrected post-audit Plate = free-plate (m+2n)^P Chladni).
params.citation: Kopf Percussion tapa = thin plywood plate; AUDIT §3-B corrected Plate Chladni model.

params.physicalTarget: Plate brightness=0.5+0.5*0.5=0.75; decay base lerp(0.4,2.0,0.5)=1.2 s
params.rationale: Plywood sits between woody and metallic — mid material gives a bright-but-not-ringing plate; brighter character is then carried by the noise/click wire layers, not the plate itself.
params.citation: Kopf: thin birch/maple plywood, bright but damped.

params.rationale: Targets the bright edge/SLAP tone (low-kHz plate energy), deliberately above the cajon's ~80-130 Hz bass/Helmholtz tone — the snare side is the high zone; the low box thump is added separately by the pitch env.
params.citation: UBC/PHYS406: above ~400 Hz the slap/low tone overtakes the bass tone; bass Helmholtz ~82-131 Hz is the OTHER zone.

params.physicalTarget: decayTime = base*exp(lerp(ln0.3,ln3,0.34))≈base*0.46 (short); overridden by b1=0.55 below for the flat floor
params.rationale: Body weight on the cajon heavily damps the tapa — the plate tone is a short 'tok', so a sub-unity decay multiplier; b1 override sets the actual flat RT60 floor.
params.citation: UBC wiki: with player seated, decay is much shorter (body damping).

params.rationale: Snare-side hits land near the top EDGE of the tapa (where the wires sit), exciting higher plate modes and giving the brighter, more inharmonic edge balance vs a centred bass strike.
params.citation: Kopf: edge strikes produce the higher snare tones; center produces bass.

params.rationale: A snare-side hit is a loud accent in a kit; slightly above the 0.8 default for presence, still under the -1 dBTP bus limiter (post-audit linear Level).
params.citation: AUDIT H-4/N-1: Level is a true linear attenuation under the bus limiter.

params.rationale: Engages the ToneShaper to gently tame the very top of the violet noise/click so the buzz reads as 'wires' not 'white-noise hiss', while keeping the slap crisp; below 20 kHz so the SVF is active.
params.citation: SoundOnSound: snare noise is band-LIMITED (LP+HP) not full broadband.

params.rationale: A gentle low-pass is the right top-end control for the bright wire/slap mix.

Stretches the plate partials further apart for the metallic, slightly bell-edged character of a struck thin plate / wires (spec-mandated 0.42; post-audit 1-indexed stretch with widened B reaches an audibly metallic spectrum).
params.citation: Spec; AUDIT stretch index + B_max fix; plate modes are inharmonic (Rossing & Peterson).

params.physicalTarget: phys = 0.4*2-1 = -0.20; global skewBias=1-0.3*(-0.2)=1.06; per-mode tilt ratio^(+0.2) favouring HIGH modes
params.rationale: Negative skew tilts decay energy toward the HIGH modes so the bright edge/buzz partials ring slightly longer than the fundamental — the snare side stays 'fizzy' as it decays (spec-mandated 0.40; post-audit per-mode tilt now applies on Plate).
params.citation: Spec; AUDIT M-5 decaySkew per-mode tilt added to Plate.

params.rationale: The snare-wire buzz is the DOMINANT perceptual layer of the snare side — pushed hot (spec-mandated 0.75) so the wires, not the plate, define the sound.
params.citation: Spec; Kopf: snare wires/strings add the buzzing that defines the snare side.

params.rationale: Puts the noise-layer energy through the ~3-6 kHz snare-buzz band so it reads as metallic wires rattling, not broadband hiss.
params.citation: Drum-kit snare wires occupy ~3-5 kHz (SoundOnSound / Sonicbids EQ guidance).

params.rationale: Slight emphasis only — the wire buzz is broadband, not a pitched peak (and the layer LP would beep if resonance were high).
params.citation: AUDIT L-6: forced-LP noise path, keep resonance low.

params.rationale: The wires buzz a touch longer than the plate 'tok' — a short fizzy tail, not the multi-second ring of a kit snare (heavily damped, hand-played).
params.citation: Cajon decays are short (body-damped, UBC wiki); wires ring slightly past the slap.

params.rationale: Metallic wire rattle and thin-plywood slap are bright, treble-tilted — violet noise matches the rising buzz spectrum.
params.citation: Snare buzz energy is high-frequency (3-5 kHz band).

params.physicalTarget: ClickLayer mix=0.72; half-amplitude into exciter feed + full into output *0.445
params.rationale: The hand-on-thin-plywood slap is a sharp transient crack that defines the attack — pushed hot (spec-mandated 0.72) and also drives the plate modes.
params.citation: Spec; cajon waveform = short sharp attack (UBC: short attack, slap excitation).

params.rationale: A thin tapa gives a very fast contact tick — short contact time for a sharp slap.
The slap crack is an upper-mid/treble tick — centres the click bandpass in the ~5 kHz region to sit with the wire buzz.
params.citation: Snare/slap crack lives in the bright 4-5 kHz band (SoundOnSound).

params.rationale: Short violet-noise contact burst — a hand slap is a brief broadband contact, longer than a hard mallet click but still short.
params.citation: Param dictionary NoiseBurst contact 2-15 ms; analog snare A≈0 (SoundOnSound).

params.rationale: Start of the short box/housing 'thump' behind the bright slap (spec-mandated 210 Hz).
params.citation: Spec; cajon box thump in low-hundreds Hz (UBC bass-tone region).

params.rationale: Settle frequency of the box thump (spec-mandated 140 Hz) — a short downward kerthump as the plate+air system relaxes.

params.physicalTarget: ms = 0.14*500 = 70 ms glide (enables the pitch env)
params.rationale: Fast settle — the box thump resolves quickly; this is the master enable for the 210->140 Hz glide.
params.citation: Cajon attack/settle is short; AUDIT H-3 pitch env now drives Plate.

params.rationale: Energy-driven box thump drops fast then settles — classic exponential pitch decay shape.
params.citation: Standard 808-style exp pitch drop; preset-gen default 0.15.

params.physicalTarget: drive=1+6*vel*0.22*env; amplitude-driven recipSqrt brightening of the plate
params.rationale: Real plate-against-wire rattle is strongly non-linear — louder hits get brighter/buzzier; a modest amount adds velocity-dependent edge (post-audit env-level waveshaping, sustained not transient-only).
params.citation: AUDIT M-3/M-4 redesign: amount-driven sustained brightening; snare/plate-wire coupling is non-linear (Ducceschi/Bilbao).

params.physicalTarget: amount=0.12 -> 8 harmonics at plate f0, 1/k amplitudes, summed pre-ToneShaper
params.rationale: A small synthetic harmonic series adds a touch of pitched 'body' weight under the bright inharmonic plate so the slap has a definable centre, without making it sound like a tom.
params.citation: AUDIT mode_inject 1/k (-6 dB/oct) fix; analog snare keeps a small tonal core (SoundOnSound).

params.physicalTarget: b1 = 0.2+0.55*49.8 ≈ 27.6 s^-1 flat damping (short RT60 floor)
params.rationale: Sets a tight flat-damping floor (overrides the Decay knob's derivation) so the plate tone is short and slappy — a body-damped, hand-played cajon, not a ringing gong.
params.citation: AUDIT damping law; UBC: seated player = short decay.

params.physicalTarget: b3 = 0.18e-3 s (light f^2 damping -> highs ring fairly long = metallic-bright)
params.rationale: Low b3 keeps the high plate modes alive for the bright metallic edge of the thin tapa (woodier would over-damp the buzz).
params.citation: AUDIT b3 = perceived material (metal<->wood); thin plywood is bright.

params.physicalTarget: ~4.5% sinusoidal frequency dither on plate modes
params.rationale: Real plywood is inhomogeneous and the wires detune the plate — a little scatter breaks up any single-pitch ring into an organic, buzzy spread.
params.citation: AUDIT mode scatter = natural-imperfection dither; real plates are not perfectly periodic.

params.physicalTarget: per-pad weight 0.62 in the global cross-pad sympathetic engine
params.rationale: The internal snare wires couple sympathetically to other hits in the kit (the cajon buzzes when nearby drums sound) — high participation (spec-mandated 0.62), audible when kit-level Snare Buzz/Global Coupling > 0.
params.citation: Spec; snare wires sympathetically rattle (Kopf, drum snare physics).

params.physicalTarget: feedforward drive 0.62: secondary shell excitation = bodyOut*0.62, mixed passively
params.rationale: The box/shell housing behind the tapa is strongly excited by the plate (spec calls for coupling 0.62 WITH secondary) — drives the secondary shell bank for the wooden-box resonance.
params.citation: Spec ('coupling 0.62 with secondary'); cajon box is a coupled Helmholtz/shell cavity (UBC).

params.physicalTarget: >=0.5 -> 24-mode secondary shell bank spun up at noteOn
params.rationale: Enables the wooden-box housing resonance behind the front plate (spec: 'with secondary').
params.citation: Spec; cajon = resonant plywood box behind the tapa.

params.rationale: The box/shell housing is larger and lower than the tapa edge tone — puts the wooden-box resonance in the low-hundreds-Hz region under the bright slap.
params.citation: Chromaphone ~0.6 shell rule; cajon box modes/bass region are sub-tapa (UBC 405 Hz box mode, 82-131 Hz Helmholtz).

params.rationale: Plywood box is woody and well-damped — moderate-low material gives a short, woody housing resonance rather than a long metallic ring.
No emphasis needed on the gentle top-end LP; a flat 9 kHz roll-off keeps the buzz natural.
params.citation: Default Butterworth; no narrow peak in a broadband buzz.

params.physicalTarget: theta=pi/4 -> gainL=gainR=1.0 (center, bit-identical to mono dup)
params.rationale: A cajon is a single front-and-center instrument; leave centered (the kit-level mix, not this voice, places it).

defaultedParams.why: No filter sweep on a snare-side slap; the static 9 kHz LP plus the noise envelope already shape the top (no-op at 0.5).
Filter envelope is inaudible while Filter Env Amount=0.5; left at defaults.
Drive is flavour-saturation; the slap/buzz is already bright and the NonlinearCoupling supplies the non-linear edge — extra harmonics would muddy it (exact bypass at 0).
West-coast wavefolding is the wrong character for an acoustic plate/wire buzz (exact bypass at 0).
No intra-hit material sweep — the snare-side timbre is static within a hit; morph off keeps the Phase-1 path (params are no-ops while disabled).
A single short 210->140 Hz segment models the box thump; the 3-point knee is unnecessary (no-op while knee off).
Air-loading frequency correction is Membrane-only; on a Plate body it is a no-op, so left at the registered default.
Energy-dependent tension glide is gated to the Membrane body only; a no-op on this Plate voice — the box thump is handled by the pitch env instead.

defaultedParams.param: FM Ratio (0.133) / Feedback Amount (0) / Friction Pressure (0)
defaultedParams.why: Secondary exciter params for FMImpulse/Feedback/Friction; no-ops while NoiseBurst is the selected exciter.
The snare side is not a mutually-exclusive open/closed pair; no choke grouping needed.
Routed to the main bus; no per-pad multi-out send required for this voice.

defaultedParams.param: Tightness / Brightness / Body Size / Punch / Complexity macros (0.5)
defaultedParams.why: Macros are neutral one-knob overlays; the recipe sets the underlying params directly, so all macros stay at 0.5 (zero delta) to preserve those values.
The pad is an active kit voice; left enabled.

physics: CAJON SNARE-SIDE ACOUSTIC PROFILE (researched). A cajon is a six-sided plywood box; the player strikes the thin front panel (the "tapa", ~3 mm birch/maple plywood) which is the radiating plate, with a sound hole in the rear forming a neckless Helmholtz resonator. Two playing zones: (a) CENTER strike = the deep "bass" tone, dominated by the Helmholtz/cavity resonance (measured fundamentals reported ~82 Hz, ~131.5 Hz for an optimised port, with the lowest box room mode (0,0,1) near 405 Hz; bass cajons target <200-250 Hz); (b) EDGE/top strike = the bright "slap" or "snare" tone, the subject of THIS archetype. The snare-SIDE sound is produced by internal snare wires or guitar strings tensioned against the inside of the tapa near the top edge: when the thin plate is struck they rattle/buzz against it, adding a sustained metallic broadband layer. PARTIAL/MODE STRUCTURE: the tapa is a thin rectangular plate, so its in-vacuo modes follow a 2-D Kirchhoff plate dispersion (inharmonic, mode density rising with frequency), NOT a harmonic series — the edge/slap tone therefore reads as a short, bright, slightly-pitched "tok" sitting on top of broadband content, with most identifiable plate energy in the few-hundred-Hz to low-kHz band (above ~400 Hz the "low tone" overtakes the bass tone perceptually). The plate is heavily damped by the player's body weight on the box, so decays are SHORT. SNARE-WIRE BUZZ: the buzz is the perceptual signature — distributed, strongly non-linear wire-against-plate rattle, spectrally broadband with its characteristic energy in the ~3-5 kHz "buzz" band (the same EQ region cited for drum-kit snare wires), riding a little longer than the plate tone itself (a fast "fffft" tail, ~120-200 ms, not the multi-second ring of an un-damped snare). TRANSIENT/CLICK: a sharp contact crack from hand-on-thin-plywood — a few-ms broadband tick centred in the upper-mid/treble (~4-5 kHz). PITCH GLIDE: the box/shell housing behind the tapa contributes a brief low "thump" with a fast downward pitch settle as the excited plate+air system relaxes — modelled here as a short 210->140 Hz drop over ~70 ms (the "kerthump" of the box, not a long 808 glide). NOISE CONTENT: bright, violet/white-tilted (rising spectrum) to match the metallic wire rattle and the thin-plywood slap; this is the dominant audible layer for the snare side. ESTABLISHED SYNTHESIS: classic analog snare synthesis (TR-808/909, SH-101 patches) builds a snare as a small set of tonal shell/membrane modes (~180 Hz & ~330 Hz region) plus a separately-enveloped band-passed noise generator for the wires (LP+HP into a narrow bright band), A=0, S=0, short D — which maps cleanly onto Membrum's "modal body + parallel noise + click" topology. DRIVING PARAM CHOICES: bright plate body f0 in the ~430 Hz region (snare/edge tone, not the bass tone); modeStretch>1 to push the plate partials further inharmonic (metallic edge); a hot, bright, fast noise layer (the wires); a hot bright click (the slap crack); per-pad coupling + secondary shell for the box housing; and a short 210->140 Hz pitch-env thump.

bodyModel: CYCLED per pad across all six bodies — Plate / Shell / String / Bell / Membrane / NoiseBody (no single body; the chaos IS the per-voice rotation). Default/seed body = Plate (free-plate Chladni, the most inharmonic/metallic and the natural home for the von-Karman cymbal-gong nonlinearity this archetype emulates).
exciter: CYCLED per pad across the three "active/energetic" exciters — Feedback / FMImpulse / Friction (i%3). Seed exciter = Feedback (energy-limited self-oscillating body↔exciter loop — the squeal/chaos engine).

params.physicalTarget: CYCLE per pad i%3 → Feedback(5)/FMImpulse(4)/Friction(3); norm shown 1.0=Feedback (round(norm·5)=5). Each pad differs.
params.rationale: The three energetic/self-oscillating exciters ARE the chaos engine; cycling them guarantees no two voices share an attack mechanism.
params.citation: loopmasters Self-Oscillation & FM; unison.audio FM 'complete sonic chaos'

params.physicalTarget: CYCLE i%6 → Plate/Shell/String/Bell/Membrane/NoiseBody; norm shown 0.2=Plate (round(0.2·5)=1). Each pad differs.
params.rationale: Cycling all six bodies maximizes timbral spread; Plate (free-plate Chladni) seeds the most inharmonic/metallic spectrum and is the home of the von-Karman cymbal nonlinearity.

params.physicalTarget: ~0.45 (cycled 0.20–0.80 across pads). Per-body brightness+decay base; ~mid = balanced metallic edge with body.
params.rationale: Mid-range cycled so each chaos voice sits at a different spectral tilt rather than all-bright or all-dull.

params.physicalTarget: f0=base·0.1^size; ~0.45 → mid pitch (cycled 0.25–0.90). Body-specific base (Plate 800 Hz).
params.rationale: Cycled mid sizes spread fundamentals across the kit; avoids every voice at one pitch.

params.physicalTarget: 0.4–0.9 cycled → 0.3×..3× body base decay; ~0.5 ≈ ~1× (medium-long ring so chaos evolves).
params.rationale: Longer rings let the nonlinear coupling / scatter / morph evolve audibly within each hit.

params.physicalTarget: 0.3 → r/a≈0.27 (Membrane) / mode-shape sampling elsewhere / pick pos (String). Default.
params.rationale: Default is fine; body+exciter cycling already supplies variation, no need to fight the mode-shape.
Slightly below default 0.8 — chaos voices are hot (drive+fold+coupling) so trim headroom before the −1 dBTP bus limiter.
params.citation: AUDIT gain-staging appendix (−6 dBFS body budget + bus limiter)

params.physicalTarget: CYCLE i%3 → LP(0.0)/HP(0.5)/BP(1.0); norm shown 0.0=LP. Each pad differs.
params.rationale: Per-voice filter cycling spreads the 14 voices across complementary spectral windows so they don't pile into one band.

params.physicalTarget: hz=20·1000^cutoff; 0.55 → ~340 Hz (cycled 0.30–0.90 → ~80 Hz..6 kHz). Engaged (≠ bypass 1.0).
params.rationale: Deliberately ENGAGED (not the 1.0 bypass) so the cycled filter type actually shapes each voice; cycled cutoff = spectral spread.
High resonance adds whistling emphasis/peaks at the swept cutoff — part of the aggressive, on-the-edge character.

params.physicalTarget: amount=2·norm−1=+0.10 → 2^(3·0.10·env) sweep (~+0.3 oct up). Slight upward sweep.
params.rationale: Small bipolar amount makes the resonant peak move per hit, adding the evolving/jittery quality.
Fast attack so the sweep starts at the transient where it bites.
Short-medium decay sweeps the filter back during the hit body — evolving timbre.

params.physicalTarget: internalDrive=1+0.45·9≈5.1× recipSqrt odd-harmonic sat (cycled 0.30–0.66). Timbre, not level (M-2 makeup).
params.rationale: Strong odd-harmonic saturation = aggressive grit; post-audit makeup means it colours without pinning the rail.
params.citation: AUDIT M-2 Drive makeup (flavour not level)

params.rationale: West-coast wavefolding injects dense high harmonics — the harsh/noisy/jittery synthetic-fx signature.

params.physicalTarget: hz=20·100^norm; cycled toLogNorm(180..530 Hz) → start ~180–530 Hz.
params.rationale: Per-hit downward pitch glide ('zap') on every body (post-audit pitch-env works on all 6 bodies, H-3).
params.citation: AUDIT H-3 pitch env all bodies

params.physicalTarget: hz=20·100^norm; cycled toLogNorm(40..240 Hz) → end ~40–240 Hz.
params.rationale: Settles low — the aggressive pitch-drop transient under the chaotic body.

params.physicalTarget: ms=norm·500; 0.12→60 ms (cycled 0.04–0.20 → 20–100 ms). ENABLES pitch env.
params.rationale: Fast glide = punchy zap; non-zero is the master enable so the Start/End above are live.

params.physicalTarget: curveAmount=2·0.15−1=−0.7 → fast initial drop (classic 808/zap exp shape).
params.rationale: Exponential fast-drop gives the snappy synthetic pitch transient.

params.physicalTarget: phys=0.5+0.6·1.5=1.4 (cycled 0.40–0.88 → 1.1–1.82). B up to ~0.01 → clearly metallic/inharmonic partial spacing.
params.rationale: Pushed well off neutral (0.333) to maximize inharmonicity — core of the 'no acoustic referent' metallic spectrum (no-op on String, accepted).

params.physicalTarget: phys=2·0.55−1=+0.10 (cycled 0.40–0.88 → −0.20..+0.76). Per-mode amplitude tilt ratio^(−skew) + global skewBias.
params.rationale: Off-neutral per-mode tilt now applies on all modal bodies (M-5) → high/low spectral balance differs per voice, evolving spectrum.
params.citation: AUDIT M-5 decaySkew per-mode tilt all bodies

params.rationale: Deliberately OFF — a clean tonal harmonic stack would FIGHT the inharmonic chaos; complexity macro can nudge it if wanted.

params.physicalTarget: amount 0.75 (cycled 0.65–0.85): drive=1+6·vel·0.75·env recipSqrt amplitude brightening, sustained.
params.rationale: Near-max = the von-Karman energy-cascade emulation: louder→brighter→more odd harmonics through the ring; the defining 'evolving chaotic brightening' axis.
params.citation: AUDIT M-3/M-4 env-level waveshaping; sciencedirect nonlinear plate energy cascade

params.physicalTarget: ≥0.5 ON for ~half the pads (i%2); Material driven Start→End over Duration per note.
params.rationale: Time-varying material = the body timbre morphs WITHIN each hit → evolving, never-static chaos.
Start darker so the within-hit sweep travels upward (brightening evolution).
Ends bright/metallic — pairs with the nonlinear-coupling brightening for a strong evolving sweep.
Long enough to hear the material sweep across the ring of a medium-decay hit.
Linear is a safe default; cycling adds variety where set.

params.physicalTarget: g=round(norm·8); spread across 0/1/2 — some pads choke each other.
params.rationale: Choke-group spreading = stuttering cut-offs between chaos voices (open/closed-hat-style mutes for synthetic-fx).
Multi-out spreading lets the chaotic voices be processed/placed separately downstream — explicit archetype requirement.
Non-integer/irrational c:m → inharmonic Bessel-sideband bell/metallic spectrum on the FMImpulse voices.
params.citation: Wikipedia FM synth inharmonic non-integer ratio; ccrma fm

params.physicalTarget: drive=vel+0.7·(1−vel), ×kMaxFeedback 0.85 → up to ~0.85 self-osc drive. Feedback exciter only.
params.rationale: High self-oscillation drive floor = sustained squeal/instability — the chaos engine; capped at 0.85 stability rail.

params.physicalTarget: bowPressure=0.1+0.4·vel+0.4·0.5 → heavier bowing. Friction exciter only.
params.rationale: Higher bow pressure = scrape/squeal-heavy struck-bow attack on the Friction voices.

params.physicalTarget: 0.78 participation in the GLOBAL cross-pad sympathetic engine (audible since kit globalCoupling high).
params.rationale: High inter-pad coupling = voices ring each other sympathetically → emergent chaotic interaction across the kit.
Pushed high — the one-knob 'more chaos' macro; stacks extra coupling + nonlinear brightening + injected modes.
Left near neutral and cycled so it adds per-voice variation without overriding the carefully-set underlying decay/material.
Neutral — the explicit Filter Cutoff and (off) Mode Inject are already set; avoid double-driving.
Neutral — Size/Stretch/Decay are set explicitly per the chaos design.
Near-neutral; the pitch env is already configured, leave the macro mostly out of it.
Moderate parallel noise = grit/hiss bed under the chaos, but kept under the body so timbre survives.
params.citation: AUDIT H-2 body must not be buried

params.rationale: Mid LP cutoff — a present but not piercing noise bed; cycled for spread.
Some resonant emphasis adds character to the noise bed without a pitched beep (forced-LP path).
Medium noise tail sustains the gritty bed through the evolving body.
Pink default, cycled toward brighter colors on some pads for spectral spread.

params.physicalTarget: 0.4 (cycled): click = 0.4·vel·env·BP-noise, half into exciter + full to out ·0.445.
params.rationale: Defined transient so each chaotic hit has a sharp onset point and drives the body modes.
Short contact = sharp tick attack.
Bright-ish tick cuts through the chaos; cycled for variety.

params.physicalTarget: b1=0.2+0.4·49.8≈20 s^-1 (cycled 0.30–0.55 → ~15–28 s^-1). Overrides Decay flat-damping.
params.rationale: Explicit mid flat-damping (controller can't store the −1 sentinel) keeps rings from running away into the rail; cycled per voice.
params.citation: AUDIT N-1 measured-strike norm; pad_config sentinel note

params.physicalTarget: b3=0.2·1e-3=2e-4 s (Bell→0 for ringing). Low f^2 damping = metallic long highs.
params.rationale: Low b3 keeps the high inharmonic modes ringing (metallic), Bell forced to 0 for maximum shimmer.

params.physicalTarget: 0 = pure Bessel (Membrane-only param; no-op on the other 5 bodies).
params.rationale: Off — air-loading 'deeper/less whistly' realism is the OPPOSITE of the whistly synthetic aesthetic; meaningful only on the Membrane voices.

params.physicalTarget: ~0.6 → ~9% sinusoidal frequency dither (cycled 0.55–0.87 → 8–13%). Modal bodies only.
params.rationale: High scatter = detuned/imperfect partials, no two hits identical — the 'algorithmic chaos' fingerprint (no-op String).
Adds a coupled shell layer for extra inharmonic body weight/beating.

params.physicalTarget: ≥0.5 ON → 24-mode secondary shell bank at noteOn.
params.rationale: On (with Coupling Strength>0) so the shell coupling is actually audible.
Shell tuned below the head adds low inharmonic weight and beating against the body.
Mid/bright shell material = ringing metallic coupled layer; cycled for spread.

params.physicalTarget: amt·3.3e-5·modalEnergy pitch jump, capped +3 st. MEMBRANE voices only (gated).
params.rationale: High tension = energy-dependent pitch glide ('kerthump'); the amplitude-pitch side of the von-Karman nonlinearity — audible only on the Membrane voices in the cycle, no-op elsewhere.
params.citation: Avanzini-Marogna-Bank 2012 (AUDIT N-1 tension redesign); von-Karman amplitude pitch glide

params.physicalTarget: 0.5 = center default (cycled toward L/R is optional). Equal-power.
params.rationale: Center seed; per-voice pan spreading is the explicit 'output-bus/spreading' axis — vary per pad to widen the chaotic image.

params.physicalTarget: 1.0 ON for the ~14 crafted chaos pads; uncrafted pads disabled.
params.rationale: Only the deliberately-built chaos voices sound; rest silenced (factory-kit policy).

defaultedParams.why: A clean 1/k tonal harmonic stack fights the inharmonic chaos; left at 0 (exact bypass) — Complexity macro nudges it if wanted.
Kept ~neutral 0.5 because Filter Cutoff, Size/Stretch/Decay and the Pitch Env are all set EXPLICITLY; driving them again via macros would double-count.
Membrane-only frequency correction whose 'deeper/less whistly' realism is the opposite of the whistly synthetic aesthetic; off and a no-op on the 5 non-Membrane bodies in the cycle.
Default 0.3 — body+exciter+filter cycling already supplies the variation; no benefit fighting each body's mode-shape per voice.
Single-segment Start→End glide is enough for a fast synthetic zap; the 3-point knee adds complexity with no audible payoff at 20–100 ms times.
Percussive one-shot filter sweep — sustain 0 / short release is the natural shape; no held note to sustain.
No-op here — none of the cycled exciters is NoiseBurst (Feedback/FMImpulse/Friction only); left at default.

physics:
This archetype has NO acoustic referent by definition — it is a pure sound-design construct, so the "physics" here is the ESTABLISHED SYNTHESIS that produces aggressive, distorted, inharmonic, evolving chaotic hits, mapped onto four well-grounded mechanisms:

(1) FEEDBACK FM / SELF-OSCILLATION. Routing an operator's output back into its own (or the body's) phase pushes the waveform from sine → complex, adding inharmonic overtones that escalate to "complete sonic chaos" at high feedback (loopmasters, unison.audio). Membrum's Feedback exciter is exactly an energy-limited body↔exciter self-oscillating loop; Feedback Amount sets the self-oscillation drive floor (drive = vel + feedbackAmt·(1−vel), capped at kMaxFeedback 0.85 for SC-008 stability). Drives toward squeal/sustain. → Feedback Amount HIGH (~0.7), but the 0.85 hard ceiling is the physical stability rail we voice against.

(2) CHOWNING INHARMONIC FM. Non-integer carrier:modulator ratios yield inharmonic bell/percussive spectra; high modulation index = burst of energy over a wide band at onset decaying to a sinusoid (Chowning drum: index≈25 fast-decay; bell: index 10, c:m 5:7). FMImpulse exciter carrier:mod ratio = 1+3·norm ∈[1,4]; an inharmonic (non-1.0, non-integer) ratio is the whole point → fmRatio cycled toward irrational values ~1.5–3.0 (J_n(I) Bessel sidebands at f_c ± k·f_m). (Wikipedia FM synth; cs.cmu.edu fm_synthesis; ccrma.)

(3) VON KARMAN NONLINEAR PLATE COUPLING (cymbal/gong). At amplitudes > plate thickness, geometric (cubic) nonlinearity couples linear eigenmodes and cascades energy low→high → bright shimmering crash, AND amplitude-dependent frequency = perceived pitch glide/bend (Touzé/Bilbao/Ducceschi; sciencedirect S0022460X15000759; researchgate von-Karman cymbal). Membrum's NonlinearCoupling is the cheap amplitude-driven-waveshaping approximation of this (drive=1+6·vel·amount·env; louder→harder odd-harmonic brightening, sustained through ring — audit M-3/M-4 redesign). Near-max coupling (0.65–0.85) = the energy-cascade brightening. The pitch-glide side of the same nonlinearity is carried separately by tensionModAmt (Membrane-only).

(4) BUCHLA WEST-COAST WAVEFOLDING + DRIVE. Folding the wave back on itself past a threshold injects dense high-order harmonics; more folds = brighter/harsher, and on percussion results are "harsh, noisy, rife with jitter and aliasing" — the exact aesthetic (buchlaarchives; perfectcircuit; DAFx17 Buchla 259). Membrum Fold = fold·π Serge sine-fold; Drive = recipSqrt odd-harmonic saturator (timbre not level after M-2 makeup). Both engaged moderately so the chaotic body is further mangled but not pinned to the rail.

Quantitative targets that drive params: inharmonicity via modeStretch (B widened to 0.01 post-audit, 1-indexed) pushed well off neutral (0.333) toward 0.55–0.80 = clearly metallic/stretched spectrum; modeScatter 0.5+ = 7–15% sinusoidal frequency dither (organic chaos, no two hits identical); per-mode decaySkew off-neutral for spectral evolution; secondary shell + global coupling for inter-voice sympathetic chaos; per-voice filter cycling (LP/HP/BP) + choke/output-bus spreading so the 14 chaos voices occupy different spectral/spatial slots rather than one mush. No fundamental/T60/material "ground truth" exists — the design goal is maximum controlled instability under the −1 dBTP bus limiter.

physics:
A hand clap is fundamentally a SHORT BURST OF BAND-LIMITED NOISE, not a pitched/modal sound. There is no fundamental and no harmonic series — the "pitch" is a broad formant set by the cavity between the hands acting as a Helmholtz resonator.

ACOUSTIC (real hands, Fletcher 2013 "Shock waves and the sound of a hand-clap"; MDPI Acoustics 2020 "Handclap for Acoustic Measurements"; Cornell/PRR 2025): The sound is generated by a shock wave / air jet squeezed out between the palms. A FLAT clap is broadband, roughly flat out to ~10 kHz. A DOMED/CUPPED clap adds a Helmholtz-type resonance: a subsidiary spectral maximum BELOW ~1 kHz (larger enclosed cavity → lower frequency), above which the spectrum rolls off faster than the flat clap. Reported A-weighted spectral centroid for a clap sits ~1.5–4 kHz with dominant energy ~1–6 kHz; some hands show two peaks — a lower broad Helmholtz peak (<1 kHz) and a higher narrower peak (>1 kHz) from the finger grooves. Overall the event is very short (a few ms of contact, hands are soft so it attenuates fast — no sustained ring, no pitch glide).

ELECTRONIC / synthesis (the established model the user references — TR-909, TR-808): White noise through a BANDPASS, with the amplitude envelope re-triggered MULTIPLE times in rapid succession to mimic several hands not hitting simultaneously. TR-909: white noise (31-stage LFSR, flat spectrum) → bandpass with center frequency ≈ 1140 Hz and Q ≈ 1.95 (forum-derived from the schematic), envelope fires ~4 times each ~11 ms apart, then a final slower AR "reverb" tail. The closely-spaced bursts read as one event with a comb/flam smear; the bandpass formant + that smear ARE the clap timbre. TR-808 clap is the same idea (bandpassed noise, multi-trigger). Noise is white into the bandpass.

DRIVERS FOR PARAMS: (1) Source must be band-limited NOISE, not a modal body → use the parallel NoiseLayer as the primary voice and the NoiseBurst exciter; keep the modal NoiseBody body weak/high (deemphasized). (2) The single most important feature is a RESONANT FORMANT around ~1.1–4 kHz → NoiseLayer resonance ~2.2 (≈909 Q≈1.95) with cutoff placing the peak. The DrumVoice forces the NoiseLayer SVF to LOWPASS, so resonance produces a resonant lowpass peak at the cutoff = the cupped-hand/909 formant. (3) The multi-burst "spread" → a LONGER NoiseBurst exciter burst (0.55 → ~9 ms) plus modeScatter to smear/decorrelate, approximating the 4×11 ms flam in one shot. (4) Contact click for the initial transient slap. (5) Short decay (~50 ms noise tail) — no ring, no glide.

bodyModel: NoiseBody

params.rationale: NoiseBody = 32 plate-ratio inharmonic modes + an internal bright noise layer = exactly the dense inharmonic metallic shimmer of clamped cymbals.

params.rationale: Correct selector value: NoiseBody is enum index 5, so norm 1.0. (Supersedes the 0.8 row above — use 1.0.)
