physics:
PITCH / FUNDAMENTAL: An acoustic hi-hat (two cymbals clamped shut, struck with a stick tip) has NO definite pitch. Its spectrum is a dense, inharmonic stack of plate/shell modes. The first six modes of a ~38 cm cymbal resemble flat-plate modes; above that the modes mix and are "far from harmonic" (Rossing holographic-interferometry study, Fletcher & Rossing, Physics of Musical Instruments ch.20; Sound on Sound "Analysing Metallic Percussion"). Perceptual "body" sits ~280-500 Hz, with the bulk of the audible energy and the recognizable "sparkle/chick" in the 5-10 kHz band and overtones reaching 16 kHz+ (Musical-U percussion-frequencies, emastered/musicguymixing hi-hat EQ references). For a CLOSED hat the two cymbals are clamped, so the low body modes are heavily damped and the radiated sound is dominated by short, bright, dense high-frequency inharmonic shimmer plus broadband stick-contact noise.

PARTIAL / MODE STRUCTURE & INHARMONICITY: Hundreds of energised modes at discrete, non-harmonic frequencies (Fletcher 2012 "Order from Complexity"; SoS metallic-percussion). At higher strike velocity the modes split and subharmonics fill in, and at high amplitude the response becomes effectively chaotic = noise-like (Fletcher & Rossing, nonlinear gong/cymbal/tam-tam studies). The closed hat does not ring long enough to develop much chaos, so the dominant character is dense inharmonic metallic noise rather than a clear tone.

PER-BAND DECAY (T60): Closed hat is intentionally SHORT — the clamp kills sustain. High-frequency modes decay fastest; the last energy in a free cymbal sits in the few-hundred-Hz modes, but a closed hat is choked before those ring out. The TR-808 service manual fixes the closed-hat decay at 50 ms (vs open-hat / cymbal 350-1200 ms variable). Real acoustic closed hats are in the same ballpark (~40-120 ms perceived). This sets Membrum's target: total audible length ~40-90 ms.

ATTACK / CONTACT CLICK: The stick tip striking clamped metal gives a sharp broadband contact transient (the "tick") a few ms long, brighter than the body. In synthesis this is the high-pass-filtered front edge.

NOISE CONTENT & SPECTRAL SHAPE: Dominantly high-frequency, bright (violet/white tilt — energy rising toward 8-12 kHz). The classic electronic emulation (TR-808/606) builds the "metal noise" from SIX inharmonically-tuned square-wave oscillators (606 measured: 246/308/367/418/440/627 Hz) bandpass-filtered (bridged-T at ~3.4 kHz body and ~7.1 kHz shimmer) then high-pass-filtered and gated with a short VCA envelope (Baratatronix 606/808 teardown; joesul.li hi-hat synthesis). The takeaway for a modal+noise engine: a dense inharmonic resonant stack (the six detuned squares ≈ Membrum's 32 plate-ratio modes) bandlimited high, plus a bright broadband noise bed and a short envelope.

PITCH GLIDE: None. A clamped hi-hat has no "kerthump"/tension glide (that is a tom/membrane phenomenon). PitchEnv Time stays 0; tensionModAmt is Membrane-only and irrelevant here.

MATERIAL: Bronze/brass alloy (B20-ish) cymbals, clamped — metallic, bright. Kit brightness varies (jazz/rock hats brighter, "wood"/dark kits warmer) but it is the same instrument: brightness is dialed with Material + Noise Cutoff, not a different body.

MAPPING TARGETS DERIVED: NoiseBody f0 = 1500*0.1^size — at size≈0.05 that is ~1337 Hz, so the plate-ratio modal stack lands its dense inharmonic partials across ~1.3-12 kHz (correct hat register). Noise bed pushed to ~9-10 kHz violet for the shimmer. Decay knob set so the NoiseBody noise envelope (60ms*0.3..3x factor) and modal decayTime give a ~40-90 ms choked "chick". Choke group 1 so closed/pedal/open hats mute each other.

archetype: Concert Bass Drum (orchestral gran cassa, kick)
bodyModel: Membrane
exciter: Mallet

params.rationale: Gran cassa is struck with a single heavy soft felt mallet (maille); the Mallet backend gives a softer, lower-bandwidth contact than the Impulse click.
params.citation: Wikipedia Gran cassa: 'played using a single heavy felt covered mallet'

params.rationale: A double-headed circular drumhead is a vibrating membrane; only the Membrane mapper gives Bessel inharmonicity + air-loading + tension-mod (all required here).
params.citation: Russell PSU circular membrane modes; Wikipedia Gran cassa double-headed

params.physicalTarget: woody/skin: Membrane brightness=0.30, baseDecay=lerp(0.15,0.8,0.3)*(1+0.1*0.9)=~0.32 s, modest stretch=0.3*0.3=0.09
params.rationale: Calf/coated skin over wood shell = woody, warm, low high-mode content; well below the metallic half of the range.
params.citation: rareinstrument concert-bass-drum: calfskin 'warm, rich, complex overtones'

params.physicalTarget: f0 = 500*0.1^0.9 = ~63 Hz natural fundamental (very large body, ~40 in drum)
params.rationale: Very large 40 in drum -> very low fundamental, longer ring-up; (1,1) audible mode ~100 Hz = low indefinite pitch.
params.citation: Wikipedia: ~40 in diameter; SoS example modes 50-179 Hz

params.physicalTarget: ~1.18x body base decay (exp(lerp(ln0.3,ln3.0,0.55))) -> medium-long blooming tail (~0.4 s body, longer LF ring)
params.rationale: Free-suspended thin-shell concert drum has lush, seconds-long resonant bloom; longer/softer than a damped pop kick but not an infinite gong.
params.citation: rareinstrument: 'broad, resonant tone with long sustain'; SoS uniform decay across low partials

params.rationale: Orchestral playing strikes off-center (not dead center, which kills the (0,1) thump) to favor the round, full (1,1)/(2,1) diametric modes.
params.citation: Russell: center strike gives pitchless quick thump; off-center favors the slower-decaying diametric modes

params.rationale: A loud orchestral single stroke; near-top per-pad loudness with master/limiter owning the ceiling post-N-1 gain staging.
params.citation: audit N-1: per-element ~-6 dBFS strike budget + bus TruePeakLimiter

params.physicalTarget: strong Rossing air-loading depression of the lowest Bessel modes -> deeper, less whistly membrane
params.rationale: A real, large, low-tuned, air-loaded double-headed drum sits lower and rounder than a pure ideal membrane; Membrane-only correction.
params.citation: Rossing 1982 air-loading curve; SoS coupled heads+cavity-air shift the low modes

params.rationale: The boom starts higher and drops; 110 Hz start gives the characteristic descending 'whoomp' of a big kick.
params.citation: SoS: pitch shifts a couple semitones start->finish as tension relaxes

params.rationale: Settles to a sub-bass ~40 Hz floor for the deep cinematic body of a gran cassa.
params.citation: SoS bass-drum model fundamental ~43 Hz; concert kick 50-80 Hz, lower with hall support

params.physicalTarget: 0.24*500 = ~120 ms glide duration (ENABLES the pitch env)
params.rationale: A slow, audible bloom-glide (slower than a snappy 808/kit kick) matching the soft-beater, large-head response.
params.citation: SoS: pitch falls over the early decay of a large slow membrane

params.physicalTarget: curveAmount = 2*0.15-1 = -0.7 (fast initial drop, classic exponential pitch decay)
params.rationale: Tension-driven pitch falls fastest at the start while amplitude is highest, then settles - the physical exponential shape.
params.citation: SoS: pitch tracks tension which is highest just after the strike

params.physicalTarget: effective ~0.12*3.3e-5 energy scale -> gentle energy-driven upward pitch jump (<<+3 st), relaxing as it decays
params.rationale: Soft felt beater raises head tension only mildly on impact -> a subtle upward 'kerthump' under the dominant downward glide (Membrane-only).
params.citation: Avanzini, Marogna & Bank JASA 2012 tension modulation; SoS pitch rises-then-falls

params.physicalTarget: modest beater-contact transient mixed in + half-amp into exciter feed
params.rationale: Soft felt contact is present but gentle - enough to define the attack without a sharp tick.
params.citation: Wikipedia soft felt mallet; SoS brief HF burst at impact

params.rationale: A soft large beater has a longer, softer contact than a stick; spreads the transient.
params.citation: SoS: HF partials last 'a few ms' after impact

params.rationale: Felt beater contact is low and dull, not a sharp high tick; centers the click low.
params.citation: rareinstrument soft mallet 'big, fluffy tone'; felt = low-pass contact

params.rationale: A subtle low air/body rumble under the modes; orchestral kick is mostly tonal body, only a little broadband.
params.citation: SoS broadband transient + air component; not a snare-like hiss

params.rationale: The noise component is the low air/shell rumble, so keep it dark and low.
Brown gives the deepest spectral tilt for a low rumble under the body.
params.citation: low-frequency rumble character; SoS low-band emphasis

params.rationale: The air-rumble blooms briefly with the strike then fades faster than the tonal body.
params.citation: SoS transient/air content is short-lived vs the ringing modes

params.physicalTarget: b3 = 0.55e-3 s -> moderate-strong f^2 (high-mode) damping = wood/skin, not metal
params.rationale: Skin-over-wood damps highs faster than lows (real drum), giving the warm, dark, no-shimmer tail; overrides material derivation for a deliberate woody tilt.
params.citation: Chaigne & Askenfelt / Aramaki frequency-squared material damping; SoS HF dies first

params.physicalTarget: off - no parallel secondary shell bank
params.rationale: Prompt: secondary head off. The single Membrane (with its two real heads already modeled by the air-loaded mapper) carries the body; no extra coupled shell needed.
Orchestral bass drum sits center in the stereo image.

defaultedParams.why: A skin membrane is naturally inharmonic via Bessel ratios; deliberately stretching partials would make it metallic - wrong for a drumhead.
Decay is roughly uniform across the important low partials (SoS); no high/low tilt of decay is wanted.
A synthetic integer harmonic series would impose a defined pitch; the gran cassa is deliberately pitchless.
A soft-beater concert drum is not a hard nonlinear plate; amplitude brightening would add an un-physical metallic edge.
Clean acoustic timbre; no saturation/wavefolding harmonics in an orchestral drum.
The body+air-loading+b3 already shape the spectrum; the post-body SVF is left transparent.
No filter is engaged, so the filter envelope is inert by design.
Secondary shell is disabled, so all coupling/secondary params are no-ops.
The membrane's intrinsic inharmonicity is enough; added detune dither is not needed for a clean concert drum (could be dialed for 'character' but kept physical here).
The Decay knob (0.55) already sets the flat-damping RT60; no need to override the overall decay floor.
The timbre does not sweep over a single hit; material is static for one stroke.
A simple monotone Start->End downward glide matches the physical tension relaxation; the 3-point knee is unnecessary.

defaultedParams.param: FM Ratio / Feedback Amount / NoiseBurst Duration / Friction Pressure
defaultedParams.why: Only meaningful for FMImpulse/Feedback/NoiseBurst/Friction exciters; the Mallet exciter ignores them.
A bass drum is not a mute-group member and routes to the main bus.
All voicing is done on the underlying params directly; macros left at zero-delta so the preset values are preserved.
Kit-level sympathetic coupling is off in this archetype, so per-pad participation weight is inaudible; left at default.
Pad is active.
The rumble noise is broadband/dark; a resonant peak would impose a pitched beep, unwanted.

physics:
FUNDAMENTAL / PITCH: A 40 in x 20 in double-headed gran cassa produces "a note of low definite or indefinite pitch" (Wikipedia/Gran cassa) - effectively a low broadband thud with no fixed musical pitch. The lowest air-loaded membrane resonances sit roughly in the 40-90 Hz region; the Sound on Sound bass-drum synthesis article gives an example drum with low modes at 50 / 93 / 136 / 179 Hz (ratios ~1 : 1.86 : 2.72 : 3.58) and a ~43 Hz oscillator fundamental for the harmonic-cluster model. Concert kick fundamentals are typically tuned 50-80 Hz. Membrum target: Membrane f0 = 500*0.1^size; at size 0.90 that is 500*0.1259 = ~63 Hz natural fundamental, with the preferred (1,1) audible mode at ~1.59x ~= 100 Hz - exactly a low, indistinct-pitch register.

PARTIAL STRUCTURE / INHARMONICITY: An ideal circular membrane is inharmonic and pitchless. Bessel-root mode ratios relative to (0,1): (1,1)=1.593, (2,1)=2.135, (0,2)=2.295, (1,2)=2.917, (0,3)=3.598 (Dan Russell, PSU; The Well-Tempered Timpani). The audible orchestral-drum modes are the low diametric family (1,1),(2,1),(3,1),(4,1),(5,1); the spectrum is "harmonic at low frequencies (shifted up ~7 Hz by the two coupled heads + cavity air), increasingly inharmonic at high frequencies" (SoS). Membrum's Membrane mapper renders 48 Bessel modes, so the inharmonicity is intrinsic - we keep modeStretch neutral (do NOT metallically stretch a skin head) and use airLoading to depress the lowest modes toward the real, deeper, less-whistly curve (Rossing 1982).

DECAY / T60: Orchestral bass drums are thin-shelled, low-tension, free-suspended, calf or coated heads chosen for "lush decay" and "long sustain / broad resonant bloom" (rareinstrument concert-bass-drum guide). Let-ring decay is on the order of seconds (low frequencies ring longest; HF dies first). Decay is roughly uniform across the important low partials (SoS: "rate is constant across all the important partials"). Target: a medium-long body decay, longer/softer than a tight pop kick (which can choke in ~0.06 s) but not an infinite gong. Membrum Decay 0.55 -> ~exp(lerp(ln0.3,ln3.0,0.55)) ~= 1.18x the Membrane base decay (base = lerp(0.15,0.8,material)*(1+0.1*size)) -> a medium-long, blooming tail with a gentle high-mode roll-off from material/b3.

ATTACK / CONTACT CLICK: Played with a single heavy soft felt mallet (maille) -> a soft, low-frequency contact, NOT a sharp stick tick. "Hundreds of short-lived high-frequency partials for just a few ms after the membrane is hit" (SoS) but spectrally dull because the beater is soft. So: a low-centered, moderate-duration contact thud. Membrum: Mallet exciter (softer beater than Impulse) + a click layer with a DULL bandpass center (brightness 0.20 -> ~453 Hz) and a slightly longer contact (0.42 -> ~3.3 ms), at modest mix - the felt "whump", not a click.

NOISE CONTENT: Broadband low rumble / air component under the tonal modes; dark spectral tilt (energy concentrated low, HF transient brief). Membrum: a modest, dark, lowpassed parallel noise layer (Brown/low cutoff, medium decay) for the air-and-body rumble.

PITCH GLIDE: On strike, membrane tension rises with displacement^2 -> pitch jumps UP briefly, then falls "a couple of semitones from start to finish" as amplitude decays (SoS). The dominant, iconic perceptual cue of a big kick is the downward settle of the boom. We render that downward glide explicitly with the pitch envelope (110 -> 40 Hz over ~120 ms, classic exponential drop) and add a gentle energy-driven upward "kerthump" with tensionMod (Membrane-only, Avanzini et al. 2012) - kept small, because a soft beater does not snap the head as hard as a hand or hard mallet.

MATERIAL: Calf / coated skin over a wood shell -> woody, warm, low-tuned, air-loaded membrane. NOT metallic. Membrum material ~0.30 -> woody brightness, longer wood-like high-mode damping (b3), softer spectral tilt.

archetype: Concert Timpani (pitched orchestral kettledrum, tom-family / kick-slot)
bodyModel: Membrane (Bessel circular drumhead, 48 modes) — the only body with air-loading frequency correction, which is exactly the physics that coaxes a kettledrum's (m,1) modes into the near-harmonic 1 : 1.5 : 2 : 2.44 : 2.9 series. Plate/Shell/Bell are metallic and wrong here; String is a waveguide. Membrane is mandatory.
exciter: Mallet (softer raised-cosine beater) — a timpani is struck with a felt-covered mallet near the edge, NOT a hard impulse. Mallet rolls off HF energy so the low (m,1) preferred modes dominate and the upper inharmonic modes form only a brief colored attack, matching the real instrument. NoiseBurst/FMImpulse would read as snare/bell.

params.rationale: Felt mallet beater: soft contact, HF-rolled-off strike so the low preferred modes dominate and only a brief colored attack burst survives.
params.citation: Reid, Sound on Sound 'Practical Percussion Synthesis: Timpani'

params.rationale: Only body with air-loading frequency correction; the kettle-air-loaded membrane IS a timpano. Mandatory.
params.citation: Rossing, Sci. Am. 1982; Fletcher & Rossing ch.18

params.physicalTarget: woody-membranous: brightness=0.32, baseDecay=lerp(0.15,0.8,0.32)*(1+0.1*size)≈0.36s base, low inherent stretch
params.rationale: Mylar/calf head over copper kettle — clearly woody, not metallic; keeps the tuned low modes clean while the b3 term damps the HF hash.
Large kettledrum, low register. Natural (1,1) lands ~100 Hz; the pitch envelope then sets the exact sounding pitch. Longer ring-up too.

params.physicalTarget: decayTime = base*exp(lerp(ln0.3,ln3,0.82))≈base*2.3 → long, musically ~1-3 s ring
params.rationale: Task spec 0.80-0.85; a timpano sustains far longer than a kit tom. b1 override below sets the actual flat-damping floor.
params.citation: task spec; Reid/SoS long note decay

params.physicalTarget: r_over_a=0.28*0.9=0.252 → strike ~1/4 radius in from rim
params.rationale: Players strike ~1/4 of the radius from the rim to suppress the non-pitched (0,1) mode and favour the (m,1) preferred series (amps ~5:4:3:1, lower modes loudest).
Trimmed below 0.8 for headroom: a large low-damped membrane builds up resonantly (audit N-1), so a single timpano hit must stay under the per-voice rail and leave room in the bus limiter.
Task spec 180 Hz start; the small upward 'kerthump'+settle is rendered as a fast glide down from the energized pitch to the tuned pitch.
Task spec: tuned sounding pitch ~85 Hz (low F2-ish timpano). Pitch env rescales the whole modal bank to this f.
Task spec 50 ms: short head-tension settle, not a synthy 808 boom; long enough to read as a struck-head pitch ease, short enough to keep a defined pitch.
Tension relaxes fastest right after the strike (energy decays), so segment-1 falls quickly then eases — a mild exponential, not the steep 808 curve.

params.physicalTarget: (m,1) modes interpolated fully onto Rossing target: (1,1):(2,1):(3,1):(4,1):(5,1)=1:1.50:2.00:2.44:2.90
params.rationale: This is the defining timpani physics — full air loading puts the diametric modes exactly on the near-harmonic 2:3:4:5:6 series that gives the kettledrum its pitch. Default 0.6 under-applies it.

params.physicalTarget: effective=0.16*3.3e-5*energy → up to ~+0.3-0.5 semitone transient pitch rise that relaxes
params.rationale: Membrane-only energy-driven 'kerthump': hard hits pitch up briefly then settle. Small amount — a timpano is far less tension-modulated than a tuned tom.

params.physicalTarget: b1=0.2+0.12*49.8≈6.2 s^-1 flat damping → ~0.16 s flat floor, long low-mode ring
params.rationale: Low flat damping for a long musical sustain; overrides the Decay-knob flat term. Low value = the tuned modes ring out like a real timpano.

params.physicalTarget: b3=0.1*1e-3=1e-4 s (Hz convention) → moderate f² HF damping
params.rationale: Damps the inharmonic high modes quickly (woody head) so only the tuned (m,1) series survives into the sustain, while keeping the low modes long.
params.citation: Chaigne & Lambourg 2001 (f² membrane loss); Rossing (HF modes decay fast)

params.physicalTarget: ~6% sinusoidal frequency dither on modal freqs
params.rationale: Real heads are slightly imperfect (the third partial runs a touch flat, Kirby) — a small scatter avoids a sterile mathematically-perfect series. Kept tiny so the pitch stays defined.
params.citation: Kirby (third partial flat) via Well-Tempered Timpani; Rossing

params.physicalTarget: mix 0.32 → modest beater contact transient, half into exciter + full to output
params.rationale: Felt mallet has a soft, present-but-not-sharp contact thump; gives attack definition without a stick tick.
Felt mallet dwells on the head a few ms (longer than a hard stick) — a soft, broad contact.
Dull-to-low-mid contact tone: a felt beater is dark, not a bright tick.

params.physicalTarget: mix 0.10 → faint parallel noise, *0.243 standalone gain → well under the body
params.rationale: Timpani are nearly noiseless; just a faint head/air chuff. Kept low so the tuned body dominates.
params.citation: Fletcher & Rossing ch.18 (timpani are tonal, low noise)

params.rationale: Dark chuff matched to the low body register; no bright hiss.
The faint air/head noise is low-frequency-weighted, not white/violet.
params.citation: derived from dark timpani noise character

params.rationale: Any noise is an attack-region chuff that dies fast, leaving the tuned ring.
The kick-slot timpano is centered; the 5-pad tuned row can be spread by the kit designer, but the archetype baseline is center.
Pad sounds.

defaultedParams.param: Filter Type / Filter Cutoff / Filter Resonance
defaultedParams.why: ToneShaper SVF left at cutoff norm 1.0 (=20 kHz, bypassed) — a timpano needs no post-body EQ; the body+damping already shapes the spectrum.

defaultedParams.param: Filter Env Amount / Atk / Dec / Sus / Rel
defaultedParams.why: FilterEnvAmount 0.5 = no modulation; with the filter bypassed there is no cutoff sweep, and a real timpano has no filter motion.
0 = exact dry. Timpani are clean; drive would add non-physical saturation harmonics (Drive is flavour, not level, post-M-2).
0 = bypass. Wavefolding is a west-coast timbre, alien to an acoustic kettledrum.
0.333 = neutral/physical. The air-loading table already provides the correct (near-harmonic) ratios; extra inharmonic stretch would detune the pitch.
0.5 = no skew. The per-mode b1/b3 damping already gives the physical decay ordering (HF hash fast, tuned modes long); a global skew is redundant and would unbalance it.
0 = exact bypass. The membrane already produces the full tuned series; a synthetic 1/k harmonic stack would add a non-physical buzzy overtone layer.
0 = exact bypass. Timpani are essentially linear at musical levels; amplitude brightening is a cymbal/gong behaviour, not a kettledrum one.

defaultedParams.param: Morph Enabled / Start / End / Duration / Curve
defaultedParams.why: Morph off — the head material does not sweep during a single struck note; Material is static.
0 = no choke. Timpani ring freely and are damped by hand, not by a mute-group retrigger.
0 = main bus. Standard routing for the archetype baseline.

defaultedParams.param: FM Ratio / Feedback Amount / NoiseBurst Duration / Friction Pressure
defaultedParams.why: Secondary-exciter params — no-ops because the selected exciter is Mallet, not FMImpulse/Feedback/NoiseBurst/Friction.
0.5 default participation — inert unless the kit enables Global Coupling/Tom Resonance; a sympathetic timpani row can use it but the per-pad baseline leaves it neutral.

defaultedParams.param: Tightness / Brightness / Body Size / Punch / Complexity macros
defaultedParams.why: All 0.5 = neutral (zero delta). The explicit per-param values above are the intended voicing; macros are left for the user to nudge.

defaultedParams.param: Coupling Strength / Secondary Enabled / Secondary Size / Secondary Material
defaultedParams.why: Secondary shell OFF. A timpano is a SINGLE struck head over a kettle — it has no second struck membrane/shell ring. Enabling the secondary bank historically doubled RMS and clipped (orchestral kit comment). Off is physically correct and required for headroom.

defaultedParams.param: PitchEnv Knee / Mid / Mid Frac / Curve2
defaultedParams.why: Knee OFF — the tension settle is a single Start→End segment; no middle breakpoint needed.

physics: Researched profile: PERCEIVED PITCH is the (1,1) one-nodal-diameter mode (the axisymmetric (0,1) gives a non-pitched thup and is deliberately under-struck). Orchestral set ≈ D2–A3 (73–220 Hz); the task's settle band 85–180 Hz = 26–32\" kettles; Reid/SoS cites a large kettle principal ≈150 Hz. MODE STRUCTURE: the enclosed kettle air mass loads the head and depresses the lower diametric modes onto Rossing's near-harmonic series f(1,1):f(2,1):f(3,1):f(4,1):f(5,1) = 1 : 1.50 : 2.00 : 2.44 : 2.90 (≈ harmonics 2:3:4:5:6 of a missing fundamental → fused pitch). Only the (m,1) modes are quasi-harmonic; (0,n) and higher-n modes stay inharmonic and decay fast. Attack amplitudes ≈ 5:4:3:1 across (1,1)…(4,1) → lower modes loudest → edge strike (~1/4 radius from rim). INHARMONICITY: small/real (third partial slightly flat, Kirby) → tiny scatter only. DECAY: kettle makes low modes radiate efficiently so the principal decays FASTER (relative ~45/73/91/84% for modes 1–4) but the note still rings ~1–3 s; HF inharmonic hash is damped fast by the b3·f² term. TRANSIENT: felt-mallet soft contact = a brief dark colored-noise/inharmonic burst (few ms), not a sharp tick. NOISE: low, dark head/air chuff. PITCH GLIDE: no synthetic boom in steady play, but energy-dependent tension makes hard hits pitch up briefly then settle (Avanzini-Marogna-Bank 2012) — captured by a 180→85 Hz / 50 ms glide plus a small tensionMod. MATERIAL: Mylar/calf head over copper kettle (woody-membranous). These drive: Body=Membrane, Exciter=Mallet, airLoading=1.0 (puts ratios on the Rossing series), pitchEnv 180→85/50 ms, low b1 (long ring) + moderate b3 (kill HF hash), edge strikePos, secondary OFF (single head), small tensionMod, minimal dark noise, level trimmed for headroom.

bodyModel: Membrane (Bessel circular drumhead, 48 modes) — single rawhide head on a staved barrel shell, optional passive secondary (shell) resonator for the wooden barrel body
exciter: Impulse (raised-cosine finger/hand contact) + always-on Click layer for the woody finger-contact tick

params.rationale: Open tone is an unpitched finger strike near the rim — a clean broadband impulse into the head, not a mallet/noise/bowed exciter.
A conga is literally a tensioned circular membrane; only the Membrane body gives the correct Bessel modes, air loading and tension glide.
params.citation: Fletcher & Rossing, Physics of Musical Instruments (circular membrane)

params.physicalTarget: warm-woody rawhide (brightness 0.45; baseDecay lerp→~0.44 s scale)
params.rationale: Rawhide head is midrange-warm with the fundamental dominating overtones — below 0.5 keeps it woody, not bright/plasticky.
params.citation: Remo conga drumhead guide; Rhythm Notes replacement conga heads

params.rationale: Sets the body's modal scale to a low hand-drum register; the heard open-tone pitch is then fixed by the pitch envelope. Lo conga 0.62, hi conga 0.50 (a tuned-apart pair).

params.physicalTarget: ~1x multiplier on baseDecay → open-tone body ring a few hundred ms
params.rationale: Open tone rings but a hand-damped single-headed drum is not sustained; mid value gives a focused, not boomy, tail.
Open tone is struck part-way out toward the rim (not dead center, not the edge) — 0.30-0.45 weights the low pitched modes while keeping definition.
params.citation: Wikipedia Conga (open tone played with four fingers near the rim)

params.rationale: Standard per-pad loudness; final make-up is master/bus, leave at the kit default.
If the tone-shaper ever engages, LP is the natural choice; here it stays bypassed (cutoff 1.0).
The membrane + damping already shape the spectrum; no post-body filter needed for an open tone.
No-op while filter bypassed.
No filter sweep on a hand drum; keeps the filter bypassed.
Acoustic open tone has no saturation flavour; Drive is post-audit a flavour stage, not needed.
West-coast wavefolding is alien to an acoustic conga.
Open-tone fundamental start; the slight downward glide is the 'pitched thump' settle.
params.citation: uspto Tuning-of-a-drum patent (conga fundamental ~200 Hz); audiorecording.me

params.rationale: Settle pitch of the open tone after the tension transient relaxes (brief's lo 150 / hi 210 targets).
Short tension-relaxation 'kerthump', not an 808-length sweep.
Tension relaxes fast then settles — exponential-style drop, matching the factory pitch-env default.
A membrane should keep its physical inharmonic ratios; air loading (not stretch) does the conga's pitch-focusing.
b3 HF damping already tilts energy low; no extra per-mode skew needed for the open tone.
A conga is inharmonic-membrane, not a tonal harmonic stack; injecting an integer series would falsely 'pitch' it.
Open tone is fairly linear; the audible nonlinearity (pitch thump) is handled by tension mod, not amplitude waveshaping.
Rawhide hand drum has only a trace of skin/air contact noise at the attack — just enough to thicken the contact.
params.citation: Remo/Rhythm Notes head guides (rawhide low-overtone, warm)

params.rationale: Keeps the small noise component dark/woody, under the body, not hissy.
Gentle, no pitched peak in the noise.
Contact noise is a brief attack thickener, not a sustained hiss.
Pink/brown skin-contact spectrum, warm; no bright violet hiss.

params.physicalTarget: 0.50·vel·env·filtered click; half also drives the body
params.rationale: The woody finger-contact tick is the signature attack of a hand drum open tone — present but not slap-loud.
Short, soft finger contact (a hand, not a stick) — brief but not razor-sharp.
Mid, woody contact tone — a dull-to-medium tick, not a high stick click; the slap articulation would raise this.

params.physicalTarget: b1 = 0.2 + 0.30·49.8 ≈ 15 s^-1 flat damping (~0.3-0.4 s ring floor)
params.rationale: Overrides Decay's flat term for a controlled, hand-drum-length open-tone ring (not a long tom boom).
Strong-ish HF damping kills the metallic high modes fast, leaving a warm woody body — the rawhide character.

params.physicalTarget: 50% morph toward the Rossing air-loaded low-mode series
params.rationale: A single-headed, bottom-open conga has MODERATE air loading — enough to focus the open-tone pitch (depress low modes toward 1:1.5:2) without the full closed-kettle timpani effect.
params.citation: Rossing 1982 / Fletcher & Rossing (air loading depresses low membrane modes)

params.rationale: Real rawhide is slightly uneven — a touch of detune dither for organic variation, far short of chaos.

params.physicalTarget: feedforward drive 0.30 from head into the secondary shell bank
params.rationale: The brief asks for head/shell coupling: the wooden barrel body adds a short woody after-ring driven by the head.
params.citation: audit §C head↔shell coupling; Chromaphone-style body coupling

params.rationale: Required for the staved wooden barrel body resonance; only audible because Coupling Strength>0.
Barrel shell resonates somewhat below the head — a moderately large body, the ~0.6 Chromaphone rule.
Wooden, short-decaying barrel — woody not metallic, dies before it outlasts the head tone.

params.physicalTarget: energy-dependent upward pitch jump ~+0.4 semitone at full velocity, relaxing as it decays
params.rationale: Membrane-only 'kerthump' — the velocity-sensitive pitch thump of a hard open tone, complementing the fixed pitch env.
Baseline center; a lo/hi conga pair would be spread modestly L/R at kit-assembly time, but the archetype default is centered.

defaultedParams.param: Filter Type / Cutoff / Resonance / Env (Amount, Atk, Dec, Sus, Rel)
defaultedParams.why: ToneShaper SVF stays bypassed (cutoff 1.0, env amount 0.5) — the membrane modes + b3 damping already shape the open tone; a post filter is unphysical here.
Acoustic open tone has no saturation or wavefolding; both are flavour stages bypassed at 0.
A conga is an inharmonic membrane, not a tonal harmonic stack — injecting an integer series would falsely pitch it.
The audible amplitude effect on a conga is pitch glide (handled by Tension Mod), not amplitude-driven brightening.

defaultedParams.param: PitchEnv Knee / Mid / Mid Frac / Curve2
defaultedParams.why: The tension settle is a single short segment; the 3-point knee is unnecessary (no-op with knee off).

defaultedParams.param: Morph Enabled / Start / End / Duration / Curve
defaultedParams.why: No intra-hit material sweep on an open tone; the rawhide timbre is static within a hit (morph off).
No-op — Impulse exciter selected, not FMImpulse.
No-op — not the Feedback exciter.
No-op — not the NoiseBurst exciter (the contact noise comes from the parallel Noise/Click layers instead).
No-op — not the Friction exciter; a conga is struck, not bowed.
Left at neutral 0.5 — global cross-pad sympathetic coupling is a kit-level decision, not part of the single-voice conga timbre.

defaultedParams.param: Macros (Tightness, Brightness, Body Size, Punch, Complexity)
defaultedParams.why: Left at neutral 0.5 so the explicit per-param values above are the literal voicing; macros are user performance offsets.

defaultedParams.param: Choke Group / Output Bus / Pad Enabled
defaultedParams.why: Routing — congas don't choke each other (open tones overlap), main bus, pad enabled; kit-assembly concerns, defaults.

physics: FUNDAMENTAL/PITCH: conga family = three single-headed Afro-Cuban hand drums tuned apart (quinto ~28cm highest, conga ~29-30cm mid, tumba ~30-32cm lowest), commonly tuned to 4th/5th intervals (Wikipedia Conga; artdrum tuning FAQ). Open-tone fundamental is LOW — teaching/patent/EQ sources place the conga open tone around 200 Hz with harmonics near 400 Hz and ~700 Hz (uspto Tuning-of-a-drum patent; audiorecording.me). This recipe voices a tuned pair: lo conga open tone ~200 Hz, hi conga ~280 Hz. PARTIAL STRUCTURE & INHARMONICITY: 2-D circular membrane → Bessel-zero modes (1, 1.594, 2.136, 2.296, 2.653...), INHARMONIC and dense, not integer (Fletcher & Rossing; Wikipedia circular membrane). The wooden shell + enclosed air load the lowest modes, pulling them toward a more pitched 1:~1.5:~2 feel (Rossing air-loading, same mechanism as timpani 1:1.5:2:2.44:2.9). Single-headed + open bottom ⇒ MODERATE air loading (less than a closed timpani kettle) ⇒ airLoading ~0.5. DECAY/T60: open tone rings a few hundred ms (focused low-mid), high modes die fastest via b3·f² damping (Chaigne & Askenfelt 1994); not a sustained instrument (hand-damped tradition). Target body ring ~0.3-0.5 s with strong HF damping → b1 norm 0.30 (~15 s^-1), b3 norm 0.10 (1e-4). ATTACK/CONTACT CLICK: woody mid-frequency finger-contact tick (open tone = four fingers near rim) — carried by the Click layer, ~2.6 ms contact, ~1.8 kHz bandpass center, moderate mix; slap would push it brighter/shorter. NOISE: minimal — rawhide hand drum is low-overtone and warm; only a trace of dark skin/air contact noise (noise mix 0.10, pink, ~440 Hz LP, ~80 ms). PITCH GLIDE: small amplitude-dependent upward 'kerthump' that relaxes as it decays (tension modulation; Avanzini-Marogna-Bank JASA 2012; Tolonen-Välimäki-Karjalainen 2000) — captured by a short ToneShaper pitch env (lo 200→150 / hi 280→210 over ~20 ms, fast-drop curve) PLUS Membrane-only tensionModAmt 0.20 (~+0.4 st at full velocity). MATERIAL: single rawhide/synthetic head on a staved WOODEN barrel shell, screw-tensioned, single-headed (Wikipedia); the barrel body is modeled by enabling the passive secondary shell resonator at moderate coupling (strength 0.30, secondary size 0.45→~0.66·head_f0, woody short decay) for the woody head↔shell after-ring the brief calls for.

archetype: Conga Slap (world hand drum — sharp edge-strike "pop")
bodyModel: Membrane (Bessel circular drumhead, 48 modes) — a conga is a single-headed wood/fiberglass shell with a tensioned natural/synthetic head; its tone IS a circular membrane with inharmonic Bessel-zero partials. Body model index 1.0 norm decode = Membrane (offset 1 norm 0.0).
exciter: Impulse (raised-cosine click). The slap is a rigid, near-instantaneous hand/finger crack at the rim — a hard, broadband contact, not a soft beater (Mallet), bandpassed noise (NoiseBurst) or bowed/FM source. Impulse delivers the sharp wideband transient that excites the high edge modes. Exciter Type offset 0, norm 0.0.

params.rationale: The slap is a hard, near-instant rigid hand/finger crack on the rim — a wideband impulse, not a soft beater or filtered-noise burst. Impulse maximises high-edge-mode excitation for the sharp crack.

params.physicalTarget: Membrane (Bessel circular drumhead, 48 modes). idx=round(0.0*5)=0 → Membrane.
params.rationale: A conga's voice IS a tensioned circular membrane with inharmonic Bessel-zero partials. Only Membrane also unlocks airLoading + tension-mod, the membrane-specific physics this drum needs.

params.physicalTarget: Membrane brightness=0.55; baseDecayTime=lerp(0.15,0.8,0.55)*(1+0.1*size)≈0.51*size-factor; mild HF damping. Woody, slightly bright.
params.rationale: Skin-on-wood is woody but a hard slap reads brighter than the open tone; 0.55 keeps a wood character while letting upper modes ring just long enough for the crack body, before b3 damping. Matches the existing slap voicing.

params.physicalTarget: f0 = 500*0.1^0.5 ≈ 158 Hz head fundamental (mid-conga sized shell; edge strike pushes perceived centroid far above this).
params.rationale: Targets the conga's ~158–200 Hz body register. The edge strike suppresses the (0,1) mode so the audible event is high; the low f0 just supplies the underlying housing weight.

params.physicalTarget: decayTime = baseDecayTime*exp(lerp(ln0.3,ln3.0,0.18))≈baseDecayTime*0.40 — a short body tail (order ~100–150 ms).
params.rationale: The slap is choked by the hand: very short body sustain is its defining contrast vs the ringing open tone. Low Decay gives the abrupt, dry pop.

params.physicalTarget: r/a = 0.10*0.9 = 0.09 → near-edge Bessel mode sampling; emphasises high circumferential modes, suppresses central (0,1) mode.
params.rationale: A slap is struck right at the rim/edge. Near-edge sampling re-weights energy to the upper inharmonic partials → the bright cracking timbre that distinguishes slap from the centre-struck open tone.

params.physicalTarget: linear voice gain 0.85 before the transparent hardClip rail; per-pad loudness make-up.
params.rationale: A slap is the loudest, most cutting conga stroke — a touch above the 0.8 default so it pops above the open tones in a pattern.
No tone-shaper filtering needed; the body+click already deliver the bright slap. Default LP, left bypassed via cutoff 1.0.

params.physicalTarget: 20*1000^1.0 = 20000 Hz → filter BYPASSED (cutoff≥20k and env amount 0.5→0).
params.rationale: Transparent. The slap brightness comes from edge strike + click, not a post filter; bypassing keeps the chain clean and CPU-free.
Filter is bypassed; default Butterworth, no emphasis.

params.physicalTarget: amount=2*0.5-1=0 → no cutoff modulation, keeps filter bypassed.
params.rationale: No filter sweep wanted on a clean acoustic slap; 0.5 is the neutral no-op that preserves bypass.
An acoustic hand slap has no saturation character; post-M-2 Drive is flavour not level, and none is wanted. Exact dry.
No west-coast folding on a natural drum.

params.physicalTarget: 0 ms → pitch envelope DISABLED entirely (returns naturalFundamentalHz).
params.rationale: A choked slap has no audible 808-style glide; the small real tension rise is handled by tensionModAmt, not the pitch env. Disabling it keeps the attack honest. (Matches the existing slap pad, which sets no pitch env.)
Inert because PitchEnv Time=0; left at struct default.
Inert because PitchEnv Time=0; left at default.
Membrane already gives inharmonic Bessel ratios; the measured 1:2:3.5 conga inharmonicity is reached via modeScatter rather than global stretch. Neutral keeps the physical membrane spectrum.
No deliberate high/low decay tilt; the short overall Decay already does the work. Neutral.

params.physicalTarget: Zero-leak bypass (no synthetic harmonic series added).
params.rationale: A slap is inharmonic and transient; adding a tonal 1/k harmonic series would falsely pitch it. Exact bypass.
The crack is already maximally bright from the edge strike + click; no extra dynamic waveshaping needed. Exact bypass keeps the transient clean (post-audit M-3/M-4 redesign).

params.physicalTarget: Material Morph OFF; voice uses static material_.
params.rationale: A single short slap has no time-varying material sweep. Off.
Conga slaps don't mute each other like hi-hats; no choke routing.
Standard main-bus routing for a kit percussion voice.
All character is set explicitly on the underlying params; macro left neutral so it doesn't double-apply.
Brightness is delivered by the edge strike + bright click, not the macro. Neutral.
Size set explicitly; macro neutral.

params.physicalTarget: deltas: tsPitchEnvStart+=expDelta(+0.35,0.5), tsPitchEnvTime-=linDelta(+0.35,0.4), noiseBurstDuration-=linDelta(+0.35,0.3) → shorter exciter contact / sharper attack. (Pitch-env deltas inert while base Time=0.)
params.rationale: The brief asks for high macroPunch — it shortens the attack/contact for an extra-sharp slap crack. The pitch-env half is a safe no-op because PitchEnv Time stays 0; the contact-shortening is the audible effect. Matches the existing slap pad.
Complexity controlled explicitly; macro neutral.

params.physicalTarget: parallel filtered-noise layer at mix 0.15 (≈ a faint bright skin-contact 'pap').
params.rationale: Hand drums have a small broadband edge/skin noise on the slap, far less than a snare. Low mix adds bite without hiss.

params.physicalTarget: exp(ln40+0.7*(ln18000-ln40)) ≈ 4.5 kHz lowpass — sits the noise in the slap presence band.
params.rationale: Targets the 2–5 kHz / ~5 kHz slap presence so the noise reinforces the crack, not low-mid mud.
Mild emphasis so the noise band is defined but not whistly. Default-ish.
The slap edge noise is a quick 'pap', gone fast with the choked body. Short decay.
A bright slap crack wants white (not brown/pink dark) content in the presence band.

params.physicalTarget: always-on click transient at mix 0.85; half-amp into the exciter feed (drives body modes) + full into output (×0.445 standalone gain).
params.rationale: The brief calls for MAXIMAL click — the slap's identity is its sharp contact crack. 0.85 makes the transient the dominant feature.

params.physicalTarget: ms = 2 + 0.1*3 = 2.3 ms (velocity shortens further) — very brief burst.
params.rationale: A slap contact is near-instant; the shortest contact gives the tightest, most cracking tick. Matches the existing slap pad.

params.physicalTarget: exp(ln200+0.85*(ln12000-ln200)) ≈ 6.4 kHz bandpass center — sharp tick at the slap presence/pop frequency.
params.rationale: Centers the click crack on the ~5 kHz slap 'pop' band so it reads as a conga slap, not a dull thud.

params.physicalTarget: b1 = 0.2 + 0.45*49.8 ≈ 22.6 s^-1 flat damping — moderately tight decay floor; overrides Decay-derived flat damping.
params.rationale: Sets a firm RT60 floor so even the lowest modes die quickly, reinforcing the choked-slap dryness. (Controller writes the explicit value off the -1 sentinel.) Matches the existing slap pad (0.45).

params.physicalTarget: b3 = 0.1*1e-3 = 1e-4 s — light f^2 damping: highs ring slightly longer than a heavy-wood thump.
params.rationale: Keeps the bright edge modes alive through the short tail (a metal-ish low b3) so the crack stays bright, while the b1 floor still cuts the whole thing short. Matches the existing slap pad.

params.physicalTarget: Membrane low-mode frequencies depressed 40% of the way toward the Rossing air-loaded curve — mild deepening of the lowest Bessel modes.
params.rationale: A conga head is air-loaded but the slap suppresses the low modes anyway; modest 0.4 deepens the housing without over-flattening the bright edge response. Below the 0.6 default and matches the existing slap pad.

params.physicalTarget: ~4.5% sinusoidal frequency dither on modal ratios.
params.rationale: Raised per the brief and to push the too-ideal Bessel ratios toward the measured inharmonic 1:2:3.5 conga spectrum and add organic strike-to-strike variation. Matches the existing slap pad.

params.physicalTarget: secondary(shell) excitation = bodyOut*0.20, passively mixed in — a faint wood-shell housing ring under the head.
params.rationale: A conga's wooden/fiberglass shell adds a little body resonance even on a slap; light feedforward coupling supplies that housing weight. Engages only with Secondary Enabled on. Matches the existing slap pad.

params.physicalTarget: ON — spins up the 24-mode secondary shell bank at noteOn.
params.rationale: Enables the wood-shell housing resonator coupled to the head, for realistic conga body.

params.physicalTarget: sizeRatio = 1-0.4*0.75 = 0.70 → shell f0 ≈ 0.70*head_f0 — a shell tuned a little below the head.
params.rationale: Conga shells resonate just under the head fundamental; 0.4 places the housing in the right low-mid register. Matches the existing slap pad.

params.physicalTarget: shell b1 = max(25,2.5*bodyB1)+(1-0.4)*15 ≈ 56.5+9 s^-1; shell b3 = 0.4e-5 — a fairly damped woody shell.
params.rationale: Wood shell, not metal: moderately damped, short shell ring. Matches the existing slap pad.
The choked slap doesn't show the open-tone tension 'kerthump'; the hand damping kills the energy follower's ring. Off (and Membrane-only anyway). The existing slap pad also leaves it off.

params.physicalTarget: ON — pad auditions and allocates a voice.
params.rationale: The pad must sound.
No multi-segment glide on a slap; and the whole pitch env is disabled. Off.

params.physicalTarget: θ=0.42*π/2; gainL=√2·cos≈1.10, gainR=√2·sin≈0.89 — slightly left of center.
params.rationale: Congas sit a little off-center in a stereo kit image (player to one side). A gentle left placement gives life without hard panning; near-center keeps it mix-safe (audit M-9).

defaultedParams.why: Filter env is inert (Filter Env Amount neutral 0.5 → 0); the filter never sweeps, so attack time is a no-op.
Filter env disabled; decay time has no effect.
Filter env disabled; sustain level has no effect.
Filter env disabled; release time has no effect.
PitchEnv Time=0 disables the whole pitch envelope, so the segment-1 curve shape is inert; left at neutral 0.5.
Morph Enabled OFF; the start material value is never read.
Morph Enabled OFF; the end material value is never read.
Morph Enabled OFF; duration is inert.
Morph Enabled OFF; curve shape is inert.
FMImpulse exciter is not selected (Impulse is); FM ratio is a no-op.
Feedback exciter is not selected; feedback drive is a no-op.
NoiseBurst exciter is not selected; only nudged indirectly by Macro Punch as a generic attack proxy, but it drives no exciter here.
Friction exciter is not selected; bow pressure is a no-op.

Per-pad participation in the GLOBAL sympathetic-coupling engine; left at neutral 0.5 — only audible if the kit-level Global Coupling/Snare Buzz/Tom Resonance are raised, and a slap doesn't need cross-pad sympathy.
PitchEnv Knee OFF and Time=0; the mid breakpoint pitch is inert. Default 0.5.
PitchEnv Knee OFF and Time=0; the mid breakpoint position is inert. Default 0.5.
PitchEnv Knee OFF and Time=0; segment-2 curve is inert. Default 0.5.

physics: researched acoustic profile (see physics field of output)

archetype: Cowbell (world) — clapperless metal bell: agogo-family clangy inharmonic idiophone. Covers the CR-78/808 electronic cowbell, the vintage-wood-mounted cowbell, and the Latin hi/lo (cencerro) pair.
bodyModel: Bell — the post-audit church-bell Chladni body (16 modes, ratios 0.25/0.50/0.60/0.75/1.00/1.50/2.00…, hum at 0.25·f0). It is the only Membrum body whose ratio table is genuinely inharmonic-yet-tuned (a clangy minor-third/fifth cluster), which is exactly the cowbell's "detuned-fifth, beating, clangorous" spectrum. Plate/NoiseBody would give a noisy cymbal wash; String/Shell give a bar; only Bell gives the two-to-three dominant clangy partials over a short ring.
exciter: FMImpulse (primary recommendation) for the CR-78/808 and Latin variants — the Chowning bell-FM transient injects the metallic inharmonic edge and the characteristic "clang" attack the way the original 808's two beating square waves do; fmRatio in the 0.42–0.55 band lands the modulator:carrier at ~2.26–2.65, a detuned-fifth-ish inharmonic ratio matching the 808's 540/800 Hz (1:1.48) two-tone. Impulse is the alternate for the vintage-wood cowbell (a cleaner stick-on-metal contact, letting the Bell body's own partials dominate without added FM sidebands). Both are paired with a strong Click layer for the stick contact and a small noise halo.

params.rationale: FM injects the metallic inharmonic clang/edge that mirrors the 808's two beating detuned-fifth squares; primary cowbell exciter.
params.citation: acetateme 808 cowbell (540/800 Hz squares); SoS Synthesizing Cowbells

params.physicalTarget: idx=round(0.8·5)=4 → Bell (16-mode church-bell Chladni, hum at 0.25·f0)
params.rationale: Only body with a genuinely inharmonic-yet-tuned clangy partial cluster (0.25/0.5/0.6/0.75/1.0/1.5/2.0) — the cowbell's detuned-fifth beating spectrum.
params.citation: bell_modes.h kBellRatios; Rossing Science of Percussion ch.5

params.physicalTarget: Bell brightness=0.7+0.3·0.78≈0.93 (very metallic, low b3); base decay lerp(0.4,1.4,0.78)≈1.13 s before Decay mult
params.rationale: Cowbell is bright struck metal; high material keeps upper clang partials alive.
params.citation: bell_mapper.h; Wikipedia cowbell (metallic, noisier than handbells)

params.physicalTarget: f0_nominal=800·0.1^0.22≈471 Hz; clang partials at 1.5·=707, 2.0·=942 Hz
params.rationale: Puts the perceived pitch in the cowbell register and the upper partials across the 808's ~540–800 Hz clang region; lo variant 0.26–0.28 (≈410–383 Hz), agogo hi 0.14 (≈579 Hz).
params.citation: natural_fundamental.h Bell 800·0.1^size; acetateme 540/800 Hz; SoS 587/845 Hz

params.physicalTarget: decayTime ≈ base·exp(lerp(ln0.3,ln3,0.3)) ≈ base·0.46 → ~0.5 s realised, short dry ring
params.rationale: Cowbell decays fast and dry (hand/mount damping), unlike a long church bell; lo cowbell slightly longer (~0.40).
params.citation: SoS (decay stops abruptly, two-stage); Wikipedia (clapperless, damped by hand)

params.physicalTarget: Bell strike azimuth θ=0.3·(π/2)=0.47 rad; |cos(m·θ)| weights partials, mid-rim soundbow
params.rationale: A stick strikes the open lip/soundbow; mid azimuth keeps the principal clang partials present.
Cowbell is a loud accent but Bell body is hot post-N1; 0.75 leaves headroom under the −6 dBFS strike budget.
2.42–2.65 region gives the clangy metallic FM sidebands matching the 808's 1:1.44–1.48 two-tone; lo cowbell 0.42→2.26, agogo hi up to 0.55→2.65.
params.citation: acetateme 808 (540/800=1.48); SoS (587/845=1.44, 'small deviations destroy the illusion')

params.physicalTarget: mix 0.55 → stick-contact transient into exciter + output (×0.445 standalone)
params.rationale: Stick-on-metal 'tick' is the cowbell's defining attack; also seeds the body's clang impact.
params.citation: SoS (high-amplitude short impact stage); Roland RC attack-emphasis network

params.rationale: Hard, brief stick tick (short contact = sharp transient).
Bright metallic tick; coincides with the SoS ~2.64 kHz cowbell bandpass center.
SoS notes a low-level noise halo around the partials during impact; keep it small so the clang dominates.
params.citation: SoS ('halo of noise…particularly during the impact phase')

params.rationale: SoS specifies pink-ish noise for the cowbell impact halo.
Keeps the noise halo in the same upper-mid band as the clang partials/SoS bandpass.
Halo is an impact-phase transient, fades fast.
Sets the short, dry overall RT60 of the cowbell ring (overrides Decay's flat term); lo cowbell similar, slightly lower.

params.physicalTarget: b3=0 → pure flat damping, no f² high-mode roll-off (long-lived upper partials)
params.rationale: Metal: high partials decay WITH the body, not faster — keeps the clang bright through the ring.
params.citation: Chaigne&Askenfelt damping law; metallic bodies have low b3

params.physicalTarget: physical 0.5+0.55·1.5≈1.33 → modest stiff-body inharmonic stretch above the bell ratios
params.rationale: Pushes the partials further from any near-harmonic alignment → more clangy/dissonant, mimicking the beating detuned pair.
params.citation: audit §3-B stretch fix (1-indexed, B_max 0.01); 808 beating inharmonicity

params.physicalTarget: ~3% sinusoidal frequency dither on the modal partials
params.rationale: Real cast-metal cowbells are imperfect; small scatter adds the organic beating/clang without smearing pitch.
params.citation: modal_resonator_bank scatter; Wikipedia (cowbell noisier than handbells)

params.physicalTarget: phys=0.42·2−1=−0.16 → slight tilt favouring upper partials (ratio^0.16 boost on clang modes)
params.rationale: Cowbell energy sits in the bright clang partials more than the low hum; mild negative skew lifts them.
params.citation: bell_mapper M-5 per-mode tilt; SoS (partials near 2.6 kHz emphasised)

params.physicalTarget: +0.15 delta → tsFilterCutoff +0.06, modeInject +0.045 (opens tone, adds faint harmonic body)
params.rationale: Lifts the overall brightness to the clangy register without engaging the filter hard.
Bell body + click already carry the spectral shaping; no need to engage the SVF (default bypass).
Cowbell pitch is static — no 808-style glide (that's the kick/tom, not the cowbell).
params.citation: SoS/acetateme (cowbell is fixed-pitch two-tone, no glide)

params.rationale: Default center; kit-builder places hi/lo cowbell pair off-center if desired.
Cowbell is a one-shot accent, not a mute-grouped open/closed pair (agogo hi/lo can optionally share a group).
Pad is active.

params.physicalTarget: 0 → velocity-only (no-op: only Feedback exciter reads it)
params.rationale: Task specifies LOW feedback; with FMImpulse selected this is a no-op, kept at 0. Latin hi cowbell optionally 0.10 for a touch of edge.
params.citation: param dict (feedback no-op unless Feedback exciter)

defaultedParams.param: Filter Type / Resonance / Env (Atk/Dec/Sus/Rel) / Filter Env Amount
defaultedParams.why: ToneShaper SVF is bypassed (cutoff 1.0, env amount 0.5=0) — the Bell body + click already shape the clang; no post-filter needed.
Left at 0 (bypass): post-audit Drive is flavour-not-level and would muddy the clean clang; the cowbell's bite comes from FM + inharmonic modes, not saturation.
Pitch env is disabled (Time=0); the cowbell is a static-pitch idiophone with no glide, so every pitch-env sub-param is a no-op.
0 (exact bypass): the Bell ratio table already supplies the tonal partials; an added integer-harmonic series would dilute the inharmonic clang. Brightness macro adds a trace if wanted.
0 (exact bypass): cowbell has no audible amplitude-driven brightening sweep; keeping it off preserves the dry, consistent clang and bit-exact bypass.
Material morph off — the cowbell timbre is static within a hit; no intra-note material sweep.
No-op on Bell (membrane-only frequency correction); set 0 for clarity. Cowbell is not an air-loaded membrane.
Membrane-only and a no-op on Bell; cowbell has no energy-dependent pitch glide ('kerthump' is a tom feature).

defaultedParams.param: Coupling Strength / Secondary Enabled / Secondary Size / Secondary Material
defaultedParams.why: Head↔shell secondary bank off — a clapperless single-shell bell has no second coupled resonator to model; keeps the spectrum to the clang partials.
Left at 0.5 default (per-pad participation in the global sympathetic engine); only audible if the kit enables Global Coupling, which a cowbell kit typically leaves off.

defaultedParams.param: Tightness / Body Size / Punch / Complexity macros
defaultedParams.why: Neutral 0.5 (zero delta) — the explicit params above already place the cowbell precisely; macros stay neutral so presets remain editable from a known baseline. Only Brightness is nudged.
No-ops with FMImpulse selected (each reads only when its matching exciter is active); left at defaults.
0.2 default (~Q 1.2) is fine for the low-level pink halo; no resonant peak wanted on the noise.
0 (main bus) — standard routing; no per-cowbell aux send needed.

physics: REAL COWBELL ACOUSTICS: a clapperless steel/iron idiophone struck with a stick (Wikipedia: tuned cowbells span F4–C7 ≈ 350–2100 Hz; standard clapperless ones give a "single note with timbral variations," and "sound much noisier than handbells"). It is a metal shell so its modes are strongly INHARMONIC and clustered — perceptually two-to-three dominant clangy partials in a detuned-fifth relationship plus a noise halo, not a clean harmonic series. The decay is SHORT and dry compared to a church bell (the hand/mount damps it; the body is small and stiff) — on the order of 150–400 ms, with the hi cowbell shorter/brighter and the lo cowbell slightly longer/lower. Strong contact transient (stick-on-metal "tick") on the leading edge. THE ESTABLISHED ELECTRONIC SYNTHESIS (the iconic reference): Roland TR-808 / CR-78 cowbell = TWO square-wave oscillators in a detuned perfect-fifth — 540 Hz and 800 Hz (ratio 1:1.48, an out-of-tune fifth) per the 808 circuit (acetateme, Baratatronix); Sound On Sound's reverse-engineering puts the pair at ~587 Hz and ~845 Hz (ratio 1:1.44) and warns "even small deviations from these pitches destroy the cowbell illusion." Because the two tones are inharmonic they BEAT, producing the metallic dissonance. The summed pair is band-passed (SoS: BP centred ≈ 2.64 kHz, 12 dB/oct + mild resonance to accentuate partials near centre; the 808's BP cuts more low end) and gated with a TWO-STAGE amplitude envelope: a high-amplitude short "impact"/attack snap followed by a faster-than-natural clangorous ring-out (Roland used parallel RC networks to "emphasise attack"). SoS also notes a "halo of noise surrounding the partials, particularly during the impact phase," recreated with low-level pink-ish noise. NO pitch glide (unlike toms/kicks — the cowbell pitch is static). Latin/agogo family: the Brazilian agogô and Cuban cencerro are pairs of clapperless bells tuned a step/third apart (hi + lo), same clangy metal character. MAPPING TO MEMBRUM VALUES: cowbell perceived pitch ≈ 400–600 Hz nominal → Bell f0_nominal = 800·0.1^size, so size≈0.20→505 Hz, ≈0.22→471 Hz, ≈0.26→410 Hz, ≈0.14→579 Hz (agogo hi); the Bell ratio table then places hum at 0.25·f0 and clang partials at 1.5·/2.0·f0 (e.g. 471·1.5=707, 471·2.0=942 Hz — covering the 808's ~800 Hz upper tone). fmRatio 0.42–0.55 → modulator ratio 1+3·norm = 2.26–2.65 ≈ inharmonic detuned-fifth-family. Decay norm ~0.25–0.40 → ~0.3–0.6× the bell base, giving the short dry ring (NOT a long church bell). b3=0 (metal: no f² damping, partials sustain together); b1 moderate (~0.32, ~tight) for the short overall RT60. Brightness macro lifted + small noise/click for the impact halo and stick tick.

bodyModel: NoiseBody
exciter: NoiseBurst

params.rationale: A crash is excited by a broadband contact, not a clean click; NoiseBurst injects a short filtered-noise burst that launches the dense mode cluster + supplies initial wash. Matches every existing Membrum crash builder.

params.physicalTarget: idx=round(0.4*5)=2... NOTE per-pad clamps to enum; target enum index 5 = NoiseBody
params.rationale: NoiseBody = plate-ratio modal bank (32 modes, dense inharmonic Chladni spectrum) + internal noise layer = the canonical cymbal/hat body. Set the enum directly (BodyModelType::NoiseBody); on-wire selector idx 5.

params.physicalTarget: NoiseBody brightness=0.7+0.3*0.93=0.98 (very metallic, near-flat HF damping); body base decay lerp(0.2,1.0)=0.94; internal-noise cutoff clamp(1500+5000*0.93)=~6.1kHz; noise reso 0.6+0.4*0.93=0.97
params.rationale: Thin bronze cymbal: maximal metallic brightness + long bright ring. Acoustic kits ~0.90, electronic ~0.95. 0.93 is the per-archetype centre.

params.physicalTarget: f0 = 1500*0.1^0.35 = ~670 Hz body fundamental; mode table stacks the inharmonic cluster upward from there
params.rationale: A large thin crash has its lowest plate modes low (few hundred Hz) but the audible mass is the dense cluster above; 0.30-0.45 brackets small bright crash -> big dark crash. 0.35 = mid 18in crash.

params.physicalTarget: decayTime = baseDecay(0.94)*exp(lerp(ln0.3,ln3.0,0.7))*skew(1.0) = ~0.94*1.66 = ~1.56 s body ring; NoiseBody internal-noise decay 60ms*1.66=~100ms
params.rationale: Crash rings long; literature spans 50ms(HF)..several s(LF), practical ~0.75s+ wash. 0.55-0.85 brackets short stab crash -> long wash; 0.70 is a sustaining crash.

params.physicalTarget: computePlateAmplitude(k,0.55): off-centre edge-ish strike sweeping radius+azimuth, exciting many (m,n) families incl. high modes
params.rationale: A crash is struck near the edge with a glancing blow, exciting a broad, bright mode set (not the bell). Mid-high strikePos broadens the excited spectrum.

params.physicalTarget: linear voice gain 0.72 pre-rail; per-pad loudness make-up with master
params.rationale: Crashes are loud but the body+noise+click sum is hot; ~0.72 keeps the voice under the -1dBTP bus limiter without crushing. Matches existing crash builders (0.70-0.78).
Lowpass is the natural post-body tone tilt; bandpass character already comes from the noise layer. Mostly transparent here (cutoff high).
A crash wants ALL its bright top end; leave the ToneShaper filter open/bypassed so the wash is unfiltered. Brightness lives in the noise layer + material.
No resonant emphasis wanted on the post-body filter (it's bypassed anyway).

params.physicalTarget: amount = 0.5*2-1 = 0 -> no filter-cutoff modulation; keeps filter bypassed
params.rationale: The crash 'bloom' is generated by ModeInject/NonlinearCoupling, not a filter sweep; leave the ToneShaper filter env neutral.
Drive is flavour/odd-harmonics, not needed; the cymbal's harmonic richness comes from the dense modal bank + nonlinear coupling. Keep clean.
West-coast folding is alien to an acoustic crash; off.

params.physicalTarget: 0 ms -> pitch envelope DISABLED (body stays at natural f0)
params.rationale: A crash has no 808-style pitch drop; the only 'motion' is the spectral bloom (handled elsewhere). Master-disable the pitch env.

params.physicalTarget: physical = 0.5+0.6*1.5 = 1.4 (stretched, more inharmonic spacing); bank stretchNorm=(1.4-0.5)/1.5=0.6
params.rationale: Real cymbals are strongly inharmonic (modified Chladni (m+2n)^1.7); stretching the plate ratios past unity (1.0) pushes partials apart for a more metallic, less tuned spectrum.
Default-neutral; the desired HF-build is created by ModeInject/NonlinearCoupling + the bright noise tail, not by tilting modal decay. Leaving 0.5 keeps the bit-identical guard.

params.physicalTarget: 8 harmonic oscillators at f0(~670Hz), 1/k (-6dB/oct), amount 0.25 mixed pre-ToneShaper
params.rationale: Models the nonlinear HARMONIC GENERATION / spectral fill of a driven cymbal (Rossing: low-amp harmonics, medium-amp subharmonic fill) - adds density/'bloom' weight without a tuned pitch. Modest so it doesn't pitch the wash.

params.physicalTarget: drive=1+6*vel*0.35*env -> amplitude-driven recipSqrt waveshaping: louder hit = brighter, sustained
params.rationale: This IS the cymbal nonlinearity: hard strike -> broadband brightening that builds and rides the decay (Stowell tap=bell/hard=broadband; von Karman energy cascade). Velocity-sensitive crash bloom.
A crash rings freely; not a choked/open-closed pair (that's the hi-hat). Per-kit a user may add a choke; archetype default none.
Crashes are commonly routed to their own bus for separate reverb/overhead treatment; the existing electronic Crash uses outputBus 1. Set to 1.

params.physicalTarget: ms = 2+0.5*13 = ~8.5 ms violet-noise burst (velocity then scales 1.2..0.8)
params.rationale: The exciter contact burst that launches the bloom; mid-length gives a full broadband kick to the modes without a percussive 'tick'.

params.physicalTarget: parallel filtered-noise layer at mix 0.5 (*0.243 standalone gain) - the broadband WASH
params.rationale: The crash is noise-dominated; this always-on layer is the core sizzle/wash on top of the modal skeleton. 0.50-0.55 per existing builders.

params.physicalTarget: hz = exp(ln40 + 0.85*(ln18000-ln40)) = ~6.5 kHz lowpass on the noise layer
params.rationale: Crash energy sits high (mid/high metallic band ~3-12kHz); a high cutoff keeps the wash bright. 0.78-0.92 brackets warm acoustic -> bright electronic.
Low resonance keeps the noise broadband/unpitched on the forced-lowpass path; a real crash wash has no narrow peak.

params.physicalTarget: ms = exp(ln20 + 0.6*(ln2000-ln20)) = ~310 ms... noise env decay; long bright tail (release=*0.5)
params.rationale: The wash should outlast the strike (delayed-HF-onset / long sizzle). 0.55-0.70 per existing crashes; 0.6 = sustaining wash that matches the body ring.
Cymbal hiss is HF-weighted; violet (or high white) gives the brightest sizzle. Acoustic kits ~white(0.6-0.7), electronic ~violet(0.85-0.92).

params.physicalTarget: attack transient at mix 0.20 (half into exciter feed + full to output *0.445)
params.rationale: The stick contact 'thwack' is present but LOW vs the wash - a crash is bloom-dominated, not click-dominated. Existing builders use 0.20.
Short, ~1-3ms stick impact per the synthesis literature 'thwack'.

params.physicalTarget: hz = exp(ln200 + 0.82*(ln12000-ln200)) = ~5.4 kHz bandpass centre -> sharp bright tick
params.rationale: A metal-on-metal stick contact is bright; high bandpass centre gives a crisp attack edge. Existing crashes 0.70-0.85.

params.physicalTarget: set off-sentinel: b1 = 0.2+0.3*49.8 = ~15 s^-1 flat damping floor (overrides Decay-derived flat damping)
params.rationale: Gives a controlled, fairly long flat-damping floor so the metallic ring sustains; existing crashes pin b1 ~0.30-0.40 to keep the body from ringing infinitely while staying bright.

params.physicalTarget: set off-sentinel: b3 = 0*1e-3 = 0 -> PURE FLAT damping, no f^2 high-freq rolloff (maximally metallic, long highs)
params.rationale: Metal has negligible frequency-squared damping; b3=0 keeps the high modes ringing = the long bright cymbal shimmer. Every existing crash sets bodyDampingB3=0.0.

params.physicalTarget: ~9% sinusoidal frequency dither on the plate modes (passed to ModalResonatorBank)
params.rationale: Real cymbals have hundreds of irregular, non-exact modes; scatter breaks the table's regularity into a denser, more organic inharmonic cloud. 0.55-0.85 per existing crashes.

params.physicalTarget: 0 -> no Bessel-mode depression (NO-OP for NoiseBody anyway)
params.rationale: Air-loading is a Membrane-only frequency correction; explicitly 0 for the plate-based cymbal (matches all existing crash builders).

defaultedParams.why: Head<->shell secondary resonator is for kicks/shells (body weight); a free metal plate has no struck-shell coupling, so secondary stays disabled (Coupling Strength 0).

physics:
FUNDAMENTAL / PITCH: A crash cymbal is a large, thin, slightly-domed free-edge metal plate. It has NO definite pitch; its perceived "centre" sits broadband in the metallic region. The lowest plate modes lie in the low hundreds of Hz (electronic TV-holography studies resolve the principal axisymmetric/diametral modes from ~ a few hundred Hz upward) and the audible energy of a struck crash is a dense cluster of partials concentrated in the mid/high band (roughly 1-12 kHz of "wash", extending to 20 kHz). In Membrum NoiseBody, f0 = 1500*0.1^size: at size 0.35 -> ~670 Hz, at size 0.30 -> ~750 Hz, at size 0.45 -> ~530 Hz. The plate-ratio mode table then stacks an inharmonic spectrum UP from f0, and the parallel filtered-noise layer supplies the broadband wash above it. So a "low f0 + bright noise" pairing is physically correct: a tuned skeleton of plate modes feathered into noise.

PARTIAL / MODE STRUCTURE & INHARMONICITY: Cymbals are governed by a MODIFIED Chladni power law f(m,n) ~ C*(m+2n)^P (m nodal diameters, n nodal circles). Classical flat-plate gives P=2; measured cymbals fit empirical exponents ~1.6-1.73 (Rossing). ~300 modes have been observed in a 16-inch cymbal via electronic holography; >100 in crash cymbals. The spectrum is therefore EXTREMELY DENSE and strongly INHARMONIC - this is exactly what Membrum's post-audit Plate/NoiseBody table now models (free-plate Chladni (m+2n)^P with P=1.7, per-family degeneracy split), so modeScatter + high mode density read as a real cymbal rather than a tuned bar.

NONLINEARITY / "BUILD-THEN-DECAY" WASH: A cymbal driven hard is nonlinear (von Karman thin-shell). Rossing/Fletcher: low excitation -> harmonic generation; medium -> subharmonics fill the spectrum; high -> CHAOTIC, very dense broadband spectrum. The audible consequence is the signature CRASH "bloom": after the strike, energy CASCADES from the initially-excited lower modes UP into the high modes over a fraction of a second (delayed HF onset / "bandwidth buildup", Stowell). The brightness RISES then the whole thing decays. This maps to: ModeInject (synthetic harmonic fill) + NonlinearCoupling (louder=brighter, amplitude-driven HF brightening) + a long, bright noise wash.

PER-BAND DECAY (T60): Strongly frequency-dependent and LONG. Cymbal-mode measurements report decay times ranging from ~50 ms for the highest-frequency modes up to several seconds (literature cites up to ~8 s) for the lowest modes; radiation efficiency shortens low modes most. Practical synthesis (SOS/Stowell) targets an overall ~0.75 s crash decay with the LP-swept "wash" persisting ~2-5 s on big crashes. So: medium-long body decay, with the noise wash carrying a comparably long, bright tail.

TRANSIENT / CONTACT CLICK: The stick strike is a brief broadband contact impulse (~1 ms "thwack", Stowell) - bright but LOW level relative to the wash (a crash is mostly bloom, not click). So a short, bright, modest-level click.

NOISE CONTENT & SPECTRAL SHAPE: Dominant. The dense unresolved upper modes are perceptually noise. Synthesis universally uses bright HP/bandpass-filtered noise (white->violet tilt) as the core of the crash; the TR808 famously uses six enharmonic square waves + bandpass bands, but real/sample-based crashes are noise-dominated. -> bright, high-cutoff, white/violet noise layer with a long decay.

PITCH GLIDE: None of the 808 "kerthump" kind - a crash has no tension-modulation pitch drop. (tensionModAmt is Membrane-only and irrelevant here.) The only "glide" is the spectral-centroid RISE of the bloom, captured by NonlinearCoupling/ModeInject, not by the pitch envelope.

MATERIAL: Thin bronze/brass alloy (B20/B8) - very metallic, low internal damping -> long ring, bright. -> high Material, near-zero frequency-squared (b3) damping.

bodyModel: Bell (church-bell Chladni partials, 16 modes; ratios hum 0.25 / prime 0.5 / tierce 0.6 / quint 0.75 / nominal 1.0 / 1.5 / 2.0 / ... 12.0). Chosen because Membrum exposes no dedicated free-disc/crotale modal set; the Bell body is the only model whose UPPER spectrum is near-harmonic with a clear strong octave partial (ratio 2.0) and a long-ringing high pitch — which is exactly the crotale's defining behaviour (a strong (3,0) one octave above the (2,0) fundamental, plus a minor-7th-related (4,0)). Plate/NoiseBody give a free-plate Chladni cymbal that is too inharmonic/washy; Shell is a bar (glockenspiel); String is a waveguide. Bell + low/zero b3 = the cleanest match to "clear, long-ringing high pitch with a relatively harmonic upper spectrum."
exciter: Mallet (offset 0 = ExciterType enum 1). Crotales are struck with hard aluminium/bronze mallets on flexible rattan; the Mallet exciter is the softer-beater struck contact that drives the body cleanly without the violet-noise broadband of NoiseBurst or the self-oscillation of Feedback. NOTE: because the exciter is Mallet, FM Ratio (offset 32) is a hard no-op for this pad — it only matters for the FMImpulse exciter. The bright metallic "ting" is carried instead by the Click layer + the body's undamped highs (b3=0).

params.rationale: Crotales are struck with hard mallets; Mallet is the clean struck-beater contact (not NoiseBurst/Friction/Feedback).

Bell is the only Membrum body whose upper spectrum is near-harmonic with a strong octave partial + long highs — matches the measured crotale (2,0)/(3,0)/(4,0) structure better than the washy free-plate cymbal models.

params.physicalTarget: Bell brightness = 0.7+0.3*0.92 = 0.976 (very metallic); baseDecayTime = lerp(0.4,1.4,0.92)*(1+0.1*size) ≈ 1.32 s

params.physicalTarget: f0_nominal = 800*0.1^0.22 = 482 Hz nominal; octave partial ≈ 0.96 kHz
params.rationale: Hi/lo crotales are the SAME instrument at a lower pitch — only Size differs between the pair. ~0.6 octave below the hi pad.

params.physicalTarget: decayTime multiplier exp(lerp(ln0.3,ln3.0,0.85)) ≈ 2.13× → body ring ≈ 2.8 s before b1 floor
params.rationale: Struck crotale rings for several seconds (long resonance); 0.85 gives a long but not infinite tail.

params.physicalTarget: Bell strike azimuth theta=0.1*(pi/2)=0.157 rad; near a meridional antinode (all modes well excited)
params.rationale: A mallet strikes the disc near the rim/soundbow, exciting the full partial set incl. the strong octave; low azimuth keeps high-m partials present (bright).
Bright cutting instrument but a single tuned ting, not a kick; slightly below default leaves bus headroom for the long ring + the post-audit −6 dBFS body budget.

physics: FUNDAMENTAL/PITCH: a 2-octave crotale set is written C6-C8 (sounds ~1047-4186 Hz for the marked (2,0) mode; Wikipedia/Kolberg). Hi/lo pads are the SAME instrument at two pitches (a chromatic disc set), so they share every voicing param except Size. MODE STRUCTURE & INHARMONICITY: Deutsch, Ramirez et al. (JASA 116(4):2427-33, 2004) identify three acoustically important modes — (2,0)=fundamental, (3,0)≈2:1 above it (one octave, the strongest radiator), and (4,0)≈7:2 above the (2,0) (=3.5×), with (4,0)/(3,0)=7:4 (a minor 7th). An ideally tuned crotale has the (3,0) EXACTLY an octave above the (2,0). So the upper spectrum is RELATIVELY harmonic (a strong octave + a minor-7th-related partial), unlike a wash cymbal. Bronze-disc inharmonicity coefficient ~0.3-0.8 (general cymbal/bronze figure). DECAY/T60: properly-made discs sustain 8-12 s when bowed (Alibaba buying guide); a struck (mallet) crotale rings several seconds, with a strongly frequency-dependent decay — low/principal modes (f<500 Hz) 3-8 s, high modes decay fast (<0.5 s) — giving the characteristic bright attack settling to a pure long-ringing tone. ATTACK/CLICK: a hard mallet on thick bronze gives a short bright metallic contact tick (a few ms, several-kHz centroid) over the modal onset. NOISE CONTENT: essentially none — crotales are clean tuned idiophones (no rattle, no air noise), so the parallel noise layer is muted. PITCH GLIDE: none of significance (small thick disc, struck at moderate level stays in the linear regime; the dramatic gong/cymbal pitch-glide nonlinearity is a large-amplitude large-plate effect), so no tension mod / no pitch envelope. MATERIAL: hard 'bell bronze' alloy, ~10 cm disc, center-weighted/thickened for tuning (Wikipedia; Kolberg) → very metallic = very low frequency-squared damping = long upper-partial tails.\n\nDRIVEN PARAM CHOICES: Bell body + b3=0 (pure flat damping = metal, long highs) reproduces the long-ringing relatively-harmonic spectrum; b1 mid-low for the multi-second ring; Size small to push the nominal as high as the Bell ceiling (800 Hz) allows; Decay high (0.85); bright short Click for the mallet tick; noise muted; airLoading 0 (membrane-only, irrelevant on Bell); modeStretch left neutral (the Bell table's near-octave upper structure already matches the measured crotale (3,0)/(4,0) better than stretching it would).

archetype: Dholak (North-Indian double-headed barrel hand drum) — two pads: HI (treble/dāyāṅ-side) and LO (bass/bāyāṅ-side)
bodyModel: Membrane (offset 1 = 0.0). The dholak is a wood-shell barrel drum with two unloaded (or lightly masala-loaded) circular skin heads. Unlike tabla/mridangam (whose central syahi loading forces a near-harmonic 1:2:3 series), the dholak heads are essentially plain stretched membranes, so the correct model is Membrum's Bessel circular-membrane bank (48 modes, post-audit air-loaded ratios), NOT a Bell/Plate harmonic-resonator. Membrane is also the ONLY body where airLoading (Rossing air-loaded ratio morph) and tensionModAmt (energy pitch glide) are live — both essential for the dholak's deep skin tone and its hallmark bass "giss" slide.
exciter: Impulse (offset 0 = 0.0). A bare fingered/palm hand strike on a skin head is a short, fairly hard broadband contact — best matched by Membrum's raised-cosine Impulse click (sharper transient than Mallet, no bowed/FM/feedback character). The bare-hand contact "tick" is then shaped with the always-on Click layer (kept short and not-too-bright) rather than a separate beater exciter, and the skin/finger friction "shh" by the always-on Noise layer.

params.rationale: Bare fingered/palm hand strike on a skin head = short hard-ish broadband contact; Impulse is the cleanest transient backend (Mallet is too soft/beater-like). Contact softness is dialed in via the Click layer, not the exciter.
Plain (un-syahi) circular skin head = inharmonic Bessel membrane; only body where airLoading + tensionModAmt are live, both needed for skin depth and the bass giss.
params.citation: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vibrations_of_a_circular_membrane; Fletcher & Rossing, Physics of Musical Instruments

params.rationale: Goat-skin treble head: medium-bright, woody-skin tone, slightly brighter than the bass head.

params.physicalTarget: brightness 0.33; darker skin, longer base decay
params.rationale: Masala-loaded buffalo bass head is darker/duller (the tar/clay coating kills highs); lower material = warmer, rounder body.

params.physicalTarget: natural f0 = 500·0.1^0.55 ≈ 141 Hz (modal-ratio/decay scale; audible pitch set by pitch env)
params.rationale: Brief spec: smaller treble head. Size sets the Bessel-ratio spacing and ring-up; the struck pitch is driven by the pitch env (below).
Brief spec: larger bass head → lower base f0, longer ring-up (weaker b3·f² at lower freqs).

params.physicalTarget: decayTime multiplier exp(lerp(ln0.3,ln3,0.30))≈0.46× → short tight treble ring (~0.2 s)
params.rationale: Tight, quick treble 'tin' stroke. (Note: b1 override below sets the actual flat damping; Decay still feeds the cache/legacy law.)

params.physicalTarget: ≈0.69× multiplier → slightly longer bass ring (~0.35 s)
params.rationale: Bass head rings a touch longer and rounder than the treble; masala still damps it short of a tom.
Treble dholak strokes are near the rim (rim-finger technique) → edge strike emphasises higher Bessel modes = sharper tone.
params.citation: carvedculture.com / musicwala (right-hand strokes on/near the rim of the higher head)

params.rationale: Bass head is tapped/slapped near the resonant centre → favours the fundamental, deep round thud.
params.citation: musicwala (left-hand near the resonant centre of the lower head)

params.rationale: Default per-pad loudness; pre-limiter linear attenuation, balanced against the LO pad and master.
Bass head reads slightly louder/fuller in the mix (low-frequency weight).
No post-body filtering wanted; the body+layers already carry the skin timbre. Default-bypass keeps the chain transparent.
Bass head: gentle top roll-off to mimic the dull masala coating (filter now engaged because cutoff < 20 kHz).
Masala = a low-pass tone control on the bass head.
Brief spec: treble head starts at 320 Hz. Pitch env rescales the whole modal bank to this start f0 (audit H-3).
Brief spec: treble settles to 220 Hz — the small downward inflection of a rim stroke.
