Brief spec: bass head starts at 220 Hz.
Brief spec: bass settles to 140 Hz — the audible 'giss' downward slide.
Slower, longer bass slide than the treble — the heel-of-palm pitch bend takes audible time.
Skin-tension pitch decays fast then settles — exponential-style segment-1 (kit-default shape).
A skin membrane is governed by its natural Bessel ratios; no extra stiffness/dispersion wanted (the inharmonicity is the air-loaded Bessel series, set via airLoading).

params.physicalTarget: phys 2·0.58−1 = +0.16 → mild tilt of energy/decay toward low modes
params.rationale: Treble: slight roundening so the upper partials don't over-ring (skin, not cymbal).
Bass head: energy concentrated in the fundamental, fast-decaying highs (masala damping). Per-mode ratio^(−skew) tilt now active on Membrane.
Harder treble strokes get slightly brighter (skin nonlinearity); kept low so it stays a flavour, not a compressor.
Bass head shows more amplitude-dependent timbre change (the slapped membrane stiffens under hard hits).
Bare-hand contact adds a little broadband contact noise; kept well below the modal body.
Masala bass head is more of a pure thud; less skin hiss.
Low-mid contact 'shh', not a bright hiss.
Darker contact noise for the bass head.
Contact noise is a brief attack artefact, not a sustain.
Skin/finger contact reads as a pink-ish broadband tick.
Bass head: darker brown-noise thud body.
Bare-hand attack definition; routed half into the exciter feed so it also drives the body modes.
Palm/slap on the bass head is softer than a fingertip rim stroke.
Short fingertip contact (a crisp rim 'tin').
Softer, slightly longer palm contact.
Finger/rim 'tup' has mid-high click energy.

params.rationale: Sets the treble head's overall short RT60 directly (overrides the Decay-knob derivation).
Bass head rings slightly longer (lower flat damping) for body weight.
Woody, not metallic: upper partials die faster than the fundamental (Chaigne loss law).
Masala bass head is duller; highs damped harder → rounder thud.

params.physicalTarget: 45% morph from pure Bessel toward Rossing air-loaded ratios
params.rationale: Real skin head over a barrel cavity depresses the low modes toward 1:1.5:2:2.4 — deeper, less whistly than an ideal membrane, but not a fully air-loaded timpano.
Larger bass head + masala loading + enclosed barrel air → deeper, more harmonic-leaning low modes than the treble head.
Hand-stretched skin is never perfectly uniform; a little detune dither makes successive hits feel organic.

params.physicalTarget: 0.30 feedforward drive into the secondary shell
params.rationale: Brief: 'coupling with secondary.' Adds the wooden barrel body weight under the treble head.
Bass head couples more strongly to the shell/air cavity (it is the resonant centre of the barrel).
Engages the barrel-shell resonator that gives the dholak its woody body; required for Coupling Strength to do anything.
Shared barrel shell sits a fifth-ish below the treble head (Chromaphone ~0.6 rule).
Same physical barrel, referenced to the lower bass-head f0 → deeper shell tone.

params.physicalTarget: woody shell damping (shell b1 raised, b3 = 0.35e-5)
params.rationale: Sheesham/mango wood barrel = a darkish, fairly damped shell, not a ringing metal shell.

params.physicalTarget: effective ≈ 0.16·3.3e-5 energy scale → ~+0.4 semitone at full velocity
params.rationale: Mild louder-hits-bend-up tension nonlinearity of a tensioned goat skin (Membrane-only).
The bass head is the one players bend most (heel-of-palm 'giss'); stronger energy-dependent upward glide on top of the downward pitch env.

params.rationale: Two heads of one drum placed slightly apart in the stereo image (treble side a touch right).
Bass side a touch left — restores a real two-headed stereo image without hard panning.

defaultedParams.why: SVF is bypassed on HI and used only as a gentle masala roll-off on LO — no resonant peak wanted on a skin drum.
No filter-cutoff motion in a hand drum; the pitch glide (not a filter sweep) carries the dynamic timbre.
Bare-hand skin tone has no saturation character; leave the flavour saturator off (exact dry bypass).
Wavefolding is a west-coast synth timbre, alien to an acoustic skin drum.
The giss is a single smooth glide; a two-segment knee adds nothing and the single Start→End segment models it correctly.
Material is static per hit; the dynamic timbre is the pitch glide + nonlinear coupling, not a per-note material sweep.
Adding a synthetic 1/k harmonic series would impose tabla-like tonal harmonicity the plain dholak head does not have; the air-loaded Bessel modes are the authentic spectrum.
Hand-drum heads ring freely and are not mute-grouped like an open/closed hat.
Both pads go to the main bus; no multi-out routing needed for a two-pad world drum.

defaultedParams.param: FM Ratio / Feedback Amount / NoiseBurst Duration / Friction Pressure (32,33,34,35)
defaultedParams.why: No-ops for the selected Impulse exciter (these only bind to FMImpulse/Feedback/NoiseBurst/Friction respectively).

Left at 0.5 default; the kit has no global cross-pad sympathetic engine (snareBuzz/tomResonance = 0), so per-pad participation is inaudible — the head↔shell weight comes from the per-pad Secondary (offset 54/55) instead.
Left neutral at 0.5 (zero delta) — every underlying param is set explicitly, so the one-knob macros must add no hidden offset.
Contact noise is broadband; a resonant noise peak would add an unnatural pitched whistle.
Both dholak pads on (1.0).

physics:
FUNDAMENTAL / PITCH. The dholak is a barrel drum with a small goat-skin TREBLE head (~5.5–8 in) for sharp high notes and a larger buffalo-skin BASS head (~7.5–10 in) for low notes; the bass head is coated with a tar/clay/sand "masala" (or mustard-oil syahi) that lowers its pitch and gives a defined tone (en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dholak; india-instruments.com; carvedculture.com). There is no published exact-Hz table for dholak heads, so I anchor pitches to the size of the heads and to the GM "hi/lo" pad convention: the brief's targets — TREBLE struck pitch ~220–320 Hz, BASS ~140–220 Hz — sit correctly an octave or so above a kick and below a snare/tom, consistent with a hand drum of these head diameters and with the brief's own pitchEnv spec.

MODE / PARTIAL STRUCTURE & INHARMONICITY. A plain (unloaded) circular membrane is strongly INHARMONIC: ideal-membrane partials follow the Bessel-zero ratios 1.000, 1.594, 2.136, 2.296, 2.653, 2.918, 3.156, 3.501, 3.600, ... (J_m zeros; en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vibrations_of_a_circular_membrane). Real drumheads are air-loaded, which depresses the lowest axisymmetric modes toward a more nearly-harmonic 1 : 1.5 : 2 : 2.4 : 2.9 series (Rossing's timpani measurements; Fletcher & Rossing, The Physics of Musical Instruments). The dholak is between the two: a barrel cavity + a lightly-loaded bass head pull the low modes partway toward harmonicity but it is NOT a tuned tabla. Membrum's post-audit Membrane mapper does exactly this morph via airLoading (membrane_mapper.h: scale = 1 + airLoading·(targetScale[k]−1) onto kAirLoadingTargetScale). I use a MODERATE airLoading so the heads read as "deep skin drum" not "whistly ideal membrane," and add a touch of modeScatter for organic skin imperfection.

DECAY / T60. Hand-drum heads ring shortly and the bass-head masala damps further; perceived decay is on the order of a few hundred ms (treble shorter and tighter, bass slightly longer and rounder). In Membrum this is the modal flat damping b1 plus the b3·f² high-frequency damping (Chaigne & Askenfelt loss law, confirmed in audit §3-A). I set a short-ish per-mode flat damping (b1 override) and a modest woody b3 so highs die faster than the fundamental — the characteristic "tup/tun" of a skin drum rather than a metallic ring. decaySkew biases energy/decay toward the low modes (rounder body, less ring on the upper partials).

ATTACK / CONTACT CLICK. Bare-hand/fingered strike = a soft-to-medium broadband contact transient (a few ms), much softer than a stick. Modelled with a short Click layer at a low-ish bandpass centre (a finger "tup", not a stick "tick"). Treble gets a slightly brighter/shorter click than bass.

NOISE CONTENT. Skin + finger contact adds a brief low-mid "shh"; the masala bass head adds a duller thud noise. Modelled with a small Noise-layer mix (pink/brown tilt, low cutoff, short decay) — present but well below the body, consistent with the audit's −18 dBFS layer budget so the modal skin tone dominates.

PITCH GLIDE ('giss'/'gissa'). The dholak's signature: the BASS head is bent in pitch by heel-of-palm pressure and by the masala, producing a downward/▽ sliding "giss" (en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dholak; blogv.it.com 'How is Dholak Played'). Two complementary mechanisms model this: (1) a per-hit ToneShaper pitch ENVELOPE (start above, settle below) that rescales the whole modal bank — the audible struck pitch — bigger drop on the bass head; and (2) tensionModAmt, the Membrane-only energy-dependent UPWARD tension glide (Avanzini-Marogna-Bank 2012; rebuilt onto modal energy in audit N-1) that adds the louder-hits-bend-up nonlinearity of a real tensioned skin. The bass head gets more of both.

HEAD↔SHELL COUPLING. A barrel drum's two heads are coupled through the enclosed air/shell; the secondary (shell) resonator with moderate coupling adds the woody barrel body weight under each hit — the brief's requested "coupling with secondary."

archetype: Djembe (world) — West-African goblet hand drum; deep bass at center + sharp tone/slap at the rim. Bass + Slap variants.
bodyModel: Membrane (body model 0 / norm 0.0). Bessel circular drumhead, 48 modes. This is the only correct choice: the djembe head is a goatskin circular membrane and its bass/tone/slap distinction is purely a strike-position / mode-shape phenomenon on that membrane (the (0,1) low mode for bass at center vs. the (2,1),(0,2),(3,1)... cluster for slap at the rim). Membrane is also the ONLY body that consumes airLoading (the Rossing air-mass frequency correction that deepens the low modes) and the ONLY body that drives tensionModAmt — both physically apt for a hand drum. NOT Plate/Shell/Bell (metallic) and NOT String (waveguide).
exciter: Impulse (exciter 0 / norm 0.0). A raised-cosine click is the right model for a bare-hand strike on a tensioned skin: the hand makes brief, near-impulsive contact and the timbre comes from WHERE it strikes (mode-shape sampling), not from a beater spectrum. Mallet would soften the attack (felt beater) and NoiseBurst/Friction/FM/Feedback are for snares/bowed/metallic/squeal characters — wrong for a hand drum. Articulation is carried by Strike Position + the always-on Click layer (hand-contact transient), not by switching exciters.

params.rationale: Hand contact is near-impulsive; timbre comes from strike position, not a beater spectrum.
Djembe head is a goatskin circular membrane; bass/tone/slap are strike-position mode selections on it.

params.physicalTarget: woody/low-brightness end: membrane baseDecayTime≈lerp(0.15,0.8,0.30)=0.345 s scaled by (1+0.1·size); brightness=0.30 (warm, fewer highs)
params.rationale: Bass voice wants a warm thump, not a bright skin; thinner/looser skin character. Slap variant overrides higher for edge brightness.

params.physicalTarget: f0 = 500·0.1^0.78 ≈ 83 Hz body fundamental (large goblet drum)
params.rationale: Lands the natural body fundamental right on the djembe bass band (65–80 Hz). 0.1-decade size law puts the deep center thump in range.

Djembe bass is a damped 'boom' (head re-contacted/relaxed), not a long sustain; medium decay. NOTE: bodyDampingB1 (offset 50) is set, so this primarily feeds the legacy/cache path while b1 sets the actual flat-damping floor.

params.physicalTarget: r/a = 0.50·0.9 = 0.45 — mid-to-toward-center strike → emphasizes the low (0,1)/(1,1) Bessel modes, suppresses high-order modes
params.rationale: THE key articulation axis. Center strike = bass: the bass technique strikes the center with the full palm to excite the (0,1) low mode and suppress higher modes. The slap variant drops this to 0.10 (rim).

params.physicalTarget: linear 0.85 pre-limiter; loud bass element make-up
params.rationale: Bass is the loudest, most foundational hit in a djembe kit; slightly hotter than the 0.8 default.

params.physicalTarget: 20·100^0.4356 ≈ 150 Hz — start of the bass pitch drop
params.rationale: Synthesizes the djembe bass 'thump' onset: the strike initially rings higher then settles. (Membrum models the bass as a low membrane f0 + downward glide, not a literal Helmholtz cavity.)

params.physicalTarget: 20·100^0.3148 ≈ 85 Hz — settle frequency, on the djembe bass band
params.rationale: Settles onto the deep bass note (65–80 Hz region); end pitch ≈ the body's natural f0 so the glide is the perceived 'boom'.

params.physicalTarget: 0.05·500 = 25 ms glide (ENABLES the pitch env; 0 would disable it)
params.rationale: Short downward glide = the percussive 'thump' contour; fast enough to read as attack pitch, slow enough to be felt.

params.physicalTarget: curveAmount = 2·0.15-1 = -0.7 — fast initial drop, classic exponential pitch decay
params.rationale: Real strike pitch falls fast then levels; exponential-style segment matches a struck-membrane glide better than linear.

params.physicalTarget: 0.65 along pure-Bessel→Rossing air-loaded curve; depresses the low (m,1) modes toward the near-harmonic 1:1.5:2:2.44:2.9 series
params.rationale: Air-mass loading of the head over the goblet cavity deepens and orders the low modes — exactly the 'deep, less whistly' djembe bass. Membrane-only param.
A hand-skinned goatskin head is not perfectly uniform; mild detune dither gives organic, non-synthetic mode spread.

params.physicalTarget: b1 = 0.2 + 0.30·49.8 ≈ 15.1 s^-1 flat damping (medium RT60 floor)
params.rationale: Sets the overall ring length directly (overrides Decay's flat-damping derivation); 15 s^-1 ≈ a controlled bass boom, not a long sustain.

params.physicalTarget: b3 = 0.10·1e-3 = 1e-4 s — light f^2 damping (highs ring a bit, not pure-wood thump)
params.rationale: Goatskin has moderate high-frequency loss; low b3 keeps some upper-mode life for the tone/overtone content without metallic sheen.

params.physicalTarget: energy-dependent upward pitch jump, clamp ~+3 st; relaxes as the body decays (membrane-only)
params.rationale: A hard hand-strike momentarily raises skin tension → a brief pitch lift on the loudest hits, the subtle djembe 'crack-then-settle'. Membrane-gated.

params.physicalTarget: feedforward drive 0.40 from head into the secondary (shell) bank; passive additive mix
params.rationale: The goblet WOOD SHELL has bell/wineglass modes that couple to the head and add body weight to the bass — the secondary shell models that.

params.physicalTarget: ON — spins up the 24-mode secondary shell bank
params.rationale: Required to hear the shell coupling; the carved hardwood goblet shell is acoustically significant to the bass.

params.physicalTarget: shell f0 = head_f0·(1-0.5·0.75)=0.625·f0 ≈ 52 Hz — a large, low shell
params.rationale: The long-necked goblet shell resonates below the head; ~0.6× the head f0 (Chromaphone-style rule) places it as a sub-body.

params.physicalTarget: woody shell: b1≈max(25,2.5·bodyB1)+(1-0.30)·15, b3=0.30·1e-5 (dark, fairly short)
params.rationale: Hardwood shell is woody and damped, not metallic; low material keeps it dark and supportive.

params.physicalTarget: always-on hand-contact transient at 0.40·kStandaloneGain(0.445); half into the exciter feed too
params.rationale: Models the soft slap of palm-on-skin contact for the bass — present but not sharp (bass is dull-attacked).
A full-palm bass strike is a slightly longer, softer contact than a fingertip slap.

params.physicalTarget: bandpass center = exp(ln200+0.40·(ln12000-ln200)) ≈ 870 Hz — dull thud
params.rationale: Bass attack is a low, dull palm thud, not a sharp tick; keep the click dark.
A little broadband 'air'/skin-friction breathiness under the bass; low, so the tonal body dominates.
Keeps the noise low/dark, matching the bass character.
Pink is the natural broadband texture of a struck skin; not bright (white/violet).
Short skin-friction puff, decays with the attack.
Mild, no pitched peak on the lowpass noise path.
The bass anchors the kit center; spread comes from other pads.

params.physicalTarget: adds +linDelta(0.85,0.40) to size norm, +0.20·delta to modeStretch, +0.20·delta to decay
params.rationale: Pushes the voice bigger/lower/longer as a one-knob 'this is the BIG drum' macro on top of the explicit size 0.78.

defaultedParams.why: ToneShaper SVF left transparent (cutoff norm 1.0 = bypass, env amount 0.5 = no mod): the djembe timbre is set by the body modes + air loading, not a post filter.
0 = exact bypass; a clean acoustic hand drum has no saturation/wavefolding character.
Single-segment 150→85 Hz glide is sufficient for the bass thump; no need for a 3-point envelope.
Left at neutral norm 0.333 (physical 1.0): a real membrane keeps its natural Bessel inharmonicity — no extra stiffness/stretch wanted.
Neutral norm 0.5 (no skew): the air-loaded mode balance is already correct; no spectral tilt needed.
0 = exact bypass: the membrane already provides the full mode set; a synthetic harmonic series would make it sound electronic, not acoustic.
0 = exact bypass: a hand djembe is fairly linear at musical levels; tension-mod already supplies the loud-hit pitch effect.

defaultedParams.param: Morph Enabled / Start / End / Dur / Curve (25–29)
defaultedParams.why: Material morph off: the timbre is static within a hit; no per-note material sweep for a natural drum.
0 = no choke: djembe voices ring out freely, not a hi-hat-style mute group.
0 = main bus: standard routing, no aux send needed.

defaultedParams.param: FM Ratio (32) / Feedback Amount (33) / NoiseBurst Duration (34) / Friction Pressure (35)
defaultedParams.why: No-ops for the Impulse exciter; only the selected exciter's secondary params apply.
Left at default 0.5 cross-pad sympathetic weight: inaudible unless kit-level Global Coupling is engaged; distinct from the per-pad head↔shell Coupling Strength.
Neutral 0.5: the explicit per-param values already voice the drum; macros left as user performance handles. (Slap variant raises Punch.)
1.0 = on: the pad is part of the kit.

physics:
FUNDAMENTAL / PITCH: The djembe produces three distinct sounds by strike position, not by retuning. BASS = 65–80 Hz, played by striking the CENTER with the full palm, exciting the low (0,1) membrane mode (and coupling to the goblet-shell Helmholtz/wineglass resonance ~75 Hz); the bass pitch is set by shell size/shape and is largely tension-independent. TONE = 300–420 Hz, fingers near the edge, emphasizing the (1,1) and low modes. SLAP = 700–1000 Hz with overtones beyond 4 kHz, fingertips/palm-edge at the rim, emphasizing the (2,1),(0,2),(3,1),(1,2),(0,3) and higher modes while SUPPRESSING the Helmholtz bass and (0,1)/(1,1). So strike position is the master articulation axis (Wikipedia/Djembe; JASA djembe modal analysis 2000).

MODE STRUCTURE & INHARMONICITY: circular goatskin membrane → Bessel-zero ratios (1, 1.59, 2.14, 2.30, 2.65, ...). Air-mass loading over the closed goblet cavity depresses the low (m,1) modes toward the near-harmonic timpani-like series 1:1.5:2:2.44:2.90 (Rossing 1982), making the bass 'deeper, less whistly'. The carved hardwood shell adds bell/wineglass modes that couple to the head (researchgate djembe modal analysis). → Membrum mapping: Membrane body f0=500·0.1^size puts size 0.78 at ~83 Hz (bass band); airLoading 0.65 applies the Rossing low-mode correction; secondary shell bank (Coupling 0.40, Secondary on, size 0.50→~0.625·f0) supplies the shell coupling.

DECAY / T60: djembe is a damped hand drum — the bass is a short 'boom', the slap a sharp short crack; no long metallic sustain. Mapped via Decay 0.45 + explicit bodyDampingB1≈15 s^-1 (medium flat-damping floor) and bodyDampingB3=1e-4 (light HF loss, goatskin). Slap variant shortens decay to 0.20.

TRANSIENT / CLICK: a bare-hand strike = brief raised-cosine impulse; articulation in the contact transient. Bass = dull palm thud (Click brightness ~870 Hz, contact ~2.7 ms, mix 0.40). Slap = sharp fingertip tick (Click brightness up to ~5.6 kHz, contact ~2 ms, mix 0.85).

NOISE: modest broadband skin/air friction (pink, low mix 0.18, lowpassed ~850 Hz) — the slap adds more rim-snap brightness but the body modes dominate.

PITCH GLIDE: TWO mechanisms. (1) An attack 'thump' contour synthesized as the body pitch envelope 150→85 Hz over 25 ms (exp curve) — Membrum retunes the modal f0, the analog of the bass settling onto the cavity resonance. (2) tensionModAmt 0.22 — a hard strike momentarily tightens the skin → a brief energy-dependent upward pitch lift (≤+3 st) that relaxes as the body decays (Avanzini-Marogna-Bank 2012), membrane-gated. Material: goatskin head on a single-log hardwood (lenke) goblet shell → warm, woody (Material 0.30, low b3, woody secondary material 0.30).

bodyModel: Plate (BodyModelType=1, free-plate Chladni (m+2n)^1.7 inharmonic metallic spectrum) as the primary recommendation; Shell (BodyModelType=2, free-free Euler-Bernoulli bar 1:2.757:5.404:8.933… ) as the documented "boxy/bar" sibling. Both are correct post-audit; the recipe gives Plate baseline values and notes the Shell deltas.
exciter: Feedback (ExciterType=5). Energy-limited self-oscillating body↔exciter loop (FeedbackExciter): SVF-lowpass + TanhADAA + DC-block + RMS energy limiter, effective drive = velocity + feedbackDrive·(1−velocity), feedbackAmount_ = drive·kMaxFeedback(0.85). Selecting Feedback also forces the body's per-sample (slow) path and keeps the in-loop soft-clip — this is the regenerative engine that turns a struck body into a sustained drone.

params.rationale: The regenerative self-oscillating loop is the entire archetype; it turns a struck body into a sustained drone and forces the per-sample body path.
params.citation: FeedbackExciter (feedback_exciter.h); E-bow/Barkhausen self-oscillation (modwiggler resonant-filter, ncbi PMC10006876 self-excited bandpass)

params.physicalTarget: idx=round(0.2·5)=1 → Plate (free-plate Chladni). Use 0.4→Shell for the boxy free-free-bar variant.
params.rationale: Free-edged metallic plate = correct inharmonic cymbal/gong spectrum (post-audit (m+2n)^1.7 Chladni); the regenerative loop rings on its dense inharmonic lattice.
params.citation: plate_modes.h kPlateRatios; auditory.org 5pMU8 (Chladni f=C(m+2n)^P); Rossing & Peterson 1982

params.rationale: High material = bright metallic ring with long base decay — the shiny, sustaining bronze character.
Low/mid fundamental = a felt metallic hum (small-cymbal / large-gong-partial band), not a screech; drone wants weight.
params.citation: plate_mapper.h f0=800·0.1^size; cymbal range ~100 Hz–5 kHz, gong f0 65–800 Hz (mosicocymbal; auditory.org)

params.physicalTarget: decay multiplier=exp(lerp(ln0.3,ln3.0,0.92))≈2.7× → plate decayTime ≈ 1.39·1.05·2.7 ≈ 3.9 s body ring
params.rationale: Metallic plates ring multi-second; a long body decay means the feedback loop only has to top up a slow-leaking resonator → smooth sustained drone.
params.citation: plate_mapper.h decay exp-map; gong T60 2–15 s, tam-tam >40 s (mosicocymbal)

params.physicalTarget: Plate amp = cos(m·θ)·cos(n·π·ρ), ρ=0.40+0.50·0.45=0.625, θ=0.45·π/2 — mid-radius, balanced mode excitation
params.rationale: Mid strike feeds a broad, balanced set of (m,n) modes so the loop has many partials to sustain (richer drone than an edge or center node).

params.physicalTarget: linear pre-rail gain 0.62 (≈ −4.2 dB) before transparent hardClip
params.rationale: A continuous drone runs hot; trimming below 0.8 leaves bus headroom for stacking and keeps the TruePeakLimiter off — Level is now a true linear attenuation (audit H-1/H-4).
params.citation: drum_voice.h linear voice + TruePeakLimiter (AUDIT gain-staging appendix)

params.rationale: A bandpass in the regenerative loop selects/sweeps which modal band sustains — the classic self-oscillating-filter drone topology; gives an evolving band rather than a static squeal.
params.citation: voice_pool.cpp tsFilterType decode; band-pass mode selection in self-excited oscillators (ncbi PMC10006876)

params.physicalTarget: hz=20·1000^0.55 ≈ 743 Hz BP center (just above f0, on the lower partials)
params.rationale: Centers the sustained band on the low/mid inharmonic partials — darker, boxy/metallic drone core that the filter ADSR then sweeps.
params.citation: voice_pool.cpp hz=20·1000^cutoff; bandpass darkens while preserving character (Valhalla plates-vs-chambers)

params.rationale: High-Q bandpass narrows the sustained band → a focused, singing resonant peak in the drone (regenerative emphasis) without full self-oscillation of the SVF itself.

params.physicalTarget: amount=2·0.65−1=+0.30 → up to +0.9 octave upward cutoff sweep (2^(3·0.30·env))
params.rationale: A slow upward filter sweep makes the drone EVOLVE — opening into the high inharmonic partials as it sustains, mimicking the gong energy-cascade shimmer growth.
params.citation: voice_pool.cpp amount=2x−1, modCutoff=cutoff·2^(3·amount·env); gong HF cascade builds post-strike (Fletcher 1999)

params.rationale: Slow-ish attack on the filter sweep = the drone blooms open rather than snapping — matches the E-bow/feedback swell, not a strike.
Moderate decay so the band falls back from its sustain peak slowly, keeping motion alive.
High sustain holds the swept band open for the life of the held drone — the filter stays part-way through its sweep, not fully closed.
Long release lets the band glide down as the loop de-energizes on note-off — a graceful drone fade.

params.physicalTarget: physical=0.5+0.45·1.5=1.175 → mild extra inharmonic stretch beyond the body's native ratios
params.rationale: Pushes the (m+2n) lattice slightly more inharmonic — the hand-hammered, never-quite-degenerate cymbal/gong fingerprint that keeps the drone from sounding like a tuned bell.
params.citation: voice_pool.cpp phys=0.5+1.5x; stiff-string/plate B inharmonicity (audit §3-B, Russell PSU)

params.physicalTarget: phys=2·0.78−1=+0.56 → per-mode tilt ratio^(−0.56) boosts HIGH partials; global skewBias=1−0.3·0.56=0.83
params.rationale: Tilts sustained energy toward high modes = the bright shimmer / high-frequency energy-cascade signature of a struck gong/cymbal; now applied to all bodies (audit M-5).
params.citation: plate_mapper.h decaySkew per-mode tilt; gong energy cascade to HF (Fletcher 1999; mosicocymbal shimmer)

params.rationale: An inharmonic metallic drone must NOT have a synthetic integer-harmonic series layered on top; that would pull it toward a tonal/pitched body and fight the inharmonic feedback character.

params.physicalTarget: env-level waveshaping: drive=1+6·vel·0.55·env → louder loop = harder recipSqrt = more odd harmonics, sustained
params.rationale: The amplitude→brightness brightening is the cheap literature-grounded stand-in for the cymbal/gong nonlinear energy cascade; heavy here so the drone breathes and brightens with loop energy.
params.citation: nonlinear_coupling.h env-level redesign (AUDIT M-3/M-4); cymbal nonlinear vibration/energy cascade (Fletcher 1999, ResearchGate 49946650)

params.physicalTarget: drive floor 0.45 → at unit velocity feedbackAmount_=0.45·… up to kMaxFeedback 0.85; raises self-sustain floor above velocity-only
params.rationale: ≥0.40 keeps the loop above the self-oscillation threshold so it drones regardless of velocity, while staying under the 0.85 stability cap (SC-008 energy limiter).

params.physicalTarget: parallel noise layer mix 0.30 (·env·filteredNoise·0.243 standalone gain)
params.rationale: A modest noise bed glues the inharmonic partials into a continuous metallic wash (the turbulent HF cascade reads as broadband hiss) without becoming a hi-hat.
params.citation: noise_layer.h; cymbal broadband HF content / wave-turbulence (arXiv:1509.02737)

params.rationale: Mid/bright noise band rides under the metallic partials, adding air without masking the body.
White noise = flat, broadband metallic sizzle appropriate to a cymbal wash (not the dark brown/pink of a drum body).
Long noise decay sustains the wash for the duration of the drone rather than a transient hiss.
A drone blooms via the feedback loop (E-bow-like), it does not strike; a beater click would contradict the sustained character.
params.citation: click_layer.h mix<=0 silent; E-bow/feedback swell (ambientguitar.net drone)

params.physicalTarget: ~6% sinusoidal frequency dither on modal freqs
params.rationale: Detunes the lattice off pure ratios = organic hand-hammered metal imperfection; keeps the regenerative loop from locking into a single sterile partial.
params.citation: modal_resonator_bank.h scatter dither; cymbal mode irregularity (Rossing normal modes of cymbals)

params.rationale: Low flat damping = long overall RT60 so the body sustains and the feedback loop only tops it up; supersedes Decay's flat-damping derivation.
Low f² damping keeps the HIGH partials alive (metallic, long shimmer) instead of a woody thump — essential to the bright metallic drone.
tensionModAmt is gated to Membrane only; on a Plate/Shell it is a NO-OP, so it is left off (the surviving amplitude motion is NonlinearCoupling).
params.citation: drum_voice.h tension gated to Membrane (audit §3-A); Avanzini et al. JASA 2012

params.rationale: Routes the sustained drone to an independent aux for separate processing/level — a continuous drone usually wants its own fader so it doesn't swamp the transient kit.
params.citation: voice_pool.cpp outputBus decode; aux buses are pre-master

params.rationale: A wide metallic drone reads best centered as a bed; per-pad pan kept neutral so it anchors the stereo field (move per voice in a multi-drone stack).
NonlinearCoupling already supplies the amplitude-driven harmonics; extra ToneShaper drive would over-thicken the sustained loop. Left off (flavour, not needed).
West-coast wavefolding is too aggressive/buzzy for a metallic acoustic drone; bypassed.

defaultedParams.param: PitchEnv Start / End / Time / Curve / Knee / Mid / MidFrac / Curve2
defaultedParams.why: PitchEnv Time=0 disables the whole pitch-glide feature; a sustained metallic drone has no 808-style boom, its pitch is set by the static inharmonic lattice.
Gated to the Membrane body in DrumVoice — a literal NO-OP on Plate/Shell, so left at 0.
A synthetic integer-harmonic series would tonalize the inharmonic metallic drone; deliberately 0 (covered in params for emphasis).

defaultedParams.param: Morph Enabled / Start / End / Duration / Curve
defaultedParams.why: Material Morph is a per-note timbre sweep; the drone's evolution is carried by the feedback loop + filter-env sweep, so per-hit material morph is left off (no audible role for a held drone, avoids muddying the steady spectrum).
Membrane-only frequency correction (Rossing air-loaded Bessel table); NO-OP on Plate/Shell, left at the harmless default.

defaultedParams.param: Coupling Strength / Secondary Enabled / Secondary Size / Secondary Material
defaultedParams.why: Head↔shell secondary resonator adds a struck-drum body weight (kick/tom character); irrelevant to a single self-oscillating plate drone and off by default — the drone IS the body, no shell layer wanted.
Per-pad participation in the GLOBAL sympathetic-resonance engine; only audible with kit-level coupling engaged, left at neutral 0.5 (no cross-pad buzz network intended for an isolated drone).
A sustained drone should not be cut by other pads; 0 = no choke.

defaultedParams.param: Tightness / Brightness / Body Size / Punch / Complexity macros
defaultedParams.why: All left at neutral 0.5 — the recipe sets the underlying material/decay/cutoff/skew/coupling directly, so the one-knob macros must stay at zero-delta to preserve those exact values.
No-ops because Click Mix=0 (drone has no beater transient).
These ARE set (the filter sweep is core to the evolving drone); listed in params, not defaulted.

defaultedParams.param: FM Ratio / NoiseBurst Duration / Friction Pressure
defaultedParams.why: Secondary exciter params for FMImpulse/NoiseBurst/Friction respectively — NO-OPs with the Feedback exciter selected; left at struct defaults.
1.0 = pad on; default, the pad must sound.
Left near default (~0.2, Q≈1.2) — the noise bed is a broad wash, not a resonant peak; a high-Q noise filter would add an unwanted pitched whistle on the forced-lowpass path.

physics:
A self-oscillating metallic plate/cymbal/gong drone is voiced against the acoustics of struck FREE-edged metal plates plus a regenerative (E-bow / Barkhausen) sustain mechanism.

FUNDAMENTAL / PITCH: Real cymbals/gongs are low-pitched, strongly inharmonic bodies. Cymbal vibration spans ~100 Hz to >5 kHz; a 16" cymbal has a low fundamental and ~300 measured modes (auditory.org/Rossing 1982). Gongs scale with size: small gongs f0 ~200–800 Hz, large gongs 65–250 Hz, 36" tam-tam ~65–70 Hz (mosicocymbal). For a *drone* you want the body sitting low/mid so the sustained partials are felt, not screechy. Membrum Plate f0 = 800·0.1^size, Shell f0 = 1500·0.1^size. Size≈0.45 → Plate f0 ≈ 800·0.1^0.45 ≈ 283 Hz (Shell ≈ 531 Hz); Size≈0.55 → Plate ≈ 226 Hz. That places the fundamental in the small-cymbal / large-gong-partial band — a low metallic hum, not a high ping.

PARTIAL STRUCTURE & INHARMONICITY: This is the defining feature. A struck cymbal/gong has FREE edges, so its partials follow Chladni's power law f(m,n)=C(m+2n)^P (m nodal diameters, n nodal circles). The classical Kirchhoff exponent is P=2; real measured cymbals sit LOWER, P≈1.6–1.73 (Rossing & Peterson 1982), which packs the upper partials denser → shimmer. Inharmonicity coefficient α for bronze alloys ≈0.3–0.8. Membrum's corrected Plate body (post-audit §3-B) IS this: kPlateRatios = (m+2n)^1.7·(1+0.11n), fundamental (2,0), no integer repeats — exactly the inharmonic fingerprint that distinguishes one metallic body from another. The Shell body is the free-free Euler-Bernoulli bar (1, 2.757, 5.404, 8.933, 13.344, 18.637 — roots of cos(βL)cosh(βL)=1), a tighter "tuned-bar / boxy" inharmonic series. modeStretch and modeScatter let you push the inharmonicity further and detune the lattice to imitate the hand-hammered irregularity of a real cymbal (no two modes perfectly degenerate).

DECAY / T60: Metallic plates ring LONG, and low partials ring longest. Small gongs T60 ~0.3–0.8 s; large gongs 2–15 s; 36" tam-tam >40 s; 16" gong 15–20 s (mosicocymbal). High partials damp fast (radiation + thermoelastic loss ∝ f²), low partials sustain — Chaigne damping R=b1+b3·f². For a self-sustained drone the *exciter* re-injects energy faster than the body loses it, so the effective ring is unbounded — the body's own T60 only sets how fast the timbre re-equilibrates after a pitch/feedback change. Membrum: Decay 0.92 → ~3× the plate base decay (plate base = lerp(0.4,2.0,material)·(1+0.1·size)), giving multi-second body ring even before feedback; bodyDampingB1≈0.30 → ~loose flat damping floor; bodyDampingB3≈0.20 → low f² damping = metallic (long highs) so the shimmer survives. decaySkew 0.78 (denorm +0.56) tilts per-mode energy toward HIGH partials (ratio^(−0.56) boosts high modes, now applied to ALL bodies post-audit M-5) — that is exactly the cymbal/gong "energy cascade to high frequency" signature: a few ms after the strike, energy builds into the high modes producing the bright shimmer (Fletcher 1999; Touzé/wave-turbulence buildup, arXiv:1509.02737). Skewing energy high reproduces that perceptual trace statically.

TRANSIENT / CONTACT CLICK: A drone is sustain-first; the attack should be a soft swell, not a stick crack. The Feedback exciter seeds with only a 2-sample impulse then lets the loop bloom (E-bow-like: no percussive onset, energy ramps as the loop reaches the Barkhausen condition). So click layer is OFF and the noise layer is a low, slow bed — the "attack" is the feedback ramp-up, mirroring how a bowed/E-bowed or feedback-sustained cymbal blooms rather than strikes.

NOISE CONTENT: Real cymbals have broadband hiss riding the modal lattice (turbulent high-mode cascade reads as noise). A modest pink/white parallel noise bed (Noise Mix ~0.30) with a long decay glues the partials into a continuous metallic wash without becoming a hi-hat. Spectrally it should be mid/bright (Noise Color ~0.55 ≈ White, Cutoff ~0.55).

PITCH GLIDE: Cymbals/gongs show amplitude-dependent pitch behaviour (nonlinear membrane/plate tension → modes sharpen/detune at large amplitude; gong "wobble"). Membrum's tensionModAmt is a Membrane-only energy→pitch glide (gated to Membrane in DrumVoice), so on a Plate/Shell it is a NO-OP and is left at default. The amplitude→brightness motion that DOES survive on Plate/Shell is NonlinearCoupling (env-level waveshaping, post-audit M-3/M-4): louder loop ⇒ harder recipSqrt drive ⇒ more odd harmonics, SUSTAINED through the ring — the cheap, literature-grounded stand-in for the cymbal nonlinear energy cascade (Buchla envelope-controlled waveshaping; Ducceschi/Bilbao von Kármán energy cascade; audit Phase-3 redesign). Heavy here (0.55).

REGENERATIVE MECHANISM (the synthesis): a band-limited positive feedback loop around a high-Q resonator self-oscillates once loop gain·resonance satisfy the Barkhausen criterion; a bandpass in the loop selects which mode(s) sustain and suppresses the rest (microcantilever/E-bow/self-oscillating-filter literature). Membrum maps this onto: Feedback exciter (loop drive, capped at 0.85 for stability SC-008) × the body modes (the high-Q resonator bank) × a ToneShaper BANDPASS with its own ADSR (selects/sweeps the sustained band → an evolving drone rather than a static squeal). feedback 0.40+ keeps the loop above the self-sustain floor; the energy limiter (kEnergyThreshold 0.35 RMS) prevents runaway, so it settles into a steady metallic drone.

GAIN-STAGING (post-audit): voice output is now LINEAR into a transparent hardClip rail + bus TruePeakLimiter(−1 dBTP); body normalised to −6 dBFS measured-strike peak. So Level 0.62 is a true linear attenuation (a sustained drone runs hot continuously — pulling level back below 0.8 leaves bus headroom for polyphonic stacking) and outputBus 1 sends the drone PRE-master to an aux for independent processing/level.

archetype: Feedback Shell Snare (experimental metallic / self-oscillating snare)
bodyModel: Shell (free-free Euler-Bernoulli bar; ratios 1, 2.757, 5.404, 8.933, 13.344, 18.637...). normToPhysical idx=2. Chosen for its strongly INHARMONIC metallic spectrum — the defining "ringing metallic" character of this experimental snare. Shell f0 base = 1500 Hz * 0.1^size.
exciter: Feedback (energy-limited self-oscillating body<->exciter loop, idx=5). At trigger drive = vel + feedbackDrive*(1-vel); feedbackAmount_=drive*0.85. feedbackAmount norm 0.40 -> a self-oscillation floor that ADDS to velocity (drive >= ~0.49 at vel=0, full at high vel), giving the sustained metallic squeal/ring-out that distinguishes this from a one-shot strike. Forces the body's per-sample slow path + keeps the internal -3 dBFS soft-clip.

params.rationale: Self-oscillating body<->exciter loop is what gives the ringing, sustained metallic snare character.
Free-free bar ratios (1,2.757,5.404...) are strongly inharmonic = the metallic ring; not a tuned membrane.
params.citation: Fletcher & Rossing, Physics of Musical Instruments, ch.2 §2.14 (free-free bar)

physics:
Researched acoustic profile (drives the param choices):
- Pitch/fundamental: Real 14" snare body resonance ~170-215 Hz (idrumtune 170/200 Hz; Physics-406 center-strike fundamental ~211-213 Hz snares-off); first edge/shell overtone ~1.5-1.7x => ~300-350 Hz. This archetype deliberately pushes the metallic body UP: Membrum Shell at Size 0.55 -> f0 = 1500*0.1^0.55 ~= 423 Hz with inharmonic free-free partials 423/1166/2286/3779/5645/7883 Hz, sitting in the 2-8 kHz crack/wire band. => Size 0.55, body=Shell.
- Partial structure & inharmonicity: free-free Euler-Bernoulli bar ratios 1, 2.757, 5.404, 8.933, 13.344, 18.637 (Fletcher & Rossing ch.2). Strongly inharmonic = metallic. modeScatter 0.42 (~6% dither) breaks the too-ordered ratios for an organic shimmer; Mode Stretch left neutral (1.0).
- Decay per band / T60: shell modes can ring T60 ~1.5 s (Chowdhury) but perceived snare envelope is short (~150-300 ms). Mapped via b1 override 14.1 s^-1 (flat damping, medium-short) and a LOW b3 (4e-5) so HIGHS ring long = metallic (metal has little f^2 damping). Noise tail ~100 ms extends just past the body. => Decay 0.58, b1 0.28, b3 0.04, Noise Decay 0.32.
- Transient / contact click: stick crack is broadband 2-8 kHz; modeled by the click layer mix 0.85, ~2.2 ms contact, ~7.1 kHz bandpass center.
- Noise content & shape: snare WIRES = high-passed white noise, energy concentrated 3-5 kHz, brightness to 7-10 kHz (idrumtune; 808/909 use a HPF white-noise generator). Mapped: Noise Mix 0.70, White color, LP cutoff ~5 kHz. The noise is most of a snare's identity (SOS).
- Pitch glide: real snares show negligible pitch decay (SOS: pitch contour 'unnecessary'); the requested 230->160 Hz over 60 ms is an electronic-drum stylization (808/909-flavored chirp), applied as a fast-exp drop (curve -0.7) to the whole modal set.
- Self-oscillation / experimental character: Feedback exciter (feedbackAmount 0.40) sustains the inharmonic shell partials for a ringing/squealing metallic snare; nonlinearCoupling 0.22 brightens louder hits (amplitude-driven HF, analogous to the nonlinear energy cascade in struck metal/cymbals). Strong head<->shell coupling (0.65) into a secondary shell bank (size 0.65 ~= 217 Hz, material 0.70) adds a second metallic resonant layer under the FX ring.
- Material: 808/909 precedent = two metallic tone oscillators (~180+330 Hz, or 238/476) + HPF noise; "the real 808 snare has a more metallic bell sound" — Membrum's Shell+Feedback metallic ring + noise layer is the direct physical-model analog.

physics:
PITCH / FUNDAMENTAL. An acoustic 14" snare has a perceived body fundamental ~170-200 Hz with the first edge overtone ~1.5-1.7x the fundamental (~300-350 Hz) (idrumtune; hyperphysics snare). It has NO clear pitch because its partials are dense and inharmonic. The classic synthesized analogue (TR-808/909) builds the tonal part from TWO decaying sine oscillators tuned roughly an octave apart -- 808 = ~250 Hz and ~500 Hz (resonant T-network osc); 909 = two oscillators + waveshapers -- mixed with high-pass-filtered avalanche/white noise gated by a "snappy" envelope (Wikipedia TR-808/TR-909; SOS Practical Snare Drum Synthesis). The FM-PLATE snare REPLACES that two-tone tonal pair with a single INHARMONIC PLATE body driven by an FM exciter, so the "two metallic modes" become a whole dense inharmonic mode bank -- a brighter, clangier, more "metal panel" snare than a tuned-membrane snare.

PARTIAL / MODE STRUCTURE & INHARMONICITY. A free metal plate / cymbal follows Chladni's law: modal frequency f proportional to (m + 2n)^P where m = nodal diameters, n = nodal circles, and "adding one nodal circle raises the frequency about as much as adding two nodal diameters" (Rossing, "Chladni's law for vibrating plates", Am.J.Phys 50 1982; UNSW; F&R Physics of Musical Instruments Table 3.2). The exponent is ~2 for an ideal flat plate but the measured value for real cymbals is lower, ~1.6-1.73 (Rossing). The resulting spectrum is "far from harmonic" with no simple ratios (SOS Analysing Metallic Percussion) -- exactly the post-audit Membrum Plate body, which uses the free-plate (m+2n)^1.7 Chladni power law (audit Phase-2 step 7, plate_modes.h). The FM exciter adds a second inharmonic layer: a non-integer carrier:modulator ratio yields an inharmonic FM spectrum (bell/metallic clang) whose brightness sweeps from bright to pure as the modulation index decays (Chowning; CCRMA FM2; mercity FM chapter). Membrum's FMImpulse uses carrier ratio 1.0 fixed, modulator ratio = 1 + 3*fmRatio, with a fast (30 ms) modulation-index decay under a slower (80 ms) amplitude decay -- the canonical metallic-FM attack.

DECAY / T60 PER BAND. Metallic percussion shows energy "moving upward through the spectrum" over several hundred ms: high-frequency modes decay fast, mid energy dominates late (SOS Analysing Metallic Percussion). A snare body is short -- tens to low-hundreds of ms before the wires take over. The "energy moving up" maps directly to a Material MORPH from 0.55->0.80 (body gets brighter through the hit) plus NonlinearCoupling (louder = brighter). Membrum decay law R = b1 + b3*f^2 (Chaigne-Askenfelt) carries per-band T60; for this snare we want a moderately short, bright-leaning plate ring (b1 mid, b3 low for metallic long highs).

TRANSIENT / CONTACT CLICK. A snare's defining feature is its sharp stick crack -- a 2-5 ms broadband contact transient, energy in the 2-8 kHz presence band (idrumtune mixing). This is the Click layer (sharp, bright, high mix).

NOISE CONTENT. The snare wires = dense, near-random inharmonic buzz, modelled in every synth as high-pass / band-limited white noise gated by a snappy envelope (808 avalanche noise HP-filtered; 909 "snappy"; Nord percussion book: white noise through lowpass-highpass chain) (Wikipedia TR-808; McGill Nord Modular percussion). Bright (violet/white-leaning), short-to-medium decay, sitting on top of the body.

PITCH GLIDE. Synthetic snares apply a short downward pitch envelope to the tonal layer for "weight" (the 808/909 oscillators are pitched and decay; designers add a fast drop). For this archetype a modest 230->160 Hz glide over ~60 ms gives body thump under the metallic clang without becoming a tom "kerthump." This is the ToneShaper pitch env (NOT tensionMod, which is Membrane-only and a NO-OP on Plate).

MATERIAL. Synthetic/metallic -- a vibrating steel/bronze panel, not a tuned skin. Hence Plate body (free-plate Chladni), high-ish material for metallic brightness, low b3 (weak f^2 damping = metal, long highs).

archetype: FM-Bell Crash (synthetic clangy metallic crash cymbal)
bodyModel: Bell (BodyModelType=4) — church-bell Chladni partial bank, 16 modes, hum at 0.25*f0_nominal, nominal at 1.0. Chosen over Plate/NoiseBody because the brief calls for a PITCHED, clangy, tuned-metal crash whose body is a tonal inharmonic FM bell rather than a noise wash. The Bell bank's sub-octave hum + clustered low partials (0.25/0.5/0.6/0.75/1.0 ratios) reproduce the measured low-frequency mode cluster of a real crash and give the "strike-note"/clang pitch that a pure noise body cannot.
exciter: FMImpulse (ExciterType=4) — Chowning bell-FM tone (carrier PM'd by a 1:ratio modulator, exp amp + faster modIndex envelope, modulation index 0.5..3.0 rad velocity-driven). This is the defining feature of the archetype: it injects an inharmonic Chowning-bell spectrum into the carrier and drives the Bell body, layering FM clang on modal clang. fmRatio is the headline param (target 0.45 norm per the brief).

params.rationale: The archetype is defined as FMImpulse — Chowning bell-FM excitation drives the carrier so the strike already carries an inharmonic metallic spectrum before the body rings.
params.citation: fm_impulse_exciter.h trigger(): Chowning c:m bell FM; Wikipedia Frequency modulation synthesis (inharmonic c:m -> bell-like spectra)

params.rationale: Bell Chladni bank gives a tuned/clangy partial set (hum 0.25*f0 + clustered low partials) — the pitched metallic ring that distinguishes this from a noise crash.
params.citation: bell_modes.h kBellRatios; Rossing, Science of Percussion Instruments ch.5; Perrin/Charnley/DePont 1983 JSV 90(1)

params.physicalTarget: Bell brightness = 0.7+0.3*0.92 = 0.976 (very low b3 high-freq damping); baseDecayTime = lerp(0.4,1.4,0.92)=1.32 s * (1+0.1*size)
params.rationale: Very metallic: high material -> minimal frequency-squared damping so the upper partials/shimmer ring long, matching bronze's slow HF decay.
params.citation: bell_mapper.h r.brightness/baseDecayTime; Mosico/Arborea cymbal-mode notes; SOS 'Synthesizing Realistic Cymbals' (extended HF tail)

params.rationale: Places the nominal/strike pitch near 280-300 Hz with a clustered sub-kHz partial set — exactly the measured 'big crash booms at ~300 Hz / clangy low cluster' region, while staying tuned not boomy.
params.citation: natural_fundamental.h (Bell 800*0.1^size); Musical-U 'Percussion Frequencies: Cymbals' (~300 Hz crash body); Wilbur & Rossing normal-mode measurements (low n=0 mode family)

params.rationale: Long sustaining wash typical of a crash (multi-second ring); near the top of the Bell sweep but under the bank's 5 s clamp.
params.citation: bell_mapper.h r.decayTime; crash decay T60 ranges 'low modes up to ~8 s, high modes ~50 ms' (ResearchGate FEM crash/splash study)

params.physicalTarget: theta = 0.32*(pi/2)=0.503 rad; per-mode amp = |cos(m*theta)| -> partials weighted by meridional position
params.rationale: Off-antinode azimuth so higher-m partials pass through nodes, giving an uneven, clangy partial balance rather than a flat bell strike on the soundbow.
Crashes sit slightly below the kit's peak drums (kick/snare) in the mix; trimmed below the 0.8 default so the long wash doesn't dominate.
Brief specifies fmRatio 0.45. A non-integer ratio ~2.35 yields a dense inharmonic Chowning-bell sideband cluster (close to classic bell territory, brighter/clangier than the 1.4 default).
params.citation: fm_impulse_exciter.h setModulatorRatio; CCRMA CLM FM2 tutorial (inharmonic c:m for bell timbres); Chowning bell uses non-integer ratios

params.physicalTarget: physical = 0.5+0.55*1.5 = 1.325; stretchNorm to bank = (1.325-0.5)/1.5 = 0.55 -> inharmonicity warp sqrt(1+B*(k+1)^2)
params.rationale: Pushes the Bell partials further off their tuned ratios toward the irregular, dispersive spacing of a real (non-ideal, often slightly warped) cymbal — more clang, less 'in-tune bell'.
params.citation: voice_pool.cpp modeStretch denorm; modal_resonator_bank.h corrected (k+1) stretch; Rossing cymbal inharmonicity

params.physicalTarget: bank sinusoidal freq dither ~0..15% scaled by 0.65 (~10% detune spread)
params.rationale: Real cymbals have dense, near-degenerate split modes (beating); scatter breaks the exact tuned-bell ratios into a shimmering cluster so the crash reads as metal, not a glockenspiel.
params.citation: voice_pool.cpp modeScatter; Wilbur & Rossing (mode splitting/beating in cymbals)

params.physicalTarget: drive = 1+6.0*vel*0.45*env; env-level recipSqrt waveshaping mixed amount*0.45
params.rationale: Cymbals are strongly nonlinear: hard hits brighten and spawn HF energy (energy cascade) that sustains then relaxes — amplitude-driven brightening models this velocity-sensitive 'tap=bell, crash=broadband'.
params.citation: nonlinear_coupling.h (audit M-3/M-4 redesign); Stowell cymbal nonlinearity; Ducceschi/Bilbao von-Karman energy cascade

params.physicalTarget: parallel lowpass-noise layer at mix 0.4 * env * filteredNoise * 0.243
params.rationale: Overlays the broadband bronze shimmer/sizzle on top of the pitched FM-bell body — the 'shhh' wash a real crash has above its clang.
params.citation: noise_layer.h; SOS 'Synthesizing Realistic Cymbals' (dense enharmonic shimmer fog); Musical-U 2-3 kHz 'shhh'

params.rationale: Keeps the noise overlay bright (shimmer/sheen up to ~10-12 kHz), matching the measured crash high-frequency sheen band.
Violet/blue tilt pushes energy up high so the noise reads as metallic sizzle, not a dull hiss.

params.physicalTarget: ms = exp(ln20 + 0.72*(ln2000-ln20)) ≈ 590 ms env decay (release 295 ms)
params.rationale: The shimmer should sustain a good fraction of the crash wash, though shorter than the long body ring (HF decays faster than LF in real cymbals).
params.citation: noise_layer.h denormDecayMs; FEM crash study (HF modes decay faster)

params.rationale: Mild lowpass resonance — no pitched peak in the noise; left near default since the shimmer is broadband.

params.physicalTarget: click layer at 0.35 -> half into exciter feed, full to output * 0.445
params.rationale: A modest stick-contact tick defines the attack/transient of the crash strike without turning it into a stick click; the FM impulse already supplies most of the bright onset.
params.citation: click_layer.h; SOS crash 'initial ping' attack transient

params.rationale: Sharp, bright contact tick consistent with a metal-on-metal cymbal strike onset.
Short contact time — a crisp transient, left at default since a crash strike is a brief impact.

params.physicalTarget: b1 = 0.2+0.3*49.8 = 15.1 s^-1 flat damping floor (long ring)
params.rationale: Low flat damping so the whole partial set rings long (the crash wash); overrides the Decay-derived flat term with an explicit metallic-long floor.
params.citation: membrane_mapper.h dampingLawFromParams override; Chaigne & Askenfelt 1994 damping law

params.physicalTarget: b3 = 0.0 -> NO frequency-squared damping (pure flat, longest possible high-partial tails)
params.rationale: Bronze has very weak HF damping; b3=0 lets the upper partials/shimmer sustain like real metal (the metal<->wood axis pinned fully to metal).
params.citation: membrane_mapper.h b3 override; Chaigne damping R=b1+b3*f^2 (b3=0 => no HF rolloff)

defaultedParams.why: ToneShaper SVF left transparent (cutoff norm 1.0 = bypass); the body+noise already define the spectrum, no post-filter shaping wanted on a crash.
Filter env amount at neutral 0.5 (=0) keeps the filter bypassed; a crash needs no swept post-filter, the nonlinear coupling supplies the dynamic brightness instead.
Bypassed at 0 — FM excitation + NonlinearCoupling already provide the harmonic richness; adding ToneShaper drive would be redundant flavour.
Bypassed at 0 — wavefolding is a west-coast timbre not characteristic of a crash; the inharmonic content comes from FM ratio + stretch/scatter.
PitchEnv Time = 0 disables the whole pitch-glide feature; a crash has no 808-style pitch drop (no 'kerthump'), it rings at a fixed strike pitch.

Left at 0 (exact bypass): a synthetic integer-harmonic series would re-tonalise the crash; the archetype's pitch already comes from the inharmonic Bell+FM bank, adding a harmonic series would fight the clang.
Material morph off — the crash timbre is static through the ring; no need for a per-note material sweep.
0 = no choke; a crash is a sustaining one-shot, not part of an open/closed mute pair.
0 = main bus; standard routing, no dedicated send needed for this archetype.
No-op: Feedback exciter not selected (FMImpulse is).
No-op: NoiseBurst exciter not selected.
No-op: Friction exciter not selected.
Left at default 0.5 participation; only audible if the kit's global sympathetic-coupling/snare-buzz/tom-resonance is engaged, which isn't part of this voice's character.
All at neutral 0.5 (zero delta) so the explicitly-voiced underlying params are preserved exactly — macros are performance one-knobs, not part of the baseline recipe.
Membrane-only frequency correction; a no-op for the Bell body.

defaultedParams.param: Coupling Strength / Secondary Enabled / Secondary Size / Secondary Material (54,55,56,57)
defaultedParams.why: Head<->shell secondary resonator off — a crash is a single radiating plate/bell, it has no coupled drum shell to add.
Membrane-only (gated to Membrane body); a no-op on the Bell body, and a crash has no tension-driven pitch glide anyway.
1.0 = on; the pad is an active kit voice.
Center 0.5 by default; per-kit placement (crashes often slightly off-center) is a mix decision left to the kit author, not intrinsic to the archetype.

physics: FUNDAMENTAL/PITCH: A crash cymbal has no single harmonic fundamental — it produces a dense inharmonic spectrum, but listeners assign it a low 'strike note' / clang pitch. For an 18" crash the lowest n=0 mode family sits low (e.g. the (3,0) mode measured ~64 Hz for large diameters; Wilbur & Rossing studied the dominant modes over 0-2.5 kHz). Mix engineers describe a big crash 'booming at ~300 Hz' in the low-mid while reaching 10-12 kHz sheen (Musical-U; gearpage). MODE/PARTIAL STRUCTURE & INHARMONICITY: modes classified by (m nodal diameters, n nodal circles); below ~1-2 kHz the modes are sparse and individually audible (the clangy region), above that they become a 'dense fog of enharmonic partials' (SOS). Strongly inharmonic, ratios deviate substantially from integers (FEM/modal studies). DECAY (T60): strongly frequency-banded — high-frequency modes decay fast (~50 ms) while low modes ring for seconds (up to ~8 s) — so a crash starts as a bright wash and settles into a long low ring. TRANSIENT/CLICK: a fast metal-on-metal contact 'ping' (SOS describes an initial bandpass-swept ping ~1 kHz over 0.2 s). NOISE CONTENT: spectrum is broadband and roughly flat, with ~40% of energy above 20 kHz; the audible 'shhh' shimmer concentrates ~2-3 kHz with sheen to 10-12 kHz (violet/blue-tilted, bright). PITCH GLIDE: cymbals exhibit nonlinear behaviour — hard strikes brighten dynamically and cascade energy upward (von Karman / energy cascade), then relax as they decay; there is no monotonic 808-style downward pitch glide, so PitchEnv is left off and the dynamic brightening is captured by amplitude-driven NonlinearCoupling. MATERIAL: bronze (B20/B8 cast bronze) — high stiffness, very low internal HF damping, hence long shimmering tails (b3=0). SYNTHESIS MAPPING: the classic synthetic crash route (SOS Gordon Reid) is FM of a bright carrier by an inharmonic modulator to spawn hundreds of enharmonic partials, plus a noise/sheen layer — this archetype maps that onto FMImpulse (Chowning inharmonic c:m) exciting the Bell Chladni bank (f0_nominal 283.8 Hz at size 0.45: hum 71 Hz, partials clustered up to 3.4 kHz) detuned by modeStretch (1.325) + modeScatter (~10%) so the tuned bell becomes a clangy cymbal, with a violet-noise shimmer overlay (mix 0.4, ~12 kHz LP) and a bright short contact click for the attack.

archetype: FM-Bell Hi-Hat (synthesized metallic hat — Bell body + FMImpulse, west-coast ringing-metallic, closed/pedal/open by decay)
bodyModel: Bell (BodyModelType::Bell, offset 1). Church-bell Chladni partial bank: 16 modes at ratios {0.25 hum, 0.50 prime, 0.60 tierce, 0.75 quint, 1.00 nominal, 1.50, 2.00, 2.60, 3.20, 4.00, 5.333, 6.40, 7.333, 8.667, 10.0, 12.0} * f0_nominal, where f0_nominal = 800*0.1^size. Chosen over NoiseBody/Plate because the brief is explicitly an INHARMONIC FM-bell pitched-metallic hat (not a noise hat): the Bell ratio set is genuinely inharmonic (0.25,0.6,2.6,5.333... are non-integer) and rings with the long upper-partial tails of metal (brightness 0.7+0.3*material drives b3 very low). This is the only body in Membrum whose ratio topology is a tuned-metal (bell) spectrum rather than a 2-D plate/cymbal fog (Plate/NoiseBody) — exactly the "pitched-metallic, ringing" character requested, distinct from every noise hat in the factory kits.
exciter: FMImpulse (ExciterType::FMImpulse, offset 0). Chowning bell-FM exciter: carrier (ratio 1.0) phase-modulated by a modulator at modulatorRatio = 1.0+3.0*fmRatioNorm; modIndex = lerp(0.5,3.0,velocity) rad with modIndexTau=30 ms decaying FASTER than ampTau=80 ms (bright→pure, the canonical bell envelope, Chowning 1973). Base carrier freq is velocity-driven 400→2500 Hz. A non-integer modulator ratio injects a dense inharmonic sideband cluster INTO the bell body, doubly reinforcing the metallic, glassy, ringing-hat timbre. This is the west-coast move: FM-bell shimmer in place of filtered noise.

params.rationale: Chowning bell-FM exciter injects the inharmonic, glassy, ringing-metal shimmer that replaces filtered noise — the defining move of this archetype.
params.citation: Chowning 1973; CCRMA CLM FM tutorial (non-integer c:m → inharmonic bell)

params.rationale: Bell is Membrum's only tuned-INHARMONIC metal ratio set (0.25/0.6/2.6/5.333...); gives a pitched-metallic ring, not a plate/cymbal fog or noise hat.
params.citation: Rossing, Science of Percussion Instruments Ch.5; bell_modes.h kBellRatios

params.physicalTarget: brightness=0.7+0.3*0.92≈0.976 (very metallic, b3 derived very low); Bell baseDecay=lerp(0.4,1.4,0.92)≈1.32 s before Decay mult
params.rationale: Near-max material = longest upper-partial ring = bright clangy metal; bodyDampingB3 override still pins the high-mode tail explicitly.
params.citation: bell_mapper.h brightness law; FR-024 very-metallic bell defaults

params.physicalTarget: f0_nominal=800*0.1^0.05≈712 Hz; partials reach 12x≈8.5 kHz, mid partials (2.6-6.4x) cluster ~1.85-4.56 kHz
params.rationale: Small body pushes the bell partial bank UP into the 3-10 kHz hat band, landing energy near the 808's 3440/7100 Hz metallic centres.

params.physicalTarget: closed variant: decayMult=exp(lerp(ln0.3,ln3.0,0.08))≈0.34 → very short ring (~50 ms with the b1 override); see defaultedParams for pedal/open
params.rationale: Decay IS the closed/pedal/open axis; 0.08→closed ~50 ms matches the 808 fixed closed-hat decay. Pedal≈0.22, open≈0.62.
params.citation: TR-808 closed 50 ms / open 90-600 ms (Baratatronix); bell_mapper decay law

params.physicalTarget: bell strike azimuth theta=0.45*(pi/2)≈0.71 rad; |cos(m*theta)| weights upper-m partials so several mid partials pass near nodes — a tighter, less hum-heavy balance
params.rationale: Off-antinode azimuth de-emphasises the low hum/prime and favours the bright meridional partials, giving a thinner hat-like balance vs a full bell.
params.citation: bell_modes.h computeBellAmplitude (meridional cos(m*theta)); Perrin et al. 1983

params.rationale: Hats sit below kick/snare in a kit; slight cut for mix balance, master/bus limiter owns the ceiling.
params.citation: AUDIT gain-staging appendix (level_ linear, post-N-1 body norm)

params.rationale: Center of the requested 0.55-0.78 window → ratio ~2.95, a non-integer ratio that sprays dense inharmonic FM sidebands across the bright band (Chowning bell / 808 'avoid even multiples').
params.citation: fm_impulse_exciter.h (1+3*norm); CCRMA non-integer c:m bell; TR-808 inharmonic osc rule

params.physicalTarget: ~6% sinusoidal frequency dither on the bell partials
params.rationale: Real cymbals are a beating fog of detuned partials; scatter adds the natural-imperfection detune so the metal shimmers/beats instead of ringing like a clean glock.
params.citation: modal_resonator_bank scatter; cymbal 'dense fog of enharmonic partials' (Sound on Sound)

params.physicalTarget: b3 ≈ 0.02e-3 s (near-pure flat damping) → high partials ring long = metal
params.rationale: Hats are metal: the upper partials must NOT be damped like wood; near-zero b3 keeps the bright tail alive (mirrors factory 'Metal hats' bodyDampingB3=0).

params.physicalTarget: b1 = 0.2+0.78*49.8 ≈ 39 s^-1 flat damping → ~25-30 ms flat-decay floor (closed); overrides Decay's flat term
params.rationale: Closed hat needs a tight overall T60; a high flat-damping floor enforces the ~50 ms gate while b3≈0 still lets the highs ring within that window. Pedal/open lower this (see defaults).
params.citation: membrane_mapper dampingLawFromParams b1 range; TR-808 closed 50 ms

params.physicalTarget: skew = 0.62*2-1 = +0.24; per-mode tilt ratio^(-0.24) lifts the upper partials (ratio>1) and trims the hum
params.rationale: Tilts spectral energy toward the high partials = brighter, hat-like; reinforces the 3-10 kHz emphasis over the low bell hum.
params.citation: bell_mapper decaySkew per-mode tilt (audit M-5); hi-hat HF-dominant spectrum (Musical-U)

params.physicalTarget: parallel noise layer at 0.22*env*filteredNoise *0.243 standalone gain → sits ~under the body
params.rationale: A real cymbal's dense fog reads as broadband sizzle; a MODEST violet noise floor adds air UNDER the FM-bell ring without turning it into a noise hat (the whole point of this archetype).
params.citation: noise_layer mix; cymbal broadband 'fog' (Sound on Sound); AUDIT noise ~-18 dBFS under body

params.rationale: Keeps the noise floor bright and HF (the 5-12 kHz cymbal sizzle band), not a dull rumble.
params.citation: noise_layer denormCutoff; closed hat '10 kHz dominates' (Musical-U)

params.rationale: Violet tilts the noise floor toward the high frequencies, matching a cymbal's HF-weighted sizzle.
params.citation: noise_layer denormColor thresholds; cymbal HF-dominant energy (Boyk; Sound on Sound)

params.rationale: Mild emphasis only; the FM-bell body carries the pitch, the noise just adds air, so no sharp peak.

params.physicalTarget: ms = exp(ln20+0.25*(ln2000-ln20)) ≈ 67 ms (closed); track the body decay per variant
params.rationale: Noise sizzle is a short attack-coincident wash on the closed hat; longer on open. Keeps the noise from outlasting the metal ring.

params.physicalTarget: click 0.4*vel*env*filteredNoise, half into exciter feed + full to output *0.445
params.rationale: A real hat has a sharp stick/contact tick before the ring; the click layer supplies the few-ms broadband attack definition.
params.citation: click_layer; hi-hat attack transient (Avanzini hi-hat modeling)

params.rationale: Sharp, bright tick (a stick on metal), not a dull thud — sits in the cymbal HF band.
params.citation: click_layer denormBrightness; cymbal HF attack content

params.rationale: Very short, crisp metallic contact tick.

params.physicalTarget: physical 0.5+0.45*1.5 = 1.175 (mild positive stretch); stretchNorm fed to bank ≈0.45
params.rationale: A touch of extra inharmonic dispersion on top of the already-inharmonic bell ratios pushes upper partials sharp = more clangy/metallic, more cymbal-fog-like.
params.citation: voice_pool modeStretch denorm; stiff-metal inharmonicity (audit §3-B B_max)

params.physicalTarget: group 1 (decode clamp(int(0.125*8+0.5))=1) — preset generator stores integer 1 directly
params.rationale: Closed/pedal/open hats must mute each other; assigning all three variants to choke group 1 gives the classic open-cut-by-closed behaviour.
Hats sit slightly off-center in a real kit image (drummer's right from audience view); modest right placement adds stereo life (audit M-9).
params.citation: voice_pool panGainsForNote; AUDIT M-9 per-pad pan

defaultedParams.why: Closed=0.08(~50ms); the pedal variant sets Decay≈0.22 (~120ms) and open≈0.62 (~450ms) — the ONLY axis that distinguishes the three hats, mirroring 808 closed 50ms / open 90-600ms.
Lowered to ≈0.45 (pedal) / ≈0.18 (open) so the flat-damping floor stops gating the ring short — open hat must sustain. Closed keeps ~0.78.
Raised to ≈0.5 (~330ms) on the open hat so the sizzle floor tracks the longer metal ring; left short for closed.
Hats do not pitch-glide (no kerthump); pitch env left fully off (Time=0 disables the whole feature).
Membrane-only physics; a no-op on the Bell body, so left at 0.
Membrane-only frequency correction; a no-op on Bell, left at default 0.6 (inert here).
Amplitude-driven brightening is an expressive extra; a hat's brightness is already carried by FM + scatter + low b3, so left at 0 (exact bypass) to keep the transient clean.

defaultedParams.param: Coupling Strength / Secondary Enabled / Secondary Size / Secondary Material / Coupling Amount
defaultedParams.why: Head↔shell coupling adds body weight (for kicks/shells); a hat wants NO extra low body, so secondary stays off and coupling participation stays neutral 0.5 (no kit coupling engaged).
ToneShaper saturation/wavefolding is flavour, not needed — the FM exciter already supplies the inharmonic harmonics; left bypassed at 0 for a clean metallic transient.

defaultedParams.param: Filter Type / Cutoff / Resonance / Filter Env (Amount/Atk/Dec/Sus/Rel)
defaultedParams.why: ToneShaper SVF left transparent (cutoff norm 1.0 = bypass, env amount 0.5 = no mod); the bell partial placement + noise color already shape the spectrum, so no post-filter is needed.
No intra-hit timbre sweep wanted on a hat; morph left off (a static metallic hit is correct).
All left neutral 0.5 (zero delta) so the explicitly-voiced underlying params are preserved exactly.

defaultedParams.param: Feedback Amount / NoiseBurst Duration / Friction Pressure
defaultedParams.why: Secondary-exciter params for Feedback/NoiseBurst/Friction respectively — no-ops while FMImpulse is selected, left at default.
0 (main bus); no multi-out routing needed for a standard hat.
1.0 (on) — the pad sounds.

physics:
RESEARCHED ACOUSTIC + SYNTHESIS PROFILE (drives the param choices):

FUNDAMENTAL/PITCH: A hi-hat/cymbal has NO single pitch — it is a free-edge metal plate radiating a dense inharmonic partial fog. Energy spans ~100 Hz to >20 kHz (Boyk: ~40% above 20 kHz). Perceptually the metallic-bright band is 3-10 kHz; a struck closed hat is dominated by ~10 kHz with secondary ~5 kHz and a low ~300 Hz contact (Musical-U, Sound on Sound). → DRIVES Size=0.05 (Bell f0_nominal≈712 Hz so the 0.25-12x partial bank lands 178 Hz-8.5 kHz, clustering the 2.6-6.4x partials at ~1.85-4.56 kHz, near the 808's 3440/7100 Hz bandpass centres) and Decay Skew +0.24 + Noise Color Violet to weight the highs.

PARTIAL STRUCTURE / INHARMONICITY: dense, inharmonic, beating. The TR-808 fakes this with SIX inharmonic square oscillators (205.3/304.4/369.6/522.7/540/800 Hz, rule 'avoid even multiples') → bandpass 3440 & 7100 Hz (Baratatronix). FM bells use a non-integer carrier:modulator ratio for the inharmonic sideband cluster (Chowning; CCRMA). → DRIVES Body=Bell (inharmonic 0.25/0.6/2.6/5.333... ratios), FM Ratio=0.65 (modulator ratio 2.95, strongly non-integer), and Mode Scatter=0.4 + Mode Stretch 1.175 for the beating-fog detune.

DECAY / T60 (the closed/pedal/open axis): TR-808 closed=fixed ~50 ms, open=90-600 ms (pedal openness), cymbal=350-1200 ms; HF partials decay faster than LF (Avanzini hi-hat model). → DRIVES Decay 0.08/0.22/0.62 (closed/pedal/open) + Body Damping b1 0.78/0.45/0.18 (flat-damping floor gates the closed ring, releases for open) while b3≈0.02 keeps highs ringing inside that window. No frequency-dependent-damping bug to fight: bell brightness/b3 already gives the right tilt.

ATTACK / CONTACT CLICK: a sharp few-ms broadband stick/contact tick precedes the ring. → DRIVES Click Mix 0.4, Click Contact ~2.45 ms, Click Brightness ~6.4 kHz (bright metallic tick).

NOISE CONTENT & SHAPE: the partial fog is so dense it reads as HF sizzle (5-12 kHz). On the FM-bell hat the body carries the pitch, so noise is a MODEST under-body floor (not the source). → DRIVES Noise Mix 0.22 (under body), Cutoff ~11 kHz lowpass, Color Violet, Resonance ~1.24.

PITCH GLIDE: none — hats do not 'kerthump'. → PitchEnv Time=0 (feature disabled), Tension Mod=0.

MATERIAL: thin metal alloy, long-ringing upper partials. → DRIVES Material 0.92 (brightness≈0.976) + Body Damping b3≈0.02 (near-flat damping = metal, not wood).

bodyModel: Bell (church-bell Chladni partials, 16 modes; nominal f0 = 800·0.1^size, hum at 0.25·f0)
exciter: FMImpulse (Chowning bell-FM ping; carrier ratio 1.0, modulator ratio = 1.0+3.0·fmRatio, index 0.5→3.0 rad decaying over ~30 ms, amp over ~80 ms)

params.rationale: Chowning bell-FM ping is the defining excitation for a synthetic FM-bell perc; drives an inharmonic sideband cloud into the body.
params.citation: CCRMA CLM FM tutorial; Chowning JAES 1973 (FM bell)

params.rationale: Bell body = canonical church-bell Chladni partials (hum/prime/tierce/quint/nominal); the tierce gives the metallic colour. Matches the 808-kit FM-bell perc slot.
params.citation: Hibberts; Rossing Science of Percussion ch.5

params.physicalTarget: bell brightness = 0.7+0.3·0.7 = 0.91; base decay lerp(0.4,1.4,0.7)=1.10 s
params.rationale: Very metallic: high brightness → very low b3 → long-ringing upper partials characteristic of bells.

params.physicalTarget: nominal f0 = 800·0.1^0.25 = 450 Hz; hum 112 Hz, partials up to ~5.4 kHz
params.rationale: Small/high bell = a bright metallic ping (not a deep cathedral bell); matches the 808-kit perc size and the cowbell's upper-mid register.
Short-to-medium ring: an 808-style perc tail (~hundreds of ms), not a multi-second carillon. Filler should be punchy, not sustaining.

params.physicalTarget: azimuth θ = 0.3·(π/2) = 0.47 rad; |cos(m·θ)| per-partial meridional weighting
params.rationale: Off-soundbow strike excites all principal partials with a natural balance; default-ish, away from the m=0 axis so higher-m partials still respond.
Slightly below unity — filler perc sits under the kit's primary voices; matches the 808-kit perc level.

params.physicalTarget: modulator ratio = 1.0+3.0·0.4 = 2.2 (inharmonic, between Chowning 1.4 and 808 cowbell 1.48)
params.rationale: Non-integer ratio → clangorous inharmonic FM sideband cloud = metallic bell ping. Task-specified ~0.4.

params.physicalTarget: b3 = 0·1e-3 = 0 s → pure flat damping, no f² high-freq roll-off
params.rationale: Metal rings its highs: zero frequency-squared damping keeps the upper partials alive (woody f² damping would dull the bell). Overrides material-derived b3.

params.physicalTarget: click ×0.15 (always-on 2–5 ms transient; half-feeds exciter, full to output)
params.rationale: Clapper contact tick — defines the attack and adds beater energy into the bell modes without dominating.
Bright metallic tick to suit a high bell ping; matches the 808-kit perc click brightness.
A clean metallic FM-bell ping has no noise bed (cowbell/perc are tonal); noise off keeps it pure.
airLoading is Membrane-only; on a Bell body it is a no-op, set to 0 to be explicit (matches 808-kit perc).
Bell ratios are already inharmonic/canonical; leave neutral so the recognisable bell spectrum is preserved for filler.
No spectral decay tilt needed; the natural bell partial balance and b3=0 already give the bright tail.
Filler perc centered by default; kit author can spread later. Bit-identical to legacy mono dup.
Bells/cowbells have no pitch glide; disable the whole pitch-env feature.

defaultedParams.param: Filter Type / Filter Cutoff / Filter Resonance / Filter Env (Amount/Atk/Dec/Sus/Rel)
defaultedParams.why: ToneShaper filter left at default (cutoff norm 1.0 = bypass, env amount 0.5 = none): the FM+bell spectrum is already the desired timbre; no post-body filtering wanted for clean filler.
Bypassed at 0 — a clean metallic ping needs no added saturation/wavefolding (Drive is flavour, not level).
No-ops while PitchEnv Time=0; bells don't glide.
0 = exact bypass; the bell body + FM exciter already supply a rich inharmonic spectrum, no synthetic harmonic series needed.
0 = exact bypass; amplitude-driven brightening not part of a simple filler ping (keeps it cheap and clean).
Material Morph off; a single static metallic timbre is the goal for filler.
Left at sentinel/derive-from-Decay so the Decay knob (0.2 → ~0.37 s) controls ring length; no need to override flat damping.
0 = pure ratios; the canonical recognisable bell spectrum is preferred over organic detune for filler.

defaultedParams.param: Coupling Strength / Secondary Enabled/Size/Material / Coupling Amount
defaultedParams.why: Head↔shell coupling and cross-pad sympathetic coupling off — a standalone metallic ping needs no shell resonator or inter-pad buzz.
0 and Membrane-only anyway — no-op on a Bell body; bells don't kerthump.
All at neutral 0.5 (zero delta) so the explicit per-param voicing above is preserved exactly.

defaultedParams.param: Feedback Amount / NoiseBurst Duration / Friction Pressure
defaultedParams.why: Secondary-exciter params for Feedback/NoiseBurst/Friction respectively — no-ops with FMImpulse selected.
No-ops while Noise Mix=0.
Default ~2.6 ms contact is an appropriate sharp tick; no reason to lengthen for a metallic ping.
0/0 = no choke, main bus — kit-routing concerns, left to the kit author.
1.0 = on for the one audible perc slot; the other perc slots in the 808 kit are disabled via the crafted list at kit level, not per-voice voicing.

physics:
REFERENT: This is a SYNTHETIC archetype with no single real-world instrument — it is "FM-bell perc as filler" (the 808 kit's perc slots, only one audible). The closest real circuit referent is the TR-808 cowbell: two square oscillators at 800 Hz and 540 Hz, ratio FREQ2/FREQ1 = 1.48 (a DETUNED perfect fifth), deliberately inharmonic so the partials beat/buzz against each other and read as "metallic"; the 808 cowbell uses a two-stage decay (fast transient snap → shorter clangorous ringing tail, on the order of ~100–400 ms) bandpassed to a few hundred Hz–~2 kHz (outputchannel.com; baratatronix). The synthesis referent is Chowning's canonical FM bell (JAES 1973): an INHARMONIC carrier:modulator ratio near 1:1.4 (≈1:√2 for maximal inharmonicity), a modulation index that DECAYS from bright→pure over the note, and a long exponential amplitude decay — those three together (inharmonic sidebands + index decay + exp amp decay) are exactly what makes "metallic bell" (CCRMA CLM tutorial; Native Instruments).

PITCH/PARTIALS: Two stacked inharmonic spectra drive this voice. (1) The Bell BODY: ratios normalized to nominal=1.0 are hum 0.25, prime 0.50, tierce 0.60, quint 0.75, nominal 1.00, then 1.5/2.0/2.6/3.2/4.0… up to 12.0 — the canonical church-bell series (hum/prime/tierce/quint/nominal; octave-twelfth-upper-octave ≈ 2:3:4); verified against Hibberts/Rossing and already pinned correct in the audit §3-A. The minor-third TIERCE (0.60) is what gives bells their plaintive metallic colour and is intrinsic to this body. (2) The FMImpulse EXCITER spectrum: carrier at the velocity-driven base freq (400 Hz soft → 2500 Hz hard), sidebands at carrier ± k·modulator. fmRatio≈0.4 → modulator ratio ≈ 2.2 (between the Chowning 1.4 bell and the 808 cowbell 1.48 detuned-fifth, firmly in the clangorous inharmonic zone), so the exciter injects a dense non-integer sideband cloud into the bell modes.

INHARMONICITY: Both layers are inharmonic by construction. No additional modeStretch is needed (the bell ratios are already non-integer); leaving Stretch neutral keeps the canonical bell spectrum, which is the safer "filler" default than warping it further.

DECAY (T60): Real church bells ring for many seconds (hum slowest), but the 808-style perc filler is intentionally short-to-medium (cowbell tail ~100–400 ms). Bell body base decay = lerp(0.4,1.4,material)·(1+0.1·size); at material 0.7, size 0.25 → ~0.85 s base, then ×exp(lerp(ln0.3,ln3.0,decay)). Decay norm 0.2 → ×0.43 → ~0.37 s body T60 — a short metallic ping, not a sustained carillon. The FMImpulse amp env (~80 ms, fixed) gives the bright FM transient that decays faster than the body ring (index env ~30 ms → bright→pure, the Chowning signature).

TRANSIENT/CLICK: A bell strike is a hard clapper contact → broadband click. The FMImpulse already supplies a sharp metallic attack; a modest Click layer (~0.15 mix, bright ~bandpass center) adds the beater contact tick and half-feeds the body to excite the modes. NO pitch glide (bells don't "kerthump"; tensionMod is Membrane-only anyway). NOISE: none — a clean metallic ping has essentially no noise bed (cowbell/cowperc are tonal), so Noise Mix = 0.

MATERIAL: Very metallic → high material (0.7) drives bell brightness 0.7+0.3·0.7 = 0.91 (low b3, long-ringing upper partials), and b3 override forced to 0.0 = pure flat damping = metal (no f² woody roll-off), per the 808-kit perc voicing.

bodyModel: Membrane (Bessel circular drumhead, 48 modes) — provides the pitched sub fundamental and inharmonic low-mode body. f0 = 500·0.1^size, rescaled by the pitch envelope. The Membrane is chosen (over Plate/Bell) because a kick fundamentally needs a strong, tunable low fundamental with a glide; the membrane's lowest Bessel modes ((0,1) fundamental, (1,1)=1.59×, (2,1)=2.14×) give a slightly inharmonic — but still low-and-weighty — body, and tensionMod (the kerthump glide) is Membrane-ONLY in this engine.
exciter: FMImpulse (Chowning bell-FM). carrier ratio fixed 1.0, modulator ratio = 1.0+3.0·fmRatio.
