params.physicalTarget: bipolar +0.30: global skewBias 1−0.3·0.30≈0.91 + per-mode tilt ratio^(−0.30) → low partials louder/longer, highs decay faster
params.rationale: Gong HF modes decay faster than LF; the low fifth (hum/prime/quint) is the longest tail — exactly a positive decaySkew.
params.citation: Ohm Store (high modes exhaust energy faster); audit M-5 per-mode tilt on Bell; brief decaySkew 0.65

params.physicalTarget: amount 0.12 of an 8-harmonic 1/k series at f0≈113 Hz (−6 dB/oct)
params.rationale: A small dose of tonal harmonic weight thickens the low body / fundamental wash under the inharmonic bell partials without making it sound pitched-synthetic.
params.citation: audit §3-B mode_inject→1/k; F&R (low partials carry the body)

params.physicalTarget: amount 0.8: drive=1+6·vel·0.8·env(RMS) → strong velocity-driven recip-sqrt brightening, sustained through the ring
params.rationale: The gong's signature: a HARD strike blooms HF energy that a soft strike never produces (a nonlinear, amplitude-dependent cascade). This is Membrum's literature-grounded stand-in for the von-Kármán energy cascade.
params.citation: Legge & Fletcher JASA 1989 (nonlinear HF transfer ∝ strike hardness); Bilbao/Ducceschi DAFx 2023 wave-turbulence; audit M-3/M-4 redesign

params.physicalTarget: morphEnabled≥0.5 → Material Morph active each note
params.rationale: Enables a per-note material/brightness sweep to mimic the spectrum OPENING after the strike (the bloom rising in).

params.physicalTarget: Material settles to 0.55 (warmer, lower partials dominate the tail)
params.rationale: After the HF shimmer fades the tail is darker and dominated by the low fifth — material relaxing from 0.85→0.55 captures that.

params.rationale: Sets the bloom-settle time to ~0.4 s, within the up-to-~1 s gong build-up window.

params.rationale: Exponential material relaxation matches the natural energy-decay shape of the bloom better than a linear ramp.

params.rationale: Smears the discrete Bell partials toward the dense, near-continuous inharmonic column of a real master tam-tam (20–50+ overtones).

defaultedParams.param: Macro Tightness / Brightness / Body Size / Punch / Complexity
defaultedParams.why: All five one-knob macros left neutral at 0.5 (zero delta). Every underlying axis they would proxy (material, decay, decaySkew, cutoff, modeInject, size, stretch, pitch-env, coupling, nonlinear) is already set explicitly to its physically-derived target, so any macro delta would only fight those values.

physics:
FUNDAMENTAL / PITCH: A large Chau gong / orchestral tam-tam has a low, only-quasi-definite pitch. Measured examples: a 27" Chau tam-tam reads ~97 Hz (G2) with the next strong partial at ~149 Hz (≈D3) — fundamental and second partial sit close to a perfect FIFTH and hold near-equal energy long after the mid partials die, giving the characteristic sustained low fifth-interval tail (Ohm Store harmonic-architecture analysis; gong/meditation literature gives 60–140 Hz for large Earth-gong/tam-tams). Bossed gongs are tuned (definite pitch); a FLAT tam-tam vibrates in many modes → a 'crash' rather than a clean note (Wikipedia/Fletcher & Rossing). Mapping: Bell body at Size 0.85 → f0 = 800·0.1^0.85 ≈ 113 Hz nominal; the Bell partial table puts the hum at 0.25·f0 ≈ 28 Hz, prime at 0.5·f0 ≈ 57 Hz, tierce 0.60·f0 ≈ 68 Hz, quint 0.75·f0 ≈ 85 Hz, nominal 113 Hz — the prime→quint (0.5→0.75) is exactly a fifth, reproducing the gong's low fifth tail; the very low hum/prime supply the 28–57 Hz subsonic 'wash'.

PARTIAL STRUCTURE / INHARMONICITY: Master tam-tams carry 20 to 50+ distinct audible overtones, strongly INHARMONIC (no integer series); the bronze plate's stiffness and non-flat profile spread partials off any harmonic ladder (Fletcher & Rossing ch.20; Rossing 'Science of Percussion'). Real tam-tams have a near-continuous dense column of inharmonic partials rather than discrete tuned ones. Mapping: Bell ratios give the discrete inharmonic skeleton; modeStretch (>neutral) bends the upper partials further off-ratio for the metallic stretch, and high modeScatter dithers the modal frequencies (~10–15%) to smear the discrete lines toward the dense, organic tam-tam column.

THE 'BLOOM' / SHIMMER (defining feature): When struck hard, a gong/tam-tam shows a slow build-up where energy CASCADES from the initially-excited low modes UP to high-frequency modes over a timescale up to ~1 second after the strike — these HF modes fail to appear at all if the gong is hit softly, proving the transfer is a NONLINEAR (amplitude-dependent) process (Fletcher & Rossing; Legge & Fletcher, 'Nonlinearity, chaos, and the sound of shallow gongs', JASA 1989). Modern physical models reproduce this with the von Kármán / Föppl nonlinear thin-plate equations as a wave-turbulence energy cascade low→high (Bilbao, Webb, Wang & Ducceschi, 'Real-Time Gong Synthesis', DAFx 2023; mdphys.org plates: 'turbulent cascade developing from low to high frequencies', 'shimmering and crashing'). Mapping: Membrum has no true von-Kármán cascade, so the bloom is approximated by (a) velocity-driven NonlinearCoupling (amplitude-driven recip-sqrt waveshaping → louder = more odd-harmonic / HF energy, sustained through the ring — the audit's literature-grounded amplitude-brightening stand-in) at HEAVY amount, plus (b) a Mallet→Bell Material Morph that sweeps brightness/material UP over the first ~0.3–0.6 s so the spectrum opens after the strike, and (c) feedback exciter energy is NOT used (Mallet selected) — the brief's 'feedback 0.10' and 'fmRatio 0.30' are no-ops for the Mallet exciter and are documented as defaulted.

DECAY / T60: Real tam-tams ring for many seconds to minutes; high-frequency modes decay FASTER than low ones, so after the shimmer blooms and fades, the low fifth-interval (hum/prime/quint) tail is what sustains (Ohm Store; F&R). Per-band: HF shimmer is transient-to-seconds, low partials are very long. Mapping: Decay 0.95 → ~3× multiplier on the Bell base decay (longest ring); decaySkew 0.65 → bipolar +0.30, which both lengthens low-mode decay and tilts per-mode amplitude toward the low partials (ratio^(−skew)) — i.e. fast highs, sustaining lows, matching the gong's HF-fast / LF-slow decay law.

ATTACK / CONTACT: A soft beater (gong mallet) gives a broad, soft contact — NOT a sharp click; the energy is dumped low and blooms upward. Tam-tams are struck about a hand-width off-center for max low output (Wikipedia). Mapping: Mallet exciter (soft beater) + modest click layer; strike position pulled off the dead-center antinode.

NOISE CONTENT: The dense HF shimmer reads partly as a bright metallic hiss/wash rather than tonal partials. A modest bright (violet/white) noise layer supports the sizzle without dominating the tonal body.

PITCH GLIDE: Large nonlinear gongs exhibit a downward pitch drop after a very hard strike (geometric/tension nonlinearity; Bilbao/Ducceschi note pitch glides from the von-Kármán nonlinearity). Membrum's tensionMod glide is MEMBRANE-ONLY (no-op on Bell), so this is documented as unavailable for this body; the pitch envelope is left off to keep the gong's steady low fifth.

MATERIAL: Bronze (~76.5% Cu / 22.4% Sn, traces Pb/Zn/Fe — Wikipedia gong alloy) — long-ringing, bright metallic. Mapping: high Material → metallic (low f²-damping), bright Bell brightness.

bodyModel: NoiseBody
exciter: Friction

params.rationale: The continuous scrape is a stick-slip friction process, not an impact; Friction's rosin-jitter/stick-slip core is the correct micro-collision excitation for a scraped idiophone.
params.citation: arXiv:2112.08984 (scraping = roughness-driven impulse train through resonant filter); Avanzini/Serafin/Rocchesso DAFx-02 elasto-plastic friction

params.physicalTarget: idx=clamp(round(1.0*5),0,5)=5 -> NoiseBody (32 plate-ratio modes + internal noise layer)
params.rationale: The gourd is a broadband, weakly-tuned resonator, not a tonal bank; NoiseBody gives loose inharmonic plate modes plus an internal noise bed = the rasping body.
params.citation: Iwami JSDD 2008 (few low-Q gourd body modes over broadband content)

params.physicalTarget: NoiseBody: internal noise cutoff=clamp(1500+5000*0.65)=4750 Hz; brightness=0.7+0.3*0.65=0.895; modal decay base lerp(0.2,1.0,0.65)=0.72 s
params.rationale: Mid-high material gives the bright, rasping upper-mid emphasis of a scraped gourd without going fully metallic; raises the internal noise cutoff into the audible rasp band.
params.citation: Wikipedia/Carved Culture (metal=higher, hardwood=lower pitch -> material is the brightness lever)

params.rationale: A güiro is a small hand resonator with no low boom; a high f0 keeps the loose body modes up in the bright region where they merely tint the scrape.

params.physicalTarget: decayTime multiplier exp(lerp(ln0.3,ln3,0.30))=0.52x on base 0.72 -> ~0.37 s modal; noise env 60ms*0.52 -> ~31 ms
params.rationale: The body only rings while the stick is in contact; short decay so the scrape stops when the gesture stops, no sustained ring.
Default off-center weighting spreads energy across modes for a broadband body color; exact position is not perceptually critical on a noise-dominated scraper.
Perc filler / accent instrument; sits below kick/snare in the mix, slightly attenuated.

params.rationale: Per spec: firmer stick pressure deepens the stick-slip rasp/squeal intensity that defines the scrape texture.

params.physicalTarget: parallel noise layer mix=0.65 (0.65*env*filteredNoise*0.243 into mix)
params.rationale: The broadband rasp IS the güiro; the noise layer is the primary voice, well above the modal body.
params.citation: Wikipedia (zipper/ratchet rasp is the defining sound)

params.rationale: Per spec: a DARKER filtered noise than a hat/cymbal -> a woody/gourd rasp rather than a hissy sizzle.

params.physicalTarget: 0.55 -> White (boundary: <0.55 Pink, else White) noise source

A single scrape stroke is short; the noise fades with the friction contact, no long tail.
Mild noise-filter resonance keeps the rasp broadband (a gourd is low-Q); no whistling peak.

params.physicalTarget: bipolar 0.55*2-1 = +0.10: skewBias=1-0.3*0.10=0.97 on decay; per-mode tilt ratio^(-0.10) lifts high modes slightly
params.rationale: Per spec: a gentle tilt toward the fast/high modes brightens the scrape and shortens low-mode ring, matching the upper-mid rasp emphasis.
params.citation: spec decaySkew present; audit M-5 per-mode tilt on all bodies

params.physicalTarget: off-sentinel -> b1 = 0.2+0.42*49.8 = 21.1 s^-1 flat damping (overrides Decay-derived flat damping)
params.rationale: A loose gourd has fairly fast flat decay; 21 s^-1 keeps the body modes short so they color but don't ring out.

params.physicalTarget: off-sentinel -> b3 = 0 (pure flat damping, highs not f^2-damped)
params.rationale: Lets the bright upper body modes survive (the rasp lives up high); material already supplies the brightness, b3=0 avoids a woody high-cut that would dull the scrape.

params.physicalTarget: scatter=0.30 -> ~4.5% sinusoidal frequency dither on modal ratios
params.rationale: Irregular hand-carved ridges/gourd geometry detune the body modes; scatter adds that organic imperfection.
Per spec: NO click. A scrape has no sharp beater/contact transient — it fades in with friction, so a click would read as a wrong percussive 'tick'.

params.rationale: Air-loading is a Membrane-only frequency correction; NoiseBody ignores it, set to 0 to be explicit/non-membrane.

physics:
PHYSICAL INSTRUMENT. The güiro is a scraped idiophone (Hornbostel-Sachs 112.23): a hollow calabash gourd (or wood/fiberglass/metal) with a row of parallel transverse notches/ridges cut along one side, played by dragging a stick (pua) or tines across the ridges (Wikipedia; Carved Culture; ipassio). The sound is NOT a struck tone — it is a "zipper-like ratchet" / rasping scrape with no definite pitch. There is no fundamental; perceived "pitch" is governed by material (metal güiro = higher, hardwood = lower) and the spacing/scrape-speed of the ridge impacts.

EXCITATION = stick-slip / micro-impact train. As the stick crosses each ridge it produces a micro-collision; the train of these impacts is the broadband excitation source. Scrape speed sets the impact rate (a long slow scrape = sustained low-rate rasp; a fast flick = staccato burst). This is exactly the source-filter / micro-collision impulse-train model of scraping sounds in the literature (arXiv 2112.08984 "Object-based synthesis of scraping and rolling sounds": a roughness-driven impulse train, force/speed-controlled, through a resonant body filter; Avanzini/Serafin/Rocchesso elasto-plastic stick-slip friction model, DAFx-02). The stick-slip junction itself (not a clean impulse) is what gives the rasp its rough, noisy, slightly squealing texture — distinct from a struck or shaken percussion transient (which is a single impact / particle cloud).

RESONANT BODY = the gourd. A hollow gourd is an irregular, weakly-tuned cavity/shell with broad, high-Q-poor, inharmonic modes and a cavity (Helmholtz) resonance at the mouth. Studies of frog-shaped güiros (Iwami, JSDD 2008) identify a handful of named body modes (e.g. "yawn"/mouth and "grinding-teeth" modes) — i.e. a small set of low-Q resonances coloring an otherwise broadband scrape, not a tonal partial series. So the spectrum is: broadband rasp (mid/high-emphasised, the audible "brightness" of the scrape) lightly filtered by a few loose body resonances; energy is concentrated in the upper-mid/high band (the characteristic bright "shhrrt"), with short decay — the body rings only as long as the stick is in contact, so per-note T60 is short (tens of ms, governed by the friction envelope ~46 ms here, not a long modal ring).

DRIVING VALUES FOR THE PATCH: (a) NO definite pitch -> keep the modal body small/high and de-emphasised, used only as a loose color filter; pitch-env OFF. (b) broadband, mid/high-bright noise IS the sound -> the parallel filtered-noise layer is the primary voice, lowpassed to a "darker filtered" rasp per spec (~2.2 kHz) rather than a hissy cymbal. (c) rough stick-slip texture -> Friction exciter with raised pressure. (d) short scrape -> short modal flat-damping + short noise decay, no long ring, no click transient (a scrape has no sharp beater attack — it fades in/out with contact). (e) material lever (metal=bright/high, wood=dark/low) -> Material mid-high for a bright-ish gourd; a metal güiro would push Material and cutoffs up. (f) natural ridge irregularity -> a little mode scatter.

archetype: Indian Temple Bell (struck tuned bell — bonshō / Hindu temple ghanta)
bodyModel: Bell (offset 1 = 4). Church/temple-bell Chladni partial bank, 16 modes, Simpson-tuned ratios hum/prime/tierce/quint/nominal = 0.25/0.50/0.60/0.75/1.00 with the hum a quarter of the nominal — exactly the published tuned-bell partial structure (Hibberts; Perrin/Rossing). This is THE correct body for a tuned struck bell; Plate=free cymbal/gong, Shell=bar/glockenspiel, String=tubular bell. f0_nominal = 800·0.1^size.
exciter: Mallet (offset 0 = 1). Soft beater (ImpactExciter, hardness lerp(0.1,0.6,vel), darker SVF brightness lerp(-0.5,-0.2,vel)). A temple bell is struck externally with a wooden beam (shu-moku) or padded mallet, not a hard metal clapper — a soft, low-centroid contact that launches the low partials and the warm hum rather than a bright metallic ping. The audit's measured-strike body norm + click layer carry the attack 'atari'.

params.rationale: Soft external beam/mallet strike of a temple bell — low-centroid contact launching the warm low partials, not a bright clapper ping.
Tuned struck bell → church/temple-bell Chladni partial bank (Simpson hum/prime/tierce/quint/nominal), the only body with the inharmonic 0.25-hum bell spectrum.
High-tin bell-metal bronze: very low internal damping, long-lived upper partials, warm-but-ringing. High material keeps the long hum and pure tone.

params.physicalTarget: f0_nominal=800*0.1^0.5=253 Hz; hum 63 Hz, prime 127, tierce 152, quint 190
params.rationale: Mid-size temple bell with a warm low hum; deliberately lower/fuller than crotales/tingsha (Size 0.16–0.25). Task-specified 0.50.

params.physicalTarget: multiplier=0.3*10^0.92=2.5x; body ring ~3s, bounded by 5s bank clamp + b1 floor
params.rationale: The bell's signature multi-second ring — bonshō oshi ~10s, Indian temple bells ~6–10s. Long, just under the bank's 5s ceiling.

params.physicalTarget: theta=0.18*(pi/2)=0.283 rad; meridional |cos(m*theta)| near antinode → full partial set, hum-weighted
params.rationale: A clapper/beam strikes a fixed soundbow near a meridional antinode, exciting the full principal-partial set with strong hum/prime — a low strikePos keeps all low partials present.
A long-ringing tonal bell needs headroom under the bus limiter; slightly below the 0.8 default so the sustained hum doesn't dominate a kit.
Real bells are not perfectly Simpson-tuned — a mild positive stretch spreads the upper partials for authentic bell inharmonicity without going gong-metallic.

params.physicalTarget: phys=0.78*2-1=+0.56; skewBias=1-0.3*0.56=0.83 on global decay; per-mode tilt ratio^(-0.56)
params.rationale: Positive skew lifts the sub-nominal partials (hum/prime, ratio<1 → ratio^-0.56>1) and trims the highs, so the long tail is the warm hum while the strike brightness fades first — exactly the bonshō atari→hum behaviour. Task-specified 0.78.

params.physicalTarget: b1=0.2+0.18*49.8=9.2 s^-1 flat damping floor (overrides Decay-derived flat term)
params.rationale: Low flat damping = very long RT60 floor so the hum sustains for seconds (high-tin bronze, low loss). Below the 0.40 default to lengthen the ring.

params.physicalTarget: b3=0.06*1e-3=6e-5 s (Hz convention); near-pure flat damping, weak f^2 term
params.rationale: Very low frequency-squared damping = metal, not wood: upper partials live long (the 1400 Hz overtone lasts nearly as long as the fundamental in temple-bell measurements).

params.physicalTarget: ~6% sinusoidal frequency dither on modal ratios
params.rationale: Real cast bells have slight per-mode detune (doublets/beating from imperfect axial symmetry) producing the characteristic slow shimmer; a touch of scatter adds that organic beating.

params.physicalTarget: mix 0.28 * kStandaloneOutputGain(0.445) attack transient, half into exciter feed
params.rationale: The soft 'atari' contact of beam-on-bronze — present but understated so the tonal body dominates.
A padded mallet/beam has a longer, softer contact than a hard stick — a slightly extended click burst.
A dull-to-mid contact thud, not a sharp metallic tick — the soft strike of a temple-bell beam.
Temple bells are nearly pure-tonal — almost no broadband content; a whisper of air at the strike at most.
A single temple bell sits centered; spatialization is a kit-arrangement choice, not intrinsic to the instrument.

defaultedParams.why: No-op with the Mallet exciter — fmRatio only feeds FMImpulse; the task's 'fmRatio 0.30' is inert here, so left at struct default 0.5 (which never reaches the exciter).
Feedback-exciter-only; no-op with Mallet.
NoiseBurst-exciter-only; no-op with Mallet.
Friction-exciter-only; no-op with Mallet.
ToneShaper SVF left transparent (cutoff norm 1.0 = bypass) — the Bell body already has the correct pure spectrum; no post-filter sculpting needed.
Filter envelope inactive (amount 0.5 = no modulation); a bell has no filter-sweep motion.
Bypassed at 0 — a clean tonal bell wants no added saturation/folding harmonics.
PitchEnv Time 0 = disabled. A struck bell has no 808-style pitch glide; its only frequency motion is partial beating (via modeScatter), not an envelope.
0 = exact bypass. Injecting an INTEGER harmonic series would contradict the bell's inharmonic spectrum and muddy the pure hum.
0 = exact bypass. A modestly-struck temple bell is in its linear regime; amplitude-driven brightening is a heavy-gong/cymbal effect, not a temple-bell character.
Material morph off — the bell's timbre is static through the ring; no intra-note material sweep.
0 = no choke. A bell rings freely; it is not a mute-group pair like open/closed hat.
0 = main bus. No multi-out routing intent for a single bell voice.
Left at 0.5 default — only audible if kit-level Global Coupling/sympathetic engine is enabled; harmless and arrangement-dependent.
All neutral 0.5 (zero delta) — the explicit per-param values already place the voice; macros are left for the user's one-knob performance tweaks.
No-op while Noise Mix ~0; noise layer is effectively muted for this pure-tonal bell.
Membrane-only frequency correction — a no-op on the Bell body; set to 0 conceptually but irrelevant.
Secondary shell disabled (off by default) — a bell IS the resonator; no separate head↔shell body to couple.
Membrane-only; gated off on Bell. A struck bell has no tension-modulation pitch glide.
1.0 = enabled (default); the pad is an active voice in the kit.

physics: FUNDAMENTAL/PITCH: Temple/Buddhist bells (bonshō) and Hindu ghanta sit low and warm; bell-physics literature places the perceived strike note near the NOMINAL, with a prominent low HUM a (nominally) double-octave below it. Indian/temple-bell summaries cite fundamentals roughly 500–700 Hz for the bright strike component with overtones near 1400/2100/2600 Hz, but the carrying 'hum' tone is far lower — a mid-size temple bell hums in the ~60–130 Hz region. Chosen Size 0.50 → f0_nominal = 800·0.1^0.5 = 253 Hz, giving hum 63 Hz, prime 127 Hz, tierce 152 Hz (the bell minor-third), quint 190 Hz, nominal 253 Hz — a full, warm, low-hum bell, deliberately lower/fuller than crotales/tingsha (which use Size ~0.16–0.25 → 550–450 Hz nominal). PARTIAL STRUCTURE & INHARMONICITY: bells are inharmonic — partials are fractional multiples of the nominal, NOT an integer harmonic series. The Membrum Bell ratios {0.25,0.50,0.60,0.75,1.00,1.50,2.00,2.60,...} already encode the canonical Simpson/true-harmonic tuning (hum 0.25, prime 0.50, tierce 0.60 = minor third above prime, quint 0.75, nominal 1.00) from Hibberts/Perrin/Rossing. A small modeStretch (norm ~0.40 → physical ~1.10) lifts the upper partials slightly to model the real bell's spreading inharmonic upper spectrum (real bells are not perfectly Simpson-tuned). DECAY/T60: the defining feature is an extremely long ring — the bonshō hum (oshi) lasts up to ~10 s and the final decay (okuri) up to a minute; Indian temple bells commonly ring 6–10 s, symbolically '~7 s'. Lower partials decay slowest (long hum), higher partials decay faster (the strike brightness fades first, leaving the pure hum). Decay 0.92 → ~2.5× base multiplier → multi-second body, bounded by the bank's internal 5 s clamp + the b1 override floor; high decaySkew biases the long tail onto the low hum partials. TRANSIENT/CLICK: a soft contact 'atari' — a clean clear strike, NOT a sharp metallic tick; modeled by a low-mix, dull-to-mid click. NOISE: temple bells are nearly pure-tonal; broadband noise content is minimal (a faint air/contact whoosh at most) — noise layer near-muted. PITCH GLIDE: a hand-held/large struck bell has no membrane tension 'kerthump'; tensionMod is Membrane-only and a no-op on Bell, so any subtle warble comes from beating between near-degenerate partials, not a glide. MATERIAL: high-tin bronze (~78% Cu / 22% Sn 'bell metal', or panchaloha) — very metallic, low internal damping → high material value, very low b3 (long-lived upper partials).

archetype: Inharmonic Plate Tom (synthetic west-coast / experimental metallic tom)
bodyModel: Plate (BodyModelType=1) — free-plate Chladni (m+2n)^1.7 inharmonic 2-D mode bank, post-audit corrected. This is the deliberate antithesis of the membrane tom: a struck plate's clangy, inharmonic 2-D spectrum used as a tuned tom.
exciter: FMImpulse (ExciterType=4) — Chowning bell-FM transient injects an inharmonic, metallic, clangy strike into the plate. Secondary recommendation Feedback (5) for a more aggressive sustained west-coast variant; Mallet (1) for a softer beater. FMImpulse is the primary because its modulator:carrier ratio is the cleanest way to seed the body with metallic, bell-like inharmonic energy that matches the plate's own inharmonicity.

params.rationale: Chowning bell-FM strike seeds the plate with inharmonic/metallic energy that matches the body's inharmonicity — the cleanest clangy onset.
Free-plate Chladni (m+2n)^1.7 inharmonic 2-D spectrum — the antithesis of the near-harmonic membrane tom; the defining choice for this archetype.

params.physicalTarget: brightness = 0.5+0.5*0.82 = 0.91 (very metallic); plate decay base = lerp(0.4,2.0,0.82) ≈ 1.71 s
params.rationale: High = bright, metallic, long-ringing highs (weak b3*f^2 damping) — a clangy struck-metal character, not wood.
Places the inharmonic plate fundamental in the rack-tom band so the clangy partials read as a 'tom' not a cymbal.

params.physicalTarget: decayTime ≈ baseDecayTime * exp(lerp(ln0.3,ln3.0,0.45))*skewBias ≈ 1.71 * 0.83 ≈ ~1.4 s (with skew tilt)
params.rationale: Medium ring: longer than a damped real tom head but bounded so it reads as a percussive metallic tom, not a sustained plate reverb.

params.physicalTarget: rho=0.40+0.50*0.62≈0.71 radius, theta=0.62*pi/2≈0.97 rad azimuth → mid-edge strike exciting many (m,n) modes
params.rationale: Off-center mid-edge strike on a plate maximizes the inharmonic upper-mode balance (clang) versus a dead-center axisymmetric hit.
params.citation: plate_modes.h computePlateAmplitude; Rossing Science of Percussion Ch.3

params.rationale: Standard per-pad loudness; downstream measuredStrike body norm (-6 dBFS) + bus limiter own the ceiling, so 0.8 is musical headroom.
If/when engaged, a gentle LP tames only the harshest top of the metallic ring; default cutoff keeps it transparent.
Just below bypass — a soft ceiling on the clangiest HF partials so the metallic tom stays focused, not fizzy.
No resonant emphasis wanted; the body supplies all the peaks.

params.physicalTarget: amount = 0.42*2-1 = -0.16 → cutoff sweeps DOWN ~0.5 oct over the hit
params.rationale: Slight downward filter motion darkens the tail (highs die first, mirroring plate damping) for a natural metallic decay.
A touch of odd-harmonic grit thickens the metallic strike (flavour, not level, after M-2 makeup) — west-coast edge.
Light wavefolding adds extra inharmonic upper partials — the signature west-coast/experimental clang.
Glide STARTS above the settle pitch to reproduce the tom 'kerthump' downward bend (Plate cannot use tensionModAmt, so the pitch env carries the glide).

params.physicalTarget: 20*100^0.5 ≈ 200 Hz settle (≈ scales body to its ~179 Hz natural)
params.rationale: Settle pitch near the body fundamental; Start>End gives a ~5-semitone downward glide proportional across all plate modes.
Short, fast glide = a HIGH-tension tom (high tension → faster, smaller bend); also the master enable for the whole pitch-env feature.

params.physicalTarget: curveAmount = 2*0.2-1 = -0.6 (fast initial drop, exp decay shape)
params.rationale: Classic 808/tom exponential pitch drop — most of the bend happens in the first few ms.

params.physicalTarget: physical = 0.5+0.6*1.5 = 1.4 → stretchNorm=(1.4-0.5)/1.5=0.6 into bank → sqrt(1+B*(k+1)^2), B up to 0.6*0.01
params.rationale: Stretches the already-inharmonic plate partials further apart for a more bell/metallic, dispersive clang.

params.physicalTarget: phys = 0.38*2-1 = -0.24 → boosts high modes by ratio^(+0.24); skewBias=1-0.3*(-0.24)=1.07
params.rationale: Negative skew tilts energy toward the HIGH inharmonic partials so the clang stays present in the tail instead of decaying to the fundamental.
params.citation: AUDIT M-5 (per-mode decaySkew tilt on Plate)

params.rationale: ModeInject overlays an INTEGER-harmonic 1/k series at f0 — the opposite of this archetype's inharmonic plate spectrum; leave OFF so it doesn't 'harmonicize' the clang.
params.citation: mode_inject.h (8 integer harmonics) — would fight the inharmonic body

params.physicalTarget: drive = 1+6*vel*0.5*env; env-level recipSqrt waveshaping → louder = brighter/clangier
params.rationale: Reproduces struck-metal nonlinear coupling: hit harder = more high-mode/broadband energy (tap=bell, hard=clang), velocity-sensitive — core to the experimental plate-tom character.
params.citation: AUDIT M-3/M-4 redesign (Stowell cymbal; Ducceschi/Bilbao von Kármán)

params.rationale: Toms ring freely; not a mute-group member.
No multi-out routing needed for a tom.

params.physicalTarget: modulatorRatio = 1.0+3.0*0.18 = 1.54 (near the 1.4 Chowning bell, slightly more inharmonic)
params.rationale: Non-integer modulator:carrier ratio = inharmonic bell/metallic FM strike that seeds the plate with clangy energy.

params.physicalTarget: 0.12 * env * lowpassed noise * 0.243 standalone gain (well under the body)
params.rationale: A metallic plate tom is tonal/inharmonic, not noisy — just a whisper of attack breath under the body.
params.citation: AUDIT gain budget (noise ~-18 dBFS under body -6 dBFS)

params.rationale: Neutral-bright noise so the small amount that is present reads as airy contact, not rumble.
Mild, unobtrusive — no pitched noise peak.
Short noise burst = attack-coupled contact, not a sustained hiss tail.
Neutral white contact noise; the body owns the spectral character.

params.physicalTarget: 0.4 * vel * env * bandpassed noise; half into exciter feed + full to output *0.445
params.rationale: A defined stick/mallet contact tick sharpens the metallic attack and also drives the plate modes.
Short, snappy contact for a crisp metallic onset.
Bright tick that matches the clangy upper partials — a sharp metallic stick attack, not a dull thud.

params.physicalTarget: b1 = 0.2+0.4*49.8 ≈ 20.1 s^-1 flat damping (overrides Decay-derived flat damping)
params.rationale: Sets the overall RT60 floor for a medium metallic ring; explicit because the controller cannot carry the -1 sentinel.

params.physicalTarget: b3 = 0.18e-3 = 1.8e-4 s (low f^2 damping → highs ring, metallic)
params.rationale: Low b3 keeps high inharmonic partials alive (metal, not wood) — the perceived material lever for clang.
params.citation: AUDIT §3-A (b3*f^2 = metal↔wood); EMT140 stainless = brighter/longer HF

params.physicalTarget: ~1.8% sinusoidal frequency dither on the plate ratios
params.rationale: Small organic detune breaks the exact generated ratios so repeated hits feel like a real imperfect plate, adding shimmer.
params.citation: modal_resonator_bank.h scatter; plate_modes.h (real plates split degeneracies)

params.physicalTarget: feedforward shell drive 0.4 (engages because Secondary Enabled on)
params.rationale: Drives the parallel metallic secondary shell — the 'metallic secondary' ring layer the archetype calls for.

params.physicalTarget: >=0.5 → 24-mode shell bank spun up at noteOn
params.rationale: Turns on the coupled secondary resonator for an extra inharmonic metallic layer under the plate.
A slightly lower secondary adds inharmonic low-mid weight and beating against the main plate — clangy complexity.
Bright, metallic secondary so the coupled layer is itself clangy, not a woody thud.

params.physicalTarget: 0 = off (and a NO-OP on Plate regardless — gated to Membrane body)
params.rationale: tensionModAmt only affects the Membrane body; on Plate it does nothing, so the tom glide is delivered via the pitch envelope instead. Left at 0 to be explicit.
Center by default; the kit author repositions toms across the stereo field per arrangement.
The pad sounds.

defaultedParams.why: No per-note material sweep wanted; the static high-metallic material already carries the clang, and the pitch-env + nonlinearCoupling provide the motion. Off by default.
Filter Env Amount is only -0.16; the default cubic ADSR (fast attack, ~moderate decay) is fine for a subtle tail darkening — no need to tune the envelope shape.
A single-segment Start→End exponential glide fully reproduces the tom kerthump; the 3-point knee is unnecessary complexity for this archetype (left at default OFF).

defaultedParams.param: Feedback Amount (33), NoiseBurst Duration (34), Friction Pressure (35)
defaultedParams.why: No-ops: the selected exciter is FMImpulse, so the Feedback/NoiseBurst/Friction secondary params do nothing. (FM Ratio (32) is the only meaningful exciter-secondary param here.)

Per-pad participation in the GLOBAL cross-pad sympathetic network is only audible when the kit-level Global Coupling/Tom Resonance > 0; left at neutral 0.5 — it's a kit-level decision, not part of the single-pad voice.

All left at neutral 0.5 (zero delta). The recipe sets the underlying material/decay/cutoff/pitch-env/coupling/modeInject directly; the macros are live-performance proxies that must stay neutral so they don't double-apply on top of the baked values.
Membrane-only frequency correction; a complete NO-OP on the Plate body. Left at the struct default 0.6 (irrelevant here).

physics: FUNDAMENTAL/PITCH: target ~179 Hz plate fundamental (Size norm 0.65, f0=800·0.1^size), placing the inharmonic partials in the rack-tom register (real toms: rack 200-500 Hz, floor 70-120 Hz — iDrumTune/DrumForum). PARTIALS/INHARMONICITY: free-plate Chladni f_mn ∝ (m+2n)^1.7 (P=1.4-2.4 measured for cymbals, Rossing&Peterson; classical Kirchhoff P=2) — Membrum's corrected kPlateRatios (1.00,1.11,1.99,2.21,3.25,3.61,3.96,4.75…) encode exactly this irregular, non-integer 2-D spectrum, the defining feature; modeStretch (physical 1.4) disperses partials further; modeScatter ~1.8% adds organic detune. DECAY/T60: Chaigne&Askenfelt R=b1+b3·f² → high modes decay faster than low (EMT140 <1 s at 10 kHz; ScienceDirect plate-reverb; Valhalla), targeted ~1.4 s overall body ring (low b3=1.8e-4 keeps highs alive=metal; b1≈20 s⁻¹ RT60 floor); decaySkew −0.24 tilts energy to high modes to sustain the clang. TRANSIENT/CLICK: FM bell-impulse (modulator ratio 1.54, near 1.4 Chowning bell) supplies inharmonic onset; added 2.75 ms bandpassed click @3.4 kHz sharpens the metallic stick attack. NOISE: minimal (tonal archetype) — Noise Mix 0.12, white, 39 ms, well under the body per the -6/-18 dBFS gain budget. PITCH GLIDE: downward 'kerthump' (high tension transiently raises pitch then relaxes down; JASA 150:202; 808 forces freq down as it fades — Baratatronix). CRITICAL: tensionModAmt is Membrane-ONLY and a no-op on Plate, so the glide is realized via the ToneShaper pitch envelope (Start 332 Hz → End 200 Hz, 45 ms, exp fast-drop curve) which post-H-3 rescales all plate modes proportionally — a fast/small glide reads as a HIGH-tension tom. MATERIAL: metal (Material 0.82 → brightness 0.91); nonlinearCoupling 0.5 adds velocity-driven brightening (tap=bell/hard=clang, Stowell; Ducceschi/Bilbao von Kármán cascade); a coupled metallic secondary shell (f0≈125 Hz, bright) adds the requested second inharmonic ring with beating against the main plate.

archetype: Kalimba / thumb-piano (mbira) — 8 pitched tines across one scale (mallet/plucked metal lamella)
bodyModel: Bell (church-bell Chladni partial set, 16 modes, nominal partial = pitch, hum at 0.25·f0). Chosen over Shell because a real kalimba TINE is a clamped-free cantilever beam with an intermediate (simply-supported) bridge, NOT a free-free bar: its overtone sequence is strongly inharmonic, non-integer, and bridge-dependent, with a dominant fundamental and weak, fast-dying upper partials (King/JASA 2012; Wikipedia Mbira). The Shell body is the regular free-free glockenspiel series 1:2.757:5.404 (too 'tuned-metal-bar / xylophone'), whereas the Bell partial set (sub-nominal hum + non-integer 0.6/0.75/1.5/2.0 partials) better reproduces the inharmonic-attack-then-pure-tone tine character plus the resonator-box weight. Pitch is set per-pad by Size; the same instrument is graded across the 8-pad row.
exciter: Mallet (ImpactExciter backend: hardness=lerp(0.1,0.6,vel), brightness=lerp(-0.5,-0.2,vel), mass 0.3 → ~1–8 ms soft contact). A thumbnail/thumb-pad plucking a metal tine is a soft, rounded, low-hardness contact (not a hard beater Impulse, not a noise burst). The Mallet's darker SVF + heavier low-velocity contact matches the soft pluck; the iconic bright metallic 'ping' of the tine onset is supplied by the always-on Click layer, which both feeds the body modes and adds the audible tick.

params.rationale: Soft thumb-pluck of a metal tine = low-hardness rounded contact, not a hard beater or noise burst.
Inharmonic non-integer partials + dominant fundamental of a clamped-free tine map better to the Bell Chladni set than the regular free-free Shell series.

params.physicalTarget: Bell nominal f0 = 800·0.1^0.3 ≈ 401 Hz (≈G4); the 8 pads sweep size 0.485→0.085 = C4 261.6 Hz → E5 659 Hz
params.rationale: Size IS the per-pad pitch; the 8-pad row is one diatonic/pentatonic octave (C4–E5). Per-pad values listed in markdown.

params.physicalTarget: Bell brightness=0.7+0.3·0.55≈0.865 (very metallic, low high-mode damping); baseDecay=lerp(0.4,1.4,0.55)≈0.965 s
params.rationale: Steel tine = metallic; graded 0.40 (low/dark tines) → 0.80 (high/bright tines) across the row so small tines ring brighter.

params.physicalTarget: decay multiplier = exp(lerp(ln0.3,ln3.0,0.45)) ≈ 0.86× on Bell base ≈ 0.83 s ring
params.rationale: Moderate metallic ring; graded 0.60 (low tines, longer) → 0.30 (high tines, shorter) — higher tines sustain less.

params.physicalTarget: Bell strike azimuth θ=0.15·π/2≈0.236 rad; near a meridional antinode → all partials excited, nominal strong
params.rationale: A tine is plucked near its free tip where all flexural modes have an antinode → strong fundamental + present overtones; low value keeps the nominal partial dominant.
Pitched melodic instrument; slightly below the 0.8 default for headroom across a chord of simultaneous tines.

params.physicalTarget: physical 0.5+0.4·1.5=1.1 → stretchNorm≈0.067 fed to bank; mild extra inharmonic dispersion
params.rationale: Adds a touch of stiff-beam dispersion above the neutral 1.0 to push the metallic, slightly-detuned tine shimmer (cantilever upper modes sit higher than the bell defaults).

params.physicalTarget: phys=0.62·2-1=+0.24; per-mode ratio^(-0.24) lifts the sub-nominal/low partials slightly and shortens the very top
params.rationale: Inharmonic upper partials are strongest in the attack then die quickly, leaving a purer fundamental — a mild positive skew biases sustain toward the lower/nominal partials.

params.physicalTarget: ClickLayer mix 0.42 → ~−18 dBFS transient, half fed to body modes
params.rationale: Supplies the bright metallic onset 'ping' (the inharmonic attack energy) and a defined pluck transient.
Short, crisp tine contact tick.
Bright metallic 'ting' of the steel tine onset, not a dull thud.
Light sympathetic-tine / box-rattle buzz characteristic of mbira, kept low (a clean kalimba is nearly noiseless).
Mid-band buzz, not a broadband hiss; matches the wah/buzz from the resonator box.
Slight resonant emphasis to give the buzz a tonal wah quality rather than flat noise.
The buzz/inharmonic rattle dies quickly, concentrated in the attack.
Neutral-bright buzz; not the dark Brown/Pink thump of a membrane nor harsh Violet of a cymbal.
Metal tine: weak frequency-squared damping so upper partials/highs ring relatively long (vs woody bodies). Set off-sentinel to pin metallic character independent of Material grading.
Moderate overall RT60 floor for a metallic ring that sustains but is not infinite; works with the Decay grade.
Bell body already carries the metallic spectrum; no post-body filtering wanted (transparent default).
No filter sweep on a clean plucked tine.
Tine tone is clean; no saturation flavour wanted.
No west-coast wavefolding on an acoustic tine.
A stiff metal tine has NO audible pitch glide; disabling keeps Start/End/Curve/Knee inert.
The body already supplies the (inharmonic) partials; an integer 1/k harmonic series would fight the tine's inharmonicity.
A small steel tine is essentially linear at playing amplitudes; no amplitude-driven brightening.

params.physicalTarget: no membrane air-loading (no-op on Bell anyway)

physics: FUNDAMENTAL/PITCH: a kalimba/mbira tine is a metal lamella behaving as a clamped-free (cantilever) Euler-Bernoulli beam with an intermediate simply-supported bridge; the bridge subdivides the tine, so the played fundamental is set by the free vibrating length (King, JASA 131(2):1289, 2012, PMID 22280717). 17-key kalimbas span ~F3 (175 Hz) to E6 (1319 Hz), centred on C4 (261.6 Hz); an 8-tine pentatonic/diatonic row typically covers about one octave (e.g. C4 261.6 → E5 659 Hz). MODE/PARTIAL STRUCTURE & INHARMONICITY: the first three transverse modes f1,f2,f3 are INHARMONIC — f2/f1 and f3/f1 are non-integer and depend on bridge position (King 2012). For an ideal cantilever the ratios are 1 : 6.267 : 17.55 : 34.39 (Penn State / D.Russell flexural-bar; free-free bar by contrast is 1 : 2.756 : 5.404 : 8.933, Fletcher & Rossing Ch.2/Ch.21). The bridge support lowers the upper-mode ratios from the ideal-cantilever values toward something intermediate. Mbira overtones are 'inharmonic … strongest in the attack and die out rather quickly, leaving an almost pure tone' (Wikipedia, Mbira) → the partials are present but transient; the sustained tone is fundamental-dominated. DECAY/T60: tine ring is metallic and moderate; higher tines sustain markedly LESS than lower tines (vibratekalimba) → decay must grade DOWN with rising pitch; box resonance lengthens low-tine sustain. TRANSIENT/CLICK: a short bright metallic onset 'ping' (the inharmonic upper partials concentrated in the first ~10–30 ms) plus a soft contact thump from the thumb. NOISE CONTENT: little broadband hiss in a clean kalimba; the characteristic extra noise is the optional buzz/wah from sympathetic adjacent tines and shell/bottle-cap rattlers on the resonator box — a light, mid-band, fast-decaying buzz, NOT a snare-like hiss. PITCH GLIDE: none (no 'kerthump' — tension modulation is membrane-only and physically absent in a stiff metal tine). MATERIAL: spring/piano steel tines on a wood/gourd box → metallic (low f²-damping, long-ish highs at the low end, but high tines damp faster). DRIVING THE PARAMS: Bell nominal f0 = 800·0.1^Size → Size sets pitch; Material rises with pitch (brighter small tines); Decay falls with pitch; modeStretch≈0.40 (physical 1.1) adds a touch of extra inharmonicity to lift the metallic shimmer above the default; decaySkew tilts energy toward the upper partials in the attack then lets them die (per-mode ratio^(-skew) tilt); a small light buzz via the noise layer; bright Click for the onset ping.

physics:
REAL ACOUSTIC SOURCE (what the LinnDrum sampled): The LinnDrum snare is an 8-bit companded (mu-law, AM6070 DAC, ~12-13 bit effective DR) digital SAMPLE of a real ~14" wood snare struck by session drummer Art Wood, recorded at 28-35 kHz (Linn LM-2 / oramics, gearspace). So the target spectrum is a REAL snare, low-fi'd and slightly distorted, with a SHORT body and dominant snare-wire noise.

ACOUSTIC SNARE STRUCTURE (Rossing & Bork, JASA 'Modes of vibration and sound radiation from a snare drum'; idrumtune; musicalinstrumentworld): two-headed ~35 cm drum; batter (top) head sets the tone, snare wires on the thinner bottom head add buzz. Two-mass air-coupled model gives mode PAIRS at low frequency; heads decouple and vibrate independently at HF.
- FUNDAMENTAL/PITCH: 14" snare body fundamental 170-220 Hz (tuned low ~160-170, high ~200; idrumtune/musicalinstrumentworld). The LinnDrum snare reads as a ~170-200 Hz body. The spec's pitchEnv 240->170 Hz is exactly this: a brief stick-energy pitch CRACK (head tenses under the strike, ~+1/2 step) relaxing to the tuned 170 Hz body -- a fast 'kerthump' but much smaller than an 808 boom.
- PARTIAL/MODE STRUCTURE: drumhead modes are INHARMONIC (membrane Bessel ratios 1 : 1.59 : 2.14 : 2.30 : 2.65..., air-loaded toward Rossing's flattened series), NOT integer harmonics. A snare is only weakly pitched -- the wires and broadband noise dominate over the tonal body. Inharmonicity is moderate (it is a membrane, not a metal plate).
- NOISE CONTENT (the defining feature): snare-wire buzz energy concentrated ~3-5 kHz, head/stick contact 6-10 kHz (izotope; Madsen Physics 406; sonicbids EQ sheet). The noise is broadband white-ish with a bright tilt; in the LinnDrum the sample is full-band but band-limited by the ~28-35 kHz rate and 8-bit grit. This is why the recipe sets noise 0.82 -- the noise layer IS most of the sound.
- DECAY / T60: SNARES-ON decays FAST -- 64% of peak lost by 0.08 s (vs 24% snares-off) per the snare-acoustics literature. So the wire-noise band T60 is short (~120-250 ms); the body tail is also short. The LinnDrum snare specifically is a tight, dry, gated-sounding hit (no long room tail in the sample) -- distinctly early-80s, thinner than the 808 (synthetic two-osc + long noise) or 909 (noise-heavy). 
- TRANSIENT / CLICK: a sharp stick CRACK / contact tick in the 6-10 kHz region defines the attack; the recipe's crack click 0.88 places a bright contact transient on top.
- PITCH GLIDE: minimal -- only the small 240->170 Hz head-tension crack; NO sustained 808-style sub glide.
- MATERIAL: real wood-shell snare, single-layer body (NOT 808/909 dual-oscillator metallic). The spec mandates NO secondary metallic body and physics off, matching the simpler single-layer noise+body LinnDrum/CR-78 sample character vs the richer 808/909 synthesis.

VALUES THAT DRIVE PARAM CHOICES: body f0 ~170-200 Hz -> Membrane Size ~0.40; pitchEnv 240->170 Hz over a short ~50-60 ms -> Start norm 0.5396, End norm 0.4647, Time ~0.11; noise dominant & bright (3-5 kHz wire band) -> Noise Mix 0.82, cutoff ~0.82 (~6 kHz LP), color hot; fast wire decay (64%@80ms) -> Noise Decay ~0.20 (~120 ms); bright stick crack 6-10 kHz -> Click Mix 0.88, brightness high; short dry body -> Decay moderate-short; physics off -> no secondary body, no coupling, modeScatter 0, nonlinearCoupling 0, minimal/clean tone-shaper.

physics:
RESEARCHED ACOUSTIC + SYNTHESIS PROFILE.

(A) The reference targets are two electronic kicks, not an acoustic drum, so the "physics" is the original synth/sample behaviour:

FUNDAMENTAL / PITCH. TR-808 bass drum: a bridged-T bandpass network kicked into self-oscillation by a trigger pulse, ringing at ~49.4 Hz (G1 +14 c; real units measure as low as 48 Hz) as a decaying SINE (Baratatronix; Werner et al. 808 BD model). CR-78 bass drum: analog voice (LFO/oscillator + dedicated decay envelope), service-manual decay ~100 ms (200 ms to fatten). Acoustic kick fundamentals sit 40–80 Hz; drum-machine kicks go lower (<50 Hz) (Sonarworks; iDrumTune). I target a glide that STARTS ~160 Hz and SETTLES ~50 Hz — the settle sits right on the 808's ~48–50 Hz fundamental, the start is higher than the 808's own 130 Hz attack peak to give the FM grit something audible to ride.

PITCH GLIDE ("kerthump"). The 808 briefly pushes pitch up to ~130 Hz at the attack peak then falls to ~49 Hz, the sweep lasting only ~6 ms (Baratatronix). This fast downward sweep is the single most identifying feature of a synth kick. Membrum's ToneShaper pitch env is the right tool; I use a short time (~20 ms, longer than the 808's 6 ms so the FM-flavoured glide is clearly perceived as a retro "doomph" rather than a click) from 160→50 Hz.

PARTIAL/INHARMONIC STRUCTURE. The 808 BD is essentially a single decaying sinusoid — almost no overtones except the attack click (bridged-T = one resonant pole). The LinnDrum kick is a SAMPLE of an acoustic kick (Art Wood sessions, 35 kHz sample rate, ~8-bit companded — Vintage Synth Explorer), so it carries real-drum overtones plus sampler aliasing/grit. The FMImpulse exciter (modulatorRatio 1.90 = inharmonic, near 2:1) injects exactly this kind of mild metallic/gritty inharmonic energy into the membrane, emulating the sampled-kick character without going full bell. Membrane body at material 0.20 keeps the body dark/woody so the FM grit reads as "sampled texture", not "FM bell".

PER-BAND DECAY (T60). 808 decay 50–800 ms (300 ms at noon); CR-78 ~100–200 ms; sampled LinnDrum kick is a short punchy decay (~150–300 ms). A retro machine kick is TIGHTER than an acoustic kick. I target a short body ring (Decay norm 0.28 → ~0.3× the membrane base) plus a tight flat-damping floor (b1≈19 s^-1).

ATTACK TRANSIENT / CLICK. The 808 "click" is the trigger pulse + the 6 ms pitch overshoot; the LinnDrum's is the sample's own beater attack plus quantisation noise. I provide a short, fairly dull click (contact ~2.3 ms, bandpass centre ~830 Hz) — present for definition but not a bright acoustic beater tick.

NOISE CONTENT. 808 BD: essentially none (pure resonator). LinnDrum: low-level sampler hiss/grit. So noise layer is kept very low (mix 0.06) — just a whisper of dirt, not a snare-style hiss.

MATERIAL. Electronic — there is no real drum head; the "material" is the resonator's damping. Membrane material 0.20 = dark, fast high-mode damping → reads as a tight synthetic low body, the correct retro substrate.

(B) Values that drive the params: settle pitch 50 Hz (= toLogNorm 0.199), start 160 Hz (= toLogNorm 0.452), glide ~20 ms, modulatorRatio 1.90 (fmRatio 0.30), fold 0.565 rad (fold 0.18) for the gritty edge, material 0.20 dark body, all PHYSICS axes (airLoading, modeScatter, modeStretch, decaySkew, coupling, tensionMod) at neutral/off because this is a stylised electronic kick, not a modelled acoustic membrane.

bodyModel: NoiseBody (BodyModelType=5): plate-ratio 32-mode bank + internal noise layer. Provides the gourd's broadband, weakly-pitched resonant cavity ("shh") rather than a tuned modal ring. f0 base 1500*0.1^size.
exciter: NoiseBurst (ExciterType=2): violet-noise bandpass burst = the stochastic bean-impact grain stream that excites the gourd. Longer burst (0.45) spreads the impacts so the hit reads as a "shake" not a single tick.

params.rationale: Stochastic violet-noise bandpass burst = the seed-impact grain stream that drives the gourd; the canonical PhISEM excitation is short noise grains.
params.citation: Cook PhISEM / STK Shakers: collisions -> short exp-decaying white-noise bursts

params.rationale: Need a noise-colored resonant cavity, not a tuned modal ring; NoiseBody = plate ratios + internal noise layer, the closest Membrum body to a gourd shell.
params.citation: task spec; param dictionary Body Model

params.physicalTarget: NoiseBody brightness=0.7+0.3*0.35=0.805; internal noise cutoff=clamp(1500+5000*0.35)=3250 Hz; reso=0.6+0.4*0.35=0.74
params.rationale: Lands the internal-noise resonance right at the gourd's ~3.2 kHz peak with a moderately high resonance, while keeping material on the darker/woodier side of the cymbal-bright NoiseBody default.
params.citation: STK MARACA_FREQUENCIES=3200 Hz, MARACA_RADII=0.96; noise_body_mapper.h cutoff=1500+5000*material

params.physicalTarget: f0 = 1500*0.1^0.4 ~= 597 Hz cavity fundamental (modes are noise-scattered above)
params.rationale: A small hand gourd: keeps the plate-ratio bank high and thin so it reads as cavity coloration, not a pitched body.

params.physicalTarget: ~unity body decay multiplier (exp(lerp(ln0.3,ln3,0.45)) ~= 0.93x) and internal noise decay ~56 ms
params.rationale: Short overall ring matching a single shake's body content; not a sustaining instrument.
params.citation: noise_body_mapper noiseDecayMs=60*factor; STK MARACA per-grain short

params.rationale: Strike position is barely meaningful for a noise cavity; mid keeps a balanced, neutral mode excitation.
Shakers sit low in a mix; slightly below the 0.8 default for a supporting percussion element.

params.physicalTarget: ~7.5% sinusoidal frequency dither on plate modes
params.rationale: Task-specified; breaks the residual plate-ratio tonality into noise-like coloration so the gourd reads as un-pitched 'shh'.
params.citation: task spec (modeScatter); param dictionary Mode Scatter

params.rationale: Longer-than-default burst so the excitation reads as a brief stream of seed impacts (a shake) rather than one tick; matches the maraca's many-impact PhISEM character.
The maraca is essentially 100% noise; the always-on parallel noise layer carries the core seed hiss, so push it well above the 0.35 default.

params.rationale: Task-specified darker cutoff; keeps the 'shh' energy below the bright cabasa 3 kHz sizzle, giving the softer lower-frequency maraca timbre.

physics: REAL ACOUSTICS: A maraca is a dried gourd (or plastic shell) partly filled with ~20-40 seeds. There is NO definite pitch and NO harmonic/modal partial series — the sound is a dense stochastic stream of many tiny seed-on-wall impacts (each a broadband click) shaped by the gourd's air/shell resonance. Perry Cook's PhISEM/STK model is the authoritative synthesis reference and gives concrete numbers: a maraca is modeled as ~25 beans (MARACA_NUM_BEANS=25) whose Poisson-distributed collisions each emit a SHORT exponentially-decaying white-noise grain (per-grain MARACA_SOUND_DECAY=0.95, i.e. a few ms), fed into ONE resonance at 3200 Hz with pole radius 0.96 (MARACA_RADII), so the gourd resonance is a single fairly high-Q peak around 3.2 kHz — a focused band, not a broadband sizzle. The overall shake envelope is long (MARACA_SYSTEM_DECAY=0.999 -> energy decays over several hundred ms per shake). CONTRAST (the distinguishing physics, task-critical): the CABASA in the same model sits at 3000 Hz BUT with pole radius only 0.70 and 512 beads — i.e. far more, smaller metallic-bead impacts and a much broader/brighter, lower-Q resonance => the cabasa/bright-shaker is a denser, brighter, sizzlier 'shh-shh' while the maraca is a sparser, darker, more focused 'shh'. So 'darker' for the maraca is achieved BOTH by fewer, larger impacts (longer per-grain content, less HF density) and by keeping the audible band lower/narrower. NOISE CONTENT: essentially 100% noise — no tonal partials, no inharmonicity to speak of (the 'modes' are just the gourd cavity coloration). DECAY/T60: per-impact grain ~2-5 ms; whole-shake amplitude envelope is an AR shape, ~25-35 ms in the artificial TR-808 emulation but realistically ~100-300 ms for an acoustic single shake. TRANSIENT: there is no sharp single contact click/'kerthump' and NO pitch glide — the attack is just the leading edge of the noise burst (TR-808 uses an attack ~3/4 of total time, i.e. a soft swell-in, NOT a hard click). ELECTRONIC (TR-808) reference: highpassed white noise through a VCA with an AR envelope, low end removed; ~25-35 ms total — confirming 'noise + bandshaping + soft AR envelope', no body resonator at all. DRIVES THE PARAMS: NoiseBody+NoiseBurst (stochastic noise into a colored cavity); long-ish NoiseBurst (0.45 -> ~8 ms) for the impact-stream feel; modeScatter to break up any residual plate-ratio tonality into noise-like coloration; darker noise-layer color (Pink) and lowish lowpass (~1.3 kHz) to keep the 'shh' below the bright cabasa 3 kHz sizzle; near-zero click (no hard contact tick); short overall decay; no pitch envelope, no tension mod (Membrane-only anyway), centered-ish pan.

archetype: Mbira (Shona mbira dzavadzimu / lamellophone "thumb piano") — pitched mallet voice, graded across 4 tines
bodyModel: String (WaveguideString, offset 1 = norm 0.6 on the 5-step per-pad selector → idx 3). Deliberate perceptual choice per the brief, NOT the physically-exact model. The mbira tine is acoustically a clamped-free CANTILEVER BAR (its true Membrum analogue is Shell/Bell — and indeed the companion kalimba in worldMetalKit is voiced on Bell). Voicing the mbira on the String/waveguide trades the exact 1:5:14 inharmonic bar spectrum for a more SUSTAINED, slightly buzzy plucked-tine tone (Karplus-Strong / Jaffe-Smith comb), giving an audible contrast against the kalimba's Bell body. Documented consequence: on String, modeStretch / decaySkew / modeScatter / bodyDampingB1 / bodyDampingB3 / airLoading / tensionMod are ALL no-ops (string_mapper.h consumes only size, material, decay, strikePos).
exciter: Mallet (offset 0 = norm 0.2 on the 5-step per-pad selector → idx 1). A soft beater drives the tine; the mbira is plucked by the thumb (a soft, fleshy release, not a hard stick), so a softened raised-cosine beater is the closest excitation. The attack tick / nail contact is carried by the always-on Click layer rather than a hard Impulse.

params.rationale: Thumb-plucked tine = soft fleshy excitation, not a hard stick; nail tick handled by Click layer.
Deliberate contrast vs kalimba's Bell — sustained, slightly buzzy plucked-tine tone; accepted cost: loses the true 1:5:14 inharmonic bar spectrum.

params.physicalTarget: pad8 0.50 → String brightness=1−0.50=0.50 (mid loop-filter damping); decay base=lerp(0.5,4.0,0.50)=2.25 s. Graded 0.50/0.62/0.72/0.82 across the 4 tines.
params.rationale: Forged-metal tine; higher tines (higher material) → darker top-partial damping = shorter top sustain, matching real tine behaviour. Strongest tonal lever on String.
params.citation: string_mapper.h L53-58; King&Bilbao JASA 2008 (upper partials decay fast)

params.rationale: Smaller size = shorter tine = higher pitch; 4 ascending pitched tines in the playable mid register.

params.rationale: Tines sing — a long, sustaining ring is the point of choosing String over Bell. Brief brief value honoured.

physics: FUNDAMENTAL/PITCH: An mbira dzavadzimu has 22-28 forged-metal tines spanning 3–3.5 octaves (Wikipedia/Mbira); musical register roughly bass G2 up to ~1 kHz. The 4 graded pads are voiced in the playable mid register ~383–505 Hz (G4–C5). String f0 = 800·0.1^size, so size grading 0.32→0.20 gives 383/420/460/505 Hz (ascending pitch = shorter tine = correct direction). PARTIAL STRUCTURE & INHARMONICITY: a tine is a clamped-free cantilever bar; the ideal bar gives strongly INHARMONIC overtone ratios 1 : 6.267 : 17.55 (BYU clamped-free bar; Fletcher & Rossing). King & Bilbao's measurement (JASA 2008, "Vibrational frequencies and tuning of the African mbira") found the prominent overtones at ~5× and ~14× the fundamental — slightly LOWERED from the ideal bar by the tine's tip geometry/added mass. These overtones are inharmonic, strongest in the attack, and DIE OUT QUICKLY, leaving an almost pure (chimelike) fundamental in the sustain. NOTE: a waveguide String produces a quasi-HARMONIC (1:2:3) comb, so the String body cannot reproduce the 1:5:14 inharmonic bar spectrum — this is the accepted cost of the contrast choice; the inharmonic attack colour is approximated by the broadband Click tick + a small NonlinearCoupling brightening, not by true bar modes. DECAY/T60: clear, percussive, sustaining tone; tines ring noticeably longer than a glockenspiel bar but the upper inharmonic partials decay fast. We target a long body decay (norm 0.78). With material grading 0.50→0.82, the String mapper base = lerp(0.5,4.0,material)=2.25→3.56 s, ×exp(lerp(ln0.3,ln3.0,0.78))≈×1.96 → ~4.4–7.0 s nominal waveguide decay time (a long, singing tine; perceptually shortened by brightness damping and Click/voice envelope). TRANSIENT/CLICK: thumb-nail contact gives a short bright tick — Click layer at mix 0.32, contact ~14% (≈2.4 ms), bandpass centre ~1.9 kHz. NOISE / BUZZ (machachara): the defining mbira texture is the buzzing rattle of bottle caps/shells ("machachara"), considered essential and used to project the sound (Wikipedia; InstrumentHeritage). It is a broadband, sustained, bright metallic rattle. Modelled with the parallel Noise layer at a modest mix (0.12), bright (violet/white) and short-to-medium decay — present but not dominating the tonal tine. PITCH GLIDE: none — a tine has no tension-modulation "kerthump"; pitch envelope and tensionMod stay OFF (tensionMod is Membrane-only anyway). MATERIAL: forged metal tine on a hardwood soundboard; the String material knob (0.50→0.82) sets loop-filter brightness (brightness=1−material) so higher/shorter tines are slightly DARKER-damped = less sustain on top partials, physically matching real tines.

archetype: Metal-Bell Hi-Hat (FX) — experimental inharmonic-bell hat with violet-noise sizzle overlay (closed/pedal/open by Decay), FX kit's aggressive metallic take on the hi-hat
bodyModel: Bell (BodyModelType=4) — tuned-inharmonic church-bell Chladni partial bank (16 modes; hum 0.25·f0 / prime 0.5 / tierce 0.6 / quint 0.75 / nominal 1.0, then 1.5, 2.0, 2.6, 3.2, 4.0 … up to 12×). This is the deliberate "Metal-Bell" choice: rather than NoiseBody's cymbal-plate skeleton (which the Acoustic/Electronic hats use), the Bell body gives a TUNED metallic clang whose lowest energy sits at the hum/prime (0.25–0.5·f0) and whose upper partials are dense and inharmonic — exactly the "metal bell" character the FX hat wants. b3 forced to 0 (norm 0) so the highs ring fully metallic.
exciter: NoiseBurst (ExciterType=2) — a short violet-noise bandpass burst that models the broadband, HF-tilted "stick/rod hitting metal" contact noise that actually excites a struck cymbal/hat. Violet (+6 dB/oct) puts most contact energy in the 2–8 kHz region where a real hat radiates, and a short burst (default ~8.5 ms here, velocity-shortened) launches all the Bell modes broadband-in-phase, giving the bright clangorous onset before the morph settles the body. Chosen over Mallet/Impulse (too clean/tonal for a metallic hat) and over FMImpulse (that's the tuned-bell-FX route, not the noisy hat route).

params.rationale: Violet-noise bandpass burst = the broadband HF 'stick-on-metal' contact that excites a real struck hat; launches all Bell modes in-phase for a bright clangorous onset.
params.citation: jstage hi-hat physical modeling; ModeAudio/Baratatronix 808 hat (noise+inharmonic osc through HP)

params.rationale: Tuned-inharmonic church-bell Chladni partial bank gives a metallic clang with a perceived pitch (the 'metal-bell' flavour) rather than NoiseBody's pure cymbal hiss.
params.citation: Hibberts church-bell partials; Rossing Science of Percussion Ch.5

params.physicalTarget: Bell brightness = 0.7+0.3·0.95 ≈ 0.985 (very metallic; minimal HF damping, long bright upper partials)
params.rationale: Maximally metallic body — bronze hat rings hot in the highs. Also the Morph START value, so the note opens at peak brightness.
params.citation: Bell modal HF damping; bronze cymbal HF 'ring' >1 kHz (rangeofsounds/jstage)

params.physicalTarget: Bell nominal f0 = 800·0.1^0.1 ≈ 635 Hz (hum≈159 Hz, prime≈318 Hz, partials up to ~7.6 kHz)
params.rationale: Small, high body placing the metal-bell partials in the hat register; lowest energy ~160–320 Hz matches the measured <400 Hz cymbal component.
params.citation: hi-hat lowest partials 200–500 Hz, energy <400 Hz (rangeofsounds)

params.physicalTarget: decayTime multiplier exp(lerp(ln0.3,ln3.0,0.08))≈0.34× on Bell base ≈ very short ring → CLOSED hat ~50–80 ms
params.rationale: Closed-hat variant: two plates clamped, fast choke. Pedal (0.04) and Open (0.6) variants change ONLY this knob.
params.citation: TR-808 closed hat ~50 ms, open 90–600 ms (Baratatronix)

params.physicalTarget: strike azimuth θ=0.3·(π/2)≈27° around the soundbow → |cos(m·θ)| meridional weighting across partials
params.rationale: Off-antinode strike de-weights some high-m partials, a slightly uneven (realistic) metallic balance; default is fine for a hat.
Hats sit a touch under kick/snare in a kit balance; linear make-up paired with master gain (post-N-1 gain staging).
params.citation: audit gain-staging appendix (Level = true linear attenuation)

params.physicalTarget: parallel noise layer at mix 0.55 → ~−18 dBFS sizzle on top of the Bell body
params.rationale: The bright HF 'sizzle/hiss' that dominates a real hat perceptually; layered ON TOP of the tuned metal partials.
params.citation: hat 5k–20k mixing band; 808/Massive hat = inharmonic tone + noise

params.rationale: Keeps the sizzle bright and HF-weighted like a real hat's radiated 5–15 kHz energy.
params.citation: hi-hat best in 10k–20k (rangeofsounds); Massive 'high HP' hat recipe

params.rationale: Violet tilt matches metal's HF-rising radiated noise and the bright synth-hat character.
params.citation: violet-noise hat sizzle; NoiseBurst is violet-based

params.rationale: Sizzle tail tracks the closed-hat body length; lengthen on the open variant alongside Decay.
Slight emphasis for metallic 'edge' without a pitched beep on the forced-lowpass path.

params.physicalTarget: ~7% sinusoidal frequency dither on the Bell partials
params.rationale: Organic detune across the metal partials → the dense, slightly-randomized inharmonicity of real bronze (avoids a sterile tuned ring).
params.citation: cymbal high spectral entropy / inharmonic spread (jstage); 808 'random' osc tuning

params.physicalTarget: b3=0 → pure flat damping, NO f² high-freq roll-off → metal-long upper partials
params.rationale: Metal does not damp its highs (woody bodies do); zero b3 keeps the bell's upper partials ringing — the defining metallic trait.
params.citation: Chaigne b3·f² law; metal = low b3 (audit §3-A, Bell mapper FR-024)

physics: REAL METAL HAT / CYMBAL ACOUSTICS: A hi-hat is two thin bronze plates; the strike spectrum is strongly INHARMONIC with high spectral entropy. Measured energy: a substantial component below ~400 Hz, lowest meaningful partials in the 200–500 Hz band, then a dense forest of inharmonic partials from ~1 kHz up past 5–10 kHz (the metal "ring"); mixing-wise the hat lives in 5k–20k. Cymbal/plate modal frequencies follow a modified Chladni law f(m,n) ∝ (m+2n)^P with P≈1.6–1.73 for bronze (free-plate), i.e. NOT integer ratios; bronze inharmonicity coefficient α≈0.3–0.8. A church/bell metal body instead has the tuned-but-inharmonic series hum:prime:tierce:quint:nominal = 0.25:0.50:0.60:0.75:1.00 (Hibberts/Rossing/Perrin), which is the "metal-bell" flavour this archetype targets — tuned enough to have a perceived metallic pitch, inharmonic enough to clang. DECAY/T60: a struck cymbal rings for seconds; a hat is choked by the two-plate contact. Roland TR-808 reference envelopes (the canonical electronic hat): closed hat ~50 ms, open hat ~90–600 ms, full cymbal 350–1200 ms. So closed/pedal/open are distinguished almost entirely by DECAY length, not by re-voicing. ATTACK/TRANSIENT: bright "clangorous" onset (dense high inharmonic partials excited in-phase by the contact) that quickly settles to a handful of stronger lower partials — the classic bell three-phase evolution (strike → strike-note → decay tail). This is modeled here by Material Morph 0.95→0.3: material starts very metallic/bright (b3-derived HF damping low, brightness high) and settles toward a slightly darker body over ~200 ms, mimicking the strike→body brightening-then-settling. NOISE CONTENT: real hats are dominated perceptually by HF "sizzle"/hiss; classic synth hats (808/909/Massive recipe) layer multiple inharmonic square oscs (≈2–5 kHz, randomly tuned) THROUGH highpass filters, optionally with white noise. Here the always-on parallel noise layer (mix 0.55, lowpass cutoff norm 0.90 ≈ 9–10 kHz, Violet color, short decay) supplies that bright sizzle on top of the tuned Bell partials. No pitch glide (cymbals/hats have no "kerthump" — that's a membrane/tom tension effect), so PitchEnv stays off and tensionModAmt stays 0 (Bell-gated no-op anyway). f0/pitch: Size 0.1 → Bell nominal f0 = 800·0.1^0.1 ≈ 635 Hz, so hum≈159 Hz, prime≈318 Hz, nominal≈635 Hz, upper partials to ~7.6 kHz — a small, high, bright metal body sitting right in the hat register, with the noise layer extending brightness above it.

bodyModel: NoiseBody (plate-ratio 32-mode metallic bank + internal cutoff/reso noise layer) — the dedicated cymbal/hat body
exciter: NoiseBurst (violet-noise bandpass burst — the stick-on-cymbal contact scrape that broadband-excites the inharmonic plate modes)

params.rationale: NoiseBody = plate (Chladni) 32-mode metallic bank + internal high-freq noise layer = the dedicated cymbal/hat body. NOTE: to land enum index 5 the selector needs norm=1.0 (round(1.0*5)=5); 0.8 lands index 4 (Bell). Use valueNorm=1.0 for NoiseBody.

physics: REAL ACOUSTICS: A hi-hat is a pair of thin spun-bronze (B8/B20) plates with free edges. Like all cymbals it is a strongly INHARMONIC free plate: its partials follow a Chladni power law in the mode index, f ∝ (m+2n)^P with measured exponents P≈1.6–1.73 (Rossing & Peterson; Fletcher & Rossing 'Physics of Musical Instruments'), NOT an integer harmonic series. There is no single perceived pitch — a dense thicket of closely-spaced inharmonic modes (low audible 'hum' region typically a few hundred Hz, rising into a quasi-continuous high-frequency cluster) reads as a bright metallic noise-like wash rather than a note. Modal density is so high above ~3–5 kHz that the spectrum is effectively noise; spectral centroid is high (energy concentrated well above 3 kHz — the 'shimmer'). DECAY: strongly frequency-dependent damping — high modes die in tens of ms, low/mid modes ring far longer; an OPEN (loosely-held) hi-hat lets the two plates ring freely so the wash sustains for hundreds of ms to >1 s, decaying smoothly. A CLOSED/pedal hat clamps the plates and kills it in ~50 ms. The closed hat physically chokes the open hat (the defining hi-hat gesture) — modelled as a choke group. TRANSIENT: a sharp broadband stick contact 'tick' (few ms) followed by the ring; little/no pitch glide (unlike toms). NOISE CONTENT: perceptually the steady part is band-limited high-frequency noise — which is why the classic analog synthesis (Roland 808/606) abandons faithful modes and instead sums ~6 inharmonically-tuned square oscillators (808: 800/540/522/370/304/205 Hz, summing to an inharmonic 'hum') through BANDPASS filters at ~3.4 kHz and ~7.1 kHz plus resonant HIGHPASS, then an AD envelope whose DECAY selects closed(50 ms)/open(90–600 ms); the LinnDrum/909 just play a bright noisy cymbal SAMPLE. The common thread across the real instrument and every synthesis: an inharmonic metallic core + a dominant high-frequency band-limited noise wash + decay-time as the open/closed control + a choke. MAPPING DRIVERS: bright high cutoff (noise centroid >5 kHz, 808 7.1 kHz band), long noise tail (open = hundreds of ms, target ~500–600 ms vs closed ~60 ms), long body decay (0.45–0.6 norm), raised modeScatter (the inharmonic Chladni thicket = frequency dither away from the too-ordered plate ratios), choke group 1 (cut by closed hat), no air-loading (membrane-only), no pitch glide.

archetype: Orchestral Concert Snare (tight high-tension field/concert snare)
bodyModel: Membrane (Bessel circular drumhead). The snare head IS a clamped circular membrane; its low (0,1)/(1,1)/(2,1) modes give the pitched "tat" weight (~250 Hz fundamental). Membrane is also the only body the membrane-only airLoading frequency correction applies to.
exciter: NoiseBurst (violet-noise bandpass burst). A stick striking a high-tension coated/calf head couples a very short broadband contact rather than a tonal beater; NoiseBurst's filtered-noise burst is the correct "snare-as-noise" excitation, and a short burst (~3 ms) drives the body's higher modes for the bright crack rather than a deep boom.

params.rationale: Snare-as-noise: a broadband violet-noise burst is the correct stick-on-tensioned-head excitation, not a tonal impulse.
The head is a clamped circular membrane; only Membrane gives the (0,1)/(1,1) snare partials and consumes airLoading.
Bright, crisp coated/cable concert head — articulate not boomy; slightly above neutral for the orchestral 'crack'.
High-tension 14" concert snare perceived/(0,1) pitch ~250 Hz (thump region 200-300 Hz).
Concert snares have controlled/quick decay; the (0,1) mode is heavily head-coupled and damped — short body tail.
Normal concert stroke (not dead-center, not rimshot); samples (0,1)+(1,1)+(2,1) for a balanced bright tat.
Snare is an accent voice; slightly above default for presence within the kit, below the rail.
Keep the broadband wire content intact; tone-shaping handled by the noise layer, not a post-body LP.

No west-coast wavefolding on an acoustic snare.
High-tension head pitch is fixed — NO glide, the defining trait vs a tom.
Real batter-head modes are slightly inharmonic/shifted from ideal Bessel ratios; a touch above neutral (0.333).

params.physicalTarget: skew=0.58·2-1=+0.16 → tilt energy/decay toward higher modes
params.rationale: Concert snare is bright/articulate; favour the faster high modes for a crisp short body (Tightness-like tilt).
Hard strikes radiate more highs / get noisier — a small velocity-driven brightening mimics that without compressing.
THE snare IS the wire: broadband rattle dominates; near-max so the buzz, not the head, defines the voice.
Concert wire 'sizzle' lives 7-10 kHz with energy above; bright, open wire buzz.
Wire buzz is broadband/flat, not a resonant peak; keep the lowpass gentle (avoid a pitched hump).
Concert wires are DRY/articulate — a crisp, fairly short buzz, not a long rock-snare tail.
High-tension steel/cable wires have a bright, treble-tilted broadband signature.

physics:
PITCH/FUNDAMENTAL: A 14" concert/field snare head is tuned high-tension; the perceived pitch / lowest membrane (0,1) mode sits ~200-400 Hz (commonly tuned ~250-330 Hz on a tight concert drum; standard 14" "sounds great" at ~170-200 Hz, higher when cranked) [drumforum / tune-bot / idrumtune]. We target the body fundamental ~250 Hz (the "thump" region is cited at 200-300 Hz) [head-fi snare frequency thread].
PARTIAL/MODE STRUCTURE & INHARMONICITY: The batter head's modal frequencies are NOT harmonically related; mode shapes 1-4 follow the Rossing (0,1),(1,1),(2,1),(0,2)... sequence but with ratios shifted from the ideal-membrane Bessel zeros, and the (0,1) fundamental is strongly damped (~5% / coupled to the resonant head) [acoustics.ippt.pan.pl vibroacoustic batter-head study; Rossing]. Air-loading depresses the lowest modes from the pure Bessel ratios (Rossing 1982). So: keep airLoading near its realistic default and add a touch of mode scatter, but the DOMINANT spectral content is broadband noise, not the tonal partials.
NOISE CONTENT & SPECTRAL SHAPE: The snare drum is fundamentally a broadband noise source — "because the snare is in fact noise it can have energy throughout the audible range" [recordingmag]. The snare WIRES, stretched under high tension against the bottom head, buzz/rattle sympathetically when the reso head moves, producing the characteristic broadband crack [Wikipedia snare; recordingmag]. Concert/orchestral wires (cable / extended-coil stainless) are deliberately DRIER, more ARTICULATE, brighter and more controlled than rock curly wires [BlackSwamp cable snares; Puresound concert]. Spectral landmarks: "thwack"/transient 1.5-2.5 kHz, "thump" 200-300 Hz, "sizzle" 7-10 kHz with harmonics to 15 kHz+ [head-fi]. Harder strikes radiate MORE high-frequency energy and become noisier [soundonsound; recordingmag] — hence velocity-sensitive brightness.
TRANSIENT / CONTACT CLICK: The stick-on-head "tat" is a sharp 1-2 ms contact transient centred in the upper-mid/high (the 1.5-2.5 kHz thwack), distinct from the wire buzz that follows.
DECAY (T60): Concert snares are prized for "controlled decay" / "quick response" and a dry, articulate tail [percussionsource; sweetwater]; the body (0,1) mode is heavily damped by head coupling so the tonal ring is SHORT, while the wire buzz is a crisp, fairly short broadband decay (a few hundred ms, dry on a concert drum) — NOT the long sustain of a rock snare.
PITCH GLIDE: NONE. Unlike a tom ("kerthump" tension modulation), a high-tension snare's head pitch is essentially fixed; there is no audible pitch drop, so no pitch envelope and no tension-mod glide.
MATERIAL: Tensioned mylar/calf head over a metal or wood shell, with steel/cable snare wires — modelled as a moderately bright, fairly tightly damped membrane plus a dominant broadband noise layer.
ESTABLISHED SYNTHESIS: Classic analog/modern snare synthesis = a couple of tuned partials (~180-330 Hz, decaying fast) PLUS a velocity-shaped, VCF'd noise generator that IS the snare, with notches; harder hits open the filter for more highs [soundonsound "Synthesizing Drums: Snare Drum"]. For an orchestral/tight snare the noise dominates further, is brighter and shorter, and the tonal body is light.
