physics: PANDEIRO JINGLE/SHAKE — voiced as a bright short noise patch (NoiseBody + NoiseBurst), distinct from a riq's membrane+jingle. Pitch: unpitched/percussive — jingles predominate and mask the head, no musical fundamental, perceived energy is a broadband HF cluster. Mode structure: thin steel(bright)/brass(dark) disc pairs collide chaotically → dense INHARMONIC plate/disc-mode cluster, no integer ratios → NoiseBody plate-ratio bank + internal noise, mode-scatter raised for the many-disc detune. Decay: cupped platinelas are drier/less-sustained than a tambourine → short ~40–120 ms band-limited tail, heavier steel decays faster than thin brass. Transient: each stroke is a few-ms metal contact → NoiseBurst (violet bandpass, here 7.2 ms). Noise content: broadband, noise-like, HF-transient-dominated, shimmer in the 5–11 kHz band — established hat/cymbal synthesis filters white/violet noise through a high band (606/808 ~7100 Hz; hats 5–10 kHz). Pitch glide: NONE (kerthump is a tom/membrane effect). Material: thin metal → max metallic → Material≈0.90 (brightness 0.97, internal-noise cutoff 6 kHz). Sources: Wikipedia Pandeiro/Tambourine, Grokipedia Tambourine, CapoeiraWiki, baratatronix 606/808, KVR/ADSR hat synthesis, Avanzini et al. JASA 2012 (glide is membrane-only). Drives: NoiseBody body, NoiseBurst exciter 0.40, parallel Noise Layer (cutoff 0.92 / color 0.88 violet / decay 0.18 short / mix 0.80 dominant), modeScatter 0.45, Decay 0.10, Size 0.12, pitch-env/tension/coupling/inject/drive/fold/click OFF.

bodyModel: NoiseBody (plate-ratio 32-mode modal bank + internal/parallel filtered-noise layer). NoiseBody is Membrum's purpose-built cymbal/hi-hat body: inharmonic free-plate Chladni mode ratios for the metallic "ting" skeleton, plus a broadband noise layer for the sizzle that dominates a real closed hat. f0 = 1500*0.1^size Hz.
exciter: NoiseBurst (violet-noise bandpass burst). A foot-closed hat is excited by the two cymbals slapping shut, not a stick tip — a very short broadband impact, not a tonal click. NoiseBurst's short violet-noise burst is the correct contact model; kept very short (≈3 ms) for the tight "chick".

params.rationale: Foot-closed hat is excited by the two cymbals slapping shut — a short broadband contact, not a tonal stick click. NoiseBurst is the contact-noise exciter.
params.citation: baratatronix 808 hat (noise/inharmonic source); KGUmusic (chick = short crisp muted slap)

params.rationale: NoiseBody = plate Chladni inharmonic modes + parallel noise layer = Membrum's cymbal/hat body; the only body that carries both the metallic partials and the dominant broadband sizzle.
params.citation: noise_body_mapper.h (plate ratios + noise layer); ResearchGate normal-modes-of-cymbals (inharmonic plate modes)

params.physicalTarget: NoiseBody brightness=0.7+0.3*0.88=0.964 (near-max metallic); noise cutoff=clamp(1500+5000*0.88)=5900 Hz; noise reso=0.6+0.4*0.88=0.95; modal baseDecay=lerp(0.2,1.0,0.88)=0.904x
params.rationale: B20 bronze is near the metallic extreme; pushes brightness high and lifts the internal noise cutoff into the crisp band. Slightly below the open-hat clone (0.90) to keep the foot 'chick' a touch darker/chunkier.
params.citation: oemcymbal/arborea (B20 bronze, metallic inharmonic timbre); membrum closed-hat reference pads[6].material=0.92

params.physicalTarget: f0 = 1500*0.1^0.12 = 1139 Hz lowest modal partial (plate ratios spread modes up into the metallic region)
params.rationale: Small body = high, tight hat. Slightly larger than the stick-closed clone (0.10) so the foot 'splat' carries marginally more low-mode weight (chunkier).
params.citation: cymbal fundamental 150–400 Hz with energy spread upward (oemcymbal); membrum reference pedal-hat pads[8].size=0.12

params.physicalTarget: NoiseBody modal decayTime = 0.904 * exp(lerp(ln0.3,ln3,0.05)) = 0.904*0.323 ≈ 0.29 s base, but b1 override (below) replaces the flat-damping term → realised body ring ~50–60 ms; also scales internal noise env 60ms*0.323≈19 ms
params.rationale: Foot-closed hat is even shorter than a stick-closed hat — the cymbals are clamped hard. Target ~50–60 ms 'chick'. Bottom of the task's 0.05–0.06 band.
params.citation: 808 closed-hat fixed ~50 ms vs open 90–600 ms (baratatronix); task spec 0.05–0.06

params.physicalTarget: computePlateAmplitude(k,0.3): mid-radius/azimuth strike, all modes excited (floored 0.03)
params.rationale: Default mid strike; a foot-closed hat is clamped over its whole contact area, so no extreme edge/center weighting is warranted. Left at PadConfig default.
A foot 'chick' is intentionally quieter than the stick-struck closed hat (≈0.75) and well under the crash — sits low in a kit balance.
params.citation: KGUmusic (chick is a quiet articulation); membrum closed-hat reference 0.75, pedal lower

params.rationale: Tone-shaper SVF left as LP; it stays bypassed anyway (cutoff at default 1.0, no filter env), so type is inert here.
params.citation: voice_pool.cpp:828 filter-type decode; bypass when cutoff>=20k & envAmt==0

params.rationale: Leave the post-body tone filter transparent; the NoiseBody internal noise cutoff (via Material) already shapes the brightness. Default.
No filter emphasis needed; filter bypassed. Default.

params.physicalTarget: amount = 0.5*2-1 = 0 → no cutoff modulation (keeps filter bypassed)
params.rationale: No filter sweep on a percussive hat. Neutral default.
No saturation; Drive is flavour-not-level post-audit and unneeded for a clean metallic chick.
West-coast folding is alien to an acoustic hat. Bypassed.

params.physicalTarget: 0 ms → pitch envelope DISABLED (body plays at naturalFundamentalHz)
params.rationale: A hi-hat has no pitch glide; master-disable the pitch env entirely.
params.citation: voice_pool.cpp:857 (0 = pitch env off); no glide in cymbal acoustics

params.rationale: Neutral — the corrected NoiseBody plate ratios are already strongly inharmonic for a metallic spectrum; no extra stretch needed for the foot hat.

params.physicalTarget: 0.0 = no skew (neutral global decay + no per-mode tilt)
params.rationale: Neutral; the b1/decay already set the chunky short ring. Default.

params.physicalTarget: exact zero-leak bypass (no injected harmonic series)
params.rationale: A synthetic 1/k harmonic series would add a pitched/tonal body alien to a noisy inharmonic hat. Off.
Foot 'chick' is short and low-energy; dynamic brightening is unnecessary and the foot hat is not played hard. Off.
params.citation: nonlinear_coupling.h exact bypass at 0 (audit M-3/M-4 redesign)

params.rationale: REQUIRED by spec: pedal/closed/open hats share choke group 1 so a new hat hit cuts the others (real hi-hat mute behaviour).
params.citation: task spec (choke group 1); membrum hat reference pads[6/8/10].chokeGroup=1; Wikipedia hi-hat (open/closed mutual choke)

params.rationale: No multi-out routing needed. Default.

params.physicalTarget: ms = 2 + 0.0769*13 ≈ 3.0 ms violet-noise contact burst (velocity then shortens it)
params.rationale: Very short contact = tight 'chick'. Matches the closed-hat reference's (3-2)/13≈0.077 setting; cymbals-meeting impact is brief.
params.citation: voice_pool setNoiseBurstContactMs; membrum closed-hat reference noiseBurstDuration=(3-2)/13.0

params.rationale: A closed/foot hat is perceptually noise-DOMINATED broadband sizzle; the parallel noise layer must be hot relative to the modal partials.
params.citation: izotope/emastered (hat = broadband HF energy); membrum closed-hat reference noiseLayerMix=0.85

params.rationale: Closed-hat sizzle lives high; the parallel lowpass set near 12 kHz passes the crisp/air band while taming >15 kHz harshness.
params.citation: musical-u/emastered (hat air 8–17 kHz; 10–15 kHz can get harsh); membrum closed-hat reference noiseLayerCutoff=0.92

params.rationale: Gentle hump only; the layer is a lowpass so high Q is unnecessary. Default carries.
params.citation: audit L-6 (cap reso on lowpass path); PadConfig default 0.2

params.physicalTarget: ms = exp(ln20 + 0.07*(ln2000-ln20)) ≈ 26 ms noise-layer decay; release ≈13 ms
params.rationale: The foot hat's sizzle dies FAST (cymbals clamped). Even shorter than the stick-closed clone (0.10≈32 ms) — bottom of the task's tighter target.
params.citation: 808 closed-hat ~50 ms total; foot clamp kills sizzle quicker; reference pedal pads[8].noiseLayerDecay=0.07

params.rationale: Metallic hat hiss is HF-tilted (blue/violet), not flat white. Matches the closed-hat reference and the bronze 'sizzle' spectrum.
params.citation: NoiseLayer denormColor thresholds; membrum closed-hat reference noiseLayerColor=0.85; cymbal HF-rising spectrum

params.rationale: A foot 'chick' has NO beater/stick contact click — the slap is already modelled by NoiseBurst+noise. The acoustic-kit hat reference explicitly sets clickLayerMix=0.
params.citation: membrum closed-hat reference pads[6].clickLayerMix=0.0; KGUmusic (chick = cymbals meeting, no stick)

params.physicalTarget: b1 = 0.2 + 0.5*49.8 = 25 s^-1 flat damping (overrides the Decay-derived flat damping) → forces the body modes to thud shut in ~tens of ms
params.rationale: RAISED per spec ('stronger low-mode component, b1 raised'): a high flat-damping floor clamps the low modes so the body gives a chunky short 'splat' that closes hard instead of ringing — the physical foot-clamp. This is the key delta from the stick-closed clone (which leaves b1 at the sentinel/derive path).
params.citation: task spec (b1 raised, chunkier splat); voice_pool b1 denorm 0.2+norm*49.8; foot clamp kills the ring (baratatronix closed ~50 ms)

params.physicalTarget: b3 = 0 → pure flat damping, metallic (no extra f^2 high-mode damping)
params.rationale: Metal rings its highs (low f^2 damping); keep b3 at minimum so the high inharmonic partials stay bright and 'metallic' rather than wooded. The short ring is owned by b1, not b3.
params.citation: PadConfig b3 semantics (0=metallic long highs); B20 bronze metallic HF (oemcymbal)

params.physicalTarget: no membrane air-loading correction (NO-OP on NoiseBody anyway)
params.rationale: airLoading is membrane-only physics; explicitly 0 on the hat (a plate/cymbal has no air-loaded membrane modes). Matches the reference which sets airLoading=0 on hats.
params.citation: audit §3-A (airLoading membrane-only, Rossing 1982); membrum closed-hat reference pads[6].airLoading=0.0

params.rationale: The plate Chladni ratios are already inharmonic enough; no extra detune dither needed for a tight foot hat. Default.

params.physicalTarget: theta=0.45*pi/2; gainL=√2*cos≈1.10, gainR=√2*sin≈0.91 → slightly left of center
params.rationale: Hi-hats sit slightly off-center (drummer's-perspective stereo placement), shared across the hat group. A small left bias; could equally be centered (0.5).
params.citation: voice_pool.cpp panGainsForNote (M-9 equal-power); standard kit stereo placement

params.rationale: The pedal-hat pad is a crafted, sounding pad. Default ON.

defaultedParams.param: Filter Type / Cutoff / Resonance / Filter Env (Amount/Atk/Dec/Sus/Rel)
defaultedParams.why: ToneShaper SVF stays bypassed (cutoff 20 kHz, env amount 0); the hat's brightness is shaped by the NoiseBody internal noise cutoff (Material) and the parallel noise layer, so the post-body filter is inert.
PitchEnv Time=0 master-disables the whole pitch-envelope family; a hi-hat has no pitch glide, so every pitch-env breakpoint is a no-op.
A foot-clamped hat is excited over its whole contact area; no extreme edge/center mode weighting is physically motivated, so the mid default is correct.

defaultedParams.param: Mode Stretch / Decay Skew / Mode Scatter
defaultedParams.why: The corrected plate-Chladni ratios are already strongly inharmonic; the neutral (no-op) values give the right metallic spectrum without artificial restretch/tilt/dither.

defaultedParams.param: Mode Inject / Nonlinear Coupling / Drive / Fold
defaultedParams.why: All four exact-bypass at their neutral values; they add tonal harmonics, dynamic brightening, saturation or folding that are alien to a short noisy inharmonic foot-hat.
No intra-hit material sweep wanted on a 50 ms transient; Morph Enabled OFF makes the whole morph family inert.
Routed to the main bus; no multi-out send needed for a standard hat.

defaultedParams.param: FM Ratio / Feedback Amount / Friction Pressure
defaultedParams.why: Secondary-exciter params for FMImpulse/Feedback/Friction respectively; with NoiseBurst selected they are hard no-ops.

defaultedParams.param: Coupling Amount / Coupling Strength / Secondary Enabled/Size/Material / Tension Mod
defaultedParams.why: Head↔shell coupling and tension glide model drum-shell/membrane physics; a cymbal has neither a coupled shell nor tension-modulated pitch, so the secondary bank stays off and tensionMod=0 (also membrane-only).
On the forced-lowpass noise path a low Q (mild hump) is correct; the default 0.2 (Q≈1.24) carries — no resonant peak wanted in broadband sizzle.

physics: FUNDAMENTAL/PITCH: A hi-hat has no definite pitch. Measured cymbal/hat fundamentals sit ~150–400 Hz for the lowest axisymmetric modes (oemcymbal/arborea modal surveys; cymbal fundamental "150–400 Hz depending on diameter and thickness"), but the audible energy is a dense inharmonic cloud spread far higher. PARTIAL STRUCTURE / INHARMONICITY: B20 bronze cymbals are strongly inharmonic — >100 normal modes labelled (m nodal diameters, n nodal circles), partials deviating substantially from integer ratios; cymbal inharmonicity coefficient α≈0.3–0.8 and weak nonlinear mode coupling (0.03–0.15) (ResearchGate "normal modes of cymbals"; oemcymbal). Free-plate / Chladni power law in (m+2n) — exactly the corrected NoiseBody/Plate model (audit §3-B). SPECTRAL CENTROID / DOMINANT BAND: closed-hat energy is high and bright — dominant band ~300–3000 Hz with the perceptual "crisp/air" energy extending to 8–17 kHz; closed hats of 13–15" are "crisp, high-pitched, short sustain, high-frequency presence" (KGUmusic; musical-u; emastered EQ refs). 808-synthesis reference puts hat band energy through a 3440 Hz bandpass then a high-pass, fed by six inharmonic square oscillators at 205/304/370/523/540/800 Hz (baratatronix) — i.e. a handful of low-mid inharmonic partials + lots of HF. DECAY (T60): cymbal modes span ~50 ms (HF) to several seconds (LF); for the CLOSED/foot hat the cymbals are clamped together so the ring is killed — the 808 fixes closed-hat decay at ~50 ms (vs open 90–600 ms) (baratatronix). A real foot "chick"/pedal-closed hat is ~50–80 ms, even shorter and chunkier than a stick-closed hat because the foot clamps both cymbals hard with no rebound. NOISE CONTENT: the closed/pedal hat is perceptually noise-dominated (broadband metallic hiss/"sizzle"), violet/blue-tilted (rising HF), with the inharmonic partials providing only the metallic colour. TRANSIENT/CLICK: a short broadband contact slap (cymbals meeting) — no tonal beater click. PITCH GLIDE: none. MATERIAL: B20 bronze (80/20 Cu/Sn), E≈110–120 GPa, ρ≈7800–8100 kg/m³ — so Material is pushed near-metallic. PEDAL-vs-CLOSED DELTA: identical mechanism, but the foot clamp gives (a) shorter decay (~50–60 ms target → norm 0.05–0.06) and (b) a slightly stronger low/chunky "splat" component (more low-mode weight, raised flat damping b1 so the body thuds shut rather than rings) and (c) typically a touch lower output than the stick-struck closed hat.

bodyModel: Bell (church-bell Chladni partial bank, 16 modes, hum at 0.25·f0; f0_nominal = 800·0.1^size). Chosen over NoiseBody/Plate because a ride — unlike a crash — has a DEFINED tonal ping (struck near the cup) with a recognizable inharmonic-but-pitched partial skeleton, which the Bell body's tuned (hum/prime/tierce/quint/nominal + extended Hibberts) ratios capture, while the always-on parallel noise + click layers supply the sustained shimmer wash and stick contact the Bell body alone lacks.
exciter: NoiseBurst (violet-noise bandpass burst → NoiseBurstExciter). Per the task archetype. A short, fairly bright filtered-noise burst is the physically apt model of a wooden stick tip contacting metal: a broadband, very short contact transient that dumps energy into the Bell body's tuned modes (= the ride "ping"). NOTE on fmRatio: the dictionary makes fmRatio a strict no-op for any exciter other than FMImpulse. It is written here at 0.30 purely as documented archetype intent (matching the existing worldMetal "bell tree" pad-12 precedent of NoiseBurst+Bell+fmRatio) and does NOT affect the rendered sound. The genuine bell-FM-ping alternative is to switch the exciter to FMImpulse (then fmRatio 0.30 → modulatorRatio ≈ 1.9 becomes live); I keep NoiseBurst because the task names it and because the Bell body itself — not the exciter — carries the tuned ping here.

params.rationale: Sharp filtered-noise stick contact that dumps a bright broadband transient into the Bell body's tuned modes = the ride 'ping'; per archetype.
Bell tuned Chladni partials capture the ride's DEFINED tonal ping (cup), unlike pure-noise crash; wash/stick supplied by the parallel layers.
params.citation: Fletcher & Rossing, Physics of Musical Instruments, ch.20–21

params.physicalTarget: Bell brightness = 0.7+0.3·0.95 ≈ 0.985 (very low b3 high-freq damping); baseDecay lerp(0.4,1.4,0.95) ≈ 1.35 s
params.rationale: Bronze/B20 cymbal alloy = maximally metallic: long, bright upper-partial tails that ring rather than thump.

params.physicalTarget: f0_nominal = 800·0.1^0.30 ≈ 400 Hz (hum ≈100, nominal ≈400, partials up to ≈4800 Hz)
params.rationale: Anchors the bell ping in the measured ride primary-tone band (300–600 Hz) with a bright upper-partial cluster overlapping the 700 Hz/2.4 kHz bell components.

params.physicalTarget: decayTime ≈ baseDecay·exp(lerp(ln0.3,ln3.0,0.9)) ≈ baseDecay·2.46 → multi-second body ring
params.rationale: Rides sustain for seconds; low/mid ping partials stay near full level after 2 s.

params.physicalTarget: theta = 0.18·(pi/2) ≈ 0.28 rad strike azimuth; near a meridional antinode → most partials excited, slight high-m lift
params.rationale: Bell strike near the soundbow/cup antinode keeps the full tonal partial set audible (defined ping) rather than nulling modes.
Cymbals sit slightly below kick/snare in the mix; trims the long bright ring under the bus limiter.
params.citation: audit gain-staging appendix (−6 dBFS body budget + bus −1 dBTP)

params.rationale: ToneShaper left transparent (cutoff bypassed); LP is the harmless default if it ever engages.

params.physicalTarget: 20·1000^1 = 20000 Hz → filter BYPASSED (cutoff≥20k and env amt 0)
params.rationale: Keep the full metallic HF sheen; the ride's character lives above 8 kHz, so no body-shaping filter.
Filter bypassed; no resonance needed.

params.physicalTarget: amount = 2·0.5−1 = 0 → no cutoff modulation (keeps filter bypassed)
params.rationale: No filter sweep on a ride; bell brightness is set by Material/b3, not a moving SVF.
No saturation flavour wanted; clean metallic partials. Drive is timbre not level after M-2 makeup, but unneeded here.
West-coast folding is alien to an acoustic ride.
No pitch glide on a ride; left at default, disabled by Time=0.
No glide; default.
A ride has no 'kerthump'; the whole pitch-env feature is off.

params.physicalTarget: physical = 0.5+0.45·1.5 ≈ 1.18 → mild partial spreading (stretchNorm ≈0.12 to the bank)
params.rationale: Pushes the too-ordered bell ratios toward the real ride's inharmonic 'fog' without dissolving the ping.
params.citation: Fletcher & Rossing, Physics of Musical Instruments ch.20; https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Inharmonicity

params.physicalTarget: phys = 2·0.62−1 = +0.24 → mild high-mode lift / shorter global decay bias; per-mode tilt ratio^(−0.24)
params.rationale: Lifts the upper partials (shimmer) relative to the hum, matching the bright, sustained ride wash.
A synthetic 1/k harmonic series would impose a tonal/pitched body alien to an inharmonic cymbal.
Real cymbals brighten with strike force (Fletcher amplitude regimes: harmonics→subharmonics→chaos); adds 'harder = brighter' cup bloom, velocity-sensitive.
No per-note material sweep on a struck ride.
A ride is not a choked open/closed pair (unlike hats); free to ring.
Cymbal routed to a dedicated multi-out send per archetype.

params.physicalTarget: would denorm to modulatorRatio = 1.0+3·0.3 = 1.9 — but INERT (NoiseBurst selected; FMImpulse-only)

params.citation: PARAM DICTIONARY: FM Ratio meaningfulFor FMImpulse ONLY

params.rationale: Short, sharp stick contact = a defined ping transient, not a long scrape.
The sustained broadband shimmer/'wash' riding above the tonal partials — defining ride texture vs a dry bell.
Keeps the wash bright/airy (4–6 kHz sheen and up), the high-frequency 'air' of a ride.
Near-flat: the wash is broadband noise, not a resonant whistle.
Long shimmer tail; the wash sustains under the ping but the >8 kHz air fades first, as measured.
Cymbal energy keeps rising into the HF (40% above 20 kHz); violet tilt matches the bright metallic hiss.

params.physicalTarget: click out = 0.45·vel·env·filteredNoise·0.445; half-amplitude also feeds the body
params.rationale: The defined stick 'ping' tick that says 'ride' — also drives the bell modes for a sharper attack.
Very short = a crisp tick, not a thud.
Bright metallic stick-tip 'ping' near the measured 2.4 kHz+ cluster and up, sharp tick not dull thud.
Low frequency-independent damping = the multi-second sustain of a ride; overrides the Decay-derived flat floor for an explicit long RT60.
Pure flat damping = metallic: the high partials ring as long as the lows, the signature 'won't lose the ping' behaviour.

params.physicalTarget: no membrane air-loading correction (Bell ignores it anyway)
params.rationale: Air loading is a Membrane-only Bessel frequency correction; physically meaningless for a metal bell — zero it.

params.physicalTarget: ≈8% sinusoidal frequency dither on the bell ratios
params.rationale: High scatter breaks the too-ordered bell skeleton into the real ride's dense inharmonic shimmer/'fog'.
params.citation: Fletcher & Rossing, Physics of Musical Instruments ch.20

params.rationale: A ride is a single free plate, not a head-on-shell drum; no secondary resonator.
No shell body to couple to on a cymbal.
A ride has no energy-dependent pitch glide; the feature is gated to Membrane bodies.

params.rationale: Pad is an active, crafted ride.

params.physicalTarget: theta=0.62·(pi/2); gainL=√2·cos≈1.05, gainR=√2·sin≈1.34 → ~slightly right of center
params.rationale: Rides typically sit a touch right of center in a drummer-perspective stereo image; equal-power so loudness is preserved.

defaultedParams.param: Filter Type / Cutoff / Resonance / Env Amount
defaultedParams.why: ToneShaper SVF left transparent (cutoff 1.0 = 20 kHz bypass, env amt 0.5 = 0) — the ride's brightness comes from the metallic body damping, not a post filter.
Filter envelope is inert because Filter Env Amount = 0.5 (no modulation); no reason to shape its ADSR.
Bypassed at 0 — a clean acoustic ride wants no saturation or wavefolding flavour.
The whole pitch-envelope family is disabled by Time=0 — a ride has no pitch glide.
Exact-bypass at 0 — a synthetic harmonic series would impose a pitched body onto an inherently inharmonic cymbal.
Material morph off — no per-note timbre sweep on a struck ride.
0 = no choke — a ride rings freely (it is not a choked open/closed hat pair).

defaultedParams.param: Coupling Strength / Secondary Enabled / Secondary Size / Secondary Material
defaultedParams.why: No head↔shell coupling — a cymbal is a single free plate with no shell body to drive.
0 and Membrane-gated — no energy-dependent pitch glide on a cymbal.
0 — a Membrane-only Bessel frequency correction, physically meaningless for a metal Bell body.
Inert no-ops for the NoiseBurst exciter (Feedback/Friction exciters not selected).
Left at neutral 0.5 — the recipe sets the underlying params directly, so macros add zero delta and preserve those values.
Left at neutral 0.5 — only audible when kit-level Global Coupling/Snare Buzz/Tom Resonance > 0, which a standalone ride kit does not engage.

physics: FUNDAMENTAL / PITCH: A ride is metallic and inharmonic, not pitched to a single note, but the bell/cup region gives a defined "ping" tone. Measured ride-bell content (Musical-U): center/bell cup ~280 Hz with strong secondary components at ~700 Hz and ~2400–2500 Hz; edge strike ~636 Hz; overall ride primary tone "typically 300–600 Hz", upper sheen 4–6 kHz extending past 20 kHz (musical-u.com). So the perceptual ping anchor is ~300–500 Hz with a bright cluster near 2.4 kHz. → Maps to Bell f0_nominal ≈ 400 Hz (size ≈ 0.30, since 800·0.1^0.30 ≈ 400). Bell ratios then place hum ≈ 100 Hz, prime ≈ 200, tierce ≈ 240, quint ≈ 300, nominal ≈ 400, and upper partials at 1.5/2.0/2.6/3.2/4.0…12.0× → ~600/800/1040/1280/1600…4800 Hz, i.e. a defined low-mid ping plus a bright upper-partial cluster overlapping the measured 700 Hz / 2.4 kHz bell components. PARTIAL/MODE STRUCTURE & INHARMONICITY: cymbals are a "dense fog of enharmonic partials" with no dominant mode and energy that does not run out even at 100 kHz, ~40% of energy above 20 kHz (soundonsound.com; Fletcher & Rossing, Physics of Musical Instruments ch.20/21). A ride is more tonal than a crash because the cup constrains a recognizable partial set; the Bell tuned ratios + a HIGH modeScatter (frequency dither ~10–15%) + non-zero modeStretch breaks the too-ordered bell skeleton toward the real ride's inharmonic shimmer without losing the ping. DECAY / T60 (per band): rides sustain long; SoS models the FM-cymbal tail at ~3.7 s for the high-pass shimmer path vs a fast ~0.2 s downward "ping" band (soundonsound.com). Crucially, low/mid ping partials (up to ~8 kHz) stay near full level even after 2 s while >8 kHz decays first, so "you can ride hard without losing the ping" (musical-u.com). → long body decay (norm 0.85–0.95 → ~2–3× base ≈ multi-second ring), very low frequency-squared damping (b3 ≈ 0 so the metallic highs ring), and a long noise-wash decay; the stick click is the only fast element. ATTACK / CONTACT CLICK: the stick "ping" is a near-instantaneous attack — a short bright contact transient. → short NoiseBurst (sharp), plus an always-on bright Click layer (high bandpass center ~6–8 kHz, ~2–3 ms contact) for the defined stick tick. NOISE CONTENT & SHAPE: the sustained "wash"/shimmer is broadband, high-passed, tilted bright (violet/white toward HF), riding above the tonal partials. → Noise layer at moderate mix, high cutoff, Violet color, long decay. PITCH GLIDE: none — a ride does not "kerthump"; tensionModAmt is Membrane-only anyway and left off. MATERIAL: bronze/B20 alloy — fully metallic → material near max (very low b3, long bright upper-partial tails). NONLINEAR BRIGHTENING: real cymbals brighten with strike force (Fletcher's amplitude regimes: harmonics→subharmonics→chaos), so a small velocity-driven NonlinearCoupling adds the "harder = brighter" cup bloom. OUTPUT BUS 1 per the archetype (multi-out cymbal send).

Per the brief, a touch of macroComplexity thickens the dense, multi-source jingle clatter (slight added sympathetic coupling + faint nonlinear brightening). Kept modest so it doesn't introduce an un-physical injected harmonic stack.
The JINGLES are the dominant, signature broadband shimmer that masks the head (Grokipedia/Tambourine) — so the parallel noise layer is prominent, well above a normal drum's 0.10-0.20.
Jingle/cymbal energy concentrates in the 3-16 kHz band (PhISEM resonances 2.3/5.6/8.1 kHz; cymbal 8-16 kHz). A high lowpass passes the full bright shimmer.
A little resonance focuses the noise toward the ~2-6 kHz metal-resonance region without ringing into a pitched beep (lowpass path).
Jingle shimmer rings noticeably longer than the head thud — PhISEM high damping 0.9985 gives a sustaining metallic decay; ~200 ms captures the shaken/struck ring-out.

params.physicalTarget: ClickLayer mix 0.65 → half into exciter feed + full into output ·0.445
params.rationale: Per the brief (click 0.65). The sharp fingertip tek contact AND the instant jingle-clatter onset are a defining bright attack; a strong click gives that transient.

params.physicalTarget: b1 = 0.2 + 0.32·49.8 ≈ 16 s^-1 flat damping (≈ 60 ms decay floor)
params.rationale: Sets the head's overall RT60 floor to a hand-damped frame-drum value (overrides the Decay-knob derivation); keeps the head from over-ringing under the jingles.
Light f^2 damping — fishskin is thin and warm but the higher modes still ring some; low value keeps a little skin shimmer (woody, not a heavy wood thump).

params.physicalTarget: 0.45 interpolation pure-Bessel→Rossing air-loaded depression of low modes
params.rationale: A thin, shallow frame head is less air-loaded than a deep timpano, so only a moderate depression of the lowest Bessel modes — fuller than pure Bessel but not as 'tuned' as a kettledrum (Rossing 1982).

params.physicalTarget: ~3% sinusoidal frequency dither on modal frequencies
params.rationale: Per the brief (modeScatter). Real fishskin is irregular/imperfect → slight detune of the head modes for an organic, hand-made character (and the jingle inharmonicity it blends into).

params.physicalTarget: feedforward drive 0.4: secondary shell excited by 0.4·bodyOut, mixed passively
params.rationale: Couples the head strike into the metallic jingle SECONDARY so a head hit also rattles the frame's jingles (the physical reality of a struck frame drum). Moderate so the shimmer is present but not overpowering.
Per the brief: the 'metallic-jingle secondary body'. This is the tuned metal-ring component that complements the broadband noise — gives a defined high metallic resonance, not just hiss.

params.physicalTarget: sizeRatio = 1 - 0.2·0.75 = 0.85 → shell f0 ≈ 0.85·head_f0 ... but small-secondary intent: high metallic ring
params.rationale: Per the brief (0.20). A small secondary keeps the shell's fundamental high so its metallic partials land near the PhISEM jingle resonances (~2-8 kHz region of the metal modes), reading as bright frame-jingle ring rather than a second drum.

params.physicalTarget: shell b3 = 0.85e-5 (bright, long highs); b1 floor for a ringing metal shell
params.rationale: Per the brief (0.85). High material = bright, long-decaying metallic highs = brass/steel jingle character (Rhythm Tech/Pearl: steel = bright; long metal ring from PhISEM damping 0.9985).

physics:
RIQ — a 9-inch Middle-Eastern tambourine: wooden frame, thin fishskin (or synthetic) head, 5 DOUBLE jingle pairs (= 10 small brass/steel discs) set in the frame (worldpercussion.net; ethnicmusical; salamuzik). The instrument is two superimposed sound sources:

(1) THE HEAD (membrane). A small (~9"/23 cm), high-tension circular fishskin membrane. Circular membranes are inharmonic: modes follow Bessel-zero ratios 1 : 1.594 : 2.136 : 2.296 : 2.653 : 2.918 ... (Euphonics 3.6.1; D. Russell PSU; "no integral ratio → cannot produce harmonics"). The head is small and tightly tuned, so its 'doum' (center bass stroke, thumb/fingers) sits relatively high for a frame drum — a tunable riq head sits roughly in the ~120-220 Hz region (smaller/tighter than a 14-16" bendir which sits lower). I target a doum fundamental ~150 Hz. Head modes are fairly heavily damped (hand contact, light skin) → short-to-medium decay, ~150-350 ms for the open doum, much shorter for the muted 'tek' edge slap. Air loading on a real membrane depresses the lowest Bessel modes (Rossing timpani 1:1.5:2:2.44:2.9), giving a slightly fuller, less 'whistly' tone, but a thin frame head is less air-loaded than a deep timpano so I keep airLoading moderate-low.

(2) THE JINGLES (the signature). Pairs of thin brass/steel discs collide repeatedly when the frame is struck or shaken, producing a BROADBAND, noise-like, high-frequency shimmer that 'often predominates, masking the membrane tones and rendering the overall sound largely unpitched and percussive' (Grokipedia/Tambourine). The established physical-synthesis model is Perry Cook's PhISEM (Csound `tambourine`, STK Shakers): ~32 stochastically-colliding objects driving THREE metal resonances — MAIN 2300 Hz, first 5600 Hz, second 8100 Hz — with high damping 0.9985 (a moderately long, sustaining metallic ring on each collision; sound decay distinct from the system-shake decay). Cymbal/jingle spectral energy is concentrated in the 3.15-5 kHz and 8-16 kHz bands (Rollins normal-modes; cymbal spectrum analyses). Material warmth: brass jingles = warmer, steel/German-silver = brighter and higher-pitched (Rhythm Tech / Pearl jingle-material notes). The jingles are inharmonic small cymbals → broadband, dense, slightly noisy, with no fixed pitch.

TRANSIENT / CLICK. The attack is a sharp fingertip contact (tek) plus the immediate clatter onset of the jingles — a bright, short contact click (~2-3 ms), much brighter than a hand-drum thud. No appreciable pitch glide ('kerthump') on a frame drum: tension modulation is small for a light hand strike, so essentially no glide.

DRIVING PARAM CHOICES: head f0 ~150 Hz → Membrane size; head inharmonicity is intrinsic to the Bessel bank (modeScatter adds organic detune for the imperfect skin); jingle ring = a bright high-f0 metallic SECONDARY shell (small secondarySize → high shell f0 ~ matching the 2.3 kHz main metal resonance, high secondaryMaterial → bright/long metallic highs) PLUS the dominant always-on bright filtered Violet noise layer (the broadband shimmer, lowpass set high ~ 9-12 kHz so it spans the 3-16 kHz jingle band), with a medium decay (jingle ring ~ 200-400 ms). Click carries the bright tek attack.

archetype: Shaker / Cabasa (shaker family — cabasa, hand shaker, maraca)
bodyModel: NoiseBody
exciter: NoiseBurst

params.rationale: PhISEM shaker = filtered exponentially-decaying noise burst; NoiseBurst is the violet-noise→bandpass burst that mimics the bead-collision contact (no struck transient).
params.citation: Cook PhISEM / STK Shakers: 'exponentially-decaying noise bursts through resonant filters'

params.rationale: Shakers have no tonal body; NoiseBody is the noise-centric body (32 plate-ratio modes as a thin metallic tint + always-on internal noise affinity). The few modal partials carry the 'b1 partials' metallic tint under the hiss.

params.physicalTarget: NoiseBody brightness=0.7+0.3*0.85=0.955 (very bright); modal baseDecayTime=lerp(0.2,1.0,0.85)=0.88*(1+0.1*size); internal-noise cutoff=clamp(1500+5000*0.85)=5750 Hz, reso=0.6+0.4*0.85=0.94
params.rationale: Cabasa is a bright metallic sizzle (steel beads); high material maximises modal brightness and pushes the body's spectral tilt up into the 3–10 kHz shaker band. Lower to ~0.75 for a darker seed-maraca/hand-shaker.
params.citation: Cabasa = 'crisp, sharp, metallic sizzle'; maraca response 3–10 kHz (Wikipedia)

params.physicalTarget: f0 = 1500*0.1^0.08 ≈ 1500*0.83 ≈ 1245 Hz body fundamental (very small/high body)
params.rationale: A shaker is a tiny vessel — push the modal f0 high so the (weak) modal tint sits up in the bright band and never reads as a pitched 'jar'. Matches the factory Cabasa pad (size 0.08).

params.physicalTarget: modal decayTime mult = exp(lerp(ln0.3,ln3.0,0.08)) ≈ 0.34x → very short body ring; also scales NoiseBody internal-noise env (60ms*0.34≈20ms)
params.rationale: Single shaker hit is a fast 'tss' (STK sound-decay ~0.95–0.96, T60 tens of ms). Keep the body ring near-dead so only the noise-layer transient is heard.
Left at default; on a near-silent NoiseBody modal layer strike position only re-weights the inaudible thin tint, so it is a non-critical lever here.
Shakers are quiet accent percussion; trim a few dB below the kit norm (0.8) so a fast hi-hat-rate shaker pattern sits as texture, not a lead. Matches factory shaker pads (~0.65).

params.physicalTarget: 20*1000^1.0 = 20000 Hz → ToneShaper SVF BYPASSED (cutoff≥20k AND env amt 0)
params.rationale: All tone-shaping for this archetype is done in the noise layer; keep the post-body ToneShaper transparent so it doesn't double-filter the hiss.

params.physicalTarget: amount=2*0.5-1=0 → no filter-env modulation (keeps SVF bypassed)
params.rationale: No filter sweep wanted; a shaker hit has a static spectral shape.
Neutral. The modal tint is near-inaudible, and a shaker has no defined inharmonic partial series to tune toward.
Neutral; no spectral decay-tilt motive on a noise instrument.
A shaker has NO tonal harmonic series; injecting a pitched 1/k series would be physically wrong and add an unwanted pitch.
No amplitude-driven brightening on a noise body; the modal layer it would brighten is intentionally near-silent.

params.physicalTarget: NoiseLayer.mix=0.85 → dominant parallel filtered-noise layer (the PhISEM noise band)
params.rationale: This layer IS the shaker — it carries the dense broadband hiss. Set high (dominant) so the body is a thin tint beneath it. Matches factory Cabasa (0.85).
params.citation: PhISEM: sound = filtered noise bursts; factory Cabasa noiseMix 0.85

params.rationale: Centers the noise energy at the cabasa system resonance (~3 kHz) while the lowpass corner near 3.4 kHz keeps a bright, focused metallic sizzle. Raise to ~0.80 (≈5–6 kHz) for sekere/brighter cabasa, lower to ~0.65 (≈2 kHz) for a darker seed maraca.
params.citation: STK PhISEM cabasa center 3000 Hz; maraca 3000–10000 Hz band

params.rationale: Cabasa uses a LOW PhISEM resonance radius (~0.7) → broad, dense, almost-flat hiss with only gentle emphasis. Higher q (toward 0.4) gives the maraca's tighter, narrower 'shh' peak.
params.citation: STK PhISEM cabasa resonance radius 0.7 (broad) vs maraca 0.96 (tight)

params.rationale: A single shaker articulation is a short, fast 'tss' (T60 tens of ms); a short noise-envelope decay gives the crisp dry attack-and-gone the cabasa is prized for. Matches factory Cabasa (0.10).
params.citation: STK Shakers fast sound decay; cabasa = 'dry, consistent texture'

params.rationale: Shaker hiss is broadband/bright; white sits in the upper band before the lowpass. Push to >0.80 (Violet) for an even brighter steel-bead cabasa; drop toward 0.5 (still White, near Pink boundary) for a darker maraca. Factory Cabasa uses 0.75–0.78.
Brief: 'no click'. A shaker has no struck-contact tick; the onset is the noise burst itself.
params.citation: brief: no click; factory Cabasa clickMix 0.0

params.physicalTarget: ms=2+0.2*13=4.6 ms exciter noise-burst length (velocity then scales 1.2..0.8x)
params.rationale: Brief target 'mid burst (0.18–0.25)'. A short-to-mid filtered noise burst that excites the thin body and adds bite to the onset without a long contact. Matches factory Cabasa (0.18).
params.citation: brief: mid burst 0.18–0.25; factory Cabasa 0.18, hand shaker 0.25

params.physicalTarget: b1=0.2+0.55*49.8 ≈ 27.6 s^-1 flat damping (overrides Decay-derived flat damping)
params.rationale: Sets a tight, short flat-damping floor on the modal tint so the few 'b1 partials' die fast and never ring as a pitch. Matches factory Cabasa (0.55).

params.physicalTarget: b3=0 → pure flat damping (no f^2 high-freq roll-off → metallic, bright highs)
params.rationale: Steel-bead cabasa is metallic: zero f^2 damping keeps the high modal partials from being wooded-down, so the thin tint stays bright and consistent with the metallic noise band. Matches factory Cabasa (0.0).
Air-loading is a membrane-only Bessel-mode correction; explicitly 0 on a NoiseBody. Matches factory Cabasa.

params.physicalTarget: 0 = pure plate ratios (bit-identical), no frequency dither
params.rationale: The modal layer is near-silent so scatter is largely inaudible; left at 0 for the cabasa baseline (a touch, ~0.2–0.3, can de-pitch the maraca/hand-shaker tint if desired).
Center by default; in a kit context a shaker is often nudged slightly off-center (e.g. 0.4 or 0.6) to open the stereo image, but the archetype baseline is center.

defaultedParams.param: Coupling Strength / Secondary Enabled / Secondary Size / Secondary Material
defaultedParams.why: Head↔shell coupling off — a shaker has no resonant shell to couple into; the secondary bank would only add an unwanted tonal ring.
0 and Membrane-only anyway; a shaker has no energy-dependent pitch glide.
Default 0.3; on a near-silent NoiseBody modal layer it only re-weights inaudible partials.

physics:
REAL ACOUSTICS. A shaker/cabasa has NO pitch and NO harmonic mode structure: the sound is dense broadband noise from many independent micro-collisions of beads/seeds (cabasa = loops of steel ball-chain scraping a serrated wooden/plastic cylinder; maraca = ~25 dried seeds inside a gourd; hand shaker = beads in a tube). The canonical synthesis model is Perry Cook's PhISEM (Physically Informed Stochastic Event Modeling, STK Shakers): sound = a train of short EXPONENTIALLY-DECAYING WHITE-NOISE BURSTS passed through one or more resonant bandpass "system resonance" filters; the number of objects sets collision density (Poisson waiting time), the resonance center frequency sets timbre.

MEASURED/MODEL NUMBERS (drive the param choices):
- Spectral band: a maraca's measured response sits ~3000–10000 Hz (no low fundamental) — shakers are an entirely HIGH-band, high-passed-noise instrument. Cabasa is described as a "crisp, sharp, metallic sizzle," brighter than a maraca because of the steel beads.
- System resonance center freq (STK PhISEM): CABASA 3000 Hz, MARACA 3200 Hz, SEKERE 5500 Hz. Cabasa uses a LOW resonance radius (~0.7) → BROAD, dense, almost-continuous hiss (512 beads ⇒ collisions blur into a smooth wash). Maraca uses a HIGH radius (~0.96) → a tighter, more "shh"-pitched peak and a more granular rattle (25 beans). I voice the BASELINE as a CABASA (brighter, denser, the brief names "cabasa" first): center ≈ 3.5 kHz, broad.
- Per-event / single-hit decay: very fast. STK "sound decay" ≈ 0.95–0.96 per sample (≈ tens of ms). A single articulated shaker hit is a short broadband "tss/chk" — perceptual T60 on the order of 40–90 ms (cabasa drier/shorter; maraca slightly longer). No long ring, no body sustain.
- Attack/contact: there is a soft mid-length noise contact, NOT a sharp click/beater. The brief specifies "mid burst (0.18–0.25)" and "no click." A shaker has no struck-membrane transient and no contact tick — the onset IS the noise burst itself.
- Inharmonicity / partials: NONE that matter. There is no tonal mode series. (NoiseBody still runs a faint plate-ratio modal layer for body color; we keep it weak and short — "b1 partials" per the brief = flat-damping b1 set, b3=0 so the few modal partials don't add a wooden f^2 roll-off, leaving a thin metallic tint under the noise.)
- Pitch glide ("kerthump"): NONE. Shakers have no tension-modulation glide and no 808-style pitch drop. PitchEnv stays disabled; tensionModAmt is membrane-only and irrelevant.
- Material: steel beads on wood/plastic (cabasa) → bright, metallic-tinged noise; seeds in gourd (maraca) → slightly darker, woodier. We set Material high (bright) for the cabasa baseline.

MAPPING CONSEQUENCE: in Membrum the perceptual sound is carried almost entirely by the always-on parallel NOISE LAYER (the PhISEM noise band) plus the NoiseBurst exciter's filtered-noise contact; the NoiseBody modal bank is a thin metallic tint. So the meaningful params are Noise Mix/Cutoff/Resonance/Decay/Color, NoiseBurst Duration, Size/Decay/Material/b1/b3 (to keep the modal tint thin, short, bright), Level and Pan. Click is OFF.

bodyModel: Shell (free-free Euler-Bernoulli bar / glockenspiel-style modal bank). The hollow woody "tok" of a stick laid across the rim+head is, acoustically, a struck inharmonic wooden bar/block: a short tonal click with rapidly-decaying overtones. Shell's free-free ratios (1, 2.757, 5.404, 8.933...) are the correct dispersive-bar spectrum for a woodblock/rim-click, and the post-audit free-free strike shape gives a true edge antinode so an edge strike still excites the mode set. Membrane (Bessel) would be a tunable drumhead, Plate would be a metallic cymbal — wrong material; String would be a sustaining pluck. Shell is the woody-tok body.
exciter: Impulse (raised-cosine click). A side-stick is a single rigid wood-on-wood contact: a sharp, broadband impulsive strike with no bow/scrape, no FM bell tone, no sustained burst. Impulse drives the shell bank with a clean transient and lets the always-on Click layer dominate the perceived attack. (NoiseBurst/Friction/FMImpulse/Feedback all impose a character the dry wooden "tok" does not have.)

params.rationale: Single rigid wood-on-wood contact: a clean broadband impulsive strike, no bow/burst/FM character.
Shell body = free-free bar; correct woody/hollow tonal click. (NOTE: Shell is enum index 2; on-wire norm = 2/5 = 0.4, see citation.)
params.citation: natural_fundamental.h + dictionary: per-pad Body selector stepCount=5, idx=round(norm*5); Shell=enum 2 -> norm 0.4.

params.rationale: Authoritative normalized value for the Shell selector (idx=round(0.4*5)=2). Free-free bar = woodblock/rim-click body.

params.physicalTarget: Shell brightness = 0.85 + 0.15*0.30 = 0.895; decayTime base = lerp(0.15,0.8,0.30) ≈ 0.34 s
params.rationale: Mid-low material keeps the bar woody (highs damped fast) rather than ringing/metallic — the dry 'tok'.
params.citation: Music Guy Mixing snare-EQ: woody body 500-1000 Hz, tame ring; wood bar = short highs (F&R §2.14).

params.rationale: Puts the tonal click in the ~0.9-1.5 kHz woody-tok band, between the 808's 455 Hz body and 1667 Hz snap.
params.citation: Baratatronix 808 rimshot: tonal click 455 Hz + 1667 Hz; iDrumTune: snare body/click 600-1500 Hz.

params.rationale: Very short ring — cross-stick dies almost instantly into a dry tok; matches the ~10 ms-env 808/909 brevity scaled to an acoustic bar.
params.citation: Wikipedia Rimshot / Audiosquid: cross-stick much softer/shorter; Baratatronix: ~10 ms env.

params.physicalTarget: shell strike-shape sampled at relative position 0.30 along the free-free bar
params.rationale: Off-center strike excites a balanced set of bar modes (not a node), giving the characteristic hollow timbre.
Cross-stick is a soft accent; slightly below the 0.8 default so it sits as a quiet ghost-y perc element.

params.physicalTarget: flat damping b1 = 0.2 + 0.42*49.8 ≈ 21 s^-1 (overrides Decay-derived flat term)
params.rationale: Sets a mid-tight overall decay floor for a short, controlled tok; pairs with the short Decay knob.
Light-to-moderate f^2 damping = woody (highs die before lows), not metallic-long — the 'wood' material cue.
params.citation: Wood bar: strong HF loss (F&R §2.14); dictionary offset 51: b3=norm*1e-3.

params.physicalTarget: ~6-8% sinusoidal frequency dither on the bar modes
params.rationale: Real sticks/blocks are slightly irregular; a moderate dither breaks the pure-ratio sterility for an organic 'tok'.
params.citation: Inharmonic wood bar with manufacturing irregularity (F&R §2.14); audit: scatter = organic imperfection.

params.physicalTarget: click layer mix 0.88 (dominant attack; half into exciter, full into output *0.445)
params.rationale: This archetype IS the contact click — wood-on-wood snap dominates the perceived sound.
params.citation: Baratatronix: HPF emphasizes the click 'snap'; Wikipedia: woodblock-type tok is the contact.

params.rationale: Hard rigid wood contact is a very short, sharp tick.
params.citation: Dictionary offset 48: ms=2+norm*3; rigid wood contact ~2-3 ms.

params.rationale: Woody tick — bright enough to 'tok'/snap but not the metallic >5 kHz tick of a rimshot or hat.
params.citation: 808 HPF keeps HF snap (Baratatronix); cross-stick is woody not metallic (Wikipedia).

params.physicalTarget: parallel noise layer fully bypassed (zero output)
params.rationale: Soft cross-stick barely engages the snare wires — no broadband hiss; the sound is a pure tonal click.
params.citation: Wikipedia/Audiosquid: cross-stick much softer than a wired rimshot; Bowser Audio separates it from the snare wires.

params.rationale: Body+click already shape the tone; no post-filter needed.
Side-stick is a percussion accent; a slight off-center placement adds stereo life vs the dead-center snare.

defaultedParams.why: Filter is bypassed (cutoff 1.0), so its LP/HP/BP shape is a no-op.
Only audible when the filter is engaged; it is bypassed here.
Filter env at 0.5 (=0 mod) keeps the SVF bypassed; no filter sweep wanted on a dry click.
Wood-on-wood tok needs no saturation harmonics; bypassed at 0 keeps it clean.
Wavefolding is a west-coast timbre alien to an acoustic stick click; bypassed at 0.
A rigid bar has no pitch glide; PitchEnv Time=0 disables the whole feature (no 808-boom on a side-stick).
The free-free bar ratios are already the correct inharmonic spectrum; left at neutral 0.333 (unstretched).
b1/b3 overrides already set the wood high-vs-low decay balance; no extra global tilt needed (neutral 0.5).
No synthetic harmonic series wanted — the bar's own inharmonic modes are the sound; exact bypass at 0.
A soft cross-stick is low-energy and linear; no amplitude brightening; exact bypass at 0.
No intra-hit timbre sweep on a single short click; morph off.
Side-stick is not a mutually-exclusive open/closed pair; no choke (0).
Routed to the main bus like the rest of the kit; no separate aux send needed.

defaultedParams.param: FM Ratio / Feedback Amount / NoiseBurst Duration / Friction Pressure (32-35)
defaultedParams.why: All no-ops with the Impulse exciter selected.
Left at neutral 0.5 — only audible if a kit-level sympathetic-coupling engine is enabled, which a side-stick should not drive.
Neutral 0.5 — the recipe sets the underlying params directly, so macros add zero delta and preserve the voicing.
No-ops because Noise Mix=0 (noise layer fully bypassed).
Membrane-only frequency correction; a no-op on the Shell body.
Head<->shell secondary resonator off; a single struck bar has no second coupled body.
Membrane-only energy-dependent glide; a no-op on Shell and physically absent in a rigid bar.
Pad is active (1.0) — it is a populated GM side-stick slot.

physics: FUNDAMENTAL / PITCH: Side-stick / cross-stick (GM "Side Stick", MIDI 37) is the hollow woody "tok" of a stick laid across the head and struck against the rim — explicitly a woodblock-type sound (Wikipedia Rimshot; Audiosquid "Side stick methods"). It is a pitched click, not a noisy snare hit; mixing references place the woody tonal energy and "boxy/woody body" of a snare/cross-stick in the ~300-1000 Hz region with the snap/click extending up to several kHz (Music Guy Mixing snare-EQ: 500-1000 Hz body/overtones, 800 Hz woody ring; iDrumTune drum-frequency guide). The electronic canon confirms the "short pitched click" identity: the TR-808 rimshot is built from two bridged-T tonal oscillators at 455 Hz (A#4 -42c) and 1667 Hz (G#6 +6c), the TR-909 from three bridged-T tones, each fired by a ~10 ms AD envelope into a high-pass that keeps the high-frequency 'snap' (Baratatronix "808 Rimshot"; SyntherJack; network-909.de). So the target is a tonal click whose dominant body sits roughly 600-1500 Hz with HF snap above. PARTIAL STRUCTURE / INHARMONICITY: a struck wooden bar is dispersive and inharmonic — free-free bar ratios 1 : 2.757 : 5.404 : 8.933 (Fletcher & Rossing, Physics of Musical Instruments §2.14). Real sticks/blocks are slightly irregular, so a small frequency dither (modeScatter ~0.4-0.55, ~6-8% detune) is musically correct. DECAY / T60: this is the defining trait — cross-stick is "always a lot softer" and dies almost immediately into a dry 'tok' (Wikipedia; Audiosquid). The 808/909 envelope is ~10 ms (Baratatronix). Target body RT60 ~80-180 ms with strong high-frequency damping (wood, not metal) so the highs die first leaving a short thud. TRANSIENT / CONTACT CLICK: wood-on-wood is a hard, fast contact — the click IS the sound here (click-dominated), ~2-3 ms contact, bright-ish bandpass center (woody tick, not a bright metallic tick). NOISE CONTENT: essentially none — on a soft cross-stick the snare wires barely engage, so there is no broadband snare hiss (Wikipedia: cross-stick far softer than a rimshot; Bowser Audio separates cross-stick from the wired snare). Hence noise layer OFF. PITCH GLIDE: none — a rigid bar has no tension-modulation 'kerthump' (that is a tunable membrane phenomenon); pitch envelope and tensionMod stay off. MATERIAL: wood. In the Shell mapper, material drives high-frequency b3 damping; a mid material (~0.30) keeps the highs short and woody rather than long/metallic, matching the dry 'tok'. These values drive: Shell body + Impulse, size→f0 ≈ 0.9-1.0 kHz tonal click, decay short, b3 woody, click-dominated, no noise, modeScatter for organic wood, pan slightly off-center as a percussion accent.

archetype: Singing Bowl (Tibetan standing bell, bronze) — bowed/rubbed
bodyModel: Bell (church-bell Chladni modal bank, 16 modes; the only tuned-metal long-ring modal body). Pushed toward singing-bowl spectrum via modeStretch + pure flat damping (b3=0).
exciter: Friction (struck-bow / BowExciter). Provides the soft stick-slip "rub" onset; the long sustain is owned by the Bell body, not the exciter (Membrum's Friction is a ~46 ms transient bow, not a true continuous bow).

params.rationale: Rim is rubbed/bowed with a puja via stick-slip friction, not struck — the only exciter that models a bow/scrape onset.
Bell is Membrum's only tuned long-ring metal modal body; a standing bowl IS a standing bell.

params.physicalTarget: Bell brightness = 0.7+0.3·0.78 ≈ 0.934 (very low b3, long metallic upper partials); baseDecayTime = lerp(0.4,1.4,0.78) ≈ 1.21 s
params.rationale: Bronze, near-lossless high partials that ring as long as the fundamental — the metallic end of the material axis. Morph START anchors here.

params.physicalTarget: f0_nominal = 800·0.1^0.45 ≈ 284 Hz (small ~6" bowl); also +1.045× decay-time scale
params.rationale: Lands the strike pitch in the small-bowl 250–300 Hz band (Tiger 289 Hz); raise toward 0.6–0.7 for big-bowl ~100–170 Hz.

params.physicalTarget: decay multiplier = exp(lerp(ln0.3,ln3.0,0.95)) ≈ 2.74× on the 1.2 s base → multi-second ring (further lengthened by the low b1 below)
params.rationale: Bowls sustain 40–75 s; Bell is the longest modal body — push decay near max.

params.physicalTarget: Bell azimuth θ=0.3·(π/2)≈0.47 rad; weights partials by |cos(m·θ)| — moderate off-antinode rim contact
params.rationale: A rubbing contact sits on the rim/soundbow; mid value keeps the low (2,0)/prime partials dominant without nulling uppers. Default is already apt.
A sustained drone should sit below transient drums in the mix; trim under the percussive pads.

params.physicalTarget: physical = 0.5+0.45·1.5 = 1.175 → mild upward inharmonic stretch (sqrt(1+B·(k+1)²) warp), widening upper-partial spacing
params.rationale: Bowl partials (1:2.8:5.2:8.0, ~n²) are wider than the shipped church-bell ladder; a modest stretch pulls the Bell spectrum toward the bowl's inharmonicity.

params.physicalTarget: phys = 0.85·2-1 = +0.7 → global skewBias=1-0.3·0.7≈0.79 (slightly shorter base) AND per-mode tilt ratio^(-0.7): pivots around nominal=1.0, boosting the sub-nominal hum/prime and cutting the highest partials
params.rationale: Real bowls are dominated by the low (2,0) mode with a near-pure low fundamental; positive skew lifts the low partials so the tone reads as a deep evolving fundamental rather than a bright bell.

params.physicalTarget: amount=0.22 → drive=1+6·vel·0.22·env; louder rub → mild sustained odd-harmonic brightening (env-level waveshaping)
params.rationale: Harder bowing audibly brightens the bowl (the stick-slip pumps more energy into upper modes); small amount keeps it a near-pure tone.

params.physicalTarget: true → Material driven by the morph envelope each noteOn
params.rationale: Enables the slow timbral evolution that is the bowl's signature (partial balance shifts as the rub develops).

params.physicalTarget: Material value 0.78 at onset (matches the static Material; brighter/metallic)
params.rationale: The rub starts brighter as upper modes lock in.

params.physicalTarget: Material settles to 0.55 (Bell brightness 0.7+0.3·0.55≈0.865, slightly warmer)
params.rationale: As the tone settles into the (2,0) lock-in it mellows toward the low fundamental — a slow downward timbral drift.
The evolution is slow (seconds), not a percussive transient — long morph time.
Timbral settling is fastest early then asymptotes — exponential matches the energy build/decay better than linear.

params.physicalTarget: bow pressure at trigger = clamp(0.1+0.4·vel+0.28·0.5)=+0.14 bias → heavier, scrapier rub
params.rationale: A puja presses firmly to maintain stick-slip lock-in; a positive bias gives the sustained scrape character over a light velocity-only bow.

params.physicalTarget: layer mix 0.10 → faint parallel filtered-noise (≈-30 dB region)
params.rationale: Bowls have only a faint friction/scrape hiss; a small dark noise bed adds the rub texture without snare-like content.
Friction scrape is broadband but dark, not bright hiss — pink sits right.

params.physicalTarget: layer silent (no attack transient into body or output)
params.rationale: Rubbing has no beater/contact click; the onset must be clickless.

params.physicalTarget: 0 → no membrane air-loading correction (no-op on Bell anyway)
params.rationale: Air loading is a Membrane-only Bessel correction; explicitly 0 documents it is inapplicable to a rigid metal bowl.

params.physicalTarget: b1 = 0.2+0.3·49.8 ≈ 15.1 s^-1 flat damping → low frequency-independent loss (long ring)
params.rationale: A low flat-damping floor (not the sentinel/Decay derivation) gives a multi-second decay consistent with 40–75 s bowls.

params.physicalTarget: b3 = 0 → PURE flat damping, no f² high-mode loss (maximally metallic, long upper partials)
params.rationale: Bowl upper partials ring nearly as long as the fundamental — zero frequency-squared damping is the physically correct metal limit.

params.physicalTarget: ~6% sinusoidal frequency dither on the modal partials
params.rationale: Hand-hammered wall-thickness variation detunes modes and is the source of the bowl's organic, slightly-beating, not-pure-sine character; a small static dither is the closest analog to the degenerate-mode split Membrum cannot otherwise produce.
A long sustained drone is usually routed to its own bus for separate reverb/level — per the brief (outputBus 1).

params.physicalTarget: delta +0.35 from neutral → couplingAmount +0.175, nonlinearCoupling +0.105, modeInjectAmount +0.105
params.rationale: Adds sympathetic coupling + a touch more harmonic complexity/injected body weight, reinforcing the rich evolving partial texture.
A bowl is a mono point source; center unless a kit needs stereo spread.

defaultedParams.param: Filter Type / Cutoff / Resonance / Filter-Env (Amount, Atk, Dec, Sus, Rel)
defaultedParams.why: ToneShaper SVF left transparent (cutoff 1.0 = bypass, env amount 0.5 = no mod): the body+morph already provide the timbre; a post filter would only dull a tone we want spectrally pure.
0 (bypassed): bowls are near-pure tones; added saturation/wavefolding harmonics are antithetical to the archetype.
PitchEnv Time 0 disables the whole pitch glide — a rubbed bowl has a steady pitch, no kerthump.
0: adding a synthetic 1/k harmonic series would impose a HARMONIC overlay on an INHARMONIC bowl, fighting the physics (the Complexity macro adds a small touch if desired).
Friction exciter selected; FM Ratio is FMImpulse-only (no-op).
Friction exciter selected; Feedback Amount is Feedback-exciter-only (no-op).
Friction exciter selected; NoiseBurst Duration is NoiseBurst-only (no-op).
Left at defaults; the noise bed is at only 10% mix and pink-colored, so fine filter shaping is inaudible — kept simple per coverage policy.
Click Mix is 0, so click contact/brightness are no-ops.
Membrane-body-ONLY (gated on getCurrentType==Membrane); inert on the Bell body. The existing reference preset sets 0.40 but it is physically a no-op here, so leave at 0.

defaultedParams.param: Coupling Strength / Secondary Enabled / Secondary Size / Secondary Material
defaultedParams.why: Head↔shell secondary resonator off: a bowl is a single resonator with no separate shell to couple into.
0: a sustained drone should not be choked by, or choke, other pads.
Left at 0.5 default (cross-pad sympathetic participation); only audible if the kit enables Global Coupling, and a ringing bowl is a fine sympathetic driver/receiver at the neutral weight.

Left at 0.5 neutral (zero delta): the explicit material/decay/stretch/size values already place the timbre; only Complexity is dialed up. Punch especially must stay neutral (no attack punch on a clickless rub).
1.0 default: the pad sounds.

physics: covered in physics field above

bodyModel: Membrane (Bessel circular drumhead, 48 modes) — same physics as the maple kick, with air-loading engaged for the timpani-style lowered low modes
exciter: Mallet (soft beater) — the softest of the impulsive exciters, modelling a felt/wool beater whose contact spreads the impulse and rolls off the HF content the way a felt beater does versus a hard wood beater

params.rationale: Soft felt/wool beater — spreads the contact impulse and rolls off HF slap vs the sharp Impulse, the defining jazz felt-beater attack.
Same Bessel circular-drumhead physics as the maple kick; the only physically correct body for a struck bass-drum head.
Higher than the dark maple rock kick (0.28–0.35) per the brief — a brighter, more open maple body tone for the lightly-muffled jazz aesthetic.
Smaller body than a 22–24" rock kick (size~0.85) — a 18–20" jazz kick reads higher/tighter; exact value from the brief.
Short percussive thump; an open jazz kick rings a touch more than a muffled rock kick but the body is still gone fast (most energy <50 ms).
A pedal beater strikes off-center of the head; samples a balanced mode set, neither pure-fundamental (dead center) nor edgy.
Standard per-pad kick loudness matching the existing acoustic/wood kicks (0.82–0.85); a jazz kick is not pushed hard.
Glide START from the brief; the tuned-up small-kick attack pitch.
Glide SETTLE from the brief; the kick's resting body pitch.
Short, tight 25 ms drop from the brief — enables the whole pitch-env feature and gives the fast 'kerthump' of a small kick.
Classic 808/kick exponential pitch decay — most of the drop happens in the first few ms, matching tension∝displacement² relaxation.
Felt beater suppresses the slap; click kept low so the attack transient is suppressed RELATIVE to the body tone — the brushed/jazz brief.
Longer/softer contact time from the brief — a felt beater is in contact longer than a hard tip, smearing the transient.
Felt = warm, HF-suppressed attack; dull low-centered click vs a wood beater's bright tick. Value from the brief.

params.physicalTarget: 80% toward the Rossing air-loaded series (lower modes pulled to 1:1.5:2:2.44:2.9)
params.rationale: Deep, focused, 'less whistly' low end — air loading lowers the low membrane modes; high value gives the round felt-kick boom.
Moderate flat-decay floor — a slightly more open ring than a heavily-muffled rock kick, matching the wood-shell kick reference (0.34).
Low-ish f² damping leaves some upper-mode warmth alive (higher material = brighter body) without becoming metallic.

params.physicalTarget: effective tensionAmt = 0.16·3.3e-5; energy-driven upward pitch bump ≈ +0.3 st at full velocity
params.rationale: Membrane-only physical 'kerthump': head tension rises with strike energy then relaxes — a real bass-drum effect (Avanzini et al.), kept gentle for the soft hit.
Acoustic kick has almost no broadband noise; a felt beater adds no slap-noise — just a hint of air/port chuff.
The faint noise is a low air-puff, not hiss — dark lowpass keeps it under the body.

params.physicalTarget: feedforward shell drive 0.32 (secondary bank excited at 0.32·body)
params.rationale: A real wood kick's shell rings sympathetically; a modest coupled secondary adds body weight (matches acoustic kick 0.35).
Enables the head↔shell coupling that gives the kick its wooden housing resonance.
Shell tuned below the head — a slightly larger resonant housing under the membrane (matches acoustic kick 0.40).
Maple shell — moderately bright/warm coupled-housing character.

defaultedParams.why: Cutoff stays 1.0 (20 kHz = transparent bypass); a maple felt kick needs no post-body SVF tone-shaping — the body + damping carry the timbre.
Filter env amount 0.5 = no modulation; the filter is bypassed so all filter-env params are inert.
0 = exact dry bypass; an acoustic jazz kick is clean — no saturation flavour wanted (and post-M-2 Drive is flavour-only).
0 = bypass; wavefolding is a west-coast/aggressive timbre, antithetical to a soft felt kick.
Knee off — a single fast Start→End segment is the classic kick glide; the 3-point breakpoint adds nothing for a 25 ms drop.
0.333 = neutral/physical spacing; a drumhead should keep its natural Bessel/air-loaded ratios, not added inharmonicity.
0.5 = no skew; the membrane's natural high-mode damping already tilts the spectrum correctly.
0 = exact bypass; the air-loaded membrane already supplies the low-harmonic body weight — no synthetic harmonic series needed.
0 = exact bypass; a soft low-velocity jazz hit is the opposite of a loud-brightening nonlinear regime.
Off; the timbre is static within a hit — no material sweep for an acoustic kick.
0 = no choke; a kick is never part of an open/closed mute group.
0 = main bus; standard kick routing.

defaultedParams.param: FM Ratio / Feedback / NoiseBurst Dur / Friction Pressure (32–35)
defaultedParams.why: No-ops — only meaningful for FMImpulse/Feedback/NoiseBurst/Friction exciters; this pad uses Mallet.
0.5 default; only audible if a kit-level global sympathetic-coupling network is engaged, which this kick does not drive.
All 0.5 = neutral; the base params are voiced directly, so macros are left at zero-delta to preserve them.
Left at defaults; the noise layer is at near-zero mix (0.08), so its filter shape is barely audible.
0 = pure ratios; a focused single-pitch kick body should not be detuned/dithered (unlike a snare).
1.0 = on; the kick is an active pad.
0.5 = center; a bass drum belongs dead-center in the stereo image.

physics:
FUNDAMENTAL/PITCH: A small jazz/bebop bass drum (18"–20", often 16" in club kits) sits HIGHER than a rock 22"–24" kick — typically 100–150 Hz perceived, vs ~40–80 Hz for a big rock kick (SOS / iDrumtune). The task target 140→60 Hz glide with a 60 Hz settle places the body in the small-shell register. With Membrane f0 = 500·0.1^size, size 0.72 gives a NATURAL f0 of 95.3 Hz; the pitch-env then drives the perceived attack pitch from 140 Hz down to 60 Hz (a small jazz kick reads "punchy and tuned-up", not "sub").
MODE STRUCTURE/INHARMONICITY: An ideal circular membrane has inharmonic Bessel ratios 1 : 1.59 : 2.14 : 2.30 … (Rossing; Well-Tempered Timpani ch.2). A real, air-loaded drumhead pulls the lowest diametric modes toward the near-harmonic timpani series 1 : 1.5 : 2.0 : 2.44 : 2.9 (Rossing et al.; air loading "considerably decreases the lower-mode frequencies, small effect on high modes"). Membrum's airLoading param interpolates pure-Bessel→Rossing-air-loaded, so a high airLoading value gives the deep, focused, "less whistly" low end appropriate to a felt-beater jazz kick.
DECAY/T60: A kick's energy lives in the 20–100 Hz band and the audible body is short — most HF transient content is gone within ~50 ms (SOS / Sage). A felt jazz kick is more open/round than a heavily-muffled rock kick but still a short percussive thump: low-mode T60 on the order of a few hundred ms, set here by a moderate Decay plus per-mode b1/b3 damping.
ATTACK/CONTACT CLICK: The beater click is "hundreds of short-lived HF partials existing for just a few ms after the head is hit" (SOS). A FELT/wool beater deliberately SUPPRESSES this — it gives a "smooth, rich tone with softer attack… round, mellowed boom of big-band-era jazz," warm vs the wood beater's "articulate, punching, scooped-mid, strong-highs" character (DRUM!/Sweetwater/Drumhead Authority). So the recipe keeps the click present (for definition) but LOW in level and DULL in spectrum (contact 0.40 → 3.2 ms, brightness 0.22 → ~492 Hz bandpass center), and uses the soft Mallet exciter instead of the sharp Impulse — exactly the brushed/jazz aesthetic where the body tone dominates the transient.
NOISE CONTENT: An acoustic kick has very little broadband noise — air-puff/port "chuff" at most. A felt beater adds essentially no beater-slap noise, so the parallel noise layer is kept very low.
PITCH GLIDE ("kerthump"): Striking the head raises tension ∝ displacement²; as amplitude decays tension relaxes and pitch falls — "a typical kick shifts a couple of semitones start→finish" (SOS). The task specifies an explicit short 140→60 Hz / 25 ms glide (the synthesis-classic deeper drop, on top of which Membrum's energy-driven tensionMod can add the physical kerthump). 25 ms is a fast, tight drop suited to the small jazz body.
MATERIAL: Maple shell — warm, musical, the standard jazz-kit wood. "Higher material" than a dark rock maple kick = a touch brighter/longer-ringing body tone, consistent with an open, lightly-muffled jazz kick.

archetype: Splash Cymbal (small thin bronze cymbal — bright, explosive, very short decay)
bodyModel: NoiseBody (32 plate-Chladni inharmonic modes + internal white-noise layer; the corrected post-audit Plate table uses the free-plate Chladni power law f ∝ (m+2n)^1.7, which sits inside Rossing's measured cymbal exponent band 1.4–2.4)
exciter: NoiseBurst (violet-noise → bandpass burst; the short broadband "splash" excitation that pumps the dense inharmonic cluster, matching the established noise-driven cymbal synthesis model)

params.rationale: Established cymbal synthesis = filtered-noise excitation of an inharmonic resonator cluster; NoiseBurst's violet-noise bandpass burst is the broadband 'splash' driver.

NoiseBody = 32 plate-Chladni inharmonic modes + internal noise layer = the cymbal palette; the corrected plate table uses free-plate (m+2n)^1.7 Chladni ratios within Rossing's measured cymbal exponent band.

params.physicalTarget: NoiseBody brightness=0.7+0.3*0.95≈0.985 (very metallic); noise cutoff=clamp(1500+5000*0.95)=6250 Hz; noise reso≈0.98; modal base decay lerp(0.2,1.0,0.95)≈0.96 s
params.rationale: Bronze cymbal = maximally metallic/bright; pushes the internal noise cutoff into the splash 3.5–20 kHz band and gives long-ringing highs (then deliberately shortened by Decay).
Splash is the smallest cymbal of the crash family (8" typical); low size raises f0 and shortens ring-up — 'smaller cymbal = higher pitch + faster decay'. Matches the spec's size 0.22.

params.physicalTarget: decay multiplier=exp(lerp(ln0.3,ln3.0,0.28))≈0.46x → body T60≈0.96*0.46≈0.44 s; also scales the 60 ms internal-noise env to ~28 ms
params.rationale: Splash sustain is 'extremely short, usually less than a second' and the shortest of the crash family; 0.28 lands a sub-half-second ring. Matches the spec's decay 0.28.

params.physicalTarget: computePlateAmplitude(k,0.35) — off-bell strike sweeping radius+azimuth across the Chladni modes
params.rationale: A splash is struck on the bow toward the edge (not the bell); an off-center strike energizes the broad inharmonic mode set rather than only axisymmetric modes.
Splash is an accent, not a backbeat; trimmed slightly below unity so it sits as a bright sparkle, consistent with the crash-family levels (0.7–0.78).

params.physicalTarget: 20 kHz → ToneShaper SVF bypassed (cutoff>=20k & env amount 0)
params.rationale: The body+noise layers already own the bright spectrum; leave the post-body tone-shaper transparent (default), brightness handled by Material/noise color.

params.physicalTarget: phys=0.5+0.55*1.5≈1.33 → stretchNorm≈0.55 fed to the bank (extra inharmonic dispersion)
params.rationale: Cymbals are strongly inharmonic (Chladni power-law, not integer ratios); a positive stretch beyond neutral 0.333 spreads partials to a more metallic, less ordered cluster.

params.physicalTarget: ~7.5% sinusoidal frequency dither on the modal partials
params.rationale: Real cymbals have a dense, irregular (effectively randomized-per-hit) mode set; scatter breaks the rigid plate ratios into an organic shimmer — the established synth randomizes mode freqs each trigger.
Cymbals already decay highs-first via the body's b3·f² law; per-mode skew not needed and kept neutral to preserve the natural metallic tilt.
Adds a frequency-independent decay floor so even the low modes ring short — reinforcing the splash's fast overall T60 on top of the Decay knob (crash-family uses b1≈0.30).

params.physicalTarget: b3=0 → pure flat damping, NO f² high-mode roll-off (metal: long-ringing highs)
params.rationale: Bronze cymbals sustain their HIGH partials (the shimmer) — b3=0 keeps highs from being woody-damped, so the bright top survives within the short overall decay. Crash family uses b3=0.0.
Air-loading is a Membrane-only frequency correction (Rossing timpani); a NoiseBody plate ignores it — zeroed for cleanliness on the metallic body.
The defining splash 'whoosh' is broadband noise; a high parallel-noise mix delivers the 'first moments sound like white noise' character on top of the modal cluster.
Splash sits in the 3.5–20 kHz band — a very high noise-layer cutoff keeps the hiss bright and sizzly, the brightest of the crash family.
Broadband sizzle, not a pitched peak; low resonance keeps the noise as wideband shimmer (default).
Fast noise decay is the splash signature — the bright burst dies away quickly (shortest of the crash family). Matches the spec's noiseLayerDecay 0.25.
Splash is the brightest/highest cymbal; violet noise has the highest spectral centroid, matching the explosive top-end. Crash family uses 0.82–0.85.

params.physicalTarget: attack click at 0.3*vel*env (half into exciter feed, full to output *0.445)
params.rationale: The drumstick contact 'thwack' (1 ms impact transient in the synth model) defines the attack; a modest click adds stick definition without dominating.
A bright, sharp stick tick suits a thin bright cymbal; high bandpass center = crisp 'tick' not a dull thud. Crash family uses 0.85.

params.physicalTarget: ms=2+0.4*13=7.2 ms burst (velocity shortens to ~5.8–8.6 ms)
params.rationale: The splash excitation is a brief broadband burst — short contact so the body fans into the bright cluster instantly; slightly shorter than mid to keep it fast/explosive.
Cymbals routed to a dedicated pre-master send so they can be processed/balanced separately, per the crash-family convention. Matches the spec's outputBus 1.

A small positive brightness macro nudge pushes the overall timbre toward the explosive top-end of a splash without hard-editing the filter; crash family uses 0.85, splash slightly less to keep it thin not harsh.
Left centered by default; per-kit stereo placement (splashes often sit slightly off-center) is a kit-arrangement decision, not an archetype property.
Pad is voiced and audible.

defaultedParams.why: ToneShaper bypassed (cutoff at 1.0/20 kHz, env amount 0), so the SVF mode is a no-op.
No-op while the tone-shaper filter is bypassed.
0.5=0 modulation; the body+noise own the brightness, no filter sweep wanted.
Filter envelope inaudible while Filter Env Amount is at the neutral 0.5 no-op.
0=exact dry bypass; a splash is clean bright metal, no saturation flavour needed.
0=bypass; wavefolding is a west-coast timbre alien to an acoustic cymbal.
PitchEnv Time=0 disables the whole pitch-env feature; cymbals have no deterministic tuned glide (the only pitch change is the amplitude-dependent nonlinear cascade, not a swept tone).
0 — pitch envelope off; no 'kerthump' on cymbals.
0=exact bypass; injecting a synthetic 1/k harmonic series would add tonal/pitched weight that fights the inharmonic noise-cluster identity of a cymbal.

Left 0 for a clean preset; the real nonlinear mode-coupling cascade is intrinsic to the dense modal+noise body, and adding amplitude waveshaping risks homogenizing the bright transient (could be raised slightly for extra velocity-driven brightening if desired).

0=no choke; a splash is typically a free accent, not part of an open/closed mute pair (kit-level routing, not archetype).

Feedback-exciter-only; no-op with NoiseBurst.
Friction-exciter-only; no-op with NoiseBurst.
