physics:
FUNDAMENTAL/PITCH: The bayan (left/bass drum of the tabla pair) free-strike fundamental is measured at F0 ∈ 80–100 Hz (Ashok & Tiwari 2025; UBC Phys341 reports the ghe/ge stroke centered ~100 Hz vs the dayan's tin ~300 Hz). Membrum size 0.72 → f0 = 500·0.1^0.72 = 95.2 Hz, dead-centre of the measured range. The bass is a single strong low harmonic that the ear tracks directly (unlike the dayan, whose fundamental is finger-muted and reconstructed psychoacoustically).

PARTIAL STRUCTURE & INHARMONICITY: Unusually for a membranophone, the tabla is NEARLY HARMONIC — Raman & Kumar (1920) and Ramakrishna & Sondhi modelled the head as a composite (two-density) membrane: the central syahi (iron-loaded paste, ~40% radius, ~9.76× denser) lowers the higher modes onto an approximately harmonic series. Loaded-membrane normal-mode ratios come out ~1.00 : 2 : 3 : 3 : 4 : 4 : 5 : 5 : 5 over the first nine modes (continuous-loading ratios ≈1.07:2:3). The bayan is the simpler of the two (fewer overtones, 'easy spectrum'). Real heads show ASYMMETRY effects: frequency splitting around the 2nd harmonic and degenerate modes around the 3rd (Ashok & Tiwari 2025). → In Membrum: keep Mode Stretch neutral (0.333=physical, near-harmonic) and add only a little Mode Scatter (~0.10) to mimic the splitting/asymmetry; do NOT add inharmonic stretch.

AIR LOADING: A bass membrane over an enclosed clay/metal pot is strongly air-loaded; Tiwari & Gupta 2017 ('Effects of air loading on the acoustics of an Indian musical drum') show the lowest modes are depressed (deeper, less whistly). → Air Loading 0.62.

PITCH GLIDE — THE DEFINING FEATURE: Pitch is controlled by the heel/wrist of the playing hand — force on the pudi and position from the maidan edge toward the syahi centre; greater force = higher pitch (chandrakantha). Players (e.g. Zakir Hussain) play melodies on the bayan purely by hand pressure and wrist slide; the canonical gesture is 'push then release' — bend the pitch UP then let it come down — within a single stroke. Resonant bass strokes are explicitly described as pitch-MODULATED by wrist pressure during the ring. This is an energy/tension-dependent upward gliss that relaxes as the strike energy decays — exactly Membrum's Membrane-only tension model (Avanzini-Marogna-Bank 2012, ΔT_avg ∝ E). → tensionModAmt 0.78 (highest in the library), giving ~1.5–2 semitones at the hard-strike modal-energy peak (clamped ≤+3 st). A SEPARATE short pitch-env (180→70 Hz / 100 ms) supplies the attack 'thump' descent.

DECAY/T60 PER BAND: Strokes split into RESONANT (Ge/Ghe/Dha — harmonic, SUSTAINED; a single strong ~100 Hz harmonic persists clearly after onset) vs DAMPED (Ka/Ke — diffuse spectrum, RAPIDLY decaying transient, higher brightness/centroid) (researchgate Harmonic & Timbre Analysis; arXiv:1510.04880). Decay rate of individual spectral components is the key discriminator of a well-played Ghe. → Ge: long Decay (0.78) + low Body Damping b1 (0.30→15 s⁻¹) for a ~0.6 s ring; Decay Skew +0.30 so the weak highs die faster than the low fundamental. Ka (damped variant): Decay ~0.10–0.20, tensionMod cut to ~0.20, brighter louder click — a short transient slap.

TRANSIENT/CONTACT CLICK: Hand/finger contact on the head — a soft, dull contact tick (not a stick crack). → Click Mix 0.45, contact 2.5 ms, brightness ~1.4 kHz (dull).

NOISE CONTENT: Resonant bass is predominantly TONAL with little noise; only a small skin/air slap on contact. Damped strokes get a more diffuse (noisier) spectrum. → Noise Mix 0.10, Pink colour, ~850 Hz dark lowpass, ~110 ms decay.

MATERIAL: Goatskin head (warm, woody, low brightness) over a clay/metal pot shell (the coupled secondary resonance below the head fundamental). → Material 0.30, Secondary shell enabled, secondarySize→~63 Hz.

STEREO: Bayan is the LEFT-hand drum → pan slightly left (0.42).

archetype: Tabla Dayan (world / treble drum) — harmonic syahi-loaded membrane, Na/Tin/Tha/Tete bols as strike-position variants
bodyModel: Membrane (offset 1 = 0 → BodyModelType::Membrane). The dayan is a circular goatskin membrane whose central black tuning paste (syahi) loads the centre and converts the normally-inharmonic Bessel spectrum into a near-harmonic, pitched one (Raman 1934/35; Ramakrishna & Sondhi). Membrane is the only body whose mapper consumes airLoading (frequency correction toward Rossing's measured near-harmonic series) and whose path supports tensionModAmt — both of which are part of the dayan's physics. The post-audit ModeInject (now 1/k, -6 dB/oct) overlays a true integer-harmonic series at f0, which is the cheap, controllable stand-in for the syahi's harmonic-alignment effect (the bare Bessel ratio bank cannot be made integer-harmonic from a knob).
exciter: Impulse (offset 0 = 0 → ExciterType::Impulse) for the sharp open/edge bols (Na, Tin, Tete) — a raised-cosine contact click that excites the high modes the dayan rings on. Tha is voiced with Mallet (offset 0 ≈ 0.2 → ExciterType::Mallet) for the softer, fuller open palm-flat stroke. Both are the physically-correct excitation: a finger/palm strike is a short broadband contact impulse, not a bowed/feedback/FM source.

params.physicalTarget: ExciterType::Impulse (idx 0; Mallet=0.2→idx1 for the Tha palm variant)
params.rationale: Finger/edge strike = short raised-cosine contact impulse that excites the high pitched modes; Mallet for the softer open-palm Tha.
params.citation: chandrakantha.com tabla psychoacoustics; UBC Phys341 Tabla

params.rationale: Circular skin membrane; only Membrane consumes airLoading + tensionMod, both part of the dayan's physics.
Goatskin is a mid woody/skin material — neither dead-dull nor metallic; mid brightness keeps the pitched modes present without hiss.

params.physicalTarget: f0 = 500·0.1^0.42 ≈ 190 Hz body base (modes then ring up to the 300–420 Hz pitched region; pitchEnv seeds the exact strike pitch)
params.rationale: Small treble drum → high f0; 0.42 places the membrane in the dayan register, matched by the pitchEnv start.
params.citation: UBC Phys341: dayan Tin pitch ~300 Hz, peaks 550/810 Hz

params.physicalTarget: decayTime = baseDecay·exp(lerp(ln0.3,ln3,0.58))≈1.0× → moderate pitched ring (Na/Tin open)
params.rationale: Open resonant bols ring a few hundred ms; 0.58 is the open-stroke length, shortened per-bol for damped strokes.
params.citation: Patel/Anand: longer decay for Tin/Teen, shorter for Na/Te

params.physicalTarget: r/a = 0.40·0.9 = 0.36 — mid-edge Bessel sampling (open Dayan/Tha). Per-bol: Na/Tin 0.10–0.18 (edge/rim, bright), Tha 0.50 (centre/open), Tete 0.10 (damped tap)
params.rationale: Strike position IS the bol — re-weights which modes ring; edge = bright pitched tin, centre = full tha. The single param that differentiates Na/Tin/Tha/Tete.
params.citation: UBC Phys341 Tabla; Harmonic & Timbre Analysis of Tabla Strokes (arXiv 1510.04880)

params.rationale: Default per-pad loudness; dayan sits forward in a kit but below the kick — trim per bol.
Seeds the exact strike pitch of the dayan's pitched tone (this also fixes the modal start f0 on every body post-audit H-3). 420 Hz = bright dayan tuning.
params.citation: prompt target; UBC Phys341 (dayan pitched-stroke 300–420 Hz region)

params.rationale: Tiny downward settle — the small tension relaxation after a hard strike, not an 808 boom.

params.physicalTarget: 0.04·500 = 20 ms glide (and ENABLES the pitch env / seeds the start pitch)
params.rationale: A fast 20 ms settle gives the characteristic crisp attack 'snap' of the dayan; Time>0 is also the master enable that applies the 420 Hz start to the modal bank.
The tension relaxation is front-loaded — fast drop then settle.
The dayan is prized for tonal clarity (near-harmonic) — keep modes UN-stretched; inharmonicity is the wrong direction for this drum.

params.physicalTarget: skew = 2·0.60−1 = +0.20 → high modes decay faster, energy tilts to lows over time
params.rationale: Real skin: high pitched partials damp faster than the fundamental, leaving the tonal ring. Per-bol: Tin 0.40, Na 0.65 (brighter sustain).
params.citation: Patel/Anand decay analysis; Chaigne & Askenfelt b3·f² HF loss

params.physicalTarget: 0.30·(8 integer-harmonic oscillators 1..8 at f0, 1/k amplitudes, −6 dB/oct)
params.rationale: This is the SYAHI surrogate: overlays a true integer-harmonic series (post-audit 1/k) on the membrane to give the dayan its definite pitch/harmonicity the bare Bessel bank lacks. Moderate amount = pitched but still drum-like.

params.physicalTarget: env-level waveshaping, drive=1+6·vel·0.20·env → light louder=brighter brightening
params.rationale: Light, velocity-sensitive: a hard dayan strike is slightly brighter/edgier than a soft one (skin nonlinearity). Kept light per prompt ('light nonlinearCoupling').
params.citation: audit M-3/M-4 redesign (Stowell tap=bell/hard=broadband; Ducceschi/Bilbao energy cascade)

params.physicalTarget: 0.45 interpolation of low (m,1) modes toward Rossing near-harmonic timpani series 1:1.5:2:2.44:2.9
params.rationale: The closest in-engine analogue to the syahi's harmonic alignment: pulls the lowest Bessel modes toward the measured near-harmonic series, deepening/aligning the low partials. Lower than a deep tom (0.6) because the dayan is small/bright.
params.citation: Rossing 1982 timpani; Membrum membrane_mapper airLoading curve

params.rationale: Tiny organic detune for a hand-made skin without smearing the pitch (the dayan must stay tonal).

params.physicalTarget: amt·3.3e-5·modalEnergy → up to ~0.5–0.7 semitone upward at hard velocity (Membrane-only)
params.rationale: Subtle energy-dependent pitch nudge on hard strikes — present but far smaller than a tom's kerthump (small tight skin). Per-bol: Tin 0.15, Na 0.18.
The dayan is tonal, not noisy — only a whisper of contact noise (well below the default 0.35 snare-ish value).
params.citation: chandrakantha: tabla is harmonic/tonal, not anharmonic

params.rationale: Neutral skin contact noise; nearly inaudible at mix 0.08 so left mid.

params.physicalTarget: 0.55·vel·env·filteredNoise (half into exciter feed + full to output ·0.445)
params.rationale: The bright fingertip contact tick is a defining part of the dayan attack (Na/Tin especially). Raised for edge bols (0.85), lowered for soft Tha (0.40).
params.citation: UBC Phys341 (sharp contact transient on edge strokes)

params.rationale: Very short fingertip contact = crisp tick. Longer (0.30→2.9 ms) for the palm-flat Tha.
Sharp, bright tick for the edge bols (Na/Tin ~0.78–0.85). Dull (0.45→~900 Hz) for Tha.

params.physicalTarget: feedforward drive 0.35 from head into secondary shell bank
params.rationale: The wooden dayan shell adds a short body resonance under the head; modest coupling gives weight without a tom-like boom.
params.citation: Chromaphone head↔shell idiom; audit L-7/L-8 feedforward topology

params.rationale: Engages the wooden-shell resonance (only active because Coupling Strength>0).
Small wooden dayan shell — slightly below the head pitch, adds a tight low body.
Wooden shell: moderately damped, short — woodier than the head.

params.physicalTarget: b1 = 0.2+0.30·49.8 ≈ 15 s⁻¹ flat damping floor (overrides Decay-derived flat term)
params.rationale: Sets the overall pitched-ring RT60 explicitly so the dayan rings cleanly but not endlessly; mirrors the existing dayan reference.

params.physicalTarget: b3 = 0.05e-3 = 5e-5 s — light f² damping (long-ish highs → metallic-leaning, keeps pitched partials alive)
params.rationale: Low b3 keeps the pitched overtones ringing (the syahi tone) rather than a dead woody thud — that tonal sustain IS the dayan.
params.citation: Chaigne & Askenfelt R=b1+b3·f²; chandrakantha harmonic sustain

defaultedParams.why: ToneShaper filter left bypassed (cutoff norm 1.0 = ≥20 kHz, envAmt 0.5 = 0) — the membrane + airLoading + ModeInject already define the dayan timbre; no post-body EQ needed.
0 = bypass; the dayan is clean and tonal — saturation/odd-harmonic flavour would muddy its pitched clarity.
0 = bypass; wavefolding is a west-coast harmonic-mangler, antithetical to a clean harmonic drum.
Single-segment 420→380 Hz glide is enough; the dayan has no two-stage pitch contour.
Off for the core pitched bols — the dayan timbre is static within a hit (Morph is used on the Na variant for a tap→bloom, but neutral here).
Left neutral 0.5 (zero delta) — the recipe sets the underlying material/decay/skew/inject directly, so macros stay as user performance handles.
Left at defaults — the noise layer is nearly muted (mix 0.08), so its filter shape is inaudible.

defaultedParams.param: Mode Inject is used but Feedback/FM/Friction/NoiseBurst secondary params (offsets 32,33,34,35)
defaultedParams.why: No-ops for the Impulse/Mallet exciter — left at struct defaults.
0 = no choke for the open pitched bols; (a real kit would optionally choke damped Tete against open Na in a group, but per-pad default is 0).
0 = main bus; the dayan is a primary voice, no aux routing.
0.5 = centre; tabla dayan sits centred (the bayan provides the left-hand bass). Pan is a kit-placement choice left at default for the solo drum.
Left at 0.5 default — inter-pad sympathetic coupling is a kit-level effect; the per-pad weight stays neutral and only sounds if the kit enables Global Coupling/Tom Resonance.
1.0 = on (default).

physics: See physics field above.

bodyModel: NoiseBody (offset 1 = 5). NoiseBody = a plate-ratio modal bank (32 inharmonic metallic modes) PLUS an internal filtered-noise layer — the correct model for a no-definite-pitch, inharmonic, broadband-metallic idiophone whose strike spectrum "moves upward through the spectrum and rings at a few kHz" (SOS metallic-percussion analysis). The plate-ratio bank gives the inharmonic skeleton of the zils; the internal noise layer + the always-on parallel noise layer give the dense, noise-like shimmer of many small discs colliding. A secondary Shell bank (secondaryEnabled) is added to capture the discrete inharmonic metallic jingle ring on top of the broadband body.
exciter: NoiseBurst (offset 0 = 2). The NoiseBurstExciter fires a short violet-noise bandpass burst into the body — physically this is the broadband "many-discs-rattling-at-once" contact event, not a single clean impulse. It seeds the metallic modes with HF-weighted energy (violet = +6 dB/oct), matching the bright, HF-dominated attack of a jingle hit. Burst duration kept short (≈6 ms) so the attack reads as a crisp shake/tap rather than a sustained scrape.

params.rationale: Broadband violet-noise contact burst = many small discs rattling; seeds metallic modes with HF-weighted energy, not a clean single impulse.
Plate-ratio inharmonic modal bank + internal noise layer = a no-definite-pitch broadband metallic idiophone (zils behave like tiny cymbals).

params.physicalTarget: NoiseBody brightness = 0.7+0.3*0.85 = 0.955; internal noise cutoff = clamp(1500+5000*0.85)=5750 Hz; reso 0.6+0.4*0.85=0.94
params.rationale: Thin brass/steel discs are very metallic and bright; high material maximises metallic high-mode brightness.

params.physicalTarget: f0 = 1500*0.1^0.20 ≈ 946 Hz (small body); plate ratios fan partials into 2–12 kHz
params.rationale: Small jingle discs → high body centre; pushes the metallic skeleton up into the 5–10 kHz shimmer band.

params.physicalTarget: decay multiplier ≈ exp(lerp(ln0.3,ln3.0,0.22)) ≈ 0.46x on the body base; short metallic body ring
params.rationale: A single tap/shake hit is short (~150–250 ms); HF metal modes are the shortest-lived.
Off-centre strike excites a wide spread of inharmonic plate modes (default; an edge-ish disc contact).
Tambourine sits as an accent layer, a touch below the kit centre to avoid harsh 5–10 kHz dominance.
Short crisp contact burst → a tight shake/tap attack rather than a long scrape.
The jingle shimmer IS broadband noise; the parallel noise layer is the loudest voice in this archetype.
Bright shimmer lives 5–10 kHz+; a near-open lowpass keeps the metallic top-end air.
Tambourine noise is broadband, not a narrow resonant peak — keep Q low to avoid a pitched whistle.
Short shimmer for a single hit; HF jingle energy is the shortest-lived band.
Violet noise concentrates energy in the HF shimmer band where colliding discs radiate.

params.physicalTarget: attack click layer mix 0.30 (drives modes + output)
params.rationale: Adds the sharp disc/frame contact tick that defines the tambourine attack.
Very short metallic contact transient (default), a tick not a thud.
Metallic tick belongs in the bright 5–10 kHz region, matching the jingle shimmer.
Air-loading is a Membrane-only timpani correction; meaningless and off for an idiophone NoiseBody.

params.physicalTarget: ~8% sinusoidal frequency dither on modal partials
params.rationale: Many imperfect discs of slightly different size → de-tuned cluster; scatter breaks single-plate regularity for organic jingle shimmer.

params.physicalTarget: b1 = 0.2+0.32*49.8 ≈ 16.1 s^-1 flat damping (overrides Decay-derived flat term)
params.rationale: Sets a moderate, short metallic flat-decay floor so the body doesn't ring like a sustained cymbal.

params.physicalTarget: b3 = 0 → no f^2 high-frequency damping (pure flat damping)
params.rationale: Metal sustains its highs; zero b3 keeps the bright metallic top-end alive (vs woody f^2 roll-off).

params.physicalTarget: feedforward drive 0.40 from body into the secondary shell bank
params.rationale: Drives the metallic-jingle secondary so the inharmonic ring sits on top of the broadband body.

params.physicalTarget: ON → 24-mode Shell secondary bank spun up at noteOn
params.rationale: The metallic secondary captures the discrete inharmonic jingle ring the broadband noise can't (per the prompt brief).

params.physicalTarget: sizeRatio = 1-0.20*0.75 = 0.85 → shell f0 ≈ 0.85*946 ≈ 804 Hz; shell ratios fan up to kHz
params.rationale: Small high-pitched shell = tiny jingle discs; keeps the secondary ring in the bright metallic band.
Metallic, bright secondary shell = brass/steel jingle character.
Nudges sympathetic coupling + nonlinear brightening + injected modes up to thicken the many-disc cluster (per brief).

defaultedParams.param: Filter Type (7) / Cutoff (8)=1.0 / Resonance (9)=0 / Env Amount (10)=0.5 / Env A,D,S,R (17–20)
defaultedParams.why: ToneShaper SVF left bypassed (cutoff 1.0 = transparent, env amount 0.5 = no mod); the noise-layer + body already shape the spectrum, no post-body filter needed.
No saturation/wavefolding wanted — the jingle is broadband-bright already; Drive/Fold add harmonics/grit not part of a clean tambourine.
Time=0 disables the whole pitch envelope; an idiophone has no pitch glide, so all pitch-env params stay inert.
The NoiseBody plate ratios are already inharmonic; default neutral stretch keeps the realistic metallic skeleton (mode scatter, not stretch, supplies the disc-cluster variation here).
No deliberate high/low spectral tilt across modes; the noise layer + material already set the bright balance.
Injecting a tonal 1/k harmonic series would impose a pitch on a no-definite-pitch instrument; left off (Complexity macro adds only a tiny amount).
Amplitude-driven brightening is for dynamic plate/shell crash character; a tambourine tap doesn't need velocity-swept brightening (Complexity macro adds a trace).
No intra-hit material sweep — the timbre is static per hit; morph off.
Tambourine isn't an open/closed mute pair; no choke grouping.
Routes to the main bus by default; no multi-out send needed for this archetype.
No-op: only meaningful for the FMImpulse exciter (NoiseBurst selected).
No-op: only meaningful for the Feedback exciter.
No-op: only meaningful for the Friction exciter.
Per-pad inter-pad sympathetic-coupling weight; inaudible unless the kit-level Global Coupling/Snare Buzz/Tom Resonance is engaged — left neutral (Complexity nudges it slightly).

defaultedParams.param: Tightness (37) / Brightness (38) / Body Size (39) / Punch (40) macros = 0.5
defaultedParams.why: Neutral; the explicit per-param values already place material/decay/brightness/size/attack — macros left at no-delta so the hand-voiced values are preserved (Complexity is the one intentional macro).
Membrane-only energy pitch glide; a NoiseBody idiophone has no tension modulation.
Pad is active (default).
Tambourine commonly sits centred or is panned per-arrangement; default centre is the neutral starting point (equal-power, bit-identical to legacy mono).

physics:
PITCH/FUNDAMENTAL: A tambourine has NO definite pitch — the zils (small thin brass/steel/bronze discs, paired) are tiny free-edged cymbals whose modes are strongly inharmonic; only the head tap implies any low component (head response > ~200 Hz). So the model must NOT be a tuned bell/membrane; it is a broadband inharmonic idiophone. Mapped f0: NoiseBody at size=0.20 → 1500·0.1^0.20 ≈ 946 Hz (the centre of the metallic body), with the plate ratios fanning partials up into the 2–12 kHz region where the jingle shimmer lives.
PARTIAL/MODE STRUCTURE & INHARMONICITY: Zils, like cymbals/tam-tams, are rich in inharmonic partials with no whole-number ratios (Wikipedia Inharmonicity / Harmonic series; HandWiki Overtone). The plate-ratio NoiseBody bank + a coupled Shell (free-free Euler-Bernoulli) secondary supply this inharmonic skeleton; high modeScatter (≈0.55 → ~8% sinusoidal frequency dither) breaks any residual regularity so multiple discs read as a dense cluster rather than a single tuned plate.
SPECTRAL SHAPE / NOISE CONTENT: The defining timbre is a broadband, noise-like, HF-dominated shimmer — energy concentrated 5–10 kHz (mix engineering consensus: tambourine "presence/brightness" sits 5–10 kHz, can be harsh and is often high-shelf-cut; jingles produce the high-frequency tones, head the low). So the parallel noise layer is the dominant voice: bright (cutoff 0.92 ≈ 11.9 kHz), violet color (brightest tilt), high mix.
DECAY/T60 PER BAND: SOS metallic-percussion analysis: on a struck metal idiophone the HF modes are the SHORTEST-lived; over a few hundred ms energy migrates downward, leaving low modes last. A single tambourine tap/shake hit is short — ~150–250 ms — though sustained rolls/shakes ring longer (beryllium-copper jingles deliberately sustain). Mapped: decay 0.22 and noiseLayerDecay 0.22 → ~55 ms noise envelope, short metallic body — a crisp single hit, not a wash.
ATTACK/CONTACT CLICK: The hit is a sharp transient contact (disc-on-disc and stick/hand-on-frame). Modelled by the always-on Click layer at a bright bandpass centre (clickBrightness 0.85 ≈ 6.7 kHz) plus the violet NoiseBurst — a metallic tick, not a woody thud.
PITCH GLIDE: None — idiophone, no membrane tension glide ("kerthump"); tensionModAmt is Membrane-only and left off. MATERIAL: thin brass/bronze/steel discs → very metallic, so material is high (0.85 → NoiseBody brightness 0.955, internal noise cutoff ≈ 5.75 kHz) and the f²-damping override b3 is set to 0 (pure flat damping = long, metallic highs).

bodyModel: Bell
exciter: Impulse

params.rationale: A tingsha is struck by a hard, abrupt bronze rim-on-rim contact — an impulse, not a soft mallet or bow. Impulse injects a broadband click that excites all the metallic inharmonic modes at once.

params.physicalTarget: Bell (enum idx 4): church-bell Chladni partials, 16 modes, hum at 0.25·f0
params.rationale: Tingsha are tuned cast-bronze cymbal-BELLS, not membranes or large free cymbals; the sparse, tonal, inharmonic partial set of the Bell body matches a small thick bronze disc better than Plate (dense crash) or NoiseBody (cymbal+noise). idx 4 = round(0.8·5).

params.physicalTarget: Bell brightness 0.7+0.3·0.95 = 0.985 (near-max), base decay lerp(0.4,1.4,0.95)=1.35 s
params.rationale: Bronze bell metal is maximally metallic: high brightness drives the material-derived b3 high-freq damping low, giving the long-lived upper partials that define the tingsha shimmer.

params.physicalTarget: f0_nominal = 800·0.1^0.12 = 607 Hz; partials span ~152 Hz (hum) to ~7.3 kHz (12×)
params.rationale: Small thick disc → high pitch. 0.12 keeps the nominal high and the upper partials in the 0.6–6 kHz band where the tingsha's above-2000-Hz bright energy lives.

params.physicalTarget: Bell strike azimuth θ=0.15·(π/2)=0.236 rad; near a meridional antinode so most partials are excited
params.rationale: A tingsha is struck on the rim/soundbow exciting the full partial set; a near-zero azimuth keeps all (m) meridional modes strongly present (computeBellAmplitude |cos(m·θ)|) for the full bright ring.

A bare bell body with no noise padding is loud and clean; trim slightly below the 0.8 default so the long bright ring sits musically under the −1 dBTP bus limiter alongside other kit pads (post-audit gain staging).
No post-body EQ wanted — the Bell body already defines the pure metallic spectrum; bypass keeps it transparent (cutoff≥20k AND env amount 0).
No filter sweep in a tingsha; neutral keeps the SVF inert and transparent.
Tingsha is a pure, clean tone — no saturation/odd-harmonic warmth. Bypass at 0.
No west-coast folding; it would destroy the clean metallic purity.

params.physicalTarget: 0 ms → pitch envelope DISABLED (returns natural f0)
params.rationale: A tingsha has no 808-style pitch drop; the strike-tone settles immediately to its modal pitches. Master-disable the pitch-env feature.

Pushes the Bell partials apart into the clearly INHARMONIC metallic regime of struck bronze cymbal-bells (α~0.3–0.8); >neutral (0.333) deliberately stretches beyond the physical bell ratios for the cymbal edge of the tingsha.

params.physicalTarget: bipolar 0.38·2−1 = −0.24; per-mode tilt ratio^(+0.24) lifts upper partials (ratio>1), trims hum; skewBias 1−0.3·(−0.24)=1.072
params.rationale: Tingsha shimmer lives in the upper partials; negative skew (norm<0.5) tilts energy toward the high inharmonic partials, matching the high pure ring rather than a low bell hum.
ModeInject adds an INTEGER-harmonic (1/k) tonal series — the opposite of a tingsha's inharmonic metal spectrum; it would make it sound like a pitched organ tone. Keep off.

params.physicalTarget: amount 0.22: drive=1+6·vel·0.22·env waveshaping; louder strike → brighter, sustained through ring
params.rationale: Struck bronze is mildly amplitude-nonlinear (harder hit = brighter, slightly sharper attack that relaxes). A small amount adds velocity-dependent metallic brightening (post-audit env-level waveshaping) without compressing the clean tone.

params.physicalTarget: parallel noise layer fully bypassed (zero output)
params.rationale: A struck bronze disc radiates tonal modes, not broadband noise — no hiss/sizzle. Tingsha is the cleanest archetype; noise off is essential to the pure tone.

params.physicalTarget: click layer 0.4·vel·env·filteredNoise; half into exciter feed + full into output
params.rationale: The bronze rim-on-rim contact gives a short bright metallic tink attack; a moderate click defines the strike without dominating the ring.
Hard, brief metal-on-metal contact — short burst for a sharp tick, not a soft thud.
Bronze contact transient is very bright/high; a high click center gives the sharp metallic tsss-tink rather than a dull knock.

params.physicalTarget: b1 = 0.2+0.08·49.8 = 4.2 s^-1 flat damping (overrides Decay's flat-damping derivation)
params.rationale: Sets a very low frequency-independent damping floor for the tens-of-seconds tail. Explicitly written low (the registered 0.5 default = 25 s^-1 would ring far too short) to realize the 10–20 s tingsha sustain.

params.physicalTarget: b3 = 0.04·1e-3 = 4e-5 s (very low f²-damping → metallic, long highs)
params.rationale: Bronze has minimal high-frequency damping, so the upper partials/shimmer persist; near-pure flat damping keeps it metallic, not woody. Slight non-zero so the very highest partials still decay a touch faster (purification).

params.physicalTarget: no Bessel air-loading frequency correction (no-op on Bell)
params.rationale: Air loading is a Membrane-only frequency table; a tingsha has no drumhead/air cavity, and Bell ignores it — set to 0 for honesty.

params.physicalTarget: ~1.8% sinusoidal frequency dither on the modal ratios
params.rationale: Hand-cast tingsha are slightly imperfect and the paired cymbals are NEARLY-equal (sympathetic beating); a little scatter adds organic detune/shimmer rather than a perfectly synthetic spectrum.
Default center placement; per-pad pan is a kit-arrangement choice, not an intrinsic tingsha property.
The pad must sound.

defaultedParams.why: ToneShaper filter is bypassed (cutoff=1.0), so its LP/HP/BP shape is a no-op.
No-op while the filter is bypassed.
Filter envelope is inert (Filter Env Amount neutral 0.5) — no filter sweep in a pure bell tone.
PitchEnv Time=0 disables the entire pitch envelope; a tingsha has no pitch glide.
No intra-hit material sweep — the metal's timbre is static through the ring; morph stays off.
Tingsha ring freely (the long sustain is the point); no mute-group choking.
Main bus; multi-out routing is a mix decision, not intrinsic to the sound.
No-op: only the FMImpulse exciter reads it, and Impulse is selected.
No-op: only the Feedback exciter reads it.
No-op: only the NoiseBurst exciter reads it.
No-op: only the Friction exciter reads it.
Left at 0.5 neutral; cross-pad sympathetic coupling is only audible when a kit-level Global/Snare-Buzz/Tom resonance is engaged (a kit decision).
All left at neutral 0.5 (zero delta) so the explicit per-param voicing above is preserved exactly; macros are performance one-knobs, not part of the baseline.
No-op: Noise Mix=0 fully bypasses the noise layer, so its filter/color params do nothing.

defaultedParams.param: Coupling Strength / Secondary Enabled / Secondary Size / Secondary Material
defaultedParams.why: Head↔shell coupling is for drum-shell body weight; a tingsha has no shell. The two-cymbal sympathetic coupling is approximated instead by Mode Scatter + long decay, so the secondary bank stays off.
Membrane-only (gated to Membrane body); a no-op on Bell, and a tingsha has no tension glide anyway.

physics:
REAL ACOUSTICS (cited). A tingsha is a pair of small (2.25–4 in / ~57–100 mm diameter), very thick, cast bronze/bell-metal cymbal-bells joined by a cord and struck rim-to-rim (Wikipedia: Tingsha; Himalayas Shop). The radiated tone is HIGH and PURE: vendor and sound-healing literature consistently place the dominant tone "above 2000 Hz," with 2500 Hz quoted for a specific instrument. So perceived pitch ~2000–2500 Hz, with the strongest audible partials in the 2–6 kHz band — a tiny thick disc puts its modes very high.

PARTIAL STRUCTURE / INHARMONICITY. A tingsha is acoustically a free-edged thick metal disc/bell hybrid, NOT a membrane: its partials are INHARMONIC (non-integer ratios), the signature metallic ring. Cymbal/free-plate modal analysis gives inharmonic ratios following a Chladni power law in (m+2n) and inharmonicity coefficients α ~0.3–0.8 for bronze. Because it is small and thick (closer to a bell than a crash cymbal) the spectrum is SPARSER and more tonal than a large crash — a few clear, widely-spaced inharmonic partials rather than a dense wash, which is why it reads as a clear pure tone rather than noise. The two near-identical cymbals couple by sympathetic resonance — vibration in one reinforces the other, amplifying and EXTENDING the ring; a slow energy exchange / beating between two almost-equal pitches.

DECAY / T60. The defining feature is an extremely long, slow decay: authentic tingsha linger 10–15 s, some 20 s–1 min. Per-band: the low/principal partials carry the long tail; the very high partials and the initial shimmer decay faster (edge/high-order cymbal modes have faster decay constants), so the tone PURIFIES toward its principal partials — the sound-bath shimmer-resolving-to-pure-ring.

TRANSIENT / CLICK. Rim-on-rim bronze contact gives a short, BRIGHT metallic tink (high-frequency contact click, a few ms), then the sustained inharmonic ring. No skin, no air cavity.

NOISE CONTENT. Essentially NONE — a struck thick bronze disc radiates tonal modes, not broadband noise. So the parallel noise layer should be OFF.

PITCH GLIDE. No deliberate large pitch sweep like an 808. Only the mild nonlinear character of struck metal (louder = slightly brighter/sharper attack that relaxes). Pitch envelope left OFF; NonlinearCoupling carries the louder-equals-brighter metal character.

MEMBRUM MAPPING. Bell body f0 law f0_nominal = 800·0.1^size. At size=0.12 → 800·0.7586 = 607 Hz nominal; the Bell ratio table places hum at 0.25·607≈152 Hz, nominal 607 Hz, upper partials up to 12× ≈ 7.3 kHz (bell_modes.h; natural_fundamental.h). High material (low b3) keeps energy/length in the upper partials (600 Hz–6 kHz), matching the tingsha band. modeStretch>neutral widens partial spacing into the inharmonic metallic regime (post-audit 1-indexed √(1+B·(k+1)²), B_max 0.01). decaySkew<neutral (norm<0.5 → physical>0, tilt ratio^(−skew) pivots at nominal=1.0) lifts upper partials and shortens the hum. Very long Decay (0.92) → ~2.66× on the Bell base for the tens-of-seconds tail. Low b1 override realizes the long flat-damping floor. Bright Impulse click = bronze tink. Noise layer OFF.

archetype: Timbales (world) — single-headed Latin metal-shell drums, hi (macho) + lo (hembra) tuned a 4th–5th apart
bodyModel: Membrane (Bessel circular drumhead, 48 modes) for the struck head, with the Secondary (shell) resonator bank ENABLED to add the metallic cáscara ring
exciter: NoiseBurst (violet-noise bandpass burst → short, bright stick-on-plastic contact)

params.rationale: Stick on plastic head = a short filtered-noise contact, not a hard impulse click; NoiseBurst gives the bright violet-noise burst that excites the head.
params.citation: RareInstrument timbales; Cook SNT noise-burst excitation

params.rationale: The struck element is a tensioned circular head; the metal shell is added via the Secondary bank, not as the primary body.
Bright plastic head with a metallic edge; mid-high keeps the cutting timbale tone without going fully metallic (the metal lives in the shell).

params.physicalTarget: Membrane f0=500·0.1^0.40=199 Hz natural (head pitch set by pitch-env to 380→280)
params.rationale: Small hi (macho) drum; head sits in the upper-200s/300s Hz band per measured timbale peaks.
params.citation: CongaChops macho F2–C4; HomeRecording peaks <400 Hz

params.rationale: Steel/aluminium timbale is bright and fast-decaying / dry.

params.physicalTarget: r/a=0.27 Bessel mode-shape sampling (off-center toward edge)
params.rationale: Sticks strike toward the edge for the bright cáscara/rim voice, exciting higher modes.
Loud, projecting instrument; sits hot relative to perc filler but under the body-headroom budget.
params.citation: audit gain-staging appendix (−6 dBFS body budget)

params.rationale: Hi-drum struck-head start pitch (brief: 380 Hz).
Hi-drum settle pitch — small downward batter/tension drop.
Fast 'kerthump' tension drop typical of a hand-struck head; also the master enable for the whole pitch-env feature.
Natural exponential tension relaxation — quick drop then settle.

params.physicalTarget: 40% interpolation Bessel→Rossing air-loaded curve on low membrane modes
params.rationale: Single open head on a shallow shell is only lightly air-loaded (vs a deep closed timpani kettle), so depress the low modes only partially.

params.physicalTarget: ~3% sinusoidal frequency dither on modal f_k
params.rationale: Small natural-imperfection detune so repeated hits aren't machine-identical.
Light velocity-dependent tension micro-glide on hard hits; membrane-gated so it is physically valid here.

params.physicalTarget: shell bank spun up at noteOn (with Coupling Strength>0)
params.rationale: The metallic cáscara shell ring is the defining timbale color — enable the parallel shell resonator.
Head energy drives the shell at a strong-but-balanced level so the shell rings audibly without swamping the head.
Shell resonance sits below but near the head pitch (metal cáscara ring), per the brief's 0.40 secondary size.

params.physicalTarget: shell b1≈max(25,2.5·bodyB1)+0.35·15; b3=0.65e-5 → bright, longer metallic highs
params.rationale: Steel/aluminium = bright, fairly long metallic ring (brief: 0.65).
Tight, articulate head decay floor; also seeds the shell b1.

params.physicalTarget: b3=0.1e-3 s → mild f^2 high-mode damping
params.rationale: Keep the bright metallic highs alive (low b3) — a head over metal is not a woody thump.

params.physicalTarget: click transient at 0.65·vel·env, half into exciter + full to output
params.rationale: Sharp stick/rim contact tick — the bright timbale attack.
Very short, hard stick contact.
Bright, cutting tick consistent with steel-shell/rim articulation.
Modest stick-contact/air hiss; supports the attack without a snare-style sizzle.
Bright broadband contact noise (not dark brown/pink).
Short, snappy head-contact burst.
Head is a near-physical membrane; the metallic inharmonicity comes from the shell bank, not from stretching the head.
No deliberate spectral tilt on the head; damping law already shapes it.
Hi timbale placed slightly left; lo placed slightly right for a realistic two-drum stereo image.
No post-body tone-shaping filter needed; the body+shell already define the spectrum.

defaultedParams.param: Filter Type / Filter Resonance / Filter Env (Amount/Atk/Dec/Sus/Rel)
defaultedParams.why: ToneShaper SVF left bypassed (cutoff at 20 kHz, env amount 0.5=0) — the modal head+shell define the timbre, no post-filter sweep needed.
0 = exact dry bypass; timbales are a clean acoustic tone, no saturation/wavefolding flavour wanted.

defaultedParams.param: PitchEnv Knee / Mid / Mid Frac / Curve2
defaultedParams.why: Single-segment glide is sufficient for the small 380→280 drop; the 3-point knee (off) adds nothing here.
0 = exact bypass; the shell secondary bank already supplies the metallic inharmonic content, no synthetic harmonic series needed.
0 = exact bypass; a struck metal-shell drum at normal dynamics doesn't need extra amplitude-driven brightening, and it would muddy the clean tick.
Morph off — material is static over the short note; no intra-hit timbre sweep in a real timbale.
0 = no choke; hi and lo ring independently (not an open/closed-hat pair).
0 = main bus; standard routing, no multi-out send for this archetype.

defaultedParams.param: FM Ratio / Feedback Amount / Friction Pressure
defaultedParams.why: No-ops for the NoiseBurst exciter (only the matching exciter reads them).

Per-pad sympathetic-coupling weight and the five macros left neutral (0.5) — kit-level coupling is low and the explicit params already voice the sound, so no macro deltas applied (per-pad-where-meaningful policy keeps neutral macros).
Left near default — the noise layer is a low-level support layer at mix 0.30; only its color (white) is meaningful, so cutoff/reso/decay stay at neutral mid values.
1.0 = on (default); the pad sounds.

physics:
FUNDAMENTAL / PITCH: Timbales are single-headed metal-shell drums (cáscara) with a plastic batter head; the hi "macho" (typ. 13"–14") and lo "hembra" (typ. 14"–15") are the SAME instrument tuned ~a perfect 4th–5th apart (CongaChops: hembra ≈ B1–B2, macho ≈ F2–C4; the interval is what matters, not absolute pitch). Measured/practical energy peaks reported around ~250 Hz and just-below-400 Hz with "lots of overtones" (HomeRecording forum, R. Aylestone). I voice the head pitch-env glide hi 380→280 Hz, lo 280→200 Hz, consistent with that and with the brief. The Membrane natural f0 (500·0.1^size) is 199 Hz at size 0.40 (hi) / 141 Hz at size 0.55 (lo), but the pitch envelope (PitchEnv Time>0) overrides it during the strike so the struck head sits in the 200–380 Hz band.
PARTIAL/MODE STRUCTURE & INHARMONICITY: A struck circular membrane radiates the Bessel (m,n) mode series 1 : 1.59 : 2.14 : 2.30 : 2.65 ... (Fletcher & Rossing). A single-headed drum head is only lightly air-loaded (shallow open shell, one head) vs a closed timpani kettle, so the lowest modes are depressed only modestly — partial airLoading (0.40), not the full Rossing timpani 1:1.5:2 harmonic morph. ON TOP of the head sits the METAL SHELL ring: steel/aluminium cáscara gives a bright, inharmonic, free-free metallic resonance — captured by the Secondary (shell) bank (free-free Euler-Bernoulli ratios 1, 2.76, 5.40, 8.93…), at ~0.70·head-f0 (secondarySize 0.40) with a bright/long metallic damping (secondaryMaterial 0.65). This is the "metallic-shell secondary body (0.40-0.50/0.65)" in the brief.
DECAY / T60: Short and articulate. Steel shells = bright, fast-decaying, cutting; aluminium = even drier (DrumCenterNH / RareInstrument). Head ring is short (~0.25–0.4 s effective), shell ring slightly longer but still tight — players often damp the shell with the stick. I set Decay norm 0.30 (hi) / 0.40 (lo) and bodyDampingB1 0.32 (tight flat damping), with a small b3 (0.10) so highs aren't over-damped (metal keeps highs).
ATTACK / CONTACT CLICK: A hard stick on a tensioned plastic head and on the metal rim/shell produces a sharp, bright transient (the cáscara/rimshot "tick"). Modelled by the always-on Click layer (mix 0.65, bright bandpass ~ a few kHz) PLUS the NoiseBurst exciter's short violet-noise contact (≈8 ms) feeding the head.
NOISE CONTENT: Modest broadband stick-contact/air hiss, brighter (violet/white-ish) — a short, low-level noise layer (mix 0.30, bright color), NOT a snare-style sizzle.
PITCH GLIDE: Real single-headed drums show a small downward batter-tension/air-load pitch drop on the hardest hits (energy-dependent tension fall) plus the intentional voiced glide. Captured by the pitch envelope (380→280 / 280→200) and a light tensionModAmt (0.22, membrane-only) for velocity-dependent micro-glide.
MATERIAL: Plastic head over a thin METAL (steel/aluminium) shell → material mid-high (0.55 hi / 0.50 lo): bright head, and the metal character carried by the shell secondary bank.

archetype: Tom-Tom / Generic Membrane Cymbal-slot (uncrafted) — NoiseBody cymbal filler, configured-then-disabled in the Acoustic Studio Kit cymbal/perc slots
bodyModel: NoiseBody (offset 1 = 5). Plate-ratio modal bank (32 modes, AVX2-aligned) + internal filtered-noise layer. f0 base 1500 Hz. This is Membrum's purpose-built cymbal/hi-hat body: the plate Chladni ratios give an inharmonic metallic line spectrum and the on-board noise layer supplies the broadband shimmer that real cymbals concentrate above ~3 kHz (modal density rho(f) proportional to f^2). It is the body the Acoustic Studio Kit already uses for every cymbal pad.
exciter: NoiseBurst (offset 0 = 2). Violet-noise bandpass burst — the established short broadband driver for cymbal synthesis (Stowell: hard cymbal strikes need an impulsive broadband "thwack"; tau_c < 2 ms broadband excitation lights up many modes at once). This matches the Acoustic Studio Kit cymbal block exactly. (The task title also names a Membrane/Plate-perc alternative for the misc-perc filler pads, which use Mallet+Plate; the cymbal slots themselves are NoiseBurst+NoiseBody.)

params.rationale: Violet-noise bandpass burst is the canonical broadband cymbal driver (Stowell); matches the Acoustic Kit cymbal block.
Plate-ratio modal bank + internal noise layer = Membrum's cymbal/hat body; gives inharmonic metallic lines + >3 kHz shimmer.

params.physicalTarget: brightness=0.7+0.3*0.95=0.985 (very metallic); modal baseDecay lerp(0.2,1.0)=0.965; noise cutoff clamp(1500+5000*0.95)=6250 Hz; noise reso 0.6+0.4*0.95=0.98
params.rationale: Bronze cymbals are extremely bright with low damping; near-max material gives long metallic ring + high noise cutoff. Exact Acoustic-Kit cymbal value.
Keeps the modal band high/dense in the cymbal 'clang' region; small norm = high f0 = bright shimmer-dominant. Acoustic-Kit cymbal value.

params.physicalTarget: multiplier exp(lerp(ln0.3,ln3.0,0.8))=~1.93x; with material baseDecay ~0.965*(1+0.1*0.3) gives body decayTime ~1.9 s; noise envelope 60ms*1.93=116 ms
params.rationale: Cymbals ring far longer than drums (lower modes multi-second); long decay knob. Acoustic-Kit cymbal value.

params.physicalTarget: computePlateAmplitude(k,0.3) per-mode weights, floored 0.03 — mid-zone strike on the plate
params.rationale: Default struck position; a bow/edge strike weighting that still excites the high modes. Left at struct default (not overridden in cymbal block).
Cymbals sit slightly below kick/snare in a kit balance; trims the bright noise. Acoustic-Kit cymbal value. (Moot — pad disabled.)

params.physicalTarget: scatter=0.55 -> ~8% sinusoidal frequency dither on the plate ratios
params.rationale: Smears the clean plate lines toward the chaotic/quasi-random spectrum of a real crashed cymbal (clear modal lines dissolve at high level). Acoustic-Kit cymbal value.

params.physicalTarget: airLoading=0 -> pure ratios (and a no-op on NoiseBody regardless)
params.rationale: Air-loading is a membrane-only deep-drum correction; a metal cymbal must NOT be air-loaded. Explicitly zeroed in the cymbal block.

params.physicalTarget: b3=0 s -> pure FLAT damping, no f^2 high-mode roll-off = metal (long-ringing highs)
params.rationale: Bronze has very low internal damping; zero f^2 damping keeps the metallic shimmer ringing instead of wood-like decay. Acoustic-Kit cymbal value.
Moderate flat damping floor so the long ring still terminates; overrides the Decay-derived flat term. Acoustic-Kit cymbal value.

params.physicalTarget: parallel noise layer mix 0.5 (on top of NoiseBody's own internal noise) *0.243 standalone gain
params.rationale: Cymbals are noise-dominant; a strong always-on parallel hiss reinforces the broadband wash. Acoustic-Kit cymbal value.
Cymbal shimmer energy concentrates above ~3 kHz; an open/high cutoff passes the bright band. Acoustic-Kit cymbal value.
Violet noise (rising spectrum) matches the cymbal's HF-weighted shimmer; the canonical NoiseBurst color too. Acoustic-Kit cymbal value.

params.physicalTarget: ms = exp(ln20 + 0.7*(ln2000-ln20)) = ~590 ms noise envelope decay
params.rationale: Long noise tail so the wash sustains under the ringing modes (cymbal sustain). Acoustic-Kit cymbal value.
Mild emphasis only — cymbal noise is broadband, not a resonant whistle. Left at struct default.

params.physicalTarget: click transient mix 0.2 (half into exciter feed, full to out *0.445)
params.rationale: A small stick-contact 'thwack' for attack definition without a dominant tick (cymbal attack is broadband, not clicky). Acoustic-Kit cymbal value.
Sharp/bright stick contact tick consistent with a metal strike. Acoustic-Kit cymbal value.
Short broadband contact (tau_c < ~2 ms class) lights up many modes. Left at struct default.
Mid-length broadband excitation burst — the NoiseBurst driver's contact time; meaningful because NoiseBurst is the selected exciter. Struct default.
NoiseBody already uses inharmonic plate ratios; neutral stretch keeps the corrected Chladni spectrum. Neutral default.
No deliberate hi/lo decay tilt; the per-mode metallic balance comes from material/b3. Neutral default.
Center placement; per-pad pan left neutral for filler. Default.

params.physicalTarget: enabled<0.5 -> VoicePool::noteOn early-returns: pad never auditions, allocates, or uses CPU
params.rationale: CRITICAL: this is the whole point — these cymbal/perc slots are DISABLED filler. The cymbal pad indices are absent from the Acoustic Kit 'crafted' list, so disableUncraftedPads() forces enabled=0. Byte-identical silent placeholder.
No 'kerthump' glide on a cymbal; tension modulation is a tom/membrane effect and is gated off NoiseBody anyway. Default off.
Cymbals have no pitch glide; master-enable left at 0 so Start/End/Curve are all inert. Default off.
Body+noise already shape the cymbal; post-body filter left transparent. Default.
No saturation flavour needed; Drive is timbre-only and left off. Default.

physics: CYMBAL ACOUSTICS (the archetype these filler slots are voiced as): A 16-inch cymbal supports ~300 measured modes (Rossing, electronic holography). The spectrum is strongly INHARMONIC — partials do not follow an integer series; bronze cymbals show an inharmonicity that produces the metallic "clang" absent from membranes. Nominal lowest/"clang" region sits in the low-mid (commonly cited 150-400 Hz fundamental depending on diameter/thickness; the audible clang stays under ~6 kHz), but the perceptually defining content is the SHIMMER above ~3 kHz, where modal density grows as rho(f) ~ f^2 (mode density up with area, down with thickness^2). DECAY: very long and frequency-dependent — lower modes ring longest (multi-second on a crash), highs decay faster; energy bleeds upward early (Stowell "bwoosh": HF fills in over a fraction of a second rather than instantly). NONLINEAR/CHAOS: at high strike level the response becomes quasi-periodic then chaotic, clear modal lines dissolve into broadband noise (Touze/Chaigne; ScienceDirect 2022 chaotic-vibration study) — the source of crash "wash". TRANSIENT: a short broadband stick contact "thwack". NOISE CONTENT: dominant — cymbals are far more noise-like than tonal, brightest in the violet/high band. NO pitch glide (no tension "kerthump"; that is a tom/membrane effect). MATERIAL: bronze (B20/B8) — very bright, low internal damping, long sustain. MEMBRUM MAPPING CONSEQUENCES: high material (bright + long), small-ish size (high f0 band), long decay, modeScatter for the chaotic line-smearing, airLoading OFF (metallic, not air-loaded membrane), bodyDampingB3=0 (pure flat damping = long-ringing metallic highs, no wood-like f^2 roll-off), violet noise layer high+open. Because this is a DISABLED filler slot, none of it sounds — but the config is the physically-correct cymbal voicing so the byte pattern is meaningful filler.

physics:
REAL SNARE ACOUSTICS + TRAP/808 LINEAGE (numbers that drive the params):

PITCH / FUNDAMENTAL. An acoustic snare batter-head fundamental sits ~180-250 Hz (drumforum/iDrumtune/tune-bot: batter 220-340 Hz, reso 1.33-1.5x higher, perceived fundamental ~200 Hz). A bright/crisp snare is tuned UP toward ~250 Hz with batter and reso close together (Chris Brush / tune-bot: tight heads = bright, both heads near same pitch = brightest). Trap snares are sharply tuned, so I target a head f0 ~210-230 Hz. The "thump" body lives 200-300 Hz; the "thwack"/transient crack is 1.5-2.5 kHz; the snare-wire "crisp" lives high, ~3-10 kHz.

MODE STRUCTURE / INHARMONICITY. A real circular membrane with a coupled bottom head + shell is INHARMONIC: head (0,1)/(1,1)/(2,1) partials at roughly 1 : 1.5 : 2.0 : 2.4 (Rossing 1982; the membrane mapper's air-loaded Bessel table morphs onto exactly this 1:1.5:2:2.44:2.9 timpani-style series). So Membrane body with airLoading gives the physically-correct inharmonic head, NOT a 1:2:3 harmonic ladder. For a trap snare the body is short and secondary to the noise, so modes matter mostly for the brief tonal "knock."

NOISE CONTENT (the dominant percept). The snare-wire/buzz is broadband, HIGH-PASSED noise. In the 808 the snare = 2 bridged-T tone oscillators (~one octave apart; service-manual 238 Hz + 476 Hz, or the common 180/330 Hz "0,1 mode" pair per Sound on Sound / Gordon Reid) PLUS a white-noise generator through a HIGH-PASS filter, the "Snappy" knob setting noise decay. Trap/modern bright snares push that noise spectrum even higher and crispier (violet/white tilt, energy concentrated 3-10 kHz) and layer a bright clap on top (claps: bandpassed noise, upper-mid emphasis). So: bright, HF-tilted noise is the headline timbre.

DECAY / T60. Snares-engaged decay is FAST: ~64% of peak gone by 0.08 s (Madsen, UIUC Physics 406), vs slow snares-off. Modal "problem" snare modes can ring T60~1.5 s but the desired musical decay is ~0.5 s (Chowdhury). Trap snares are tighter still: a snappy noise tail ~120-300 ms and an even shorter body knock. Snappy/noise decay is the perceptual length.

TRANSIENT / CLICK. The stick "thwack" is a sharp 1.5-2.5 kHz-and-up contact transient (musicalinstrumentworld; SOS). Trap snares exaggerate this into a hard, very bright tick (sub-millisecond-to-few-ms click, HF-centered ~8-11 kHz) for "crisp snap" that cuts a dense mix.

PITCH GLIDE. Acoustic snares show a brief upward tension "crack" (energy-driven), but the dominant musical glide in synthesized/trap snares is a SHORT downward body drop (the 808/909 tone oscillators are decaying sines that also droop slightly; producers add a fast pitch-env on the body for "thump"). The brief here specifies 250->170 Hz, consistent with a short body knock that settles to a ~170 Hz fundamental — a fast downward thump.

MATERIAL. Steel/wood shell + Mylar heads + steel snare wires: the BODY is woody-to-tight (moderate material), the NOISE is the metallic/bright element. So body=moderate material + tight damping; brightness comes from the noise + click + LP filter envelope, not from a long metallic body.

VALUES THAT DRIVE PARAM CHOICE: head f0 ~210-230 Hz (Membrane size); noise HP/bright tilt 3-13 kHz (noise cutoff 0.95 -> ~13.3 kHz LP top-end, violet color); sharp click ~10.6 kHz center (click brightness 0.97); fast noise tail ~120-200 ms (noise decay); short body knock 250->170 Hz over a fast glide (~40-60 ms); inharmonic air-loaded head (airLoading on Membrane); LP filter env sweep for punch.

bodyModel: NoiseBody (plate-ratio 32-mode modal bank + internal noise layer) — the inharmonic, broadband cymbal/hat body. f0 = 1500·0.1^size; at size 0.10 → ~1191 Hz, so the dense plate-ratio partials sit in the 1.2–15 kHz "sparkle" band. The always-on parallel NoiseLayer (LP, Violet, ~13 kHz) supplies the bulk of the audible hat hash; the modal bank adds the faint metallic ring that keeps it from being pure noise.
exciter: NoiseBurst (violet-noise → bandpass burst, 2–15 ms). The correct physical analogue of two cymbals being struck/clashed: a very short broadband contact that excites the body and is itself heard as the initial "tss". Brightest-centroid exciter (violet noise) — matches the synthetic-hat brief.

params.rationale: Violet-noise bandpass burst = two-cymbal clash contact, highest spectral centroid of the exciters.
Plate-ratio inharmonic modes + internal noise = the canonical cymbal/hat body in Membrum.

physics:
REAL/SYNTHETIC ACOUSTICS. A hi-hat is two thin bronze cymbals. Acoustically its spectrum is densely inharmonic: the fundamental of the metal sits ~150–400 Hz depending on diameter/thickness, with partials following non-integer scaling laws (cymbal mode ratios ≈ f0·(n²+αn), α≈0.3–0.8 for bronze) and a perceptually "noise-like" hash above ~2 kHz extending to 15–20 kHz (Rollins normal-modes study; oemcymbal modal analysis). Most of the audible "shine/sparkle" lives 5–10 kHz; the "body" only ~300–500 Hz. Higher modes damp faster (τ_n ∝ ρh/(η·ω_n)), so a cymbal becomes duller as it rings. There is NO pitch glide — unlike toms/808-kicks there is no tension "kerthump". Transient: a fast broadband contact click from the stick/cymbal clash.

SYNTHESIS (the established trap-hat method, which Membrum should imitate, not the raw plate physics). Modern trap and the classic TR-808/909 lineage build hats as bandpassed/highpassed noise+oscillator hash with a fast AD envelope: the 808 sums six square-wave oscillators (≈205/304/370/523/540/800 Hz, deliberately inharmonic) → bandpass → series highpass, then a closed-hat decay ≈50 ms vs cymbal/open decay ≈350–1200 ms (808 service manual; baratatronix). One 808 cymbal bandpass centers ~7100 Hz; recreations recommend a 8–12 kHz bandpass + steep HP. The web-audio/Massive approach is essentially the same: bright (violet/white) noise → high HP/BP (cut everything below ~2 kHz) with resonance for metallic ring → short attack, no sustain, decay length sets closed vs open. "Multi-decay" trap hats are just several closed voices with graded short decays (~20–50 ms) for 1/16–1/32 roll programming plus one long open voice.

VALUES THAT DRIVE THE PARAMS. (1) Brightness/spectral tilt → Material 0.92 (NoiseBody brightness 0.976) + Noise Color Violet (norm 0.95, +6 dB/oct) + Noise Cutoff ~13 kHz (norm 0.95) — brightest hat in the kit. (2) Noise-dominant timbre → Noise Mix 0.85, modal bank deliberately quiet/scattered (Mode Scatter 0.25 de-pitches the metallic ring per cymbal inharmonicity). (3) Graded decays → noiseLayerDecay 0.05/0.08/0.12 → ≈25/29/35 ms (closed roll voices) + Body Damping b1 0.78/0.68/0.58 (≈39/34/29 s⁻¹) for tight closed rings; open = noiseLayerDecay 0.50 (~230 ms), b1 0.30 (~15 s⁻¹), measured against the 50 ms-closed / 350–1200 ms-open 808 spec. (4) Contact transient → NoiseBurst 8.5 ms + a faint bright Click (mix 0.18, ~9.4 kHz). (5) No pitch glide → PitchEnv Time 0 (feature disabled). (6) Choke group 1 across all variants for the open/closed mute behavior. (7) ToneShaper filter bypassed (cutoff 20 kHz) so nothing dulls the top — brightness is owned by the noise layer.

bodyModel: Membrane (BodyModelType::Membrane, norm 0.0). A struck circular drumhead Bessel bank is the closest physical model to the TR-808's bridged-T self-oscillating sine: at large Size the low Bessel modes are spread far apart and the b3·f² damping is near-zero on the fundamental, so the body decays as an essentially pure low sine — the 808's signature. Critically, Membrane is the ONLY body the tension-modulation pitch glide (offset 58) is gated to, and an exaggerated tension glide is core to this archetype, so the body choice is forced.
exciter: Impulse (ExciterType::Impulse, norm 0.0). A raised-cosine click is the digital analogue of the 808's ~1 ms trigger pulse that 'kicks' the bridged-T oscillator into motion. It deposits energy into the modes in-phase and gets out of the way, leaving the body to ring as a decaying sine. Mallet would soften the contact (less 808-like snap); NoiseBurst/Friction/Feedback all add sustained or broadband character that fights the clean sub. The audible front-of-transient 'tick' is supplied by the dedicated Click layer instead, which is the right tool (tunable contact time + brightness, half-fed into the body and half to output).

params.rationale: Digital analogue of the 808's ~1 ms trigger pulse that kicks the bridged-T oscillator into a decaying sine; deposits in-phase energy then gets out of the way.
Large-Size membrane fundamental rings as a near-pure low sine (808-like), and Membrane is the ONLY body the tension glide is gated to.

params.physicalTarget: brightness≈0.10; baseDecay=lerp(0.15,0.8,0.10)=0.215 s; minimal HF content
params.rationale: Very low/dark material keeps the body a clean sub sine with almost no upper-mode energy (808 is a near-pure sine, no metallic/woody overtones).
Huge body puts the natural fundamental at ~56 Hz — within the 808's measured 48–56 Hz range and the 35–60 Hz trap sub window — and weakens b3·f² damping for a long ring.

params.physicalTarget: decayTime multiplier exp(lerp(ln0.3,ln3,0.65))≈1.34× on a 0.215 s base ≈0.32 s legacy term (flat damping then set by b1)
params.rationale: Long tail so the 808 sustains as a tonal bassline note; the 808 decay control spans 50–800 ms and trap pushes it to the long end.
Default off-center strike favours the fundamental without over-exciting the (1,1)/(2,1) modes; a sub kick wants the lowest mode dominant.
Hot per-pad make-up so the sub sits loud as the bassline element; post N-1 measured-strike norm keeps it under the −6 dBFS body budget and bus −1 dBTP limiter.
No post-body filtering wanted; the body is already a clean low sine. Default 1.0 keeps the SVF bypassed.
Filter stays bypassed; no sweep needed.

params.physicalTarget: internalDrive=1+0.18·9=2.62 into reciprocal-sqrt, unity small-signal makeup
params.rationale: Gentle odd-harmonic warmth so the sub reads on small speakers without a fundamental (post-M-2 this is flavour, not level); a distorted 808 adds harmonics for phone/laptop playback.
Wavefolding is west-coast aggression, wrong for a clean 808 sub.
Exaggerated, audible boom: start well above the settle so the slow downward sweep is a tonal feature, not the 808's near-inaudible 6 ms attack snap.
Settles to a 35 Hz sub — bottom of the 30–60 Hz trap-808 fundamental window, tunable as the bassline root.

params.physicalTarget: 0.06·500 = 30 ms glide duration (master enable for the pitch env)
params.rationale: 30 ms is a slow, dramatic-but-musical 808 glide (trap glides span 30–60 ms quick to 100–200 ms slow); non-zero enables the whole pitch-env feature.

params.physicalTarget: curveAmount = 2·0.15−1 = −0.7 (fast initial drop, classic 808 exp decay)
params.rationale: Exponential-style fast initial drop matches the 808's pitch-env shape (envelope-controlled, rapid then settling).
No inharmonicity wanted; an 808 sub is the fundamental, partial spacing irrelevant. Neutral.

params.physicalTarget: phys = 2·0.45−1 = −0.10; skewBias=1+0.03=1.03; per-mode tilt ratio^0.10 (slight HF lift / low-mode-relative-shorten)
params.rationale: Mild tilt toward keeping the low fundamental dominant through the long tail (a touch longer-ringing lows vs highs) without coloring it.
The body fundamental IS the tone; no synthetic harmonic stack needed for a clean sub.
No dynamic brightening; keep the sub clean and the velocity dynamics intact.

params.physicalTarget: parallel noise layer fully bypassed (zero output)
params.rationale: An 808 sub has no hiss/breath component; the bridged-T output is a pure sine.

params.physicalTarget: mix 0.42; half into exciter feed + full to output, ×0.445 standalone gain
params.rationale: Supplies the audible attack 'tick' that defines the transient on small speakers (the body alone is sub-only), standing in for the 808's brief HF snap.
Short, tight contact = a defined attack click rather than a smeared thud; ~the 808's few-ms transient region.
A mid-low click (not a sharp tick) — present enough to define the attack on phones but dark enough not to clash with the clean sub aesthetic.
Explicit flat-damping floor sets the long sustaining tail length directly (overrides the Decay-derived flat term) — a controlled ~0.46 s 808 ring.
Moderate HF damping so any upper modes die fast and only the sub fundamental sustains — reinforces the pure-sine 808 character.

params.physicalTarget: pure Bessel ratios (no Rossing air-loading depression)
params.rationale: Air-loading is a real-drum timpani correction; for a synthetic 808 sine we want the clean ratios so Size/pitch-env fully control the fundamental.
No organic detune wanted on an electronic sub; bit-identical clean ratios.
No head↔shell body weight needed; the sub IS the body. Off.
No coupled shell resonator for a pure 808 sine.

params.physicalTarget: amt·kTensionEnergyScale(3.3e-5) energy-driven upward glide, capped ~+3 semitones, Membrane-only
params.rationale: Extreme energy-dependent glide: a hard hit briefly pitches the body up then relaxes as it decays — an exaggerated nonlinear 'kerthump' layered on top of the ToneShaper pitch env for the signature trap 808 movement.
Sub-bass must be mono/centered for phase coherence and translation on club/large systems.
Active kick pad.
