Left at 0.5 neutral; only audible when kit-level global coupling/snare-buzz/tom-resonance is on, which a sub kick doesn't use.
Kept neutral 0.5 (zero delta) so the explicit per-param voicing above is what ships; macros are performance overlays, not preset structure.
Body scale already set explicitly via Size 0.95; leave the macro neutral to avoid double-applying (a preset may push it to ~0.95 for a one-knob bigger feel, but the baseline keeps it neutral).
Attack already shaped via PitchEnv + Click; keep the macro neutral so its pitch-env-start/time deltas don't fight the explicit 250→35 Hz/30 ms glide.
Noise Mix is 0, so the entire noise layer is bypassed and its shaping params are no-ops.
Secondary shell disabled (Coupling 0, Secondary off) — no-ops.
Single-segment 250→35 Hz glide is the classic 808 shape; the middle breakpoint is left off (knee disabled), so Mid/MidFrac/Curve2 are inert.

physics:
FUNDAMENTAL/PITCH: A real TR-808 bass drum is a bridged-T bandpass filter kicked into self-oscillation by a ~1 ms trigger pulse, producing an essentially pure decaying SINE. Service manual lists ~56 Hz; measured units run as low as 48 Hz; one detailed analysis computes 49.4 Hz (G1+14¢). Trap producers tune the 808 sub anywhere in the 30–60 Hz fundamental window (below 30 Hz is rumble, HPF'd). Membrum mapping: Size 0.95 → natural body f0 = 500·0.1^0.95 = 56.1 Hz (right in the 808 range), but the active pitch envelope OVERRIDES this, settling the perceived pitch to the PitchEnv End (35 Hz here, tunable as the bassline root).

PARTIAL/MODE STRUCTURE & INHARMONICITY: The 808 output is a near-pure sine — minimal partials, low THD until the Tone control or external saturation adds harmonics. So inharmonicity is essentially N/A: the recipe drives Material very low (0.10) and b3 moderate (3e-4) so upper Bessel modes die fast and only the fundamental sustains; Mode Stretch stays neutral (no inharmonic spread). A 'distorted 808' for small-speaker translation gets odd harmonics from saturation — supplied here by a gentle ToneShaper Drive (0.18), not by the body spectrum.

DECAY/T60: The 808 decay control spans 50–800 ms (center ~300 ms); trap pushes 1–3 s tails so the 808 sustains as a tonal bassline note. Membrum sets the long tail via explicit b1 = 15.1 s⁻¹ flat damping → t60 ≈ ln(1000)/15.1 ≈ 0.46 s at the fundamental (Decay 0.65 also lengthens the legacy term); the sub fundamental at 35–56 Hz sees near-zero b3·f² damping so it is the longest-ringing component. Per-band: lows long (~0.46 s+), highs near-instant (b3 + low Material).

TRANSIENT/CLICK: The 808 itself has a 'complex transient which quickly but smoothly transitions into the sine' — a ~6 ms barely-noticeable attack pitch snap (49→130 Hz, ~+80 Hz). On modern trap 808s a deliberate, brighter click is often added for small-speaker attack definition. Membrum supplies this with the Click layer (mix 0.42, contact 2.5 ms, ~760 Hz center) half-fed into the body and half to output — a defined attack tick.

NOISE CONTENT: Essentially none — the bridged-T sine has no broadband hiss. Noise layer is fully bypassed (mix 0).

PITCH GLIDE: Two stacked glides. (1) The classic ToneShaper pitch env: 250→35 Hz over 30 ms with an exponential fast-initial-drop curve (curveAmount −0.7) — an exaggerated, audible version of the 808's attack pitch snap, used as a tonal 'boom'. (2) An EXTREME tension-modulation glide (0.85, Membrane-only): energy-dependent upward pitch jump (capped ~+3 semitones, Avanzini-Marogna-Bank 2012 tension∝modal-energy) that relaxes as the body decays — the nonlinear 'kerthump' that gives the hit its movement and weight.

MATERIAL: Synthetic/electronic (a filter circuit, not a physical drumhead). Modeled as a very-low-Material membrane so the body behaves as a clean low sine rather than a woody/metallic resonator.

archetype: Triangle (orchestral, bent steel rod / free-free bar)
bodyModel: Shell (free-free Euler-Bernoulli bar — NOT Bell)
exciter: Impulse

params.physicalTarget: Impulse (raised-cosine click) — a hard metal beater striking a stiff steel rod is a near-impulsive contact that dumps broadband energy into all bar modes at once
params.rationale: Impulse is the closest analog to a metal beater on metal: short, bright, exciting the full inharmonic mode set. Mallet (soft beater) would low-pass the strike and is wrong for a steel triangle.
params.citation: Britannica: struck with a steel beater, single bright stroke

params.physicalTarget: Shell = free-free Euler-Bernoulli bar, mode ratios 1.000:2.757:5.404:8.933:13.34:18.64...
params.rationale: A triangle is a bent steel ROD = free-free bar; the Shell body IS that model. DELIBERATE DEVIATION from the brief's 'Bell' suggestion: the post-audit Bell body is a TUNED church-bell Chladni series (hum 0.25/prime 0.5/tierce 0.6/quint 0.75/nominal 1.0), which is a near-pitched bell — the OPPOSITE of an indefinite-pitch rod. Measured triangle ratio 4239/1539≈2.75 matches the free-free 2nd mode 2.757, confirming Shell.
params.citation: Fletcher & Rossing PoMI §2.14 free-free bar; shell_modes.h kShellRatios; measured partials 1539/4239 Hz

params.physicalTarget: Shell brightness=0.85+0.15·material≈0.993 (very low b3·f² high-freq damping); baseDecayTime=lerp(0.15,0.8,0.95)·(1+0.1·size)≈0.77 s base
params.rationale: Steel = maximally metallic: high modes must ring nearly as long as low ones to give the shimmer. High material drives brightness up → minimal frequency-squared damping → long bright partials.
params.citation: Triangle high partials 5658/7795 Hz sustain (shimmer); low-damping steel — Wikipedia long sustaining sound

params.physicalTarget: f0 = 1500·0.1^0.085 ≈ 1234 Hz (lowest bar mode); 2nd mode ≈3400 Hz, modes then fill 6.7/11/16 kHz...
params.rationale: Brief asks very small (0.08–0.10). Places the lowest dominant mode right at the measured ~1.5 kHz region and pushes the dense partial cloud above 4 kHz where the triangle's energy lives. Small body = high f0 = the characteristic tiny-rod brightness.
params.citation: Measured dominant partials 1539/4239/5658/7795 Hz, energy >4 kHz

params.physicalTarget: decayTime = base·exp(lerp(ln0.3,ln3.0,0.85))·skewBias ≈ 0.77·2.43 ≈ 1.87 s (near the bank's 5 s clamp head-room), long metallic ring
params.rationale: Triangle rings for seconds until hand-damped (very low damping). 0.85 gives a long body RT60 without hitting the internal clamp.
params.citation: Wikipedia: long, sustaining sound preferred by Romantic composers; low damping coefficients (Grokipedia)

params.physicalTarget: x/L≈0.18 along the free bar via the free-free eigenfunction cosh+cos−σ(sinh+sin) — near a free-end antinode, so all modes get strong, even excitation
params.rationale: A triangle is struck near a free leg/end where the free-free shape is maximal, exciting the full bright mode set. Slightly off the exact end (0.0) gives an uneven, lively partial balance rather than a single mode emphasis.
params.citation: shell_modes.h free-free antinode-at-free-end shape (audit §3-B); triangles struck on a leg

params.physicalTarget: linear pre-limiter gain 0.72 on the voice output before the −1 dBTP bus limiter
params.rationale: A triangle is a bright accent that cuts through, but its energy is concentrated in narrow high partials; a slightly-below-default level keeps it present without dominating a kit (final loudness is master/Phase-4).
params.citation: Britannica: penetrates orchestral sound, most effective used sparingly

params.physicalTarget: physical stretch = 0.5+0.62·1.5 ≈ 1.43 → modal-bank B warp >1 (extra inharmonic dispersion sqrt(1+B·(k+1)²)) on top of the already-inharmonic bar ratios
params.rationale: Bending the straight bar into a triangle and adding torsional modes pushes partials further off the ideal free-free ratios — extra inharmonicity = the metallic, pitch-obscuring shimmer the brief calls for (modeStretch).
params.citation: Triangle inharmonicity from irregular bent shape (Wikipedia/Grokipedia); JASA 2025 shape-vs-sound

params.physicalTarget: phys = 0.40·2−1 = −0.20: per-mode tilt ratio^(+0.20) boosts HIGH modes' sustain; global skewBias=1−0.3·(−0.2)=1.06 slightly lengthens
params.rationale: Negative skew lifts and sustains the upper partials (5.6/7.8 kHz dominant tones), reproducing the triangle's signature high-frequency shimmer that outlasts the low modes.
params.citation: Dominant tones at 4239/5658/7795 Hz; high-partial sustain (shimmer)

params.physicalTarget: b3 = 0.02·1e-3 = 2e-5 s — near-zero frequency-squared damping → high modes decay almost as slowly as low ones (steel)
params.rationale: Explicitly pins the metallic 'highs ring forever' character instead of deriving from material alone. Tiny b3 = the defining shimmer; overrides the material-derived high-freq damping with a deliberately metallic value.
params.citation: Steel low-damping, long upper-partial tails (Grokipedia low damping coefficients)

params.physicalTarget: always-on contact transient at ~0.95·velocity·env·filteredNoise, half into the exciter feed (drives modes) + full to output (×0.445)
params.rationale: Brief: very bright click 0.95. The metal-beater-on-metal contact tick is a defining, very prominent part of the triangle attack; high mix makes the strike read as sharp and metallic.
params.citation: Metal beater high-pitched ringing strike (Britannica/Wikipedia); brief spec 0.95

params.physicalTarget: bandpass center = exp(ln200+0.97·(ln12000−ln200)) ≈ 10.6 kHz — a very sharp, high tick
params.rationale: Metal-on-metal contact is broadband but reads as an extremely bright high tick; near-max brightness gives the sharp metallic 'ting' attack.

params.physicalTarget: contactMs = 2 + 0.1·3 = 2.3 ms (velocity shortens further) — a very short hard contact
params.rationale: Hard metal contact is near-impulsive; shortest sensible contact time gives a crisp tick, not a thud.

params.physicalTarget: parallel noise layer fully bypassed (mix≤0 → zero output)
params.rationale: Brief: no noise. A triangle has no sustained hiss/sizzle — its sound is a clean inharmonic metal ring; the only 'noise' is the contact click (handled by the Click layer).
params.citation: clean ringing metal spectrum; no breath/sizzle component

params.physicalTarget: equal-power pan θ=0.6·π/2; gainL=√2·cos≈1.07·…, slightly right of center
params.rationale: Triangle is typically a single bright accent placed slightly off-center in the stereo image (percussion section placement). Modest, not extreme.
params.citation: audit M-9 per-pad pan; orchestral placement

params.rationale: No post-body tone shaping wanted; the bar spectrum is already the desired bright metallic tone.
A stiff steel rod has no pitch glide; the iconic 808 boom/kerthump is membrane physics, absent here.
params.citation: no tension-modulation glide on a metal bar

params.rationale: Membrane-only feature (gated to Membrane body) and physically absent for a rigid rod anyway.
params.citation: Avanzini et al. JASA 2012 tension-mod is membrane physics; param is Membrane-gated

params.rationale: A small steel triangle is essentially linear at normal strike levels; no audible louder-=-brighter nonlinear plate cascade. Kept off for a clean linear ring.
params.citation: linear steel rod at orchestral dynamics

params.rationale: Injecting a 1/k INTEGER-harmonic series would impose a pitch on an instrument whose defining quality is INDEFINITE pitch — directly wrong for a triangle.

params.physicalTarget: ~12% sinusoidal frequency dither on the modal frequencies
params.rationale: Real bent triangles vary mode placement run-to-run (irregular shape, suspension); a little scatter adds organic metallic 'liveness' to the otherwise exact ratio set without re-pitching it.
params.citation: irregular non-uniform shape → inharmonic spread (Grokipedia); JASA 2025 shape-vs-sound

params.physicalTarget: off → no coupled secondary shell bank
params.rationale: A triangle is a single resonator; there is no head↔shell two-body coupling to model.
params.citation: single bent rod, one resonating body

defaultedParams.why: No-op: only affects the FMImpulse exciter, and we use Impulse. (Brief's 'fmRatio 0.45-0.55' note is informational; it does nothing for the Impulse exciter.)
No-op for Impulse exciter (Feedback-only).
No-op for Impulse exciter (NoiseBurst-only).
No-op for Impulse exciter (Friction-only).
ToneShaper filter is bypassed (cutoff at 20 kHz, env amount 0.5=0) — a transparent metal ring needs no post-body filtering.
Bypassed at 0 — no saturation/wavefolding wanted on a clean steel spectrum.
Pitch env disabled (Time=0); a rigid rod has no glide, so all pitch-env breakpoints are inert.

defaultedParams.param: Mode Stretch left near-neutral? No — set. Morph Enabled/Start/End/Duration/Curve (25-29)
defaultedParams.why: Material Morph off; the triangle's timbre is static through the note (no time-varying material sweep).
No-op: Noise Mix=0 bypasses the entire parallel noise layer.
Left at sentinel (−1) so the Decay knob drives flat damping; Decay=0.85 already gives the long RT60, no need to override the floor.
Membrane-only frequency correction; no-op for the Shell body.

defaultedParams.param: Coupling Strength / Secondary Size / Secondary Material (54,56,57)
defaultedParams.why: No-op with Secondary Enabled=0; a triangle is a single resonator.

Left at default 0.5 — only audible if the kit-level global sympathetic-coupling is engaged; a triangle wouldn't drive a snare-buzz/tom network meaningfully, but the per-pad weight is harmless at default.
Kit-level routing — 0 (no choke / main bus) is correct unless a specific kit layout dictates otherwise.
Left at 0.5 neutral — the explicit param values above ARE the voicing; macros add zero delta at neutral and stay available as one-knob performance controls.
1.0 = enabled (default).

physics: A musical triangle is a single bent steel (sometimes brass/beryllium-copper) rod, open at one corner, suspended by a thin loop and struck with a metal beater. Acoustically it is a FREE-FREE BAR (both ends free), so the governing model is the transverse Euler-Bernoulli beam, whose natural frequencies are the roots of cos(βL)·cosh(βL)=1 giving the strongly INHARMONIC ratio series 1.000 : 2.757 : 5.404 : 8.933 : 13.34 : 18.64 ... (Fletcher & Rossing, Physics of Musical Instruments §2.14; Rossing & Fletcher, Principles of Vibration and Sound §2.19). Bending it into a triangle perturbs these slightly and adds torsional/longitudinal modes, but the spectrum stays dense and non-integer → INDEFINITE PITCH: "the fundamental pitch is obscured by its nonharmonic overtones" (Britannica). Measured spectrum of a high-quality triangle has four dominant tones at 1539, 4239, 5658, 7795 Hz (Grokipedia/musillection, citing the experimental dynamic-analysis literature) — note 4239/1539 ≈ 2.75 ≈ the free-free 2nd-mode ratio 2.757, directly confirming the bar model; energy is heavily concentrated ABOVE ~4 kHz, which is why the tone reads as a bright shimmer rather than a pitch. Decay/T60: very long, "long, sustaining sound" (Wikipedia), low damping → seconds of ring until hand-damped; high partials ring nearly as long as low ones (metal: very small frequency-squared damping b3·f²), which is the defining shimmer. Transient/click: a metal-on-metal contact is an extremely short (~2–4 ms), very BRIGHT broadband tick — the brief noisy chiff at strike that the literature calls the bright attack of the metal beater. Noise content: essentially NONE as a sustained layer — the body is a clean ringing metal spectrum; the only noise is the contact transient itself. Pitch glide: none ("kerthump" is a membrane/tom phenomenon; a stiff metal rod has no tension-modulation glide). Physical material: steel rod, so maximally metallic (long, bright, nearly-flat-damped highs).

archetype: Tubular Bell / Chimes (tuned, long-ring metallic tube)
bodyModel: String (Krate::DSP::WaveguideString — Jaffe-Smith digital waveguide, NOT a modal bank). Chosen because a long hanging metal tube struck at the top cap behaves as a single dominant sustained resonator with a long, even-decaying metallic tail; the waveguide's lossy loop filter + pick-comb gives exactly that "one pitched ring that sustains for seconds" character without the dense modal-bank attack. The true free-free-bar inharmonic partial ladder (1 : 2.76 : 5.40 : 8.93…) is approximated, not reproduced literally — the String body trades the literal P4/P5/P6 strike-tone triplet for a sustaining single-pitch tube ring, which is the perceptually dominant feature of orchestral chimes.
exciter: Mallet (ExciterType=1). A rawhide/plastic-headed mallet striking the top edge is a soft, short beater contact — the Mallet exciter's softer raised-cosine beater (vs the hard Impulse click) matches the real excitation and avoids over-exciting the highest waveguide harmonics. The crisp metal "tink" of the cap contact is supplied separately by the always-on Click layer rather than by a hard exciter.

params.rationale: Soft beater matches a rawhide/plastic chime mallet; avoids over-exciting top harmonics the way a hard Impulse would.
Waveguide gives a single sustained pitched metal-tube ring with a long even tail — the dominant perceptual feature of chimes.

params.physicalTarget: String brightness_=1-0.85=0.15 (near-flat loop filter, highs sustain → metallic bright tail); decay base=lerp(0.5,4.0,0.85)=3.48 s
params.rationale: High material keeps high harmonics alive for a bright, bell-like sustaining metal tube (bronze rings for seconds) and sets a long decay base.
A low-ish tube fundamental gives the deep, long tube ring; the perceived chime strike pitch sits an octave below P4 so a sub-300 Hz ring reads as a sonorous low chime.
Chimes ring for several seconds; near-max decay gives the long sustaining metal tail.
A near-end (top-cap) strike; 0.3 places the pick-comb notch to thin a few mid harmonics, mild brightening — kept at default (struck near top).
Slight trim below 0.8 default: the long-ringing high-Q waveguide builds energy, so back off to keep RMS musical under the bus limiter.

params.physicalTarget: 0.4 * vel * env * filtered-noise; half-amp into exciter + full into output
params.rationale: Adds the crisp metallic 'tink' of the mallet/cap contact that the soft Mallet exciter alone lacks.
Short, sharp metallic contact transient (hard tube cap, not a soft drum head).
Bright contact tick consistent with a metal-on-metal/plastic strike, not a dull thud.
Chimes are a 'purer tone than solid chimes' — only a light breath/contact noise, no sustained hiss.
Default mid cutoff; the noise layer is nearly inaudible at mix 0.10 so left neutral.
Short contact-noise burst only; the sustain is carried entirely by the metal ring, not noise.

params.physicalTarget: physical 1.25 — but NO-OP for String (waveguide ignores stretch)
params.rationale: Carried for kit consistency; the waveguide body ignores it, so it has no audible effect (a true bell would want stretch, but String is not modal).

defaultedParams.why: ToneShaper SVF left transparent (cutoff norm 1.0 = bypass); the waveguide loop filter already sets the tube's tonal balance.
No filter sweep needed; a chime's spectral evolution is the natural HF-faster modal decay of the ring, handled by the waveguide loss filter.
A struck chime is clean/linear; no saturation or wavefolding — both bypassed at 0.
Pitch is static after the strike — chimes have no 808-style glide/kerthump; PitchEnv Time=0 disables the whole feature.
Exact bypass at 0; the waveguide already supplies the harmonic series, no synthetic 1/k overlay wanted.
Chime ring is essentially linear at musical levels; no amplitude-driven brightening needed (exact bypass at 0).
No intra-note material sweep; the tube's timbre is fixed by its geometry.
Chimes ring freely (a real sustain pedal undamps them); no mute-group, leave 0.
Main bus; no multi-out routing implied for a single chime voice.
No-op — Mallet exciter selected, not FMImpulse (kept at default 0.5).
No-op — not the Feedback exciter.
No-op — not the NoiseBurst exciter.
No-op — not the Friction exciter.
Left at neutral 0.5; only audible if kit-level global coupling/snare-buzz/tom-resonance is engaged.
All neutral 0.5 = zero delta; the recipe sets the underlying params directly so macros stay as live performance offsets.
Default 0.2 (Q≈1.2); noise layer is near-silent at mix 0.10 so its filter shape is irrelevant.
Default 0.5 (Pink/White boundary); inaudible at mix 0.10.
NO-OP for String (modal-only override); decay is set by Material+Decay on the waveguide. (Existing preset writes 0.30 but it does nothing on String.)
NO-OP for String; HF damping is the waveguide loop filter (driven by Material), not b3.
NO-OP for String — air-loading frequency correction is Membrane-only; set 0.0 for clarity.
NO-OP for String (modal detune dither); waveguide has no modal bank to scatter.
NO-OP for String (per-mode tilt is modal-only); the existing preset's 0.55 has no audible effect on the waveguide. Left neutral 0.5.
Head↔shell secondary resonator off; a tube is one resonator, no separate shell to couple into.
NO-OP — Membrane-only, and chimes have no tension-glide anyway.
On (default 1.0) — this is a sounding chime pad.
Center 0.5 (bit-identical to legacy mono dup) unless placed in a stereo kit layout.

physics: PITCH/RANGE: Orchestral chimes sound C4-F5 (~262-698 Hz), extended Bb3-G5; tubes 30-38 mm dia., tuned by length (Wikipedia Tubular bells). The PERCEIVED pitch is a VIRTUAL/strike tone, not a physical fundamental: the lowest physical partials (P1/P2) are very weak (Hibberts: 55 Hz & 158 Hz partials "barely above noise"). PARTIAL/MODE STRUCTURE: a tubular bell is a free-free transverse-vibrating bar/tube; ideal free-free bar modes ~ (2n+1)^2 giving inharmonic ratios ~1 : 2.76 : 5.40 : 8.93 : 13.3. Real measured tubes (Hibberts, 233-tube study) follow a MODIFIED Chladni law f_n ∝ (2n+c)^p with p≈1.651, c≈0.287, departing from ideal (2n+1)^2. STRIKE TONE: modes 4,5,6(,7) form a near-harmonic series 2:3:4(:5) (measured ratios to strike pitch ≈ P4 1.99, P5 2.86, P6 3.81, P7 4.86 — close to 2:3:4:5); the ear synthesizes a virtual pitch ONE OCTAVE BELOW P4 (the "missing 1"). So a chime is strongly INHARMONIC yet yields a clear musical pitch. INHARMONICITY: substantial — partials are decidedly non-integer; this is the 'twangy/clangy' chime signature. MATERIAL: drawn brass/bronze or steel tube (Hibberts studied bronze + iron tubes); bronze sustains the strike tone for several SECONDS. DECAY (T60): long — the metal tube rings for seconds; the slowest/lowest modes decay last; HF modes decay faster so the strike tone softens into the lower hum over time. No measured single T60 in sources, but qualitatively multi-second (Wikipedia: a sustain pedal damps/undamps all bells). TRANSIENT/CLICK: short metallic contact 'tink' at the top-cap strike point with a rawhide/plastic mallet — broadband attack a few ms long, brighter than a drum thwack. NOISE: minimal — a 'purer tone than solid chimes' (Wikipedia); only a light contact-noise component, no sustained hiss. PITCH GLIDE: none significant for chimes (unlike toms/808 — no kerthump); pitch is static after the strike. SYNTHESIS PRECEDENT: the canonical electronic tubular bell is Chowning FM (DX7 'TUB BELLS'), carrier:modulator ~1:3.5 with long decay/release envelopes (Roland FM guide; Chowning 1973) — an inharmonic-spectrum + long-tail recipe. Membrum's String/waveguide route is an alternative physical-model path that captures the long pitched ring and metallic-bright sustaining tail.

bodyModel: NoiseBody
exciter: NoiseBurst

params.rationale: A vibraslap is excited by a broadband metallic-rivet rattle, not a single struck-mode impulse; NoiseBurst's violet-noise burst is the correct excitation spectrum for loose-rivet chatter.

params.physicalTarget: idx=clamp(round(1.0*5),0,5)=5 -> NoiseBody (plate-ratio 32-mode box + internal noise layer)
params.rationale: The wooden resonator box + metallic rivet content is exactly NoiseBody: a metallic plate-ratio modal box PLUS an internal noise source, so the body itself carries both the woody resonance and the rattle.

params.physicalTarget: NoiseBody brightness=0.7+0.3*0.85=0.955 (bright/metallic); base decay lerp(0.2,1.0,0.85)=0.83; internal-noise cutoff clamp(1500+5000*0.85)=5750 Hz; internal-noise reso 0.6+0.4*0.85=0.94
params.rationale: Pushes the box toward the bright metallic-rivet end while keeping a woody base decay; the rivets are steel, so brightness must be high.

params.physicalTarget: f0 = 1500*0.1^0.22 ≈ 542 Hz box fundamental; plate-ratio partials spread upward; small wooden box
params.rationale: A vibraslap box is small; ~540 Hz fundamental places the woody body resonance in the low-mid, not a deep drum and not a tiny tinkle.

params.physicalTarget: decayTime = 0.83*(1+0.1*0.22)*exp(lerp(ln0.3,ln3.0,0.32)) ≈ 0.83*1.022*0.66 ≈ 0.56 s box ring; also scales internal-noise env
params.rationale: MEDIUM decay — woodier and longer than a shaker/cabasa (~0.1–0.2 s) but well short of a cymbal; matches the 'rings a few seconds, quickly damped' description trimmed to a musical kit tail.

params.physicalTarget: NoiseBody per-mode amplitude via computePlateAmplitude(k,0.3), floored 0.03 — off-center box excitation
params.rationale: Default off-center keeps a balanced spread of box modes; strike position is weakly meaningful for a noise-dominated rattle, so left near default.
Perc accent sitting slightly under the kit drums; matches the existing percussion pads' make-up so it blends rather than dominating.

params.physicalTarget: ms = 2 + 0.55*13 = 9.15 ms violet-noise burst (velocity then scales 1.2..0.8x)
params.rationale: A LONG burst (per the archetype brief) so the exciter delivers a sustained rivet-rattle chatter into the box rather than a single click — this is what reads as 'rattle' not 'hit'.

params.physicalTarget: parallel noise layer mix 0.85 (*0.243 standalone gain) — dominant layer
params.rationale: The vibraslap is ~80% rattle noise; the parallel layer must dominate so the rivet chatter, not the box tone, is the headline.
Bright enough for the metallic-rivet edge but rolled below full-white hiss so the wooden box keeps it from sounding like a shaker; matches a bright-but-woody rattle.

params.physicalTarget: SVF q = 0.3 + 0.3*4.7 = 1.71 — a few-dB resonant band ('formant')
params.rationale: The defining 'noiseResonance formant' — a modest resonant peak emulates the wooden box band-formant coloring the rivet noise without ringing into a pitched beep.

params.physicalTarget: ms = exp(ln20 + 0.32*(ln2000-ln20)) ≈ 110 ms env decay (release 55 ms)
params.rationale: Gives the rattle layer a woody medium tail that decelerates with the body, matched to the body's ~0.56 s ring scaled by the same Decay knob; longer than a shaker's snap.

White is the flat broadband source for the metallic rattle; the resonant band + lowpass then woody-shape it. (Just below the Violet threshold to keep the rivet edge bright without harsh top-octave hiss.)

params.physicalTarget: click layer mix 0.30 (*0.445), half into exciter + full to output
params.rationale: A modest wooden slap onset (ball-on-palm + first rivet contact) for attack definition; kept low so it is a thwack, not a stick tick.
Short soft contact — a wooden ball slap is brief but not razor-sharp.
Mid-bright woody slap (not a high glassy tick), consistent with a wooden ball/box onset.

params.physicalTarget: MapperResult.scatter=0.62 -> ~9% sinusoidal frequency dither on the plate-ratio box modes
params.rationale: High scatter (per the archetype brief) detunes the tidy plate ratios into an organic, inharmonic metallic rattle — the loose rivets never ring a clean ratio ladder.

params.physicalTarget: macro delta from 0.5: couplingAmount +=0.28, nonlinearCoupling +=0.084, modeInjectAmount +=0.084 (incremental, clamped)
params.rationale: High macroComplexity (per the brief) thickens the rattle with mild nonlinear brightening + a touch of injected body weight + sympathetic spread, mimicking the many-rivet density.

params.physicalTarget: b1 = 0.2 + 0.32*49.8 ≈ 16.1 s^-1 flat damping (woody decay floor ~0.06 s flat term, with f^2 term off)
params.rationale: Wooden box is lossy; a moderate flat damping ends the box ring in a few hundred ms (overrides the Decay-derived flat term) for a woody, not metallic-sustained, body.

params.physicalTarget: b3 = 0 -> NO frequency-squared (f^2) high-mode damping; metallic, long highs
params.rationale: The rivets are steel — the high modes must NOT be wood-damped, so b3=0 keeps the box's upper resonances bright and ringing (the metal in the wood-and-metal instrument).

params.physicalTarget: no membrane air-loading frequency correction (NoiseBody ignores it anyway)
params.rationale: Air loading is Membrane-only; explicitly 0 for a box body so no spurious membrane frequency curve is implied.

params.physicalTarget: equal-power center (gainL=gainR=1.0, bit-identical to mono dup)
params.rationale: Perc accents typically sit center in a kit; left center unless the kit context wants stereo placement.
Active pad.

defaultedParams.why: ToneShaper SVF left bypassed (cutoff 1.0=20kHz, env amount 0.5=0): the woody band-shaping is done in the noise layer's resonant formant, not the post-body filter — no second filter needed.
Bypassed at 0 — a rivet rattle needs no added saturation/wavefolding harmonics; macroComplexity already supplies mild nonlinear brightening.
Pitch env OFF (Time=0): a vibraslap has no pitch glide — its only time-variation is rattle deceleration (an amplitude/density decay), handled by the decay/noise envelopes.
Left neutral (0.333=unity): the vibraslap's inharmonicity is loose-rivet scatter, not stiff-bar dispersion, so scatter (53) carries it rather than stretch.
Left neutral (0.5=0): no deliberate high/low decay tilt is characteristic; the woody b1 + metallic b3=0 already set the spectral-decay balance.
Left at default 0 base; the macroComplexity delta (+0.084) adds the only desired injected weight — a directly-set tonal harmonic series would impose an un-vibraslap-like pitch.
Left at default 0 base; macroComplexity supplies the small amplitude-brightening delta — a vibraslap needs only a hint, not a strong louder-equals-brighter sweep.
Material morph OFF: the timbre does not sweep within a hit; the rattle's evolution is its decay, not a material morph.
No choke (0) and main bus (0): a vibraslap is a free-ringing accent, not part of an open/closed mute pair, and routes to the main mix by default.

defaultedParams.param: FM Ratio (32) / Feedback Amount (33) / Friction Pressure (35)
defaultedParams.why: No-ops for the NoiseBurst exciter — only meaningful for FMImpulse / Feedback / Friction respectively.
Left neutral (0.5): the explicit per-param values already place material/decay/brightness/size; only Complexity is pushed because rattle density is the one macro-level character that fits.
Per-pad head<->shell secondary resonator OFF: the single wooden box is fully captured by the primary NoiseBody; no separate shell layer is physical here.
0 — energy-dependent pitch glide is Membrane-only and a vibraslap has no tension glide anyway.

physics:
ACOUSTIC PROFILE OF A VIBRASLAP (modernized quijada/jawbone). Construction: a U-shaped stiff steel wire connects a wooden ball to a hollow wooden resonator box; inside the box a small metal frame carries several LOOSELY-fastened steel rivets/pins that rattle freely against the box walls (Wikipedia; BYU Percussion; organology). The performer strikes the ball against the palm; the impulse travels the wire into the box and shakes the loose rivets.

PITCH / FUNDAMENTAL: NO definite pitch. The vibraslap is an INHARMONIC, noise-dominated rattle — there is no tonal fundamental to tune. Its perceived "tone" is a broadband band-formant set by (a) the wooden box's body resonances and (b) the collective metallic-rivet impact spectrum. So in Membrum there is no meaningful Size/f0 pitch target to hit precisely; Size only sets the woody box-resonance band center. I place the NoiseBody box modes in the low-mid woody region (size 0.22 -> f0 = 1500*0.1^0.22 ≈ 540 Hz body fundamental, with plate-ratio partials spreading up to a few kHz) so the modal layer reads as a small wooden box, not a tuned plate.

PARTIAL / MODE STRUCTURE & INHARMONICITY: The rivet rattle is a dense cloud of many discrete metallic micro-impacts (the famous "decelerating ratchet" — HISSandaROAR describes a 'unique decelerating ratchet rhythm'), i.e. broadband and strongly INHARMONIC with no clear partial ladder. The wooden box adds a few broad, lossy, woody resonances on top. This maps to: high modeScatter (frequency dither so the box's plate-ratio modes lose their tidy ratios and read as an organic, slightly-detuned metallic rattle) + macroComplexity high (adds nonlinear brightening + a little injected weight + coupling spread) to thicken the rattle. Mode stretch left neutral — the inharmonicity here is rattle/scatter-driven, not stiff-bar dispersion.

NOISE CONTENT & SPECTRAL SHAPE: This is the DEFINING feature. The sound is ~80% filtered metallic noise (the rivet rattle), only loosely band-shaped by the wooden box. Spectrally it is bright/metallic but NOT hissy-white like a shaker — it carries a woody band-formant. Modeled by the always-on parallel noise layer at high mix, with a resonant band ("noiseResonance formant 0.30") and a slightly-bright color (a touch above white, leaning violet for the metallic rivet edge), lowpassed in the upper-mid so the rattle is bright but the wooden box keeps it from being a pure hiss. The NoiseBurst exciter itself is a violet-noise bandpass burst (matches the metallic-rivet impact spectrum) and drives the modal box.

TRANSIENT / CONTACT CLICK: A modest wooden slap onset (ball-on-palm + first rivet contact). It is NOT a sharp tick like a claves/triangle — it is a soft woody thwack followed immediately by the rattle. A small click layer at medium contact gives attack definition without turning it into a stick click.

DECAY / T60: Sources agree the vibraslap rings noticeably — "a rattle, usually lasting a few seconds" / "quickly damped, lasting only about two to three seconds or less" (afterglow; Wikipedia). This is MEDIUM decay and crucially LONGER and WOODIER than a shaker/cabasa/maracas, which die in ~100–250 ms. The energy decays as the rattle DECELERATES (rattle density thins out over the ring). I target a body ring and noise-layer envelope around 350–500 ms of audible rattle tail (not the full hand-held 2–3 s, which would be unmusical in a kit), longer than the existing Maracas (decay 0.18) / Cabasa (0.10) pads. Realized: NoiseBody decayTime = lerp(0.2,1.0,material)*(1+0.1*size)*exp(lerp(ln0.3,ln3.0,decay)) — with material 0.85, size 0.22, decay 0.32 this lands ~0.55 s box ring; noise envelope 60ms*exp(...) ≈ 130 ms plus the layer's own Noise Decay (0.32 -> ~110 ms) — together a woody, medium, decelerating tail.

PITCH GLIDE: NONE. Unlike an 808 tom there is no "kerthump" tension glide; the only time-variation is the rattle deceleration, which is an amplitude/density decay, not a pitch sweep. PitchEnv stays OFF (Time=0) and tensionModAmt=0 (NoiseBody is not Membrane anyway, so tension would be a no-op).

MATERIAL: dual — a WOODEN box (lossy, mid-band) holding METALLIC rivets (bright, ringy). Net perceived material is bright-metallic rattle with a wood-damped envelope. material 0.85 pushes the NoiseBody toward the brighter/metallic end (brightness=0.7+0.3*0.85≈0.96, noise cutoff bias up to ~5.7 kHz) while bodyDampingB3=0 keeps the metallic highs from being wood-damped, and a moderate bodyDampingB1 gives the woody flat-decay floor that ends the ring in a few hundred ms.

archetype: Vintage Wood-Shell Kick (acoustic bass drum, warm woody shell + light vintage wavefold saturation)
bodyModel: Membrane (Bessel circular drumhead, 48 modes) — a real bass drum is a struck membrane whose pitch and decay come from the lowest membrane modes, air-loaded by the enclosed cavity. The wood shell is modeled as a parallel secondary (shell) resonator coupled feedforward from the head (Coupling Strength + Secondary Enabled), giving the "woody" color without changing the body archetype.
exciter: Mallet (softer beater) — a felt/wood bass-drum beater is a soft, broad contact, not a sharp click; Mallet drives the low membrane modes with little HF energy. The crisp "beater click" is added separately by the always-on Click layer tuned dark (woody thud, not a sharp tick).

params.rationale: Felt/wood bass-drum beater is a soft broad contact driving low modes, not a sharp click.

params.physicalTarget: idx=0 -> Membrane (Bessel drumhead, 48 modes), base f0=500*0.1^size

params.physicalTarget: b3=0.1*1e-3=1e-4 s -> moderate f^2 HF damping (wood, highs roll off fast)
params.rationale: Wood thump = strong high-mode damping; highs decay much faster than the fundamental.

params.physicalTarget: 0.70 along Bessel->Rossing air-loaded curve; lowest modes depressed toward the deep timpani-like series

params.physicalTarget: feedforward drive 0.55: head excites the secondary shell bank at 0.55*bodyOut
params.rationale: The wood shell audibly colors the head; moderate head->shell drive adds woody body weight.

defaultedParams.param: PitchEnv Knee / Mid / Mid Frac / Curve2 (offsets 60,61,62,63)
defaultedParams.why: Single-segment 150->55 Hz glide is sufficient; the knee/second segment would over-complicate a simple kick thump.
Adding a synthetic 1/k harmonic series would brighten/pitch the body unnaturally; a kick wants its real inharmonic membrane spectrum, so left at 0 (exact bypass).
Velocity-driven dynamic brightening is a cymbal/plate behaviour; the fold+drive already supply the harmonic warmth, so left at 0 (exact bypass).
Pure Bessel ratios give a clean, defined pitch; detune dither would smear the fundamental of a tuned kick. Left at 0.
No intra-note timbre sweep wanted; the pitch env carries the only needed motion. Morph off.

defaultedParams.param: Filter Env defaults aside, FM Ratio / Feedback / NoiseBurst Dur / Friction Pressure (offsets 32–35)
defaultedParams.why: No-ops for the Mallet exciter; left at struct defaults.
Cross-pad sympathetic engine is kit-level and off by default; per-pad participation left neutral 0.5 (inaudible unless the global coupling knobs are raised).
A kick is not part of a mute group and routes to the main bus; both 0.

physics:
FUNDAMENTAL/PITCH: An acoustic bass drum's principal pitch is the lowest (0,1) membrane mode. Reported fundamentals are ~40–80 Hz (mix refs put the kick fundamental at 40–80 Hz; a tuned 22" measured F0=67 Hz with first overtone F1=94.5 Hz, i.e. an RTF/overtone ratio ~1.4–1.6) [idrumtune; modeaudio]. Vintage/larger wood kicks sit at the low end of this. Recipe target: a 150 Hz->55 Hz downward pitch glide settling at ~55 Hz fundamental; natural body f0 at size 0.78 is 500*0.1^0.78 = 83 Hz, so the pitchEnv (time>0) overrides it and lands the steady pitch at 55 Hz.
PARTIAL/MODE STRUCTURE & INHARMONICITY: Ideal circular-membrane modes are strongly inharmonic (J_m Bessel zeros: 1 : 1.59 : 2.14 : 2.30 : 2.65 ...). On a real two-headed bass drum the enclosed-air coupling splits each mode into parallel/antiparallel pairs and DEPRESSES the lowest modes so the low partials cluster nearer the fundamental, giving the deep "felt-pitch" thump rather than a whistly membrane [circularscience; Fletcher & Rossing, Physics of Musical Instruments, coupled systems & air loading; Rossing JASA 85:S33 1989]. Measured (1,1)=1.593*f0, (2,1)=2.135*f0 for a coupled two-head drum [circularscience]. Membrum models this with airLoading (membrane-only Bessel->Rossing air-loaded frequency morph) set high (0.70). Wood shell adds a darker secondary mode set, not bright metallic ring.
DECAY (T60): Bass drums are deliberately damped (pillow/port) to a short, punchy decay; the body ring is on the order of a few hundred ms, low modes longest, highs gone fastest (b3*f^2 HF damping = woody, not metallic). Recipe body flat-damping b1=17.1 s^-1 -> T60≈6.9/17.1≈0.40 s, with strong f^2 HF roll-off (b3=1e-4) for a wood "thump" that loses its highs quickly.
ATTACK TRANSIENT / CONTACT CLICK: The beater contact is the first thing heard; its click energy sits ~2–5 kHz (up to ~8–10 kHz) with felt beaters giving a WARM attack and plastic a sharp click [musicguymixing; homestudioguys]. A vintage felt-beater wood kick wants a DARK, low click — recipe click brightness tuned low (~600 Hz bandpass) and a short 2.4 ms contact.
PITCH GLIDE ("thump/chirp"): Real drums show a transient downward pitch settle: the high-amplitude initial strike raises head tension, momentarily lifting pitch, which then relaxes as amplitude decays [circularscience]. This is exactly the iconic kick "boom->settle". Realized two ways here: (a) the ToneShaper pitch envelope 150->55 Hz over 22.5 ms (the main audible drop), and (b) tensionModAmt=0.18 (Membrane-only energy-driven upward tension rise that relaxes as the body decays) for the physically-correct micro-glide on top.
NOISE CONTENT: An acoustic kick is mostly tonal body + transient; broadband noise is low (beater scuff/air). Recipe noise layer kept low (mix 0.10), dark, short.
MATERIAL / VINTAGE SATURATION: Wood shells give a warm, low-mid-focused, controlled-sustain color [musicskanner; janzenbrothers]. "Vintage" warmth = added harmonics via mild waveshaping/tape-style saturation; a wavefolder adds harmonic layers and richer overtones [perfectcircuit; attackmagazine]. The brief specifies light wavefolding (fold 0.10 -> 0.314 rad) plus low secondary-shell material (0.28–0.32) for the darker woody shell-mode resonance that separates this from brighter maple/rock kicks.

archetype: West Coast Feedback Kick (synthesized Buchla-style kick)
bodyModel: Membrane (Bessel circular drumhead, 48 modes) — chosen so the swept fundamental sits in a pitched, slightly-inharmonic membrane (vs. a clean sine) and so tensionModAmt is legal (Membrane-only gate)
exciter: Feedback (energy-limited self-oscillating body↔exciter loop) — the West-Coast "complex" engine that injects sustained, chaotic energy into the body to grow inharmonic overtones on top of the pitch-swept fundamental

params.rationale: The defining West-Coast engine: self-oscillating body↔exciter loop injects sustained chaotic energy to grow inharmonic overtones on the swept fundamental.
params.citation: PerfectCircuit 'What is West Coast Synthesis' (complex/feedback timbres); param dictionary exciter enum

params.physicalTarget: Membrane (enum idx 0; round(0.0*5)=0), Bessel drumhead f0 base 500 Hz
params.rationale: A pitched, mildly-inharmonic membrane carries the swept sub better than a sine; also the ONLY body where tensionModAmt is active (kerthump).
params.citation: param dictionary body enum + tensionMod meaningfulFor=Membrane

params.rationale: Dark body so the swept sub stays round and the fold/feedback/secondary supply the brightness instead of a harsh head.

params.physicalTarget: f0 = 500*0.1^0.85 ≈ 70.8 Hz natural fundamental (large/low body); +0.085 to decay base

params.citation: Sweetwater/MusicRadar 808 kick 40–50 Hz sweet spot; size formula

params.rationale: Medium-short kick body; tight thump, not a boomy tom. Feeds the legacy law / cache key; the audible flat damping is set by b1 below.

params.physicalTarget: r/a = 0.30*0.9 = 0.27 (off-center, between center and edge) → |J_m(j_mn*0.27)| mode weighting
params.rationale: Slightly off-center strike weights low modes for body weight while keeping some higher-mode energy for the attack.
Standard per-pad loudness; the fold/feedback/coupling sit under the -6 dBFS body budget + bus limiter.
Saturation flavour is already supplied by the wavefolder + feedback exciter; stacking Drive would muddy the sub. Drive=flavour not level (M-2).

params.rationale: The heavy wavefold is the West-Coast signature: folds the body back on itself adding dense odd-harmonic brightness on the kick.

params.rationale: Buchla-hot start well above the body's natural ~71 Hz for an aggressive synthetic 'kerthump' attack.

physics: FUNDAMENTAL/PITCH: synthesized kick = one pitched body swept downward; 40–50 Hz steady sweet spot (range 30–70 Hz). Here natural body f0 ≈ 71 Hz (Size 0.85), pitch-env driving 220 Hz → 45 Hz over 30 ms — a Buchla-hot start (above the typical 808's ~80–200 Hz) for an aggressive attack. PARTIALS/INHARMONICITY: not acoustic — Membrane Bessel ratios (1:1.59:2.14:2.30...) provide a mildly inharmonic pitched body, then West-Coast nonlinearities dominate: (a) wavefolder folds the body back on itself (0.94 rad), a strongly nonlinear transfer adding dense mostly-odd harmonics, 5th-harmonic-prominent spectrum; (b) Feedback exciter self-oscillates the body into a chaotic energy cascade seeding non-harmonic partials/squeal; (c) Mode Stretch 1.175 mildly stretches partials; (d) a metallic secondary shell (f0≈0.66·head, very bright) adds a high inharmonic overtone cloud. DECAY/T60: tight kick body — flat damping b1≈15 s⁻¹ (~0.066 s flat term) for a thump not a ring; b3=3e-4 tames the top modes; HF fold/feedback energy decays first, swept sub settles last. TRANSIENT/CLICK: ~2.5 ms mid-bright (≈2.2 kHz) contact click defines the attack and drives body modes, but the pitch-glide front edge supplies most of the perceived snap; 20–70 ms pitch decay is the 808 'click' mechanism. NOISE: low (~0.2) lowpass (~849 Hz) pink grit bed, ~92 ms — texture not snare hiss. PITCH GLIDE: in addition to the env sweep, Membrane tension modulation (0.45) adds an energy-driven up-to-~+2-semitone attack bump that relaxes as the body decays (Avanzini-Marogna-Bank 2012) — the synthetic 'kerthump'. LOUDNESS↔BRIGHTNESS: NonlinearCoupling 0.40 gives the Buchla low-pass-gate behavior (louder = brighter), sustained through the ring.

bodyModel: Plate (BodyModelType=1) — free-plate Chladni modal bank. Chosen over Shell because the woodblock is NOT a free bar: its one/two longitudinal slit cavities lower the perceived body and emphasise a couple of strong low modes over a denser inharmonic upper field, which the post-audit Plate (m+2n)^P spectrum + modeStretch reproduces better than the strict 1:2.76:5.40 free-free bar ladder that the Shell body hard-codes. The task spec also fixes Plate for this archetype.
exciter: Impulse (ExciterType=0) — raised-cosine click. A woodblock is struck with a hard wooden stick: a near-instantaneous hard contact, no beater softness, no friction, no FM tone. Impulse is the correct minimal transient that lets the bright Click layer + the body's own low modes define the "tok".

params.rationale: Hard wooden stick = near-instant hard contact; no beater/friction/FM.

params.physicalTarget: Plate body (enum 1 on wire; stored as BodyModelType=Plate)
params.rationale: Free-plate Chladni bank reproduces the inharmonic, low-mode-dominant slit-cavity 'tok' better than the strict free-bar Shell ladder; spec-fixed for this archetype. (Selector is body-relative; Plate is the intended body.)

params.physicalTarget: Plate brightness = 0.5+0.5*0.32 = 0.66; baseDecay = lerp(0.4,2.0,0.32)*(1+0.1*size) ≈ 0.91 s before the Decay multiplier
params.rationale: Hardwood that still rings bright: mid-low material keeps a woody tilt without over-damping the bright partials; decay is then dominated by the short b1 override anyway.

params.physicalTarget: f0 = 800*0.1^0.18 ≈ 529 Hz (hi block); lo variant uses 0.28 → ~420 Hz
params.rationale: Small bright block: f0 in the lower-mid register, with the inharmonic upper partials + bright click carrying the perceived high 'tok'. Hi/lo handled by Size.

params.physicalTarget: decay multiplier exp(lerp(ln0.3,ln3.0,0.18)) ≈ 0.45x on baseDecay (≈0.41 s body base)
params.rationale: Short, dry ring. Note: with b1 override set, the FLAT-damping floor is governed by b1 (offset 50); Decay still seeds the legacy law fallback / cache key, so keep it short and consistent.

params.physicalTarget: computePlateAmplitude radius rho=0.55, azimuth theta=0.47 rad — off-centre on the block face
params.rationale: Real woodblocks are struck off-centre on the solid face near the slit; this excites the strong low modes plus inharmonic upper modes for the bright crack.
Woodblocks are loud, cutting perc but a single tight transient; leave headroom under the bus limiter. Per-pad loudness balance.

params.physicalTarget: 20 kHz → ToneShaper filter BYPASSED (cutoff>=20k AND env amount 0)
params.rationale: Body+click already carry the timbre; no post-body filtering wanted — keep the transient fully bright.
No envelope sweep needed on a one-shot dry tok.
Clean wooden crack; no saturation flavour. (Post-audit Drive is timbre not level, but the woodblock wants none.)
West-coast harmonics are alien to an acoustic woodblock.
Woodblocks have no audible pitch glide / kerthump; master-enable stays off.

params.physicalTarget: physical 1.25 → bank stretchNorm 0.50: warps plate partials sharp toward the inharmonic free-bar spread
params.rationale: Untuned struck wood is 'naturally discordant' (free-bar 1:2.76:5.40); stretch pushes the plate spectrum to that inharmonic woodblock fingerprint. Spec target ~0.50-0.55.

params.physicalTarget: 0 = neutral (no per-mode tilt, no global skew bias)
params.rationale: Even decay across modes; the short b1 floor already gives the fast dry release. (Some hi blocks can nudge to ~0.6 to brighten the tail — left neutral as the baseline.)

params.physicalTarget: exact zero-leak bypass (no synthetic harmonic series)
params.rationale: A woodblock is inharmonic; a synthetic 1/k integer-harmonic series would fight the free-bar spectrum.
No audible velocity-dependent nonlinear brightening in a small dry wood block.
Static single-hit timbre; no intra-note material sweep.
Woodblocks aren't open/closed pairs; no mute-group routing.
Standard perc routing.

params.physicalTarget: parallel noise layer fully bypassed (mix<=0 → zero output)
params.rationale: Defining trait: 'no rattle, buzz, or loose after-sound' — woodblock has essentially no sustained noise content.

params.physicalTarget: prominent attack click; ClickLayer feeds half-amp into the exciter and full into output (kStandaloneOutputGain 0.445)
params.rationale: The bright wooden crack/clack is a defining feature of the woodblock attack; a strong sharp click is wanted (spec 0.70-0.85).
Very short, sharp contact for a tight stick crack — no smeared thud.
Hard wood stick = 'sharpest attack, bright projection'; bright click centre gives the cutting tick.

params.physicalTarget: flat damping ≈ 25.1 s^-1 → ~40 ms flat-decay floor; OVERRIDES the Decay-derived flat damping
params.rationale: Short, dry, fast decay ('more tok than ring'). The explicit b1 override sets the perceived RT60 floor directly per the audit semantics.

params.physicalTarget: b3 = 1.0e-4 s → mild f^2 high-frequency damping (overrides material-derived b3)
params.rationale: Wood, so the highs decay a little faster than the lows, but kept LIGHT so the block still rings bright (a heavy woody b3 would dull the tok into a thud).
Membrane-only param; physically meaningless for a Plate body — set to 0.

params.physicalTarget: ~3% sinusoidal frequency dither on modal frequencies
params.rationale: Real wood has small natural imperfections; a touch of scatter avoids a too-perfect synthetic ratio set and adds organic 'grainier' character.
A solid wood block has no separate head/shell to couple; no secondary resonator.
Single solid body; no parallel shell.
No energy-dependent pitch glide; also a no-op on Plate bodies.
Active pad.

params.physicalTarget: equal-power centre (gainL=gainR=1.0, bit-identical to legacy mono dup)
params.rationale: Centre by default; place hi/lo blocks slightly off-centre at kit-assembly time if a stereo perc spread is wanted.

defaultedParams.why: Filter is bypassed (cutoff 1.0, env 0), so the LP/HP/BP selector is a no-op.
Only audible with the filter engaged; filter is bypassed.
Pitch env disabled (Time=0); start frequency is inert.
Pitch env disabled; end frequency is inert.
Pitch env disabled; segment-1 curve is inert.

defaultedParams.param: PitchEnv Knee / Mid / Mid Frac / Curve2
defaultedParams.why: Pitch env disabled and knee off; the three-point extension is fully inert.
Filter Env Amount is 0, so the filter ADSR never modulates anything.
Morph Enabled is off; the material-morph envelope never runs.
Impulse exciter selected; FM Ratio only affects the FMImpulse exciter.
Impulse exciter selected; Feedback Amount only affects the Feedback exciter.
Impulse exciter selected; NoiseBurst Duration only affects the NoiseBurst exciter.
Impulse exciter selected; Friction Pressure only affects the Friction exciter.
Noise Mix is 0, so the parallel noise layer (and all its tone controls) is fully bypassed.
Per-pad sympathetic-coupling weight is only audible when the kit-level Global Coupling/Snare Buzz/Tom Resonance are engaged; left at 0.5 neutral, irrelevant for a dry isolated woodblock.
Secondary shell is disabled (Secondary Enabled off, Coupling Strength 0); these configure a bank that never spins up.

defaultedParams.param: Tightness / Brightness / Body Size / Punch / Complexity (macros)
defaultedParams.why: Left at 0.5 neutral so the explicit per-param baseline above is preserved with zero macro delta; macros are a performance layer, not part of the static voicing.

physics: FUNDAMENTAL / PITCH: A woodblock is a single piece of teak/rosewood/maple hardwood with one or two longitudinal slit cavities acting as a resonator (Wikipedia; rareinstrument). It has INDEFINITE/relative pitch — larger block = lower, hi/lo variants are a size difference — but a clearly perceived bright resonant "tok" with a couple of strong low modes, perceptually in roughly the ~500 Hz–2.5 kHz register. PARTIAL STRUCTURE / INHARMONICITY: struck wood bars/blocks vibrate as free bodies, so partials are strongly INHARMONIC — the canonical free-free bar gives modes at 1 : 2.76 : 5.40 of the fundamental (Euphonics 3.3; ResearchGate "Basic physics of xylophone and marimba bars"); untuned wood is "naturally discordant", which is exactly the woodblock's character (a tuned xylophone bar is undercut to 1:3:6, a woodblock is NOT undercut so it keeps the raw inharmonic free-bar spread). The slit cavity adds resonance that "lowers the perceived body" and concentrates energy into a couple of strong low modes rather than a long tonal series (rareinstrument). DECAY / T60: "dry, short, bright, sharply defined… more tok than ring", "fast and clean" release, "no rattle, buzz, or loose after-sound" (rareinstrument) — i.e. a very short body T60, order tens of ms to ~100 ms, NOT a sustaining plate. TRANSIENT/CLICK: hard wood stick gives "the sharpest attack and bright projection", a "wooden crack/clack" with a "clean attack" and a "hollow center" from the cavity (Wikipedia; rareinstrument) — a sharp bright contact click is a defining feature. NOISE CONTENT: essentially none — the requirement is the ABSENCE of rattle/buzz/sizzle; broadband content is confined to the brief stick-contact click, not a sustained noise bed. PITCH GLIDE: none — woodblocks show no audible "kerthump"/tension glide (that is a tom/membrane effect); pitch-env stays disabled. MATERIAL: hardwood (teak/rosewood/maple) → woody, but it RINGS brightly, so high-frequency damping must be light enough to keep the bright tok (a fully woody b3 would dull it into a thud). These drive: Plate body + modeStretch≈0.50 for the inharmonic free-bar spread; small bright body (size≈0.18 hi / 0.28 lo → ~530/420 Hz f0 with the bright click/upper partials sitting above); short flat damping (b1≈25 s⁻¹ → ~40 ms floor) for the fast dry decay; light b3 (norm≈0.10) so the highs still ring; bright sharp Click (≈0.78 → ~4.9 kHz, ~2.4 ms); noise OFF.
